## Thread: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees — 56 this month / 59 total

_Thread started [2000-05](2000-05.md#t-1ae4b5a1da)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** "Robert Kovacic" <rjk@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<25iZ4.9890$c5.15927@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com> <20000531160517.08369.00000815@ng-cq1.aol.com>`

```
The local Merant office advised me that the ten pack licenses were the
minimum cost PER SITE and could NOT be broken up between customers.

Given their inconsistency and pricing policy we would be foolish to base any
more business plans on programs compiled with their NetExpress product. Once
burnt ...

Regards, Robert Kovacic.

Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000531160517.08369.00000815@ng-cq1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
>
> I received a call from Joe Beede who is a regional sales rep. for
NetExpress.
> According to Joe the runtime fees of NetExpress are going to be similar to
the
> UNIX fees.  A ten pack of licenses will cost $847.00 US dollars.  They
will
> however let you break up the ten pack between customers so that in effect
there
> will be a $84.70 US dollars license fee per user.  I explained to Joe that
the
> retail price of my product was under $1,000.00 US dollars and that I could
not
> afford to pay Merant 10% of the retail price of my product.  He is going
to
> talk with his boss and also send me the name of the person who is in
charge of
> the NetExpress product.
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393683a5.53486432@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com> <20000531160517.08369.00000815@ng-cq1.aol.com> <25iZ4.9890$c5.15927@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <8h4kf3$j9$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8h5eum$2c4h9$1@fu-berlin.de>`

```
On Thu, 1 Jun 2000 10:46:56 GMT, "Mayer Goldberg"
<gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il> wrote:

>
>On  1-Jun-2000, "Kenneth P. Mullins" <kenmullins@mindspring.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Fujitsu has no runtime fees for the normal distribution.  The do have
runtime fees for multi-threaded distribution ($40.00) and large
runtime fees for PowerBsort ($225).  

Fujitsu is geared toward PC development.  I can see runtime fees for
Mainframe Express -- where you offload development, but not
deployment.  Personally I HATE runtime fees.  

For PC targeted development I recommend Fujitsu

For Mainframe offloading, I recommend Ca-Realia Worbench
```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message
>
>Fujitsu has no runtime fees for the normal distribution.  The do have
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Speaking of multi-threading (on a PC), does anybody know how to package and
execute EXE files into/from a DLL?  I'd like to get rid of a whole lot of
Cobol EXE's in my distribution to tidy things up. I want to execute them,
though.  I do not want to call them. Placing them into the DLL is trivial,
as is calling them.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39368DFC.EF25CC5@fridaycs.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com> <20000531160517.08369.00000815@ng-cq1.aol.com>`

```
This is one of the reasons that I am choosing Fujitsu Cobol v5 and
Flexus sp2 to replace my very old MS-Cobol v4.5 DOS compiler. MS-Cobol
had no runtime licences, neither does Fujitsu and their annual
maintenance fees are also very reasonable.

As MS-Cobol was MF Cobol rebadged my 'natural' progression would be to
NetExpress. I looked at it, but didn't like the price, runtime
licencing, annual maintenance or the sales peoples (in UK) attitude.

I also looked at RM/Cobol and AcuCobol, both good products but also with
runtime licences. Both of these came out above NetExpress in my
evaluation because you could buy the parts of the system needed rather
than having to have the lot (unless Merant have changed this since I
last looked).

With an evaluation copy of Fujitsu v5 I have ported part of a system
(with help from this newsgroup and Fujitsu support) so I am happy that
the compiler will do the job with not too much rewriting.

Brian


Bob7536 wrote:
> 
> Good Afternoon,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16678860.a2089ff7@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Well.... This is a bunch of BS! I just got off the phone with
Leslie Carstons (Merant sales rep). We discussed this issue for
45 minutes and I think someone at Merant is starving for money.
I have about 120 clients using my software (not many compared to
others). Based on the $84/$85 per licence fee (and I don't know
if this is per year/monthly/weekly/daily or will you have to buy
another license for each NetExpress upgrade), it will cost my
clients $10,200. I provide working demos of MY software, do I
have to purchase a license for each demo using NetExpress 3.1?
(Leslie didn't think so, since the software is a demo, there
would be a charge). If demos are no charge, then what if I give
my software away, would I have to get a license?

What if I write a web application with NetExpress, do I have to
charge each person that accesses the web application a license
fee? (I know, this is absurd, but what if?). This may be the
solution to the problem, make MY software a web based
application that works on a server computer in my office and
just sell access to the web application to new clients.

What about the 'bugs' that are outstanding with NetExpress 3.0?
If I don't upgrade to NE 3.1, is Merant obligated to fix the
reported problems with NE 3.0?

I am really #$% !$#%& *(%^ (pardon me). I feel I have been taken
advantage of by Merant. Not only are they doing this, but they
don't even support a users conference any more.

Bah Humbug.

Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

This is indeed BS....And yes, they are taking advantage of the fact that you
use NetExpress and how difficult it is to convert products/applications to
another product...I would have abandoned them a long time ago, if I had not
had so many lines of code in existing applications and products...

BUT keep in mind new products can be produced with other cobol
compilers...Maybe even look into VB as an option...I'm installing it today
myself...Power Builder? Access?

At a minimum, it's time to shell out for another vendor's cobol compiler...

I'd call my sales rep, IF I NEW WHO HE/SHE WAS???

kenmullins
atlanta, ga

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** Raymond Leech <rayleech@home.xxx>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13a3a3e35fd46f12989680@news>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com> <16678860.a2089ff7@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`

```
Rich,
I've responded to a couple of your comments.
> another license for each NetExpress upgrade), it will cost my
> clients $10,200.
We are in some fixed-price, five-year contracts. It will cost my 
_company_ $128,000 to upgrade anytime before 2005 (assuming 10-packs 
cannot be split).

The web license is even more laughable. $10,000 is 2.5 times what I 
spend on each web server including hardware, OS and software. Its more 
than 2 times what it cost to write the app. Thank heavens its also an 
easy part to port to another vendor!!

Its hard enough to convince some people that COBOL is still a viable 
platform, harder to convince them that its economical even when it costs 
7 times more than VB and nearly impossible to justify an 11%-20% 
per/seat royalty is justified. Hopefully someone inside Merant still has 
their senses and drops this.

> I think someone at Merant is starving for money.
From this Apr 14 article, maybe this was the only way they could build 
profits. Maybe the royalties make up the other 1/3. I found this on 
quote.yahoo.com

"Merant said its fourth-quarter results will suffer at the hands of non-
recurring charges of between 6 million and 9 million pounds ($9.51 
million to $14.26 million) as a result of its planned business 
realignment. The company was upbeat on its prospects for the fiscal year 
2001, saying e-commerce would make up two-thirds of its business revenue 
by the end of that period."

----
rayleech@home.xxx -- replace xxx with com to reply
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8he5aj$8nb$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com> <16678860.a2089ff7@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <MPG.13a3a3e35fd46f12989680@news>`

```
I think they are going to be in real trouble if they do not clean up their
act.  It could even result in a few lawsuits.  If someone sold me a compiler
on the basis of it being royalty free on distribution, then changed the
rules drastically *after* I had paid my dollars and invested two or three
man years in software, I think I would see a lawyer at the same time that I
changed compilers.

It is a bit like selling a car, then after the purchase is paid for, telling
the customer that you have to buy gasoline at $100 per quart from the car
seller.  I doubt very much if it is legally enforceable if the original
purchase did not have the restriction.

Raymond Leech wrote in message ...
>Rich,
>I've responded to a couple of your comments.
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>rayleech@home.xxx -- replace xxx with com to reply
```

I'm not sure a lawsuit would work. No one 'forced' me to upgrade. I'm 
sure the deal would be 'upgrading indicates your acceptance...' or some 
such crud. There used to be a $50/seat fee (before discount) for some of 
the MF runtime stuff. It was a happy day when that was over. As someone 
else said, 'good things come to an end'... too bad. I thought they had 
finally seen the light of other PC tools with no or low runtimes, guess 
I called that one wrong.

I'd like to hear from people using another compiler. What are the "after 
purchase" fees (support, royalty, runtime, etc)? After almost 16 years 
with MF, maybe its time to review my options.

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

```
My sympathy to all,

It seems that one of the greatest Cobol suppliers decided to abandon Cobol
!!! Nice !!! And then we are talking about other (pure) languages and the
strength of Cobol and the new standard and OO and ... bla bla bla... while
on the other hand Sun gives for free JDK, JRE, Forte 4 Java , documentation,
on-line support ...bla bla bla
HOW THE HELL these people expect to attract new programmers and software
houses near to Cobol ????? Maybe on the next few days we will hear that
MERANT has built a powerful IDE for Java (IoI)

I am (and  i'm not the only one) very disappointed because after many years,
just for a moment, i thought that Cobol had a chance ....

I believe, now merant has done the begining, we should expect the  same
behavior from other vendors ...

To Thane : Ok. I will move on to Fujitsu. And what about my OO code ? Is
there any compatibility between the class libraries ? I don't think so ...
And you believe that Fujitsu will not charge the same after  2 years ? I
don't think so ...

So, people, let's go comp.lang.java

Wish luck to all of u
Costas
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3937d71c.140402226@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h7tps$q6i$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
On Fri, 2 Jun 2000 12:11:05 +0300, "Costas Giannoulis"
<diavasi@otenet.gr> wrote:

>My sympathy to all,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you use the "Standard" defined OO - the code is compatible.  If you
used Merants specific extensions it's not.

>So, people, let's go comp.lang.java
>
>

Java/2 requireds licensing.

Before we get all upset and abandon COBOL because of one vendors
idiocy - there are alternatives.

The"Closest" to MicroFocus COBOL is Fujitsu.
For Mainframe offloading - use Realia Workbench II.
Or you can try AcuCOBOL, Liant RM/COBOL (Both with Runtime Fees) or
Realis COBOL with no PC runtime fees on distribution.

I think Fujitsu will behave better in relation to runtime fees - but
they have already krept in with the runtime for the multi-threaded
applications.  However, the fees are REASONABLE (Max $40.00 with lower
numbers as volume increases).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I just received a call from John Billman, the NetExpress product
manager (he has taken this position over from Wayne Rippen). He
and I discussed the impact Merant's run-time fee will have on
my 'small' company. He will be recommending a restructuring of
the fee based on the sales price of the end user product. Oh
BTW, there is an annual maintenance fee on the run-time license
too! From this discussion, you would purchase a package of 10
licenses for US$870.00 and split the package between 10 clients,
so if you have 100 clients, it will cost you US$8,700.00. If I
heard John correctly, the maintenance on the package (again 10)
is US$170, so, for 100 clients, you would be looking at US$1700
annually.

A question to Merant, what will Merant do to help market my
product so 'they' and I will make the run-time license fee
profitable? If I put forth the effort to market and close sales
and all of the necessary steps to sell one package, they should
also participate in that risk. Why should Merant reap the
benefits of the work I perform?

I also asked of John, how are other COBOL vendors approaching
the license fee process and he indicated that most vendors do
charge a runtime fee (Fujitsu in Japan for example)? He
indicated that Merant (Micro Focus) has been charging a run-time
fee in Europe for many years and it is a part of the package and
those users in Europe have structured their pricing accordingly.

So my question (to the list using NetExpress), if we move from
Merant NetExpress to another COBOL compiler, what would prevent
that new COBOL vendor, not currently charging a run-time license
fee, to start  charging a fee? I am sure the answer is, there is
NOTHING to prevent a COBOL vendor to start charging a run-time
fee!

Thanks for allowing me to express my views.

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

Then why not just stay at NetExpress v3.0 or 3.01?  That's what I'm
going to do.  I have been dealing with MF / Merant for a number of years
now and one thing I've learned is this - they want to make a buck no
matter what they need to do.  Whether it's selling someone a product
that they don't need or will never use, or changing their policy on
run-time fees, the end result is the same - how much money can they
make?

Quite frankly, I am VERY surprised when I learned that basic parts of
the v3.0 RTS was royalty free!  I couldn't believe it, then I read the
license agreement for myself - I was very pleased, thinking that they
(MF / Merant) had turned over a new leaf.  Alas, all good things come to
an end no matter  - no matter how short-lived they are!

TL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** mojo@chesapeake.net (Mike Madara)
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sjnui27j5ri183@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`

```
Per Rich's points made below:

1)  There's nothing to stop the other compiler companies from
      charging runtime fees.  Fujitsu programmers, what are the 
      runtime fees, if any, associated with their product?  But, I 
      think the  major point per Merant in this thread is that an 
      add-on of $87 is too much for small developers to pass on to 
      their customers.

2)  What motivated Merant to change the runtime fee structure
      in NetExpress 3.0 to the new one in v 3.1?   Is it that they
      want to get out of the standalone/small developer  business and

      only want to support the 'big guys' that have large programming 
      shops, large amounts of users on  the same servier and large
      amounts of bucks to spend?

3)  Are we sure we can 'split' the 10 runtime license pack to 
     mutilple servers/PCs?  I've seen conflicting info in the 
     newsgroup.  At last count, I had about 350 users for my app, and 
     in most cases they were running on different servers or PCs.

4)  A couple of folks have brought up the point of sticking
     with Workbench 4.0 or NetExpress 3.0.  For the most
     part, I totally agree.  The only problem, as I see it, is that
     they won't be updated to any new standard (2002?) that
     comes out.  This might be a problem in my shop since
     the  'standards police' want us to keep current with
     the  ANSI standard whenever possible.

5)  I 'grew up' using Micro Focus COBOL products to develop
     apps for PCs and unix boxes.  And overall, I've been very
     happy with the products and their capabilities.  I'd hate
     to change after using their tools for 13 years, but the bottom
     line is the bottom line.  For everybody.  How much heartburn
     could one expect porting an Ansi '85 Merant COBOL app
     to Fujitsu?

6)  Plus,  I'm still pissed off they did away with the annual users
     conference!!!  :)  :)  :)

Mike Madara
Prince Frederick, MD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000605195819.25126.00000864@ng-me1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<16260340.f3cf197d@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I spoke with John Billman this afternoon who is the NetExpress product manager
as Rich had mentioned earlier.  From talking with John this new pricing plan is
not cast in stone and people at Merant are listening to what the developers
have to say.  John mentioned a number of other ideas that others in this thread
have also mentioned (i.e. a percentage of the retail instead of a set fee). 
John also mentioned something that was noted here before that I was unaware of
the fact there could be an ongoing maintenance charge for the end user runtime
license.  He mentioned that this has been about 21% of the original runtime
fee.  There is not way the my company can afford to spend $18.00 ( 84.70 * .21)
per year per end user.  I think the people at Merant are beginning to
understand that small developers can not afford to spend a one time fee of
$84.70 plus a yearly fee of $18.00 per customer.  From speaking with John it
appears that most compilier companies charge runtimes fees outside of the US. 
For instance I am told Fujistu charges a runtime fee in Japan.  Could some of
our international developers please comment ?  In closing I believe they are
starting to listen.  I am enclosing the e-mail addresses of the top people in
Merant management.  If you have not done so please e-mail them with your
concerns.

Gary Greenfield
President of Merant

Panos Anastassiadis
V.P. World Wide Distribution 

Tony Hill
VP and General Manger of ACT (MicroFocus)

You may call them at 1-800-582-1600 or e-mail them at
firstname.lastname@merant.com

Please be respect in all communications with them and lets try and get them to
change this decision.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Robert Kovacic" <rjk@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_CX_4.14171$c5.23210@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`

```
I think one of the fundamental issues with Runtime Fees is their impostion
mid-stream. ie. No company should be put in a position where it develops a
business plan, commits many man-years and $'s of development and support
resources and then finds that it is financially unviable because of a large
new overhead cost per product (viz. runtime fees). Continually changing this
cost based on how loudly developers scream also does nothing for a
sustainable business plan.

I have decided to go with Fujitsu COBOL (which we were already using for
some small projects) based on their "commitment" in the Frequently Asked
Questions at http://www.adtools.com/products/windows/cobol.htm#8.
However after seeing some other messages in this newsgroup which indicate
they do apply runtime fees in Japan, and their recent imposition of runtime
fees on threaded faciliites, I am reconsidering.

Unless a compiler vendor such as Fujitsu can respond positively to this
issue I am afraid that COBOL development on PC's will die completely and our
only choice will be to move to languages such as Visual C, Visual Basic,
Delphi, etc.

With these other languages I believe that any attempt at imposing runtime
fees similar to COBOL (ie. for simple compiled programs - NOT for add-on
products) would be howled down by a storm of protest. These other languages
may be the only viable way for small software developers to survive and
prosper.

A copy of this has been sent to suppport@adtools.com (Fujitsu) and I would
urge them to also send a response to this newsgroup (comp.lang.cobol).

Regards, Robert Kovacic.

Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid> wrote in message
news:00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com...
> I just received a call from John Billman, the NetExpress product
> manager (he has taken this position over from Wayne Rippen). He
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…

<_CX_4.14171$c5.23210@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>...
>I think one of the fundamental issues with Runtime Fees is their impostion

this
>cost based on how loudly developers scream also does nothing for a

Yes.  I know of no other language that imposes this kind of nonsense ... it
is close to making me rethink Cobol.

It is not just the problem of putting a product into the too expensive
realm.  There is also the problem of demo's, and the problem of any kind of
shareware.  In effect, no MF Cobol project can ever be given away, or
demonstrated on a customer machine.

It is doubtful that anybody would ever buy an expensive software package
without seeing it. Merant's policy makes it impossible for me to make a CD
and send it to a customer.  That means it is unsellable. The package can
only be used "in-house".

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`

```
On Mon, 05 Jun 2000 08:29:37 -0700, Rich Rohde
<richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid> wrote:

>
>A question to Merant, what will Merant do to help market my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is conjecture - but I think you can see this.  For your
development tool's maint fee, you get support for your devleopment.
However, when you deploy in the field and you find something that just
doesn't work - you call Merant for support.  They are now supporting
your in the field install of their runtime.  I do think they might
deserve some money for this.

FOR THE RECORD - I HATE RUNTIME FEES.  However, that said, I am also a
reasonable person.  Here is the letter *I* sent to Merant:


Good Day Gentlemen,

First let me introduce myself.  My name is Thane Hubbell and I am 
the author of Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.  In 
addition, I've found myself in the position of being a major COBOL 
apologist in the internet community.  I'm a COBOL advocate and 
enthusiast.  My articles have appeared in IEEE Software 
magazine, and in "The COBOL Report".  

I am employed full time as a COBOL programmer, but also do 
development work and consulting on the side.  I have a couple of 
end user software systems written in COBOL that sell for between 
$50.00 and $500.00.  

Previously I worked for a Retail Store service company that utilized 
MicroFocus Work Bench and Dialog System in their product 
development.  I've closely watched Micro Focus over the years 
(having been a user of the 1.1 product through NetExpress 2.0).  

I'm writing to you, as I am sure you have guessed, about the 
apparently new -- or perhaps only clarified -- Runtime Fee policy 
with respect to distributing applications developed using 
NetExpress.  

Let me explain that I do not presently utilize Merant products for 
my development.  I am simply writing because I feel that the recent 
actions are bad for COBOL in general.  Merant is the recognized 
World leader in developing COBOL tools.  As goes Merant, so 
goes the industry.  In my development I use Ca-Realia COBOL and 
Fujitsu COBOL - both offering Royalty free runtime systems.  
Considering my price point, this is a necessity.

However, as an individual who desires that COBOL continues to 
thrive, I can recognize the need for a continuing revenue stream for 
COBOL tool developers.  Many achieve this by maintenance fees.  
However, many small developers such as myself, often find 
themselves not renewing maintenance, in a cost savings measure.  

I would like to offer you a solution to these problems that is fair
and 
equitable and that should be accepted by any of your reasonable 
customers.  

As I mentioned at the beginning of my letter, I am a book author.  I 
get paid for my work via a Royalty.  I get a certain percentage of 
the unit cost of my book when a retailer purchases it for resale.  

Rather than a fixed price for your runtime system, I would like to 
recommend that you move to a percentage based royalty solution.  
This would allow small developers and large developers alike to 
share their success using your products to develop software, with 
you - the provider of the development software.  Charge a 5% 
runtime fee based on the selling price of the software that is 
developed with your tools.  If the developer charges a yearly 
maintenance fee, you would get 5% of this fee as well.  Cap the 
high end of the fee for extremely large software sales if larger price

point customers are concerned.  Continue to charge your yearly 
maintenance fees to support the developers.  The continuing 
Royalty revenue will be good for your company, and will NOT be a 
severe burden on existing or new customers.  

One concern I have heard voiced is in the area of upgrades for 
existing customers of the developers.  With software deployed that 
is paid for by the end user already, the developer cannot expect 
these end users to pay for the new runtime fee.  I recommend 
waiving the fee for those users until such time as their customers 
pay an upgrade or maintenance fee, at which point you would 
receive your royalty percentage.

The only area this leaves uncovered is in Web based or internet 
based applications.  If these applications are in house, I would 
charge a per server runtime royalty based on the actual usage or 
capacity of the server.  If the application is an ASP type of 
application where the users pay to access it, I would revert to the 
per end customer royalty percentage.  If the application is a free 
application accessed on the internet by the public, I would waive 
the runtime fee -- the company is not receiving any direct revenue 
from the product.

In closing, I hope you find my ideas useful and adoptable.  I think 
you will find widespread acceptance where currently you are finding 
that you have alienated a large portion of the COBOL development 
community.

Thank you for your time in reading my thoughts.

Respectfully,

Thane Hubbell
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** Raymond Leech <ray@noisp.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13a6339b2d2cd3e5989682@news>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane,
I agree Merant should be paid for 'field support' but I think the per-
incident phone support covers this. If something goes wrong in the field 
and I cant reproduce it locally, calling for support is usually the last 
thing on my list. Now if Merant were offering to take the calls for me, 
the $85 would be a steal!!!

For example, just today a client who is still using a 4.0.38 build of 
the product found a problem. After examing everything, it looks like the 
compiler simply generated some bad object code. Works great as INT but 
fails as GNT or OBJ. Examined the data at every stage with a hexeditor 
and the variable is zero (x"30"). But in the code fragment below, 
"statement" gets executed every time unless running as INT. Regardless 
if the client or I found it, a bug is a bug. Now, had I missed a period 
somewhere (or something else I did wrong), then I'd say charge me the 
phone support fee for my mistake. In my mind, that certainly doesnt 
justify a royalty.

if variable = 2 or variable = 3
   statement
end-if

By the way, change it to the following and it works great in INT, GNT or 
OBJ.
if variable = 2
   statement
end-if
if variable = 3
   statement
end-if


In article <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com>, 
thaneH@softwaresimple.com says...
> This is conjecture - but I think you can see this.  For your
> development tool's maint fee, you get support for your devleopment.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> deserve some money for this.
---
rayleech@home.xxx -- replace xxx with com to reply
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Norman Juers" <agri@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DHC%4.1954$oH1.196199@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com>`

```

The problem with using a percentage of the sale price is trying to establish
the sale price.  We sell a service where we give away the software as part
of our service.  What is the price of the software in this case.  I'm sure
that MERANT would not be happy with 5% of nothing.  We charge for annual
maintenance on our product but not all of that is for the software.  Part is
to cover support.  I'm not going to give 5% of my support revenue to MERANT.
I don't believe that software products should be subject to an annual fee.
I would agree that a percentage is preferable to a fee of $87.40.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3947CBF4.C794299F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 05 Jun 2000 08:29:37 -0700, Rich Rohde
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Good Day Gentlemen....... <Snip>

Just read this one Thane - I just think it is too damn complicated. This
is providing a compromise solution to a problem which shouldn't have
happened. I don't know the practicalities of this, but it seems to me
that a more realistic and simpler approach would be to charge an
up-front fee for the development suite, a cost per module.

Take NetExpress as it currently stands, (and this is not an exhaustive
list) :-

- Base compiler (includes 'standard' features - Screen handling,
flat     files, Win APIs etc. - and the IDE and Animator)
- OO and OO GUIs
- Dialog System
- Fileshare
- Web stuff (Internet, HTML, ActiveX, Java etc.. )
- etc.......

Have a pricing structure for each of the modules. Currently I use :-

- Base compiler
- OO and OO GUIs

If I want to get into Fileshare and Web stuff then obviously I'm
producing more than just a simple one-off system for a single client.
Merant would be justified in charging me an up-front fee for these
additional modules; reflect this is how they did it in DOS with Toolbox
etc.. - and on the basis that the end-user is wanting multi-user or Web
access I am also justified in passing on these costs as part of my
charges to him/her.

From the Merant perspective if I'm only using the modules I've indicated
then they only have to offer me technical support for those specific
modules. (Other than my initial queries about OO GUIs when I first
started - they currently don't get any questions from me on the
Answerlab. I get the sort of answers I want from this NewsGroup).

I noticed that Howard took a slightly 'holier than thou' attitude about
runtime fees. It's OK for you buster, the U of C pays for the software
you use !

Meanwhile this all blew up while I was in UK - and I haven't received a
notification that Version 3.1 was available, although I knew it was
imminent. So Bob or Rich, ignoring the sales blurb about V3.1 on the
Merant site, if you have it, care to send me a copy of the readme file
telling me what if any new features there are that would justify my
upgrading to V3.1 - and oh yes - any dollars involved over and above the
very questionable runtime fees ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8ia8o7$4pb$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com> <3947CBF4.C794299F@home.com>`

```
James,

If you existing Net Express 3.0 product is under a MERANT
maintenance contract then there is no charge for upgrading
to Net Express 3.1.  Simply contact your local MERANT office
and they will arrange for the product to be sent to you.

A summary of the new features are:

COBOL and Java interoperability.
Support for Enterprise Java Beans (EJB).
Combination class support.
OLE automation enhancements.
SQL Option for DB2.
OpenESQL enhancements.
Large file support in the data tools.
Production debugging and diagnostic tools.
Animator enhancements.
Dialog System enhancements.
Revolve integration.
Support for Microsoft Termninal Server.
Support for IBM WebSphere 3.0 Enterprise Edition.
Support for Microsoft Windows 2000.
Support for Microsoft Internet Explorer 5.
Support for Siemens BS2000 COBOL dialect.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<056faa27.118af227@usw-ex0105-040.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com> <3947CBF4.C794299F@home.com> <8ia8o7$4pb$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Paddy,

You neglected to mention that many fixes (including at least
6 'cases' I reported) to NE 3.0 are included with NE 3.1 NOT
3.0, but since there is a license fee to distribute NE 3.1, I
just flushed a years worth of maintaince fees down the toilet.
My maintenance agreement comes due next month and I will
seriously consider dropping support due to Merants effort to
weed out the small users of NetExpress.

I have spent most of my effort discussing the lack of printing
support with the PC_PRINT... library routines and many of those
changes have now been implemented, but with 3.1 not 3.0.

I believe I have arrived to the conclusion Merant should wave
runtime fees for companies using NetExpress prior to NE 3.1. My
reasoning is that we have de-bugged NetExpress and have given
direction for the enhancements you have listed.

One major concern about NetExpress 3.1, the product did not go
through the beta testing 3.0 had gone through and with the
problems 3.0 had should we be concerned about the quality of 3.1?

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8icube$f60$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8h8ot2$s6i$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20000602132945.29307.00001515@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8h8slr$8so$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8h99em$a86$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <00930600.8b699499@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <393c5b2a.83927431@news.cox-internet.com> <3947CBF4.C794299F@home.com> <8ia8o7$4pb$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <056faa27.118af227@usw-ex0105-040.remarq.com>`

```
Rich,

I cannot comment on your particular fixes.  However, all new releases of
software
contain fixes.  I know that Microsoft have fixed a few things in Windows
2000 and
that these issues have not and probably will not be addressed in NT 4.0.

I can honestly say that I am not aware of any "effort" to weed out small
users
of Net Express.  My Team supports Customers of varying sizes (in EMEA) and
we aim to provide the best level of Customer care we can.  At present my
department has the highest Customer satisfaction rating in MERANT (>90%).

We agree that you should have access to the latest fixes and features.  That
is why
we provide new releases of software to Customers on maintenance for no
additional charge.

As for runtime charges - see Tony Hill's (my big boss <g>) previous posting.

As I said above, my Team supports Net Express on a daily basis.  Net Express
3.1 has been available since May 11th and we have not had any major problems
with it.  So far the quality looks very good and seems to build on 3.0.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International

## Thread: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea? — 47 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
I was talking to someone the other day who was bemoaning the fact that there is
so much competition for new grads and those newbies are demanding bucks that
this person doesn't believe they deserve because they haven't proven themselves
etc etc (you get the drift).

Now everyone has heard that COBOL is dying etc etc and us old war horses that
survived the Y2K stuff should be looking to go out to pasture etc etc... so we
talked some more and wondered if this would be an interesting proposition:

This is for COBOL programmers with about 20 - 25 year experience.  A job that
would start at about 65K, full bennies etc etc. You would be sent to school
full time (at full pay) to learn the latest in JAVA, HTML, OO, WebSphere, etc
etc for between 3 - 9 months (depending on how many courses and so on) then off
to work you go.  Ok - there's a catch - you have to commit to 2 years after
training, you may have to relocate, and LOTS of travel.

What are peoples thoughts?

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3935F9C8.E162F76D@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> This is for COBOL programmers with about 20 - 25 year experience.  A job that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> training, you may have to relocate, and LOTS of travel.

I'll pass, thanks (not enough money & too much travel).

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UrqZ4.2811$HD6.52169@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>This is for COBOL programmers with about 20 - 25 year experience.  A job that
>would start at about 65K, full bennies etc etc. You would be sent to school
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>What are peoples thoughts?

Hmmmmmmm... the rate seems a bit low (my views are... 'tainted' by my
being near Washington, DC  USA) and I wonder, given things like
telecommuting, about the necessity for relocation/travel (folks 40+ may
look at this differently than folks who are younger... the oldsters may

DD

#### ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8jceai.hon.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
On 01 Jun 2000 03:26:38 GMT, YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote:
	[snip]
>This is for COBOL programmers with about 20 - 25 year experience.  A job that
>would start at about 65K, full bennies etc etc. You would be sent to school
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>training, you may have to relocate, and LOTS of travel.

Personally, I don't like it at all. Also, I'm wondering who is going to be
maintaining the "legacy" systems that keep the company going while all the
experienced COBOL programmers are doing all this "neat" stuff? Newer 
companies without any legacy systems won't go for it. They seem to want
young "hot shots" who are inexpensive. The fact that their systems may
have "holes" in them (such as backup/recovery, scalability, and security)
don't seem to be important. That's due to the companies' inexperience. 

For established companies, if they are stupid enough to think that
JAVA, Visual Basic, et al. are going to replace all their COBOL applications,
then I don't want to work for them. Their likely to go bankrupt trying to do
such a conversion. But I will admit that they'll have a better chance of 
doing it if they train the current "dinosaurs" rather than some inexperienced
newbie from college. And I really hope that my bank doesn't get this idea.
If I knew that they had such plans, I'd take my money and put it in my
mattress. It would be much safer!

Just my thoughts,
John

Oh, just to balance things out a bit. I do think that there are places
for things such a VB, JAVA, et al. I just don't think that the
"back office" functions such as accounting are a good place for them.
For "front office" things such as simply inquiries or data entry, they
have some nice facilities.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39366203.FFFDF492@cusys.edu>`

```

> 
> Personally, I don't like it at all. Also, I'm wondering who is going to be
> maintaining the "legacy" systems that keep the company going while all the
> experienced COBOL programmers are doing all this "neat" stuff? 

At least this way they keep the COBOL programmers on site, even if they
are 6 months doing the vital stuff and 6 months doing the "neat"
stuff.   To keep technology people happy, they need to offer chances to
grow.

Create career plans and paths and then don't betray the workers later.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8jdqq3.ja6.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com> <slrn8jceai.hon.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net> <393685cf.54040859@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
On Thu, 01 Jun 2000 15:49:15 GMT, Thane Hubbell 
<thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
	[snip]
>I may be reading into things but I think this was in an effort to make
>"room" for entry level COBOL programmers.  I find the idea very
>interesting.

Ah, I had not read it that way. In that case, yes. Actually, I think that
I futher misunderstood. For some reason (brain rot?) I thought the original
post was about old-timers more or less abandoning COBOL and taking a pay
cut for the "privilege" of travelling & learning "bleading edge" technology.
I don't know how many people in their 40s or older still think that travelling
is really neat. Some travel is OK, but a large amount is bothersome to me.
Maybe I'm just not flexible. But then again, I have taught myself the basics of
C, C++, Perl, and Python. I might get into HTML & PHP, but that would be
dangerous. Why? Because there is some interest at work about a Web server
on the mainframe (S/390 running OS/390). If it is known that I have some
experience in this area, I get that responsibility ADDED TO my current ones.
Not replacing, adding onto! I have enough to do and it has been many 
years since I thought that working until 10 pm or later was "neat" and "fun".

As for doing stuff on Microsoft OS's, not really interested. I just don't
view them as production quality. They are just to easy to crash and corrupt
if I make a stupid mistake. And I do make stupid mistakes sometimes.

John
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39366106.4F7A3C0E@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama wrote:
> 
> This is for COBOL programmers with about 20 - 25 year experience.  A job that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> training, you may have to relocate, and LOTS of travel.

I won't travel anymore.

It is not hard to get a job where the company will promise that you will
get experience in new technologies in 6 months to a year.  But once you
get that job, they keep finding reasons to keep you where they need you
the most - in COBOL.  Ends up that they don't keep you as you don't
trust the company anymore.

Companies need to make plans and budgets designed around keeping skilled
workers.  This should include education and above else, tours of duty. 
Come June 1, even if your project is behind, you will be reassigned to
the Java team for 6 months.  Certainly it is better to loose you down
the hall, where they can find you, and where you will return in 6
months, than to lose you across town.

So many companies lie about their offers of new technology - or have
wishful thinking that COBOL programmers won't be as vital in 6 months
that they will be able to treat them honestly, that we have stopped
believing them.  Finally when we get near retirement, we stop caring,
but the companies are screwing themselves worse than they are screwing
us.
```

<Major Snippage>

OK, let's see. 20-25 years experience, should put you in your early to
mid fourties (at the very least). Most "dinos" that I know would not
touch this, simply because of the "Unknowns" as well as the salary (way
too low), long-term committment and travel (ever been re-routed through
Bulgaria ?).

The only way to do this RIGHT is with a written contract, so there are
NO surprises or any "Oh, by the way" schemes down the road. Get a
lawyer involved, regardless whether this is a salaried position or not.
(A salaried position should not necessarily prohibit a contractual
agreement). Judging all the "fluff" being spewed here, you'd better
protect yourself in some way, shape or form as these "promises" have a
nasty way of losing their direction and definition.

If they sidestep the contract and get annoyed or question your
intentions (you may not play well with the other children), then
they're NOT for real and fully committed to YOU (although YOU are
supposed to be fully committed to THEM) and you'll wind up getting
screwed (IMHO). It is then time to graciously exit, stage left.

Seen too many of these put in front of me and turned them away. If it
looks to good to be true, it probably is.

All of this stuff is great and wonderful, providing it's legally
binding. Talk is cheap.

I hope this is NOT a Marketing Firm ! (RUN don't WALK if this is the
case).

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

>In article <20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>,

>
What kind of "dinos" do you know. I am in my mid 50's and love all new
stuff. Sad to tell you, that very few newcomers (less 5 years of experience)
impress me much. They are very afraid of the "unknown", because of
commitment to wife, children, pets, friends and neighbours.

I can tell you, that the last 5-10 years of my professional life will be
fun, fun and fun only. Doing exactly as I please is the most important to
me. I am very eager to learn and I will gladly pay for wisdom.

In this business you will find many old rock'n rollers, protest singers and
hippies. The difference from then till now is that we are rich, that we
insist to have fun, and we are not afraid of anything.

We will retire, when we cannot match the youngsters on equal terms. They
haven't got a chance!
:-))))

Ib
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "brucepbarrett" <brucepbarrett@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#v1qSQZz$GA.432@cpmsnbbsa08>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com> <8h6raj$ksl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8hbhr4$k59$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8hbja7$mle$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Sent one these messages to a friend of mine the other day.  Here are his
thoughts.



Bruce,
     If the old war-horses leave this arena, who in the hell will maintain
the old mainframe
software.  With Y2K we did not get rid of legacy systems, only made
management aware of
our old feeble hands that are around their testicles.  New COBOL software
is still being
written each day.  Do you want to trust your bank statement or pension
check to the "newbie"
that are coming out of college today.  Their idea of testing is passing 10
records through the
program to see if it works.  I'm not even sure that they know what a "hex
dump" or "Hex output
mode" really is all about.  With the mainframe you actually have to think
like "the machine"
and in most cases have to out think it.  I'm  afraid that the old war-horse
will be very disappointed
when they see how the "newbies" are doing T&E (Trail and Error) testing.
When I was in
production support we used to joke about "If it runs, it's production; if
not, it's a test".  I've
seen over the years that almost all new college grads hitting the work
force feel this way.

Thank goodness we have enough experienced people in the work force to carry
these
newbie,  till they know what in the world the field of computers is all
about.  I have seen
this to be especially true of the foreign programmer.  They seen to feel
that they have to
show the native US programmer that they are so great and that they know
everything.
The old war-horses realizes that they only know a small amount and that
staying and doing
the job that is necessary, their skills will get old and obsolete.  The
industry is in such a
growth pattern.  We need these heroes, but as soon as they have
accomplished what is
needed, do we thank them and reward them and retrain them for the next
large project?
Heck no,  we'll just bring in a newbie that I can hire for much less and
will let the old programs
and processing carry the new projects which are having double over-runs on
their cost
and 1-2 years late on their deliverables.

This may be happening because the management of most companies is the new
MBA
type of professional.  I have a feeling that we are in for some very
serious times in the
fairly near future.  If you remember even the Navy brought back out of
retirement one of
the old war-horses when they re-commissioned Grace Hopper.  This may happen
again, but most of us old war-horse have one common attitude "screw you -
you
shafted me one, not twice"

egj






"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:8hbja7$mle$1@news.igs.net...
> Old age and treachery will overcome youth and enthusiasm any day.
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> >In this business you will find many old rock'n rollers, protest singers
and
> >hippies. The difference from then till now is that we are rich, that we
> >insist to have fun, and we are not afraid of anything.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hcgbi01ejt@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com> <8h6raj$ksl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8hbhr4$k59$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8hbja7$mle$1@news.igs.net> <#v1qSQZz$GA.432@cpmsnbbsa08>`

```
I am "just out of college" but doing very well at my job thank you.  I 
started programming on an Atari 400 when I was 8(?) years old.  There was a
magazine called COMPUTE! and it had programs in it that you could key in.
After I got them to run I would record them onto my 410 tape drive.  I
thought that was so cool.  When my grandparents bought me a disk drive a
couple years later, well I just about shit myself, and haven't turned back
since.

I know what a hex dump is and what Hex output mode (set mode x in ROSCOE) is
also and know how to get down to the bit.  I'm not trying to be arrogant.
Experience is necessary for sure, but just because someone is just out of
college doesn't mean they can't be a good programmer.

I'm sure Thane and Judson and Donald and IB and Howard and (maybe) DocDwarf
and so on have probably always been programmers.  You mean to tell me that
you all sucked when you were in your early 20s?

Thank you for your time.

Jeff

----------

<brucepbarrett@email.msn.com> wrote:

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com> <8h6raj$ksl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8hbhr4$k59$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8hbja7$mle$1@news.igs.net> <#v1qSQZz$GA.432@cpmsnbbsa08> <8hcgbi01ejt@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I am "just out of college" but doing very well at my job thank you.  I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> since.

I subscribed to that magazine as well.
 
> I know what a hex dump is and what Hex output mode (set mode x in ROSCOE) is
> also and know how to get down to the bit.  I'm not trying to be arrogant.
> Experience is necessary for sure, but just because someone is just out of
> college doesn't mean they can't be a good programmer.

Certainly not - but the operative word here is "can".
 
> I'm sure Thane and Judson and Donald and IB and Howard and (maybe) DocDwarf
> and so on have probably always been programmers.  You mean to tell me that
> you all sucked when you were in your early 20s?

You would be surprised on how many programmers "suck".  The advantage of
a newbie is that many of them can be molded, if we give them a chance to
work with good and bad code.   You really can't KNOW what is good unless
you have discovered for yourself in the real world what causes problems
and what works.

I happened to take a break from programming in my early 20s thanks to
the USAF.  But my suspicion is that my coding was what I would not like
to be maintaining today.  At least you guys are taught structure, we
weren't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i2krj$43g$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com> <#v1qSQZz$GA.432@cpmsnbbsa08> <8hbqu5$qn4$1@news.igs.net> <8hd7fc$rep$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <31r_4.3400$HD6.71194@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
I've been off line for a couple of days (urgent matters). Sorry for the late
answer.

Yes, many people like to think back in time, but read the thread. My views
are about today. I have seen the changes in committment to the job/project
from young newcomers, and I am not impressed.

In one project I urged the management to hire 5 young men and women directly
from school. I thought that a mix of old and young project-members would
give us nice working conditions, and we would give at least 5 young people a
good start. We had to say goodbye to 3 of them.

 Everyone I talk to has similar expieriences. Too many of the hopeful
youngsters are not qualified or prepared to work in teams or fixed
assignments. They read PC-World and Computerworld, and they talk, but it is
very demanding for them to sit down and do what they are paid to do.

On the other hand. When you are lucky, you will see youngsters, who can
produce a large amount of work - even after just 2 weeks of introduction.
The 2 young people in my example won our respect. In fact, when the talk
about salary were brought up in the project, we all told the management to
put our raise in a pool and give it to our 2 newcomers as a reward. At the
end of the project they had a salary close to ours because they had earned
it.

I am not an enemy to young people in the business, but my expierience tells
me that many (more than I like) are lazy, selfish and poorly educated. We
can brush up their education when we get them, we expect to do that, but
their parents should have learned them something about good social
behaviour.

Still, I recommend to take 20-30 % newcomers into new projects. You give
them a chance and even when you end up with less, you have done something
for the continuity of your business.

Long story, but as said before: "when I cannot match them, I'll retire and
spend all my money on wine, women and gambling".

Regards
Ib

NA skrev i meddelelsen <31r_4.3400$HD6.71194@iad-read.news.verio.net>...

Ib Tanding wrote:
>

> 
Ib,

I kinda ignored this thread but picked up on your message for a
sampling.

Having just returned from UK visiting favourite cousin, her husband used
the phrase "dumbing down", (i.e. lowering down to the lowest
denominator) when referring to both education, entertainment(TV) and
politics in UK.

Here in N. America, I am seriously pondering whether I will renew my
subscription to Time Magazine - as somebody called it accurately a
couple of years back, it is 'Infotainment' - and it is getting worse.
Any coverage of US politics is superficial with the exception of Billy
Boy's escapades in the White House.

My end-user has complained of youngsters' work ethics in the same terms
that you did.

Kinda looks like all that supreme effort to fight for and retain
democracy in mid-20th century has been dumbed-down by a universal
cock-eyed attitude to education and culture.

Jimmy

I was merely pointing out, old boy, that I for one (next year I will
hit 50) am sick and tired of globe hopping, listening to all the
promises and am quite sceptic of a deal such as this and many others.

Yes, learning new technology would be a tremendous benefit, but this
deal is a crap shoot, anyway you look at it.

It has never crossed my mind that technology would out pace my
abilities and I'm not worried at all right now nor for the future.

We as old war horses will have PLENTY to do down the road, so I
wouldn't worry about much it for now. I'm at the moment mastering
something called "MQ" (I'm sure this sounds familiar). FWIW, having the
ability to understand and implement cross-platform methodology is where
it's at.

Don't worry, be greedy....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta

#### ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hb826$7kl$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
Eileen,
I think that many oldies could benefit from your idea. I imagine that many
very experienced programmers are sitting in a locked position - still a lot
to do with Cobol, keeping existing business running, but new and exiting
projects are given away to others.

This could bring these oldies in focus again, combining long experience with
popular skills, we could call it 'value added development'.

If someone like to be busy en the next few years, Europe will offer many
jobs. Converting national currency to EURO will for some countries be a huge
task - probably 4-5 times more demanding than Y2K. All systems must be
changed, because national currency must be shown together with the new
common European currency. All calculations must me done with 6 decimals to
optain a satisfactory precission.

That's why I think that some programmers might be able to find a job in
Europe, don't you.

Regards
Ib
YukonMama skrev i meddelelsen
<20000531232638.14039.00001213@ng-cl1.aol.com>...
>I was talking to someone the other day who was bemoaning the fact that
there is

that
>this person doesn't believe they deserve because they haven't proven
themselves
>etc etc (you get the drift).
>
>Now everyone has heard that COBOL is dying etc etc and us old war horses
that
>survived the Y2K stuff should be looking to go out to pasture etc etc... so
we
>talked some more and wondered if this would be an interesting proposition:
>
>This is for COBOL programmers with about 20 - 25 year experience.  A job
that
>would start at about 65K, full bennies etc etc. You would be sent to school
>full time (at full pay) to learn the latest in JAVA, HTML, OO, WebSphere,
etc
>etc for between 3 - 9 months (depending on how many courses and so on) then
off
>to work you go.  Ok - there's a catch - you have to commit to 2 years after
>training, you may have to relocate, and LOTS of travel.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Eileen

some remarked the salary was too low this was sorta taken from the area in
which I live which has a cost of living much much lower than the DC area :). 
Telecommuting isn't much used in these parts either. Something else to be added
to the concept.

Thanks for the input all.

Eileen

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hbi0v$mb0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8hb826$7kl$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <20000603124737.13997.00001359@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
One of the big questions that I would have is regarding the "lots of
travel".  Would the budget include enough "hometrips" that I could maintain
this residence for the two year period?

I think the biggest factor for an older, single programmer is that, unlike a
beginner, they have established roots.  That means an arrangement that makes
it possible to live in one country, for example, and work in another.

Next year, for example, I am going to need a pro for the best part of a year
in Europe.  How do I do it?  A European resident that is freelance, a North
American resident that likes Europe, or a beginner that will move there for
a year, while I do the travel to supervise?

YukonMama wrote in message <20000603124737.13997.00001359@ng-cl1.aol.com>...
>Just for the record this is NOT for some marketing or consulting firm :)
While
>some remarked the salary was too low this was sorta taken from the area in
>which I live which has a cost of living much much lower than the DC area
:).
>Telecommuting isn't much used in these parts either. Something else to be
added
>to the concept.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>(who's still having major problems with Deja.com)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Good Idea? Bad Idea?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hd9as$182$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8hb826$7kl$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <20000603124737.13997.00001359@ng-cl1.aol.com> <8hbi0v$mb0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I think it is important to evaluate if your one year in Europe will give you
more work over here in the years to come. A freelancer can be dangerous for
your image, but chosen carefully, it could be OK for a once-in-a-lifetime
job far away. Later ad hoc tasks as well.

If you expect a good business in the future, I would advise you to put a
trusted person, a friend in charge. The distance can be fatal. The right
person and reliable progress reporting is what you need.

You could consider a joint venture with a respectable local softwarehouse.
If the business expands you can establish something yourself.

Just an idea

Regards
Ib

P.S. To that last.  If you are hiring older people, then you probably want
to be much more variable in that salary stuff.  Sixty-five thousand in
salary that I had to pay tax on would bankrupt me. Better a salary in the
low ten thousand range so that the "bennies" apply, a discretionary expense
account for travel and equipment, and a corporate fee structure.

Donald <I might smoke pot, but I have a good tax accountant>

"Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>The # of jobs will slowly decline, because schools don't teach it anymore.

what the schools teach usually has little to do with the job market.
50% are part time instructors whose qualification means being able to
solve the papers given out by the professor. and the professor hasn't
usually been in the market in years, but does manage to have tenure.

proof? the systems the schools themselves use are not written by the
comp sci department.

>Students are now versed in Visual Basic, Access, etc (are they really
>programming languages?)  The trend will be toward that.  Also, more 'canned'
>packages, bought off the shelf, will be used.  All the old time COBOLers
>will retire and maintenance/support of the old systems will begin to fail.

yes, there will be more application wizards and tools developed, but
thats all they are, tools. when you need to get your hands dirty for
something other than what they have decided they have made macros for,
then you go back to 3rd generation languages again. all the glue to
the systems will always be in 3rd gen languages. all the tools written
will need 3rd gen programmers.

>With that said, in a shop like ours, the mainframers still command the data.
>There is no better language to handle data in than COBOL.  Many of the PC
>databases are merely daily copies of the real data.  There is still a real
>need to keep business data on a mainframe with COBOL.

>COBOL will not disappear suddenly.  It will be chronic.  Businesses will
>require even more data to be stored and processed, countering efforts.  It

there is only so much data which is valuable, if you do garbage
collection.

>will take years and years to totally dismantle our data and processes.  In
>our shop, the legacy is just too big and important.  Our company would have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>programs to be maintained.

>Our shop writes new COBOL programs everyday.  One of the great things about
>COBOL is it's expandability and standardness.  Most COBOL programs look the
>same.  Grab a program from here, cut this, grab some snippets from there,
>add a little magic, and viola, it's done.  And most COBOL shops that are
>worth anything have standards.  This is what makes the system long-lived.

the system lasts log because its cheaper to employ a cobol programmer
than to employ a cobol / 4th gen language programmer to switch it all
over, and then employ a 4thgenlan programmer again. it has nothing to
do with the superiority of one language or another.

>I am in no fear of my job.  And if I was, I'd just have to learn the new
>stuff.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: print a rectangle

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h8k2o$i1p$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8h3kgq$26s$1@duke.telepac.pt> <8h62i1$cpt$1@news.cerf.net> <8h69t8$bf8$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
"Raimundo Carvalho" <ray@mail.telepac.pt> wrote in message
news:8h69t8$bf8$1@duke.telepac.pt...
> YEs but i have some dificult to understand the language used i think that
it
> is a C language, I only know Cobol and i want to know the calls i must do
to
> make a rectangle.
> I know the instructions to make the rectangle but nothinh happens , and it
> is necessary to make a brush or a solid brush before and i don�t know how
> can i do it in Cobol.

It is most certainly written in C, and yes I agree, C can sometimes be
difficult to understand. Then again, how to translate C definitions and code
to Cobol depends heavily on you Cobol dialect. If I recall correctly, you
are using MicroFocus, which I unfortunately don't know. You would need
someone that knows MicroFocus Cobol to help you there. However, what I would
recommend is to try to find a complete C sample that shows how to draw a
rectangle (pretty sure there is some on MSDN), then try to translate this
one step by step into Cobol. Once you overcome the few basic differences
this is usually trivial. The most common trap people go into when dealing
with this is that C usually passes parameters by address rather than value.
Also pay attention to the call convention of the functions used. As of
WinAPI, this usually is pascal (WINAPI).

When this is said, I want to warn you a bit, doing gui at this level is
difficult, and there are a lot of stuff to do, and in a particular order.

Sorry I can't help you more.

Cheesle
```

INVALID_SET_FILE_POINTER

As documented:

I run into things like this all the time.  It's not C - it's
Microsoft.

Hi Thane -

Save yourself a lot of grief and download the latest "Microsoft Platform
SDK".  At a minimum you get all the include files and a bunch of other
stuff.  The total install size I have is 84MB but you can probably get it
down from there.

Here's where the definition lives - in Winbase.h.  Here's a snippet from the
file...

    #define INVALID_HANDLE_VALUE ((HANDLE)-1)
    #define INVALID_FILE_SIZE ((DWORD)0xFFFFFFFF)
    #define INVALID_SET_FILE_POINTER ((DWORD)-1)


Regards,

Greg Condon
[doing lots of Win32 programming from MF 4.0.38     "and loving it..."
:^)    ]
