## Thread: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts — 11 this month / 88 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2000-05](2000-05.md#t-e64f1909a4)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed and binary usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqj_4.2043$c75.327958@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FFF09.5D9E7526@cusys.edu> <39330565.1387015@news.gw.total-web.net>`

```
Anytime your PICture has enough digits to cause a BINARY item
to be larger than 4 BYTES, use COMP-3.  The compiler will generate code to
convert it and (usually) anything used with it to COMP-3.
I cut 20% of  CPU  time from a program that reads a file of messages
from a RISC that has 2 or 4 4 byte BINARY values that are decoded
to determine 1 or 2 64bit bitmaps.  The original program just divided
them 4 bytes at a time and checked the remainder.  By moving the
4 byte BINARY item to a PICS 9(18) COMP-3 (which the compiler
did anyways) doing the Divide loop, the test run went from 2.45 seconds
to 2.2, AND the assembler code (for the divide and check remainder)
went from two screens to about 12 lines.

Paul Knudsen <pknudsen@gw.total-web.net> wrote in message
news:39330565.1387015@news.gw.total-web.net...
> Binary for counters, Packed for arithmetric using data which arrives
> as packed or decimal.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> SuSE Linux Users meet at: http://clubs.yahoo.com/clubs/suselinuxusers
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed and binary usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hdc1c$41p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FFF09.5D9E7526@cusys.edu> <39330565.1387015@news.gw.total-web.net> <bqj_4.2043$c75.327958@news3.mia>`

```
Out of curiosity, with which (IBM) compiler did you do this testing - and
with which compiler options?  The newer (newest?) compilers with TRUNC(OPT)
and OPT(FULL) compiler options do MUCH better optimization of binary
arithmetic - than older (even VS COBOL II) compilers.

Having said that, if you have a binary field over 4-bytes long, it may well
still be best to use USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL (aka COMP-3) - I haven't tried all
combinations of fields and statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
First of all, my thanks to Rick, Bill, and Michael for the clarification
on EXIT PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE.  I would probably use them if
they were available in COBOL for MVS & VM.

Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> This is a case where I may have been unclear.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Smith

But couldn't it be said that every non-infinite loop can only be exited
by some condition that is tested?  If we read records until end-of-file,
isn't the end-of-file condition some kind of flag or switch?  

I support a batch COBOL program that used to have a deliberate infinite
loop.  We PERFORM'ed a paragraph until a variable was set to a certain
value, and we deliberately never set the exit value.  I know it may
sound crazy, but we had good reasons for doing it at the time.  Later,
business requirements changed and we could no longer cancel the program
without suffering data loss, so we changed it to receive a signal from
an external process, and then it could exit the loop and shut itself
down gracefully.

My own taste in flags is alphanumeric, and since I don't have SET to
FALSE available to me, I may do something like this:

01  END-OF-FILE-FLAG     PIC X VALUE 'N'.
    88  ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED  VALUE 'Y'.
    88  THERE-ARE-MORE-RECORDS VALUE 'N'.

SET THERE-ARE-MORE-RECORDS TO TRUE
PERFORM UNTIL ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED
    READ MY-INPUT-FILE INTO WORK-RECORD
    AT END
        SET ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED-TO-TRUE
    NOT AT END
        ADD +1 TO INPUT-RECORD-COUNT
        PERFORM PROCESS-1-INPUT-RECORD
    END-READ
END-PERFORM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h52ip$119f$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net> <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley wrote in message <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net>...
>First of all, my thanks to Rick, Bill, and Michael for the clarification
>on EXIT PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE.  I would probably use them if
>they were available in COBOL for MVS & VM.
>
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> I would prefer to avoid flags for loop control; not all flags.
>>
>> Whether unconditional loops are a problem depends more
>> on how they are used; not whether they are used.
[...]
>But couldn't it be said that every non-infinite loop can only be exited
>by some condition that is tested?

Yes.

>  If we read records until end-of-file,
>isn't the end-of-file condition some kind of flag or switch?

IMO, technically, no. The runtime sytem needs to maintain
some kind of end-of-file flag, internally; but that internal
flag cannot be tested directly by the program. This means
that the AT END condition is not a flag or switch to the
application program.

However, the AT END condition is tested by the runtime
and an EXIT PERFORM within the AT END phrase makes
the loop non-infinite.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<os%Z4.808$Gb7.52310@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net> <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net> <8h52ip$119f$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Hi Rick,

I'm wondering about definitions here.  An object COBOL program needs a
runtime system, and without it, the parts of the runtime needed are
included in each object program I thought.

Your definition, it seems to me, makes the runtime program an OS utility.
I have trouble with that concept.

Warren Simmons
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
news:8h52ip$119f$1@news.hitter.net...

```
Just because earlier in this thread it DID mention IBM (mainframe) COBOL, I
thought I would either clarify or confuse things by saying there is little
difference (today) between the operating system and the COBOL run-time system
in that environment.  LE (Language Environment) which is the "joint" run-time
for all the various programming languages is "tightly" bundled into the
OS/390 operating system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hc7n9$23b$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net> <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net> <8h52ip$119f$1@news.hitter.net> <os%Z4.808$Gb7.52310@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
When memory was small and overlays were common, compilers
would create an object file which was then linked with only the parts
of the runtime that were need for execution of the program. This was
done, in part, to fix (relocate) the memory addresses for the program
and, in part, to keep the size of the load modules small.

Now that memory is large and virtual, the common practice is to load
a separate runtime system, in one or more parts, that contains
the runtime support routines for the language. This runtime may
include routines that are not needed by the program. The link step
for the object program generally adds code to load the runtime
system, or, where the runtime is shared, to locate an already loaded
copy.

I guess the separate runtime system may seem as if it were an OS
utility.
------------------
Rick Smith

Warren Simmons wrote in message ...
[...]
>I'm wondering about definitions here.  An object COBOL program needs a
>runtime system, and without it, the parts of the runtime needed are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have trouble with that concept.
[...]
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
>news:8h52ip$119f$1@news.hitter.net...
>>
>> Arnold Trembley wrote in message <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net>...
[...]
>> >But couldn't it be said that every non-infinite loop can only be exited
>> >by some condition that is tested?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>> the loop non-infinite.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C8wZ4.403$RZ5.104291@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net> <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net...
>
> But couldn't it be said that every non-infinite loop can only be exited
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> loop. .... Later,  business requirements changed and we could no longer
cancel the program
> without suffering data loss, so we changed it to receive a signal from
> an external process, and then it could exit the loop and shut itself
> down gracefully.

Well, this gets into semantics, but a "loop" structure is by definition,
infinite. If it were finite, it would be an "iteration" rather than a loop.
(e.g., PERFORM 100 TIMES ....END-PERFORM).

As far as your '"deliberate infinite loop" program above which required the
job be canceled to terminate, I do believe had it been my quality control
signoff to make, I wouldn't have made it.  Shamelessly promoting the
profession of many here, if you can't figure out how to set up a job/process
to run "a long time" or "until I say so" without eating system resources and
termination requires canceling the job, this is really a situation where an
investment in a consultant is indicated. No, not one of your body shop
code-pounders; I mean someone who actually "consults."  Apparently you did
not require an outside consultant (because you were able to change the
software yourself)  but I cannot imagine why your shop allowed the "infinite
loop" job to be a part of your production system in the first place.
Terminating a program externally from the O/S seems to me rather drastic
action which should only be undertaken in case of emergency, not considered
a "normal" shutdown. (This may not be a universally held view, BTW).

I also do not understand how the program was allowed to lose data when
cancelled. There are several ways to design software and use system services
to not lose anything but the transaction in process even if external
cancellation is required (or "happens"). Here, again, is something a
consultant could help with.
```

that how operating systems must work?  Any data entry program would work
the same way.  Process until told to do something different.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39373840.1569D116@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net> <3935FC2C.CD5A47EA@worldnet.att.net> <C8wZ4.403$RZ5.104291@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> a "normal" shutdown. (This may not be a universally held view, BTW).

Without getting into a lot of details, this particular decision was made
(to install a batch COBOL program with a deliberate infinite loop) in
order to improve performance and reduce system resource utilization for
a group of CICS regions linked by IRC/MRO.  The infinite loop program
needed to be up 24x7, and the simplest way to take it down for an IPL or
a major release upgrade was to cancel it.  It ran as a started proc in
an MVS/ESA environment.

> 
> I also do not understand how the program was allowed to lose data when
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> consultant could help with.

Please accept my apologies for not being sufficiently clear.  The
infinite loop program did not lose data.  Business requirements
changed.  In order to boost performance again, the program needed to
reduce I/O during its active cycle.  But doing that meant that the
normal operator cancellation procedures would cause data loss.  Since
data loss was not considered acceptable, an interrupt to allow a
graceful shutdown had added along with the I/O enhancement.  For
technical reasons, we could not add support for the MVS operator console
MODIFY command.  We had to find a different way pass a signal to the
infinite loop program.  It had no screen or operator interface of any
kind, so adding the interrupt was a challenge.
```

As I read the entries in comp.lang.cobol I am surprised at how much COBOL
development is going on.

Who are these small businesses that have COBOL development happening?

How do you find them?

I thought most small businesses used MS Access, Visual Basic, etc.

I'm a mainframe COBOL programmer and would like to get into the PC side of
things but am not sure whether to learn VB or Java or stick to COBOL since
it looks like COBOL now does everything these other languages do.

It's all very confusing.

blk...

It's not so much small companies that are using Cobol, but
rather the large Fortune 500 companies.

Look at banking, insurance, credit card and other large
firms and you'll see that they are the ones operating in an
MVS environment.  They may use VB and Access for smaller
desktop apps, but when it comes to the backbone of the
business, you'll see that MVS and Cobol are what keeps
things running.

dedicated to learning COBOL and JCL.  It involved lecture and hands-on
structured coding, debugging, testing and documenting of programs
utilizing OS/MVS, JCL, COBOL, COBOL II, Microfocus Cobol, TSO/ISPF, and
VSAM using SPF/PC and MVS Workshop.  COBOL programs were written to
include random and sequential updates, VSAM retrieval, control breaks,
internal and external sort routines, Y2K modifications, and table
inserts.  Along with each COBOL program, the appropriate JCL was
written for its execution.   Advanced testing and debugging procedures
were emphasized.  This program was designed to give students six months
of COBOL experience in a span of five weeks.
>
> SKILLS:
> C, C++ (DOS, Windows, and Unix environments), Visual BASIC, COBOL,
JCL, FORTRAN, Paradox (ObjectPal), HTML, SQL Server, DOS, Windows
3.1/95/98/NT, Unix, Linux, OS/MVS, TSO/ISPF, Microsoft Word & Excel,
Viasoft products, Compuware Dataager & Hiperstation, Quality Assurance,
Technical Writing, Advanced Math & Physics.
>
> EDUCATION
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-c142413d46"></a>
## Thread: Do we have to ? for an index file. — 1 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [2000-05](2000-05.md#t-c142413d46)._

#### ↳ Re: Do we have to ? for an index file.

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bxtZ4.256$fE6.139009@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8guquf$37i$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

M S <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:8guquf$37i$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk...
> Hi
> just wanted to know that, Do we have to use a SEPARATE COBOL PROGRAM
for
> creating an INDEX FILE ?

No.

SELECT ISAM-FILE ....
   ACCESS IS DYNAMIC...


OPEN OUTPUT ISAM-FILE
(read sequential file - write ISAM file)
CLOSE ISAM-FILE.

OPEN I-O ISAM-FILE
(misc processing)
CLOSE ISAM-FILE.
DELETE FILE ISAM-FILE.
STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing a rectangle -help

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h8joh$hs8$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8gk2me$mut$1@duke.telepac.pt> <8gm8ui$l44$1@news.cerf.net> <8h6a0d$p43$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
"Raimundo Carvalho" <ray@mail.telepac.pt> wrote in message
news:8h6a0d$p43$1@duke.telepac.pt...
> No i did not. How can i do it?

This gives you a solid brush:
SelectObject(hDC,CreateSolidBrush(RGB(r,g,b))))

This gives you the pen
hPen = SelectObject(hDC,GetStockObject(BLACK_PEN));
            SelectObject(hDC,hPen);

Where hDC is the handle to your printer device context. Look up this
functions in MSDN for an explanation.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-ba58d91977"></a>
## Thread: LINAGE IS ? LINES — 3 msgs

### LINAGE IS ? LINES

- **From:** "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h6bg3$c44$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
 Hello! again - this time please let me know about: How to use the
 Clause "LINAGE IS...LINES"

 My printer prints maximum 60 lines on one page.

 for eg. Say if I want to produce a report in a program on the printer
 with HEADINGS on each page (as usual) then follows the DETAIL LINES..
 But when the report finishes and the program terminates, It has
 to prints out THE FINAL TOTAL LINES (before STOP RUN)
 In my program I have to print 14 final lines.
  I did use the following linage clause which I have a very little
 knowledge about. I did use various values for ?a ?b ?c and ?d

 FD  PRINT-FILE
     LINAGE IS ?a LINES
     WITH FOOTING AT ?b
     LINES AT TOP ?c
     LINES AT BOTTOM ?d.
 01  PRINT-REC                   PIC X(110).

 Can someone please guide me in the right direction, also please
 let me know if any of the words are optional. What possible
 values can I use for ?a ?b ?c and ?d. I apologise for asking
 a homework question, I tried everything and could not find
 any comprehensive material on LINAGE clause.

----------------------------
 Another question :
----------------------------
 How would we create a file for TEST DATA which holds a NEGATIVE FIELD.
 Some hints please also on this.Thankyou.

 Regards
 MS
```

#### ↳ Re: LINAGE IS ? LINES

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3936B34A.ED22C69C@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<8h6bg3$c44$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Try LINAGE IS 60 LINES.  Then just define how many lines you need for
the page headings and footings.  The report footing, printed at the end
of the report, should be part of the body of the report and has nothing
to do with a page footing.  Normally we do not use the LINAGE clause (in
IBM world) unless you need to explicitly control pagination on special
forms.

Joe

M S wrote:
> 
>  Hello! again - this time please let me know about: How to use the
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>  MS
```

#### ↳ Re: LINAGE IS ? LINES

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h6hr3$ejf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8h6bg3$c44$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Probably NOT a good solution for your homework assignment, but this is the
type of thing that Report Writer does *so* well.  You might want to ask your
instructor if you will be learning about this in your class (many places do
NOT teach it any more).
```

<a id="t-48a46aec56"></a>
## Thread: What is the purpose of 9's complement? — 10 msgs

### What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** Tony C. <trx32@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```

         I use MicroFocus Cobol, and the product I work on uses 9's
complement on dates to look up certain records.


			Why do they use 9's complement? What is the
purpose?

			thanks very much,

					Tony C.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<znPZ4.17$R71.697@client>`
- **References:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```
Tony C. <trx32@home.com> wrote in message
news:ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com...
>[snip] the product I work on uses 9's
> complement on dates to look up certain records.
>
> Why do they use 9's complement? What is the
> purpose?

Most COBOL indexed file implementations now support READ PREVIOUS.  However
this was not always the case and the standard does not include READ
PREVIOUS.

You most likely will find that the 9s complement date field(s) are used in
indexes (as you say, the product uses this to look up certain records).  By
this means one can read records in reverse chronological order using
standard COBOL.  Your lookup probably does a START followed by one or more
READ NEXT.

Regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LuQZ4.3052$HD6.60048@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```
In article <ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>,
Tony C.  <trx32@home.com> wrote:
>
>         I use MicroFocus Cobol, and the product I work on uses 9's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>purpose?

Smells like homework to me... but what the heck:  Inverting the sequence
for sequential reads. Consider the keys:

20000101
20000201
20000301
...
20001231

As written a series of sequential reads on that key will you ascending
date sequence.  If you want to reverse the sequence:

99999999 - 20000101 = 79999898
99999999 - 20000201 = 79999798
99999999 - 20000301 = 79999698
...
99999999 - 20001231 = 79998768

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h8lbg$qnp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```
While it is impossible to know why anybody does anything without reading the
code, the main reason for 9's compliment is to allow negative numbers
without using a sign.  That could be so that things could be read in reverse
order, or it could be for some obscure arithmetic reason. Certainly BCD math
using 9's complement works as well as converting to binary one's compliment.
I have never used it in Cobol, but it is/was common in assembler.

Tony C. wrote in message ...
>
>         I use MicroFocus Cobol, and the product I work on uses 9's
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tony C.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3937d9cb.141089246@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```
To reverse key the dates so most recent is retrieved first.

>
>         I use MicroFocus Cobol, and the product I work on uses 9's
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>					Tony C.

```

#### ↳ Re: What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5u6_4.756$cb2.10908380@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```
In article <ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>, Tony C. <trx32@home.com> wrote:
>
>         I use MicroFocus Cobol, and the product I work on uses 9's
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
To enable reading the file in reverse chronological order.

#### ↳ Re: What is the purpose of 9's complement?

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393BB9CF.57C34772@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<ngefjs4a1kbps1fk0vackpg2hnrgt28j4k@4ax.com>`

```
"Tony C." wrote:

>          I use MicroFocus Cobol, and the product I work on uses 9's
> complement on dates to look up certain records.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In billing telephone calls, the last field in the rate table file key
was a 9's complement of the date that rate went into effect.  Once the
date the call was made was converted into 9's complement, READ ... NEXT
could find the correct rate.  This allowed new rates to be entered
before their effective date.
```

<a id="t-f157b35162"></a>
## Thread: Problem with Fujitsu Powercobol V-4 projects in V-5. — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Problem with Fujitsu Powercobol V-4 projects in V-5.

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h90ru$1nbe$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Good Afternoon,

Although I've E-mailed this question to Fujitsu support, I thought that I
might try this newsgroup with my question, since I see at least some of you
work with the PowerCobol product.
I may or may not get an answer from Fujitsu today, and I'd really like to
start working on a program this weekend.  Here's a snip of my E-mail to
them.

<<I recently uninstalled Fujitsu Cobol Version 4 and installed Version 5.  I
saved all of my version 4 projects into a backup folder, then copied them
back to the C:\Program Files\FujitsuCobol\COBOL\Samples\Powercobol\ folder.
I'm sure that this is a path problem of some sort, but I cannot seem to find
the place to fix it.  The version 4 link process had a link to
C:\FSC\COBOL97\F3BICBLR.LIB.  I changed this to C:\Program
Files\FujitsuCobol\COBOL\F3BICBLR.LIB. Once the link part of the build
process starts, I get the error:
    LINK : fatal error LNK1181: cannot open input file "C:\PROGRAM.obj"
Is there something special I need to do to the version 4 projects to compile
and link under version 5?>>

Actually, the "uninstall" was done automatically by the Version 5 install,
but basically the C:\FSC folder is gone regardless. I'm sure this is
probably a "duh" on my part, but I think I've gone brain dead at the moment.
Has anyone else run into this situation yet?  If so, how can I fix it?

Thanks in advance.

Denny
```

moment.
> Has anyone else run into this situation yet?  If so, how can I fix it?

I had a similar problem. and found that my path was incorrect.

Check your path in autoexec.bat.  It seems to me that your path is still
pointing to C:\FSC.  The 4.2 used a directory called COBOL97. It's now just
plain COBOL.

I compile and link at the DOS prompt.  If you would like my DOS  batch
files, let me know and I'll send them to you.

Joe Hunter
pcs@usaor.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with Fujitsu Powercobol V-4 projects in V-5.

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hb06t$2ota$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8h90ru$1nbe$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <39382687.160737083@news.cox-internet.com>`

```

Thanks to all who are trying to help on this project! I'll try to provide
more information.  The Version 5 install actually sets the "path" and "lib"
options in the autoexec.bat file for you. I already removed all references
to c:\fsc\...whatever. The Powercobol build process works fine as long as
you don't add any addition link options.
In the Powercobol build process, you right click on the Module name, do a
properties, click on the link tab, then enter any additional modules that
you might need for external references.  As long as this field is blank, it
works OK. When I change the lib reference from the version 4 project to the
version 5 lib path, I get the error after the .rc file is created. I
remember with the regular Fujitsu Cobol, you had to set the program to be
"Main", but I didn't need to do that with Powercobol, at least in version 4.
If I get time, I'm going to create a new project with external lib
references to see if I get the same results. I'm also looking for any .ini
files that might be involved.

Right now, I have a garage to clean.

Denny
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with Fujitsu Powercobol V-4 projects in V-5.

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hgdj5$4u7e$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8h90ru$1nbe$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <39382687.160737083@news.cox-internet.com> <8hb06t$2ota$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Hi all!  Problem solved.  I just knew it had to be something simple that I
wasn't considering.  Actually, all of you were correct in a way, but
Frederico gave me the real clue.  I went to the Microsoft support site
regarding spaces in a path statement and they are legal as long as they are
in quotes.  The Fujitsu install, puts quotes on the path statements in the
autoexec.bat file, but if you do a PATH command from a DOS prompt, you don't
see them.

So I put quotes on the link module, i.e. "C:\Program Files\Fujitsu
COBOL\COBOL\F3BICBLR.LIB" and it linked fine.  I suppose I should have known
better, but I didn't.  I thought I would pass this along, in case anyone
else runs into this.  Now I need to E-mail Fujitsu with a humble pie
attachment.

Thanks again, everyone.

Denny
```

<a id="t-d551f4cfef"></a>
## Thread: Netexpress 3.0 and 3.1 runtime rip off — 4 msgs

### Netexpress 3.0 and 3.1 runtime rip off

- **From:** "SAMA Teams" <ais4c@ais4c.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<960113315.25217.0.nnrp-06.9e987ec8@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

My organization bought 25 developer copies of Netexpress 3.0 when it first
came out to replace MF COBOL 4.  We haven't rolled the Netexpress runtime
out to the customer because of Merants every changing runtime costs.

When we first purchased NE3.0 the licence agreement text file said you could
roll out the basic runtime for free but if you use anything from
'Application Server' you pay.  When you upgrade to Service Pack 1 NE3.0 the
text file now effectively says you pay regardless !  Try it out.

My advice is stick with MF COBOL 4 as it does everything Netexpress does
except the web stuff, Dialog is still bug filled and the compiler is
basically the same.

Nick Miles

EDS AFPAA(Inns) - nick@ais4c.demon.co.uk
```

## Thread: Mr. Oracle's Technology Wealth Now Exceeds That of Mr. MSFT — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Mr. Oracle's Technology Wealth Now Exceeds That of Mr. MSFT

- **From:** jer_mm@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,alt.politics.nationalism.white,alt.politics.white-power
- **Message-ID:** `<8h8d12$kiv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The following is an excerpt from the Treatise on the Downward Wage
Equalizing Effects of Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO):



EQUAL EMPLOYMENT OPPORTUNITY EQUALS $250,000,000 SALARIES?


That's right.  That's exactly what it means.  But these income levels
are reserved only for the select few.

If you have read the previous articles in this series, you would
understand that the growing numbers of low quality people adhering to
low personal standards are putting these people into the very vast
majority.

Conversely, the number of people who have the track record and ability
to keep an organization of low quality players going as a viable concern
is diminishing and diminishing drastically.  With a high demand for
executives who can handle a delicate balancing act and with a dwindling
supply of these executives, you see the compensation levels for these
executives going through the roof.

Why aren't these executives subject to the downwardly equalizing effects
of equal employment opportunity while the rest of us are having our
salaries trampled upon?  First, remember that salaries are being
downwardly equalized to the value of the least preferred candidate
WITHIN job category.  Even the least preferred job candidate for the CEO
position who can handle the aforementioned balancing act is worth a
humongous salary in this very odd business environment.  Secondly, with
profit sharing, stock options, and other aspects of a high level
executive job, the high level executive becomes more like a business
owner than a member of labor.  Owners of successful businesses are
making out like bandits by profiting from the declining wage levels of
their workers.  These cost savings are significant, and the results are
going right to the bottom line of the corporate income statement, not to
the bottom lining of the pockets of labor.

Bear in mind that con artists always earn a premium income.  Business
executives, as part of their delicate balancing act, frequently switch
hats between their roles as managers and con artists.  What is this
writer getting at?  One way or another the company CEO has to
successfully put eroding wage levels over on his employees and yet keep
those employees blissfully busy and ignorant as to what's really going
on.  What's really going on is that the company absolutely does not want
to pay to the unpreferred workers what it would be willing to pay to the
preferred workers; likewise it does not want to put forth incentives for
unpreferred job applicants to come and sue the company for jobs at
premium wages. So wage levels must be brought down, and the skilled and
glib executive will make up any seemingly plausible excuse, but he
absolutely will not be forthright and blame equal employment opportunity
concerns.  This fancy footwork and con artistry adds up to a value of
$250,000,000 per year for the right man for the job!

Right now businesses are in a fever-pitched mode of price competition.
With most workers having joined the ranks of the majority of society
adhering to low personal and performance standards, businesses are
having a difficult time competing on the basis of anything other than
price. Business leaders are at a loss to compete on the basis of
quality, and they are likewise at a loss to find ways to work smart and
efficiently. So the big-bucks-CEO guys are constantly putting over on
their employees the importance of being the low cost producer in the
industry.  They keep drilling into their employees that wage concessions
are essential for successful survival and competition.  In actuality
what we are seeing are those pesky, indistinctive self-correcting market
forces which are downwardly equalizing every worker to the value of the
least preferred job candidate.  We are also seeing workers work hard but
not smart, with slave drivers bearing down on them!

At one prominent company in the transportation industry, the con
artistry and fancy footwork reached unprecedented proportions.  Two
tiered wages systems were imposed upon the employees effectively
downwardly equalizing incoming employees.  Then the two tiers were
merged back into one tier but at significantly lower wage levels, thus
downwardly equalizing everyone. Then the company successfully put an
employee buyout over on the workers, getting wage concessions far in
excess of what the purchase price of the company's stock was worth.  A
year later when the employees started to realize how they had been
taken, there was so much tension and dissension among the ranks that it
was reported that workers were attacking each other with mechanical
screwdrivers, but by then the deal was long consummated.  Is it any
wonder why certain guys are getting $250,000,000 per year while the rest
of us are getting mechanically screwed?

With the proletariat observing its purchasing power diminishing at the
same time as the compensation package of the CEO swells from
$250,000,000 to $500,000,000, it's enough to make the mental stability
of anyone crack.  Is it any wonder why insanity is at epidemic
proportions in the United States?  But don't let any of this bother you
excessively.  What we are seeing are symptoms of a very perverse system
in transition to its ultimate state.  With the federal debt hanging over
us like the Sword of Damocles and with multitudes of workers being let
go in mergers and consolidations, then nobody will be earning half a
billion dollars a year . . . or even minimum wage for that matter.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: win32 api cobol reference book?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3938590d.173672806@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<241b7eb8.effbdd3a@usw-ex0103-024.remarq.com>`

```
On Fri, 02 Jun 2000 15:48:00 -0700, trinh
<trinhNOtrSPAM@aol.com.invalid> wrote:

>hi, does everyone know of a win32 api book which use cobol as
>reference .. intead of c.  please reply with title and author or
>where i can get one.  thanks

There isn't one.  One of the main reasons is that each of the COBOL
vendors supports the interlanguage calls differently.  The COBOL
standard for calling programs does not (yet) support a standard method
for calling, C, Pascal, C++ etc programs.  Each is different.

However, once you learn to interpret the API you can usually figure it
out.  (With some help from everyone here!).  What compiler are you
using?  There are specific hints for each.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

## Thread: AMR/Northwest Merger Portends Third World Living Standards 4 U — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### AMR/Northwest Merger Portends Third World Living Standards 4 U

- **From:** jer_mm@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,alt.politics.nationalism.white,alt.politics.white-power
- **Message-ID:** `<8h8bsd$jm0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hey, computer consultants and company computer programmers, the
following is relevant to you because it has a direct bearing on your
wage earning potential.  It's an excerpt from the Treatise on the
Downward Wage Equalizing Effects of Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO).



Question:  Why do companies merge?

Answer:  Companies merge for several EEO related reasons.  First, the
purchasing power of consumers is constantly being reduced by lower,
downwardly equalized wages.  This reduced purchasing power cannot
support the economic activity necessary to sustain a large number of
competitors within an industry, so the competitors consolidate thereby
enabling themselves to "share" a diminishing market.  Secondly, job
candidates with preferred demographics are becoming a vanishing breed,
and so certain players in an industry will want to "scoop up" the
remaining preferred workers by "raiding" the demographics of an acquired
company.  Thirdly, since wages have not yet been completely downwardly
equalized, labor is perceived to be too expensive, in which case
companies will want to use labor sparingly.  Think about it: if the
price of table salt goes to $100 per pound, you will use it sparingly!
Companies can achieve their "frugal" use of labor by merging and
eliminating redundant functions and personnel.  Areas which will be
impacted the most will be data processing, accounting, legal,
advertising, among others.  Lastly, EEO has sent performance standards
into a downward bidding spiral among all workers making it difficult, if
not impossible, to manage an enterprise properly. Companies reorganize
and reorganize in a feeble attempt to counteract the low performance
standards of their workers.  These companies eventually come to the
conclusion that the ultimate reorganization, that is, merger, while not
necessarily raising performance standards, will at least remove certain
competitive challenges in the marketplace by "swallowing" that
competition, and so low performance standards become much less of a
pressing concern.

It is essential that you remember:  the consolidation of companies
within industries reduces the number of bidders to compete and bid up
the buying price of labor, and so labor prices will continue to go
downward.  Additionally, consolidation reduces the number of bidders to
compete and bid down the selling prices of products and services, and so
selling prices will end up going upward.  The typical worker in the
merger game ends up getting torn between lower real wages and higher
market prices for the things he would like to buy.  Mergers and
acquisitions are expected to usher in Third World living standards for
us.

jer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **References:** `<WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net> <7e0U4.1481$d36.464361@news1.mco> <8gttev$j24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, once again, you make statements that would do a politician Proud.
'Addressability', what does that mean?  Well WordPerfect's Thesaurus doesn't
have that word, BUT it does have 'Addressable' and it offers 'abode'
'dwelling' 'house'
and finally 'residence'.  What do these all have in COMMON?  if you know the
address, you can get there!

This is EXACTLY how IBM(and by implication the IBM mainframe Clones
AMDAHL, FUJITSU), HONEYWELL and WANG handle this.
As a matter of fact, WANG at least as far back as 88 gave 'C' and Assembler
programmers the option to have their programs use the Language std
interface, OR what they called 'CLE' for Common Language Environment.
And IBM as far back (that I personally can verify) as 76 used this on all
their machines no matter the size.  IT WORKS.
Let's see what venerable old IBM says:

If the BY REFERENCE phrase is either specified or implied for a
parameter, the corresponding data item in the calling program
occupies the same storage area as the data item in the called program.
(this would be the normal 'CALL USING'
    Note:  If defined in the Linkage Section or File Section,
    you must have already provided addressability for
    identifier-2 prior to invocation of the CALL statement.
    You can do this by coding either one of the
    following:  SET ADDRESS OF identifier-2 TO pointer
    or PROCEDURE/ENTRY USING.

6.2.4.3 BY CONTENT Phrase
If the BY CONTENT phrase is specified or implied for a parameter,
the called program cannot change the value of this parameter as
referenced in the CALL statement's USING phrase, though the called
program can change the value of the data item referenced by the
corresponding data-name in the called program's Procedure Division
header.  Changes to the parameter in the called program do not affect
the corresponding argument in the calling program.
(This would be the 'CALL USING BY CONTENT' which like PASCAL
copies the data and gives you a 'local' copy.  You would still access the
same way!
You just wouldn't be operating on the Caller's data. (ADA does the same
thing
when you define parameters as 'IN' 'OUT'(call by content)  versus 'INOUT'
(call by ref)

Pasted in is a sample in 'C' from 93 on a Wang 12K and from 99
assembler on an IBM 97XX system (CMOS).
/* ChangeCase subroutine.  Makes all items in a string which are letters
*  into the selected case: all to upper or all to lower. Ignores others.
*  Accepts: switch (1 char), short count of string length, string of chars
*  &, a short ret-code.
*  Modifies the string as is.  Max string length is 128.
*/

#define SUCCESS         0
#define FAILURE         -1
#define NUMBER_OF_ARGS  4

void  ChangCas( Passed )  /* COBOL passes one Address)
/* actually ( swtch, count, str_i_o, ret_code )    CALL 'CHANGCAS' using
WS-SWITCH, */
/* WS-SIZE, WS-STRING, WS-RC  */
{
   char *swtch, *str_i_o, *Ptr_to_Char, *Ptr_to_int_as_Char;
   char Two_Bytes[3];
   short count, *ret_code;
   int  List_Array[NUMBER_OF_ARGS], i;
   /* args come in as  AddressOfSWITCH,..AddressOfRC */
   int  *Start_Of_List;
   Start_Of_List = &Passed;     /* put Address of Passed Address into
StartOfList */
   for ( i = 0; i < NUMBER_OF_ARGS; i++ )
         List_Array[i] = *Start_Of_List++;

   swtch = List_Array[0];
   Ptr_to_Char = List_Array[1];
   for ( i = 0; i < sizeof(short); i++ )
      Two_Bytes[i] = *Ptr_to_Char++;
   Ptr_to_int_as_Char = &count;
   for ( i = 0; i < sizeof(short); i++ )
         *(Ptr_to_int_as_Char + i) = Two_Bytes[i];
   str_i_o = List_Array[2];
   ret_code = List_Array[3];

  if (count > 128)
      { Ptr_to_int_as_Char = ret_code;
        for ( i = 0; i < sizeof(short); i++ )
              *(Ptr_to_int_as_Char + i) = 0xff;
        return;
      }

  for ( i = 0; i < count; i++ )
        if ( *(str_i_o + i) >= 'A' && *(str_i_o + i) <= 'Z')
            {
            if ( *swtch == 'L' )
                  *(str_i_o + i) += 32;
            }
        else
           if ( *(str_i_o + i) >= 'a' && *(str_i_o + i) <= 'z')
               {
               if ( *swtch == 'U' )
                    *(str_i_o + i) -= 32;
               }

 Ptr_to_int_as_Char = ret_code;
 for ( i = 0; i < sizeof(short); i++ )
     *(Ptr_to_int_as_Char + i) = '\0';

return;
}

THIS CODE WORKS Wang, HoneyWell, IBM Minis and Mainframes AND
IBM PCs / Unix PCs because if you check the examples of how to get command
line
arguments, they explain that you get them by derefencing the Pointer to
Pointers to the Strings

Now  here is some very current Assembler Code

*                I N P U T     P A R A M E T E R S                    *
00110000
*                                                                     *
00120000
*     UPON INPUT TO THIS ROUTINE, REGISTER ONE (1) WILL POINT TO      *
00130000
*     A TWO (2) WORD PARAMETER LIST.  PARAMETER LIST 1 IS THE         *
00140000
*     ALLOC/UNALLOC INFORMATION.  PARM LIST 2 IS THREE 4 BYTE         *
00150000
*     RETURN CODE FIELDS: SVC 99 RC, SVC 99 ERROR RSN, SVC 99 INFO    *
00160000
*                                                                     *
00170000
*     THE FIRST PARM IS A POINTER TO:                                 *
00180000
*                                                                     *
00190000
*        1)  OPCODE   - (1)  OPERATION CODE FOR THE ROUTINE     ANUM  *
00200000
*                            O - ALLOCATE FOR OUTPUT                  *
00210000
*                            I - ALLOCATE OR INPUT                    *
00220000
*                            D - DELETE FILE                          *
00230000
*        2)  DSNAME   - (44) DSNAME FULLY QUALIFIED OR GDG RELATIVE   *
00240007
*        3)  LRECL    - (5)  LOGICAL RECORD LENGTH              NUM   *
00250007
*        4)  BLKSIZE  - (5)  BLOCK SIZE                         NUM   *
00260007
*        5)  RECFM    - (4)  RECORD FORMAT                      ALPHA *
00270007
*        6)  RELEASE  - (1)  SPACE RELEASE INDICATOR (Y/N)      ALPHA *
00280007
*        7)  SPACEVAL - (5)  SPACE VALUE (BLKLENGTH/TRK/CYL)    ANUM  *
00290007
*        8)  PRISPACE - (5)  PRIMARY SPACE QUANTITY             NUM   *
00300007
*        9)  SECSPACE - (5)  SECONDARY SPACE QUANTITY           NUM   *
00310007
*        10) VOLSER   - (6)  OPTION VOLUME SERIAL NUMBER        ANUM  *
00320007
*        11) UNIT     - (4)  UNIT TYPE (DISK DEFAULT/TAPE/CART) ANUM  *
00330007
*        12) LABEL    - (2)  LABEL TYPE (SL DEFAULT/NL)         ANUM  *
00340007
*        13) DENSITY  - (1)  TAPE DENSITY                       NUM   *
00350007
*                                                                     *
00360000
*     THE SECOND PARM IS A POINTER TO:                                *
00370000*

*        1)  DYNALL31-RETURN-CODE                                   *
00390001
*        2)  DYNALL31-ERROR-RSN                                     *
00400001
*        3)  DYNALL31-INFO-RSN                                      *
00410001
*                                                                     *
00420000

*01500 01  DYNALL-MODULE             PIC X(08) VALUE 'DYNALL'.
01490004
*01501

*01510 01  DYNAL-PARM-LIST.
01510004

01770004

01780004
*03800
01790004

01800004

*07810        CALL DYNALL-MODULE

***********************************************************************
08210000

08220000
***********************************************************************
08230000
DYNALL31 CSECT
08240001
DYNALL31 AMODE ANY
08250001
DYNALL31 RMODE ANY
08260001
     SAVE  (14,12),,DYNALL31.V1.2.JAMESDKING.&SYSDATE:&SYSTIME
08270003
         LR    R11,R15        INITIALIZE FIRST BASE REG
08280000

08290000
         LA    R12,2048(R12)      SECOND BASE REG
08300000
         USING DYNALL31,R11,R12 SET PROGRAM ADDRESSABILITY
08310001
         LR    R15,R13        SAVE CALLER'S SAVE AREA ADDR
08320000

08330000

08340000
         ST    R15,4(R13)     STORE OLD SAVE ADDR IN NEW
08350000
==>      L     R2,0(R1)       LOAD ADDRESS OF FIRST PARM
08360000
*this stmt transfers 4 bytes at the address pointed to by R1+0 into R2
         MVI   ISLINK,C'N'
08370000
==>      USING PARM,R2        SET ADDRESSABILITY TO INPUT PARM
08380000
*this stmt says, what is pointed to by R2 LOOKS like what follows 'PARM'
         CLI   OPCODE,X'00'   IF EXEC'ED THEN BYTE1 OF 2BYTE LENGTH
08390000
         BNE   NORML
08400000
         CLI   PARMLNG,X'00'  EXEC'D W/O PARM=
08410000
         BE    SCREWED
08420000
         CLI   PARMLNG,X'05'
08430008
         BH    EXECD
08440008
* MUST HAVE BEEN EITHER "HELP" OR "/?"
08450008
         BAL   R14,SHOWUSG
08460022

PARM     DSECT   <== kind of like a '01 Record' in COBOL
19980000

20060000

20070000

20080000

20100000

20110000

20130000

20140000

> The "attached" is an incredibly "implementation specific" definition.
What
> the ANSI COBOL Standard says is that if you pass a parameter (explicitly
or
> implicitly) BY REFERENCE, that you get "addressability" to that item (with
or
> without any indication of the length of the item passed).   What was in
the
> "attached" note is certainly how many implementations work (ignoring for
the
> moment the assumption that a "pointer" is 4 bytes long).
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

8
> > means bit leftmost is on
> > to signify the END-OF-LIST) - 99./100 times that is how it is done.)
> > The COBOL routine will load 20200 into the 'parameter register' and
Branch
> > to the routine.

Bill, as I recall, (and I just verified in the Lang Ref)
you can code
Identifier |IS| |NOT| [POSITIVE | NEGATIVE | ZERO]
where Positive means ALGEBRAEIC Value is greater than ZERO
or ALGEBRAEIC VALUE is less than ZERO (you're right, must be ZERO)
or EQUAL to ZERO.  As ZERO can have a + or -, (certain operations
cause a 'negative' zero), it is poorly named.  It is really a NON-ZERO
compare
more like the 'C' compare where your RC is -, + or 0 depending upon
which is >, item 1or item 2.

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:8gvddb$k2j$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> Just in case this is still of interest to anyone,  It is an "easy to miss"
> part of COBOL that two different statements (related to zeroes) are
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> The second is a "sign test" (like IF POSITIVE/NEGATIVE) and the zero may
NOT
> be spelled zeroes or zeros - although IBM and some other vendors allow
this
> as an extension).

<a id="t-e56e770364"></a>
## Thread: $0 = Value Of COBOL Experience Today? (Was Yo'(Yukon)Mama's BAD Idea) — 8 msgs

### $0 = Value Of COBOL Experience Today? (Was Yo'(Yukon)Mama's BAD Idea)

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000602221243.22139.00000550@ng-bk1.aol.com>`

```
Yukon Mama's post brought to mind a disturbing, but interesting, development in
corporate mindsets today - that experience has little, if any, value.

For example, a number of Y2K contract programmers (often receiving $50+/hour)
with 20-25 years experience at my last client were offered disturbingly lowball
permanent employee offers - about $50K per year - while those straight out of a
local 9 month "learn to program" school started at $44K, often with a several
thousand dollar signing bonus, and entry level employees of consulting firms
with no experience commanded billing rates of $60+/hour.

In fact, it's VERY rare indeed to find any permanent employee COBOL positions
that pay even twice entry level salary, even though a person with 10+ years of
good experience most likely will outperform an entry level person by 400%-2500%
(if the entry level person even contributes anything positive in the first year
or two!).

Any comments?
```

In article <20000602221243.22139.00000550@ng-bk1.aol.com>, theodp@aol.com 
says...
> Yukon Mama's post brought to mind a disturbing, but interesting, development in
> corporate mindsets today - that experience has little, if any, value.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have seen a disturbing trend to outsource all application support out 
of house. Most of these are to offshore firms in India or else where. I 
would be curious to know of the experiences of other shops which have 
done this...

I am particularly interested in shops where all application support is 
contracted out to a third party vendor....   we have gone one better and 
also contracted out our DBA / Technical Support / Operations + 
hardware...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: $0 = Value Experience MOVING SUPPORT OFFSHORE...

- **From:** "Stephen Thomas" <talkingcats@gobble.clara.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t%4_4.742$x7.94163@nnrp3.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<20000602221243.22139.00000550@ng-bk1.aol.com> <MPG.13a257c213ab6b329896cc@news>`

```
Charles Haatvedt Jr. wrote in message ...
>In article <20000602221243.22139.00000550@ng-bk1.aol.com>, theodp@aol.com
>says...
>> Yukon Mama's post brought to mind a disturbing, but interesting,
development in
>> corporate mindsets today - that experience has little, if any, value.
>>
>> For example, a number of Y2K contract programmers (often receiving
$50+/hour)
>> with 20-25 years experience at my last client were offered disturbingly
lowball
>> permanent employee offers - about $50K per year - while those straight
out of a
>> local 9 month "learn to program" school started at $44K, often with a
several
>> thousand dollar signing bonus, and entry level employees of consulting
firms
>> with no experience commanded billing rates of $60+/hour.
>>
>> In fact, it's VERY rare indeed to find any permanent employee COBOL
positions
>> that pay even twice entry level salary, even though a person with 10+
years of
>> good experience most likely will outperform an entry level person by
400%-2500%
>> (if the entry level person even contributes anything positive in the
first year
>> or two!).
>>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>remove space in email address....

That's the way it is increasingly going in the UK.  I think it's generally
recognised - in the UK at any rate - that being an IT in an end-user company
is a career path to nowhere.  They'll always be a role for techies in
end-user companies but only if they reinvent themselves as 'business
facilitators' - which the best IT people always were, of course.  But now, I
think, that phrase it just code for putting up with reduced career
opportunities.

Also, if you work at an end-user company, you're seen as a revenue consumer
and treated accordingly.  If you work for an outsourcer or consultancy then
you're a revenue earner for your company, and have more chance of being
treated better.  I made the move from end-user company to consultancy two
years ago; those are certainly my experiences.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: $0 = Value Experience MOVING SUPPORT OFFSHORE...

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hb8om$8pp$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<20000602221243.22139.00000550@ng-bk1.aol.com> <MPG.13a257c213ab6b329896cc@news> <t%4_4.742$x7.94163@nnrp3.clara.net>`

```
I agree,
As a consultant you have a constant challenge and good motivation to follow
the trends, because you are 'hired' on your knowledge.

I made the change 15 years ago. We had completed a 3 years development, and
I looked in the mirror and said: "If you stay in this job, your skills
woun't change the next 5 years, you will be too busy to maintain the system
to look around", and then I ran away to be paid per hour.

Regards
Ib

Stephen Thomas skrev i meddelelsen ...

company

I

>opportunities.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-98eeda27be"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Jobs - Where? — 33 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### COBOL Jobs - Where?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JEr_4.3432$HD6.71274@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

YOW!  I pointed my browser over towards http://www.careermagic.com and did
a keyword search on COBOL OR COBOLII OR CICS OR DB2 for the mid-Atlantic
states (DC, DE, MD, VA, WV).  I have *no* idea what I did wrong but the
*only* position returned was:

--begin quoted text:


   Company:  GIANT FOOD
   Position: Warehouse Vacation Relief.
   Job Description: OVERVIEW: Select product for Giant Food stores 
                    Remove empty carts from trailers Put empty carts on
                    trailers Clean trailers Select product from picking
                    slots Stack product on carts and/or pallets Maintain
                    cleanliness of warehouse 

--end quoted text

Times *are* rough, I guess.

DD
```

>
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Cleaning compound/solution for propellers.

I will be the first to admit that there are things outside of my own
limited, petty experiences, true...

... but according to said experiences if a kid fresh out of tech school
comes into the shop asking for a bottle of prop-wash he'll get sent from
airman... whoopsie, showing my age, airperson to airperson in that shop,
each of whom will pull a Long, Serious Face and then send the kid along...
and the last person will say 'Nope, gee, I guess we're fresh outta that,
maybe you should head down to (next building) and ask there...'

... and so on, down the line, until someone takes pity.

DD
```

I was told in the Navy, new seamen were sent to fetch a bucket of "red and
white running oil".  If the seaman asks someone in the know, he will be told
to put his bucket under a certain lever.  No oil comes out, but a lot of
people will ask why he blew the ship's horn.

my own

tech school
> comes into the shop asking for a bottle of prop-wash he'll get
sent from
> airman... whoopsie, showing my age, airperson to airperson in
that shop,
> each of whom will pull a Long, Serious Face and then send the
kid along...
> and the last person will say 'Nope, gee, I guess we're fresh
outta that,
> maybe you should head down to (next building) and ask there...'
>
> ... and so on, down the line, until someone takes pity.
>
> DD

    That is of course what is supposed to happen.  But rumor
has it that on at least one occasion somebody must have either
taken pity or decided to reverse the joke by actually producing
prop wash.

    The airperson was not really that lucky though - he ended
up washing at least one propeller.  I would have liked to have
seen the sargent when he showed up with the stuff.

    This is all hearsay of course - I was never in the Air force.

    If someone comes up with a counter stretcher, many of us
might
be able to use that on our desks.  (Old railway diner car joke).
```

- **References:** `<JEr_4.3432$HD6.71274@iad-read.news.verio.net> <e6oxHywz$GA.83@cpmsnbbsa07> <nxR_4.3617$HD6.78243@iad-read.news.verio.net> <OGa$Qhxz$GA.310@cpmsnbbsa07> <BqS_4.3624$HD6.78912@iad-read.news.verio.net> <#J8K7Eyz$GA.325@cpmsnbbsa07> <393D1808.F1805300@capgemini.co.uk> <393E642F.6389D8CC@zip.com.au>`

You can get some bits for the bit bucket by hooking a memory chip up to the
wrong voltage.  The chip explodes, and the bits come out as a grey haze.
You have to be fast to catch them, however, as they tend to disperse fairly
quickly.

;<)

<a id="t-229291fcc0"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Sort Routine — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### COBOL Sort Routine

- **From:** "Mark Muniz" <whtwolf@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QEl_4.3916$Md1.94241@news.flash.net>`

```
Ok, a bit of advice is needed.  My project calls for a sort to be done on a
customer file.  In this file are balances for CURRENT, 30-Day, 60-Day &
90-Day.  I need to create a report that looks like:

(Report Page)
                                       OUTSTANDING BALANCES
CUST NO.     NAME     CURR-BAL     30-BAL     60-BAL     90-BAL    DATE


(Summary Page)
                                                 OUTSTANDING BALANCES
                                   TOTALS
CUMALATIVE
                      $ AMOUNT           PERCENT       $ AMOUNT
PERCENT
90                  9999999999              99%                9999999999
99%
60                  9999999999              99%                9999999999
99%
30                  9999999999              99%                9999999999
99%
CURRENT    9999999999              99%                9999999999
99%
GRND TOT   9999999999             999%               9999999999
999%

My real question is when to do all the calculations.  Should I add up all
the customer totals and the day balance totals before releasing the sort
record?  What about the the division for the percent?  A bit unsure in the
way I need to get the output.

Any help appreciated.

Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Sort Routine

- **From:** "Mark Muniz" <whtwolf@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d%l_4.3927$Md1.95534@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<QEl_4.3916$Md1.94241@news.flash.net>`

```
Sorry for the way the summary page looks!!

 (Summary Page)
                                           OUTSTANDING BAL
                       TOTALS                     CUMALATIVE
                       $AMT    %                     $AMT     %
90                   $$$$$    %                      $$$$$     %
60                   $$$$$    %                      $$$$$     %
30                   $$$$$    %                      $$$$$     %
CUR               $$$$$    %                      $$$$$     %
GR-TOT         $$$$$    %                      $$$$$     %

Hopefully this is better.

Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Sort Routine

- **From:** Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393AE6ED.2401E485@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<QEl_4.3916$Md1.94241@news.flash.net>`

```
In our shop, the sort would be done in a separate step and 
the sorted data file would go into a print program. I am sure that's
not what your teacher wants however. I suggest you do a SORT...USING 
and do all the calculations and printing in the output procedure.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Sort Routine

- **From:** "J. Michael Covington" <JCovington@InfoAve.Net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393B3572.A4069F12@InfoAve.Net>`
- **References:** `<QEl_4.3916$Md1.94241@news.flash.net> <393AE6ED.2401E485@swbell.net>`

```
I am currently taking cobol and our instructor has advised us to use
logical files and printer files whenever possible (whenever working with
externally described files).  I personally like them better myself, less
coding.

Ron wrote:

> In our shop, the sort would be done in a separate step and
> the sorted data file would go into a print program. I am sure that's
> not what your teacher wants however. I suggest you do a SORT...USING
> and do all the calculations and printing in the output procedure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Sort Routine

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U_U_4.1232$Hs5.159155@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<QEl_4.3916$Md1.94241@news.flash.net> <393AE6ED.2401E485@swbell.net> <393B3572.A4069F12@InfoAve.Net>`

```
J. Michael Covington <JCovington@InfoAve.Net> wrote in message
news:393B3572.A4069F12@InfoAve.Net...
> I am currently taking cobol and our instructor has advised us to use
> logical files and printer files whenever possible (whenever working with
> externally described files).

Your instructor is taking you down the corect path. While in mainframe batch
environments the external sort is very common, learning the use of SORT
[USING] [INPUT PROCEDURE IS..] [OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS...] will prepare you for
environments where external sorts are not an option, not to mention give you
general experience with SORT and MERGE applications. (In the real world, the
user always asks for data to be sorted any way EXCEPT the way it is
stored!).

MCM
```

---

<a id="t-74df4f1c4d"></a>
## Thread: Problem in MicroFocus Dialog System (GUI) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Problem in MicroFocus Dialog System (GUI)

- **From:** "Awais Baig" <awais22@khi.comsats.net.pk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hdnh5$119a$1@news.interpacket.net>`

```
Please help me if someone knows the solution of this
problem.

I want to execute my application in differet
windows 95 sessions silutaniously more than one
time from the same folder and same data folder.

I created an Icon on my desktop to execute this
appilication. After one time successfully execution
when I again open that application from that Icon
while previous one is already open, the system
is not opening another sessions, it just show me the
previous screen. Its mean that user is now restricted
with only one microfocus runtime product in one time.

But in character based (Dos or Unix based) Microfocus
runtime product I was able to execute my application on the
same machine simultaniously as many time as my system
memory support. I think this problem is due to using
Dialog System (GUI). Other wise charecter base works
very well. Other windows applications like MS Word can
be opened in many sessions (in one time we can open lot
of MS Words).  Please help me if someone knows the
solution of this problem.

I am using following Product of Microfocus.

Compiler :
Micro Focus COBOL
Version 3.2.46

RUN TIME ENVIRONMENT :
Micro Focus COBOL Toolset Version 3.2.46

Dialog System - V2.5.42
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem in MicroFocus Dialog System (GUI)

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hfmn0$268$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh5$119a$1@news.interpacket.net>`

```
Awais,

I am afriad this is a restriction of 16 bit Windows EXEs.  I believe
they are LARGE MODEL and only one instance can be run.  This
restriction has been removed in 32 bit.

They are a couple of ways around this...

1) You can create multiple EXEs and assign a shortcut/icon to
each.  This is not very elegant but it does work with the built
environment.

2) Some OBJ modules called MFREUSED.OBJ and MFREUSEX.OBJ
are provided which can be linked in to EXE/DLLs.  These allow EXEs and
DLLs to be reused.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International


Awais Baig <awais22@khi.comsats.net.pk> wrote in message
news:8hdnh5$119a$1@news.interpacket.net...
> Please help me if someone knows the solution of this
> problem.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem in MicroFocus Dialog System (GUI)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393C99BF.3F2C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh5$119a$1@news.interpacket.net> <8hfmn0$268$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Paddy Coleman wrote:
> 
> I am afriad this is a restriction of 16 bit Windows EXEs.  I believe
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Version 3.2.46

See page 12-33 of the Cobol User Guide for MF 3.2.  Usinf the MF
supplied mfreusex.obj causes the program (.EXE) to be renamed when it
loads so that another copy can be loaded (and this takes another new
name).
```

<a id="t-f11eef40df"></a>
## Thread: Total # of Records Problem. — 9 msgs

### Total # of Records Problem.

- **From:** "Awais Baig" <awais22@khi.comsats.net.pk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hdnh9$119a$2@news.interpacket.net>`

```
is there any command in microfocus cobol to calculate
total no. of records in the file.
actually i have program which process the complete
file, but the user should know how much time it will
ake or how much percentage is completed.
if i know the total no of records before
start process then it will be
very much easier to calculate the percentage
completed. If i use "read next at end of file" and
add 1 in a counter then i know the total no of records
but it will take too much time. if anyone knows any procedure
to solve this problem please let know.
```

#### ↳ Re: Total # of Records Problem.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393a6ebf.310319611@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh9$119a$2@news.interpacket.net>`

```
Where I last worked the solution was to have a header record in each
file, with a low-value key.  In this record was kept the file record
count.  When a record was added, the count was incremented.  When one
was deleted, the count was decremented.


On Sun, 4 Jun 2000 16:45:47 +0500, "Awais Baig"
<awais22@khi.comsats.net.pk> wrote:

>is there any command in microfocus cobol to calculate
>total no. of records in the file.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Total # of Records Problem.

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13a46bc510a77a2f9896cd@news>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh9$119a$2@news.interpacket.net> <393a6ebf.310319611@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
If the file being processed is a sequential file, one possible solution 
is to add another output file to the process which creates it. The 
additional file would have control counts, We have this as a standard in 
just about all of our programs. This is accomplished via a small 
subroutine which creates the control report from a passed table of 
control counters and descriptions...

Then in any process which reads the file in question, you could also read 
the control report file from the job which created the file in 
question...    this should give you the information you need

				hope this helps.....

                              chuck haatvedt


In article <393a6ebf.310319611@news.cox-internet.com>, 
thaneH@softwaresimple.com says...
> Where I last worked the solution was to have a header record in each
> file, with a low-value key.  In this record was kept the file record
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

#### ↳ Re: Total # of Records Problem.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393ADC48.B38A4621@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh9$119a$2@news.interpacket.net>`

```
Awais Baig wrote:
> 
> is there any command in microfocus cobol to calculate
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> to solve this problem please let know.

Take a directory listing and find out how many bytes there are. 
Divide this by the record length (plus 2 on DOS, plus 1 on Unix). 
This will give you the answer fairly quickly.

Now your challenge is to find the directory size, this is compiler
dependant.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Total # of Records Problem.

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hfn7j$28u$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh9$119a$2@news.interpacket.net> <393ADC48.B38A4621@zip.com.au>`

```
Awais,

If you are using Net Express 3.0 or later then you can use the following
method for indexed files:

...
select testfile assign ...
   organization indexed
   access mode dynamic
   record key is ...
...
fd  testfile.

01  tf-record                        pic x(20).
...
working-storage section.

01  ws-rec-count                  pic x(2) comp-x.

linkage section.

01  ls-fcd.
copy "xfhfcd.cpy".

procedure division.
     ...
     open i-o testfile.
     set address ls-fcd to address of fh--fcd of testfile.
     move fcd-relative-key to ws-rec-count.
     ...

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:393ADC48.B38A4621@zip.com.au...
> Awais Baig wrote:
> >
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

This method should work on file formats 3, 4 and 8 on NE30+.  It will not
work on OCDS but should be fine on Server Express.  Another point, this
method will only work on files created with NE30+ and SE.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393BA755.432D8797@cusys.edu>`

I would explore to see if you can ask your operating system how many
records you have.  Maybe you can run an API to see the current file size
and then divide by your record length.  What OS are you using?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Total # of Records Problem.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393C9BFC.7E1C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8hdnh9$119a$2@news.interpacket.net> <393ADC48.B38A4621@zip.com.au> <8hfn7j$28u$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <393BA755.432D8797@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I would explore to see if you can ask your operating system how many
> records you have.  Maybe you can run an API to see the current file size
> and then divide by your record length.  What OS are you using?

That would only work if the file was sequential and fixed length.  You
would also need to know of any record headers and/or trailers and of
file headers and of slack space at end of file.

With any file that allowed logical deletion of records this would fail.

I do know that MF Level II indexed files hold a 'last record' in the
.IDX file.  I have a C program which I wrote (decades ago it seems) that
will display key details, record size etc. But it must scan the data
file to count the undeleted records.
```

On Sun, 04 Jun 2000 05:46:26 GMT, William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>traffic there). So I thought I'd post here.

>suggest a good book for him to learn the terminology & basic concepts?


In my bookshelf:

James G. Janossy : CICS/ESA Primer - ISBN 0471309915

Is intended for programmers, but gives a nice CICS overview in chapter 2

Eugene S. Hudders: CICS A Guide to Internal Structure - ISBN 0471521728
Is starting point for sysprog type info

Josepg J. Le Bert: CICS Essentials for application developers and programmers - ISBN
0070358699
Chapter 3: Basic concepts and terminology

HTH

Volker Bandke
