<a id="t-0eacb2d143"></a>
## Thread: Runtime fees for software developers? — 28 msgs

### Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** "Norman Juers" <agri@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Assuming that MERANT is going to charge runtime fees what would be the best
approach for software developers. It is obvious that application of runtime
fees for small software developers based on flat fees, such as, $870 for 10
runtimes is unworkable.  The model used here is clearly a corporate model.

Perhaps a constructive thing to do would be to suggest ways that MERANT
could structure the runtime fee for software developers.

The following are some ideas to start the discussion:

1.  Fee based on a percent of software sale price.
2.  One time buyout of fees.
3   Fees have a cap at a set amount.
4.  Volume discount with fees that phase out
5.  None of the above. There is no good way

A fee structure should take the following into account:

1.  Developers should be able to market low-end products.
2.  Fees should have a relationship to revenue.
3.  Accounting for fees should be simple and verifiable.

I would prefer not to have any runtime fee at all, but if MERANT is going to
charge a runtime fee I hope it is something that software developers can
live with.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** "Timothy O. Nicholson" <kf4rtx@nospam.nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60X_4.30815$Ad6.217833@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I agree!  I think it's absolutely rediculous for Merant to charge the kinds
of fees they do for runtimes.  $870 for a pack of 10 licenses, plus the $147
per year maintenance per 10 pack is rediculous!  And Merant isn't the only
one.  AcuCOBOL also charges runtime fees, but theirs is a bit less ($55 for
the first user, $11 for each additional - this as long as you are an ISV,
which will cost you about $5500 per year).  As a project manager for a
software developer, I find it financially unfeasable to pay this kind of
runtime fee (or any runtime fee for that matter) when other development
platforms such as Visual Basic and Java do not charge fees.  COBOL vendors
need to wake up and smell the coffee.  They are going to lose the software
developer arena.

I must put a plug in for Fujitsu COBOL here.  They do not charge runtime
fees for their single threaded runtimes.  And their product is about 1/2 the
cost of Merant and AcuCOBOL when using the Fujitsu competitive upgrade
offer.  My company was able to work out a deal with Merant, otherwise we
would have to look seriously at another COBOL vendor, probably Fujitsu.

to
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394033B4.921C7DB0@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <60X_4.30815$Ad6.217833@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <8hj5ch$2t8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <393d72d8.155536492@207.126.101.100>`

```
This brings up an interesting point.  Where run-times were required in
the mainframe environment, they were generally purchased by the
purchaser of the software.  Thus if you purchased package X that
required the PL/1 run-time, you had to purchase the PL/1 run-time from
IBM.  I believe one of my previous shops had to license the Pascal
run-time because it was required by ISPF/PDF (or we didn't and couldn't
use one of the features of ISPF/PDF).  This wasn't as onerous as it
might seem because a shop normally would have that run-time for their
own development.  Also all file support was and is a part of the
operating system.  Data Base support is paid for separately.  I have not
heard of application vendors bundling compiler or data base run-times
with their packages.  The sort also is purchased/licensed by the shop
with the computer, not supplied by an application vendor.   

Another related point is what happens if I buy package A from vendor X
and package B from vendor Y, both of which are for the same set of PC's
and both of which use the Merant run-time?  What if both of them are
from Fujitsu and use the Power B sort or the multi-threaded run-time?    

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 6 Jun 2000 11:31:04 -0400, "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jo405.36$075.11050@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <60X_4.30815$Ad6.217833@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <8hj5ch$2t8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <393d72d8.155536492@207.126.101.100> <394033B4.921C7DB0@istar.ca>`

```
Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:394033B4.921C7DB0@istar.ca...
> I have not
> heard of application vendors bundling compiler or data base run-times
> with their packages.

Microsoft Visual BASIC and Visual C/C++ applications for micros require
runtimes; they are shipped as Windows DLL's and usually are bundled with the
application packages. (These runtimes do not require separate, explicit
Microsoft EULA's).

Microfocus COBOL applications (PC, Unix) have always AFAIK required
runtimes. I've seen those runtimes both bundled (e.g., Macola) and
separately user-licensed (e.g., the company I used to work for, System
Design).

So it's not at all an 'event' when an application vendor requires the
end-user to license and/or install runtime support.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hqopd$4ij$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <60X_4.30815$Ad6.217833@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <8hj5ch$2t8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <393d72d8.155536492@207.126.101.100> <394033B4.921C7DB0@istar.ca> <jo405.36$075.11050@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Personally, I consider it an 'event' for the small time developer...We go
out and spend many thousands of dollars on a compiler...We pay extremely
high (US$600.00+) annual maintenance fees to MF...We develop hundreds of
thousands of lines of code into a product...We then sell the product at a
reasonable price to make a living...We have made critical business decisions
on MF NE not having runtime fees...How many small businesses can afford
US$10,000.00 for a web page app runtime fee?...How many customers will
upgrade to the new version of software if they have to pay US$10,000.00
additional dollars???

Implementation of runtime fees should be grand-fathered in since part of the
reason for existing customers having NE in the first place was because there
were no runtime fees (unless you developed a client server app or a Dialog
system app)...Let the new customers who are considering purchasing NE decide
if the product merits having to pay runtime fees, not the existing
customers...Instead, MF seems to care very little for the individual
developer...I have definitely seen this over the years...Support has been
far less than acceptable, etc...MF should not charge existing customers this
new runtime fee...Also, they should not make us stay back leveled with the
compiler...After all, we help them find more problems with the compiler than
their own internal technical staff...

Why bundle so many products into NE?...Dialog system for example...I have no
interest in Dialog system...Why should I have to pay for it?...I currently
have no interest in half the stuff that is part of NE...Separate out this
stuff into individual products, and let the people who want to use Dialog
system pay for it, not those that do not want to use it...

If what I have read here is true, I will not be upgrading to the newer
version of NE anytime soon either...I will also probably discontinue paying
US$600.00 annual maintenance for NE...After all, it will not be updated and
repaired as required since it will be back leveled...No annual maintenance
means even less money for MF...

QUESTION: Where was it published that MF will be charging these fees...As a
customer, currently under annual maintenance, I have seen nothing other than
the postings here in the newsgroup...Hell, I don't even know who my sales
person is anymore...No one has contacted me...No one has even told me what
improvements are in the new version of NE that warrant such a major change
in direction for the runtime...No mailings, no calls, NOTHING from MF...

kenmullins
Atlanta, GA


"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:jo405.36$075.11050@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> runtimes; they are shipped as Windows DLL's and usually are bundled with
the
> application packages. (These runtimes do not require separate, explicit
> Microsoft EULA's).
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
> -Are you outraged by the high price you had to pay for such product
and the overpriced Compiler maintenance fees?

NO - I use MF v 5.0 and SP2

>
> -Do you have to pay exorbitant fees for copies of runtime when you
distribute your COBOL applications?

NO

>
> -Are you uncomfortable with installation locks that your current
COBOL Compiler forces you to use?

NO , there are none

>
> -Are you tired of bugs in your current COBOL Compiler?

No, I am not aware of any.

>
> -Are you frustrated by the lack of response from the Technical
Support?

NO, never need it.

>
> -Are you disappointed by the poor quality of the GUI tools in your
current COBOL Compiler?  The lack of features and options of your COBOL
Compiler for developing sophisticated software for Client/Server and
the INTERNET using 100%> COBOL/OO COBOL?

NO, I use SP2 - OO is not the way to go.

>
> -Are you always busy rewriting your COBOL applications just because
you had to> upgrade your current COBOL Compiler to the new version (to
supposedly fix the> bugs of the previous one!)

NO, never need to do that.

I think that Merant needs to charge runtime fees (if they are)
because they can't survive selling COBOL compilers and will go
out of business if they don't find a way to make more money.

I stick to COBOL 60 and minimalist methods and therefore avoid
all this sort of aggro.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

```
"Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in
message news:8hqopd$4ij$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
<much snippage>
> ...Instead, MF seems to care very little for the individual
> developer...I have definitely seen this over the years...

Over the years I have OFTEN disagreed with Ken (and probably will in the
future). HOWEVER, this is a true statement (from my perception) - has been
for many years and probably will be in the future.  (Other than the fact that
it is now MERANT and not Micro Focus).

The thing that strikes me as relatively new (and more significant) is the
fact that they now really care "minimally" about COBOL itself (even for large
shops).  If you missed it, the following is from their June 6th press release
(concerning their annual results),

'A YEAR OF TRANSITION

Sexton said that more generally, the results reflected Merant's transition
into a modern technology firm.

"We took a company based on our traditional COBOL products and services and
moved towards becoming an e-business," he said in a telephone interview.

The company had been unable to replace services as quickly as expected, with
demand for its traditional products waning, but its Egility operations had
grown fast.

Merant Egility operations, which forms the core of the company's e-business
software, now makes up over half of its revenue, and Sexton said he expected
it to constitute two thirds of the business in the next financial year. '

   ***

For the full press release, see

 http://moneycentral.msn.com/scripts/webquote.dll?iPage=news&Symbol=MRNT

(this may require a sign-on, I don't know)
```

```
> Personally, I consider it an 'event' for the small time developer...We go

> > So it's not at all an 'event' when an application vendor requires the

Oh, no, I meant that the idea of a commercial application requiring the
end-user to license some kind of run-time is not an "event" in the sense
that it has been hitherto unknown by homo sapiens.

In the circumstance being reviewed, I find that Merant's actions are not
acceptable

"Use my compiler to develop applications for the commercial market and get
us lots of users, please."

"OK, did that. Lots of customers."

"Well, here's a new release with some great new features. Since you probably
want support  - and BTW, you know how it goes when there is a new release,
and of course I can't say this "officially",  but support for previous
releases gets, well, "iffy?" - I will now demand a tribute for the customers
you earned."

At best, this seems a variation of Ye Olde Baite and Switche.

To use a more timely analogy, it sounds a lot like the business practices of
some guys in NewYork City who specialize in garbage pickup and whose
surnames often end in vowels.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** "Ron Langer" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UMc05.1090$907.32700@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

At Fujitsu Software, we are extremely committed to the COBOL market and
always try to put the needs of COBOL programmers first. We believe that
it's the key to earning your respect and building a long-term mutually
beneficial business partnership. We believe that COBOL programmers deserve
a reasonably priced COBOL compiler with high performance, unmatched
reliability, superior scalability, and royalty free COBOL run-times.

We want to make sure that the COBOL development community understands that
all existing Fujitsu COBOL run-time features that have been free in the
past ARE STILL FREE! In Version 5, we have created a new, optional
multi-threaded run-time system targeted for enterprise server-based
applications. We have tried to keep the prices low and to make the
licensing simple. There will be a one-time charge for this OPTIONAL
run-time system. COBOL programmers will continue to have access to the
royalty-free Fujitsu run-time system and may develop and deploy COBOL
applications with no license fees required (i.e. no strings attached.).
These applications may include Internet (web) based components that are
single threaded and may run on servers with no license fee required.

Fujitsu Software's business model regarding English-language COBOL products
(sold outside of Japan, Korea and China) is to provide a robust, high
performance, single-threaded COBOL development environment with no required
run-time licensing fees. This allows developers to create and deploy COBOL
applications without paying additional run-time fees or deal with tracking
run-time licenses. Since we coexist with other established development
products such as Visual Basic�, C++, and Java, we are trying to keep the
price of our COBOL compiler and associated development tools competitive.

We believe that COBOL has a bright future. Our intention is to continue to
invest in creating quality tools and COBOL compiler technology. We plan to
keep our prices consistent with this optimistic outlook and help keep COBOL
at the forefront of business programming in the 21st century.

Sincerely,

Ron Langer
Director, COBOL Sales and Marketing
Fujitsu Software
Web: www.adtools.com
Email: cobol@adtools.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** tonyhill5007@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ibga0$f7d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I'm the General Manager of the ACT Business Unit in MERANT and my
responsibilities include Net Express. On behalf of MERANT, I would like
to thank this group for their comments on the Net Express run-time
licensing policy.

Following a thorough review of your comments, and extensive discussion
within MERANT,  I have discovered that, although we have had a policy
of charging run-time licence fees for Net Express for some time, we
have not communicated this at all well to our customers in North
America. Accordingly, I am pleased to inform you that, effective
immediately, we plan to implement a North American run-time amnesty
program for Net Express 3.1 targeted at small development
organisations.

The two principal qualifying criteria for the run-time amnesty are that
you have a total of five or less Net Express development licences and
your application is sold for less than $1000 per user. Further details
of the amnesty can be obtained from your normal MERANT contact or by
calling our SupportNet team on 1-800-443-1601, option 6.

Finally, thanks again for your interest in MERANT. Please feel free to
e-mail me directly on tony.hill@merant.com if you have any urgent
comments.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
I think that this sounds like too little too late, the discussion has
raged for a considerable period of time.  If this was done a week ago
then it would have killed the discussion dead, now there is a major
resistance to the concept of runtime and my vendor has proclaimed zero
run time fees are here to stay.

that the agreement may expire leaving a new development or open source

software developer puts on an extra programmer and doubles the cost of
his software?

I believe that in order to retain and gain new business in this market
you should seriously consider removing the run time licensing in it's
entirety.

Ken Foskey
```

## Thread: string concatination ? — 125 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### string concatination ?

- **From:** "Clayton Brendish" <claybrendish1999@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Is there an easy way in cobol to do a string concatination


ie

Variable1    is a   Pic X(20)  contains "abcdef                   "
Variable2  is a     Pic X(20) contains  "ghi                          "


I want to program variable3 to contain "abcdefghi             "


How can I do this, I am learning cobol, would appreciate any suggestions


Clayton
```

```

>

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: string concatination ?

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393bf631.7859481@wingate>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
On Mon, 5 Jun 2000 20:13:20 +0100, "Clayton Brendish"
<claybrendish1999@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Is there an easy way in cobol to do a string concatination
>ie
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I want to program variable3 to contain "abcdefghi             "

Clayton, 

There is a very easy way, assuming your data is always terminated with
a space:

STRING VARIABLE-1 
	VARIABLE-2 DELIMITED SPACE INTO VARIABLE-3.

If your data might be terminated with other characters or might
contain an embedded space, it gets more complex; you might look into
the UNSTRING verb for more possibilities.

STRING and UNSTRING are incredibly useful for text manipulation...

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

#### ↳ Re: string concatination ?

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10e60abc.bfe16f73@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Research the STRING statement:

The STRING statement provides juxtaposition (don't ask me what
juxtaposition is) of the partial or complete contents of two or
more data items into a single data item.

General Format

       |                    |             |identifier-2|| |
STRING | |identifier-1| ... |DELIMITED BY |literal-2   || |  ...
       | |�literal-1  |     |             |SIZE        || |

    INTO identifier-3

    [WITH POINTER identifier-4]

    [ON OVERFLOW imperative-statement-1]

    [NOT ON OVERFLOW imperative-statement-2]

    [END-STRING]

Please note: this is the documentation for Merant NetExpress.

Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```

decide which one you like the best.
```

#### ↳ Re: string concatination ?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```

- posted and emailed -

In article <8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>,
Clayton Brendish <claybrendish@hotmail.com> wrote:
>Is there an easy way in cobol to do a string concatination
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>How can I do this, I am learning cobol, would appreciate any suggestions

Well, a part of learning *anything* is learning how to ask other people to
do your homework for you... so here's a solution!

First you have to define your data properly.

01  WK-VAR1.                                                
    05  VARIABLE1 PIC X(20) VALUE 'ABCDEF              '.   
    05  VAR1CHAR REDEFINES VARIABLE1 OCCURS 20 PIC X.       
01  WK-VAR2.                                                
    05  VARIABLE2 PIC X(30) VALUE 'GHI                 '.   
    05  VAR2CHAR REDEFINES VARIABLE2 OCCURS 20 PIC X.       
01  WK-VAR3.                                                
    05  VARIABLE3 PIC X(20) VALUE LOW-VALUES.               
    05  VAR3CHAR REDEFINES VARIABLE3 OCCURS 20 PIC X.       
01  SUB1 PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE +0.                          
01  SUB2 PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE +0.                          
01  SUB3 PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE +0.                          

... and now it is simple!

*                                                       
     PERFORM A57182-RELOCATE-VAR1 THRU A57182-EXIT      
      VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1                          
       UNTIL SUB1 > 20 OR VAR1CHAR (SUB1) = X'40'.      
*                                                       
     ADD 1, SUB1 GIVING SUB3.                           
*                                                       
     PERFORM B91373-RELOCATE-VAR2 THRU A91373-EXIT      
      VARYING SUB2 FROM 1 BY 1                          
       UNTIL SUB2 > 20 OR VAR1CHAR (SUB1) = X'40'.      
*                                                       
     INSPECT WK-VAR3 REPLACING ALL X'00' BY X'40'.      
*                                                       
 A57182-RELOCATE-VAR1.                                 
     IF VAR1CHAR (SUB1) = X'40'                        
         MOVE +9999 TO SUB1                            
     ELSE                                              
         MOVE VAR1CHAR (SUB1) TO VAR3CHAR (SUB1).      
*                                                      
 A57182-EXIT.                                          
     EXIT.                                             
*                                                      
 B91373-RELOCATE-VAR2.                                 
     IF VAR2CHAR (SUB2) = X'40'                        
         MOVE +9999 TO SUB2                            
     ELSE                                              
         MOVE VAR2CHAR (SUB2) TO VAR3CHAR (SUB3)       
         ADD +1 TO SUB3.                               
*                                                      
 B91373-EXIT.                                          
     EXIT.                                             
*                                                      

DD
```

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>

```
> 
> In article <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> for, though!

Also a good example of why exits are not a good idea. If the standards
mandate them, code them; but that doesn't make it a good idea. (Please
let's not get into another discussion of how many consultants can dance
on a stack of $100 bills <G>). Really not intended as a flame, NA; but I
think the STRING approach is much cleaner (I would do this exercise that
way, e.g., STRING  ... ... DELIMITED BY SPACE ...). Yours smacks of
someone who was coding Assembler many moons ago.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393D0202.A523E008@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> NA wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> mandate them, code them; but that doesn't make it a good idea. 

Being burned like this is a big part of my dislike for exits.

> (Please
> let's not get into another discussion of how many consultants can dance
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone who was coding Assembler many moons ago.

STRING is nice, until we start getting data with embedded spaces.  If we
are looking for the last non-space, instead of the first space, STRING
falls short.  Fortunately reference modification fits well here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393D20FC.913B0307@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <393D0202.A523E008@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
(snip)
> > >
> > > My apologies... that should be B91373-EXIT.  That's what compilers are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Being burned like this is a big part of my dislike for exits.

My point exactly!

> > (Please let's not get into another discussion of how many consultants can dance
> > on a stack of $100 bills <G>). Really not intended as a flame, NA; but I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> falls short.  Fortunately reference modification fits well here.

True, but the guy who originally posted this (Clayton - anybody remember
Clayton?) only had trailing spaces. If I had to eliminate spaces and the
input might contain embedded spaces, I'd probably loop through the data,
moving only bytes greater than spaces to the output field. The subscript
on my PERFORM VARYING ... would be my pointer to the input byte and I'd
maintain my own subscript for the output byte. (yes, I said subscript)

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hjan4$4qs$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <393D0202.A523E008@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message >
>STRING is nice, until we start getting data with embedded spaces.  If we
>are looking for the last non-space, instead of the first space, STRING
>falls short.  Fortunately reference modification fits well here.

I am surprised that no one suggested the NUMVAL function. That is what I am
using for all numeric data now, and it seems to be more reliable than
unstring.  It will handle a number delimited by NULL, quite nicely, for
example.  That would break the unstring method, and is common with data
coming from C.

COMPUTE proper-cobol-numeric =
    FUNCTION NUMVAL( screwy-input-data-string).

To do the same with unstring, you really need:

UNSTRING screwy-input-data-string
    DELIMITED BY (NOT NUMERIC)
    INTO proper-cobol-numeric.

Which, I believe, is syntactically incorrect, though it would be nice to be
able to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4Na%4.3809$HD6.86280@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <393D0202.A523E008@cusys.edu> <8hjan4$4qs$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <8hjan4$4qs$1@news.igs.net>,
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>COMPUTE proper-cobol-numeric =
>    FUNCTION NUMVAL( screwy-input-data-string).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>able to do.

Aye... I believe the targer of an UNSTRING has to be an alpha field.
Since I seem to be in a code-writing mood how about...

01  WHACKY-INPUT-REC.                                              
    05  CHARFLD01 PIC X(20).                                       
    05  CHARFLD02 PIC X(20).                                       
    05  INPUT-SHOULD-BE-NUM PIC X(10)                              
    05  CHARFLD03 PIC X(20).                                       
01  WKFLD.                                                         
    05  SHOULD-BE-NUM-CHAR PIC X(10) JUST RIGHT.                   
    05  SHOULD-BE-NUM REDEFINES SHOULD-BE-NUM-CHAR PIC 9(10).      
                                                                   
    MOVE ZEROES TO SHOULD-BE-NUM                                   
    PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 10 BY -1                             
     UNTIL SUB1 < 1                                                
        IF INPUT-SHOULD-BE-NUM(SUB1:1) NUMERIC                     
            MOVE INPUT-SHOULD-BE-NUM(1:SUB1) TO SHOULD-BE-NUM-CHAR 
        MOVE +0 TO SUB1                                            
        END-IF                                                     
    END-PERFORM                                                    
    .                                                              

... and if I had a manual I could even replace that 'FROM 10' with
something like 'FROM (FUNCTION (LENGTH OF INPUT-SHOULD-BE-CHAR))' or the
like.

DD
```

```
I have no idea if your assumption was correct or not.  The problem, as
stated(by someone else), did not say, which is precisely why I suggested
NUMVAL, as it is designed to do the job.  I also suggested that the UNSTRING
verb would be nicer if you could use conditionals (IE:- NOT NUMERIC) as
delimiters instead of littorals, as it would come closer.  However, you
cannot.

Regardless of all that, however, I think I prefer one line that works to
five lines that work some of the time.
```

```
If my assumption was incorrect, however, and INPUT-SHOULD-BE-CHAR

DD
```

```
as a whole, is not a good idea.

>(Please
>let's not get into another discussion of how many consultants can dance
>on a stack of $100 bills <G>).

At the moment I, myself, am the only one of those creatures whom I'd like
to be doing such a C-note Charleston.

>Really not intended as a flame, NA; but I
>think the STRING approach is much cleaner (I would do this exercise that

manuals *and* did your own homework, right?

>Yours smacks of
>someone who was coding Assembler many moons ago.

Ain't is a stroll down memory lane, though?  Shall I ask for a show of
hands as to who read that snippet (coded all off-hand, of course... hence
the typo) and was tempted to punch out the screen?

('It's *him*!  *He* wrote that crap!  I'd know that style *anywhere*, that
son-on-the-beach cost me more sleep at three-inna-mornin' readin' through
that *trash*... lemme at 'im!  I'll moider da bum!')

DD
```

```
NA wrote:
> 
> In article <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net>,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Between laughing and shaking my head, I'm surprised at you, DD.  You
forgot to include MOVE LOW-VALUES TO WK-VAR3 (so expressively named, as
well).  How many times have you made that mistake?  Personally, I think
you stole that code out of one our dwindling supply of 22-year-old
legacy masterpieces.  And I can't believe you actually snuck X'40' past
IKFCBLOO.

My personal favorite, although it has gone on to its greater reward, was
a macro-level CICS COBOL program that had 40 odd paragraphs similar to
the following:

A58612.
    ALTER B79943 TO PROCEED TO B41523.

Given that example, your A57182-RELOCATE-VAR1 is a model of
self-documentation.  

Using a STRING command?  Why, that's, that's ... cheatin'!
```

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> ;<)

Fortunately, I was never able to identify the miscreant, because the the
original author failed to sign his work (I wonder why).  There was also
no DATE-WRITTEN comment in the program, although it was pretty old when
I first saw it in 1987.  My guess is that it was written around 1972,
based on somewhat similar programs in the same application.

None of the paragraph names or data names in that program contained any
hyphens, and none were longer that 8 characters, which led me to believe
it was converted from assembler.  It also had conditional assembly
copybooks expanded in the pre-processor.  That application was shut down
in 1992, and the source code was discarded.

I sometimes wish that I had saved a copy as an example of putrid code.
```

```
      ' FILSTAT = ', F85943-CUSTMAST-FILSTAT
       DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO ERRLOG-REC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Efficient and inefficient code was Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393D3F15.B5061613@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <r0Z_4.3705$HD6.82899@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net> <yl7%4.3767$HD6.85550@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
I know that the various flavors of COBOL 85 from IBM have cleaned up
their act and generate fairly good and in some cases downright awesome
code.  Is STRING/UNSTRING still inefficient?  Are all of the variations
of these verbs inefficient?  Parenthetically, I was pleasantly surprised
at the code generated for reference modification when I wrote a parsing
program to find all uses of CALL and COPY in a program.  

On the other hand I have found these areas of inefficiency:
1.  FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DAY and FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE (generates a
lengthy preamble, calls a subroutine that returns the integer in
binary).  I have written routines in COBOL that are substantially
faster.

2. ADD FIELD-A TO FIELD-B where both fields are PACKED-DECIMAL generate
add packed, zero add packed.  I think the ZAP is to take force a
potential negative zero obtained by an addition that also causes an
ignored overflow such that the remaining digits are zero.

3.  ADD FIELD-A TO FIELD-B where both fields S9(9) BINARY generates
double precision binary arithmetic.  I believe this is because the
compiler doesn't differentiate between this and ADD FIELD-A FIELD-B ...
TO FIELD-N where you have to take into account intermediate overflow.

4.  DIVIDE A INTO B GIVING C REMAINDER D where all operands have the
same number of decimal places doesn't take advantage of the fact that
the divide operation provides a remainder.  The code generated is DIVIDE
A INTO B GIVING C, COMPUTE D = B - (A * C).

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca

     
NA wrote:
> 
> In article <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net>,
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hkf5q$34p2$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net> <yl7%4.3767$HD6.85550@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D3F15.B5061613@istar.ca> <lei%4.4359$HD6.89954@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393EC8E9.7624334C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <r0Z_4.3705$HD6.82899@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net> <yl7%4.3767$HD6.85550@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D3F15.B5061613@istar.ca> <iyr%4.16586$Gh.193907@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> called GETMAIN). It that still true?

Definitely not true (from COBOL II, anyway). The code generated depends
on what you've specified (quelle surprise). If it's a bunch of things
all DELIMITED BY SIZE, I think you just get a string (no pun intended)
of MVC's. This has been such a long thread, I'm going to check a couple
out (providing I remember to do so).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficient and inefficient code was Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hm0f9$iih$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <r0Z_4.3705$HD6.82899@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net> <yl7%4.3767$HD6.85550@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D3F15.B5061613@istar.ca> <iyr%4.16586$Gh.193907@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
I know that you MAY use STRING/UNSTRING under CICS.  What I don't know is
whether this is because it no  longer needs to get its own storage - or if it
is because it uses EXEC CICS GETMAIN rather than an OS GETMAIN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficient and inefficient code was Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hm0cp$npm$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <r0Z_4.3705$HD6.82899@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net> <yl7%4.3767$HD6.85550@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D3F15.B5061613@istar.ca>`

```
Clark,
  For your arithmetic expressions, what values did you have for NUMPROC and
OPT compiler options.  My guess (and I haven't tried this) is that if you
have NUMPROC(PFDSGN) and OPT, that you will find the generated assembler (at
least a little) more efficient than with NOPFDSGN or MIG (for NUMPROC).

The manual SAYS that PFDSGN "assumes" that your signs are correct, so at
least some of the "fix up" code shouldn't be there.  If, on the other hand,
you use NOPFDSGN, it says that it *will* do sign-fix-up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficient and inefficient code was Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3940312B.13CBBB39@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393C2654.BBC91998@worldnet.att.net> <r0Z_4.3705$HD6.82899@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393CEB4B.F5B1E8D7@attglobal.net> <yl7%4.3767$HD6.85550@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D3F15.B5061613@istar.ca> <8hm0cp$npm$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The compile options were OPT(FULL) TRUNC(OPT) and NUMPROC(MIG).
NUMPROC(PFD) would be a disaster in that shop since they use CSP.  Also
they have mixed F and C zones in fields defined as numeric.  In
addition, the NUMPROC(PFD) should not matter in this instance.  I am
fairly certain that the Add Packed, Zero Add Packed and Subtract Packed,
Zero Add Packed combinations are to guarantee that -1 + -999 = +0 not -0
for a field defined as S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL.  I suspect a new compile
option to allow negative zero would be required in order to eliminate
the Zero Add Packed.

In regard to the binary arithmetic using double precision for fields
defined as S9(9) BINARY, I am guessing that it is the some code
construct used so that 900,000,000 + 900,000,000 + 900,000,000 -
800,000,000 - 800,000,000 - 800,000,000 = 300,000,000 and not some
strange answer due to overflow.   What I would like to see is a
recognition in the code generation for binary arithmetic involving just
2 operands, it doesn't matter if you use double precision to handle
overflow, you get the same answer as if you didn't.  Note that this is
for TRUNC(OPT) and I believe the same thing is true for TRUNC(BIN) from
testing I did quite a while ago. 

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca 
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Clark,
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qyD%4.4510$HD6.97473@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393dcc04.178367953@207.126.101.100> <zZo%4.4381$HD6.92003@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393e95d0.230035725@207.126.101.100>`

```
In article <393e95d0.230035725@207.126.101.100>,
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
>On Wed, 07 Jun 2000 09:54:39 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net (NA) wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Maybe.

... and maybe not.  At my present pig-of-a-contrct jobsite we just got a
New Directrix, a woman who occupies the Corner Office of Corner Offices.
She called together all Managers and told them about New, Great Changes
she was instituting... like no more casual Fridays... and no more
microwaves or refrigerators... and every group now needs a sign-in sheet,
where every person must sign in and out at the beginning of the workday.

I heard this and asked 'So tell me... which Manager said 'Oh, *my* people
don't need to deal with that... nobody can get on our floor without a
mag-card for entrance, I'll just tell everyone they have to 'swipe' in and
get their entry-time from the logs.'?'

There was a quiet laugh from the assembled group and I continued 'No?
Well, which manager said 'No need for *my* people to do that; I'll just
have them log on when they come in and get their time from the
server-log'... no?  Well, which manager said 'No need for *my* people to
do that; I'll just have them sign into Lotus Notes and get their time in
from there'... no?  Well, which manager said 'No need for *my* people to
do that... I'll just have them log onto the OS390 and get their time from
the logs there'?

You see, *that* is what a Manager does, in part, in my eyes... a Manager
stands up and says 'You want to spew foolishness?  Fine, but spew it at
*me*, don't you *dare* spew at My People... *My* People have more
important jobs to do than to listen to you prattle on about sign-in
sheets.'

So... do you still think 'maybe'?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:
> 
> In article <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> for, though!

And if there was an actual A91373-EXIT then the compiler will be silent
and we have a nice little GO TO bug.  It may be that A91373-EXIT is
below the B91373-EXIT so a little extra code is executed on each PERFORM
and this may be entirely innocuous _most_ of the time, until one day it
blows up.

This is an excellent example of why PERFORM THRU and GO TO EXIT are best
not used, they are porone to these sort of silly errors.  Of course
there are some who have checkers to enforce 
and pick up these errors - just don't rely on the compiler doing it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)

```
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

>below the B91373-EXIT so a little extra code is executed on each PERFORM
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

best not used because someone might choose a paragraph-name badly?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> > Eh?  PERFORM THRU and GO TO -EXIT (the latter I did not use, notice!) are
> > best not used because someone might choose a paragraph-name badly?
> 
> Of course not, it's simply bad technique.

If good structured design can be done without using exits, and
occasionally exits will cause problems (with cut and paste coding), then
the only argument is whether it is easier to maintain code with exits. 
This argument will be solved once and for all once exit paragraph
commands are available.
```

```
one might be a bit... cautious about inserting a few bars of, say, a Lindy
Hop and instead choose to...

... go for Baroque.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393D3B0E.EBF19BF3@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <UQ9%4.3795$HD6.86400@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D2F95.44E6E247@cusys.edu> <Tta%4.3804$HD6.86472@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:
> 
> >The argument will be that we won't need to design code with exit
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> for maintenance of existing code.

I don't see the relationship between these two paragraphs.  Should I
look at the quality of existing programs to decide how good I need to
make my new programs?  If the percentage was only 60%, would writing
good new programs be worth considering?  

I have never seen standards for new code and standards for existing code
to be the same.  We NEVER retrofit old code to fit current standards -
instead we make the minimal amount of changes to solve the current
problem well.  We don't rename variables to fit new standards, we don't
even remove ALTER statements.  

Maybe this is because I have worked in mainframe environments where we
like to isolate our changes as much as possible.  (this safety factor of
isolation may be why OO hasn't caught on in the enterprise).   But
gradually standards do evolve.  Even with 70-80% of the budgets
allocated towards existing code, new programs get written.  Most shops
have standards for these new programs, and gradually higher percentages
of the shop fit recent standards.


Your statement is as if I came up with a better road making material. 
You say this fails to take into account that 60-70% of the road budget
is spent on maintenance.  And I am confused as to why this SHOULD take
that into account.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Upgrading standards on existing programs was Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394036EE.619E85F8@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <393D2F95.44E6E247@cusys.edu> <Tta%4.3804$HD6.86472@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D3B0E.EBF19BF3@cusys.edu> <vlb%4.3821$HD6.85849@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
While I would not rewrite a program when making a minor change, I have
rewritten major areas of code when I had to change them anyway.  In a
couple of cases, they were my programs where I spent the time to
eliminate ALTER statements and make the programs structured.  When
rewriting a vendor's date routine for efficiency, I eliminated all GO TO
statements and made it completely structured. I also got 20 - 50 percent
reduction in CPU time.  I also have sometimes rewritten code because it
completely confused me and I wanted to save the person who came after me
the same amount of confusion.  Some rewriting has been done so that the
code would meet new shop standards or to fix hidden bugs.  Those in
charge have been aware that this was being done and approved in
advance.  After all they were paying the bill.  

In short, the amount of required change, the condition and probable life
span of the code, and the amount of testing that would be needed anyway
all influenced the amount of change I made. 

I might add my last shop finally drove the stake through IKFCBL00.  VS
COBOL II remained for CSP and certain vendor programs where we wanted to
keep the old compile options until after the Year 2000 rollover was
complete.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca 

NA wrote:
> 
> In article <393D3B0E.EBF19BF3@cusys.edu>,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>
  snippage from both Howard's and Doc's postings, resuming with what I
believe   to be Doc's statements 
> >
> >I have never seen standards for new code and standards for existing code
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

```
>
> If good structured design can be done without using exits, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> commands are available.

I got ten bucks sez when EXIT PARAGRAPH becomes widely available, we are
gonna see a bleepload of paragraphs with MULTIPLE 'EXIT PARAGRAPH' in them,
in which case they may as well have been GO TO EXIT

Worse, we'll see code like

NEW-FANGLED-PARAGRAPH.
PERFORM
  IF J = 1 THEN
    EXIT PERFORM
  ELSE
   IF J=2
     EXIT PARAGRAPH
   ELSE
     IF J=3
        EXIT SECTION
     ELSE
        IF J=5
           EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
        END-IF
    END-IF
   END-IF
 END-IF
 ADD 1 to J
END-PERFORM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3947D2BB.70D8@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <393ECBBC.C7B50729@worldnet.att.net> <rYK%4.23$Uo3.8065@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
> > >
> > > I got ten bucks sez when EXIT PARAGRAPH becomes widely available, we are
> > > gonna see a bleepload of paragraphs with MULTIPLE 'EXIT PARAGRAPH' in
> them,
> > > in which case they may as well have been GO TO EXIT

'EXIT PARAGRAPH' is neutral in terms of complexity or proneness to
bugs.  GO TO xxxx-EXIT is not neutral.  Actually it is not the GO TO
that is the problem, it is the fact that it needs a label to be the
target for the GO TO.  The EXIT PARAGRAPH does not require such a
label.  

The addition of the xxxx-EXIT label into the program to be the target
for the GO TO introduces several requirements, each of which can be
error prone.  

For example: 

* The 'paragraph' must now be PERFORMed THRU, or must be a SECTION
(which requires that it be terminated by the next 'paragraph' also being
made a SECTION).  

* There must not be any code inserted between the 'paragraph' and its
EXIT label (wheras when there is no GO TO and only paragraphs are
performed there is no restriction on where new paragraphs can be
added).  

* Every GO TO and every THRU must be checked that the correct EXIT
paragraph is being referenced.

None of these problems occur with EXIT PARAGRAPH.

None of these problems occur if the only PERFORMs allowed are of
paragraph labels only, no THRU, no SECTIONs.

It seems that those who use GO TO don't realise that it is not the GO TO
that is the problem but the paragraph label that is the target.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

```
        I do not believe that it is possible to have
both EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT PERFORM
in the same PARAGRAPH.

    It does seem likely that these exits will
encourage people to perform sections.  If you perform
a section, then you can EXIT SECTION.  But EXIT
PARAGRAPH will NOT get you out of a perform thru.

coding), then

with exits.

paragraph
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393E4B8F.D5B2DF30@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8hksa6$nqa$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:
> 
>         I do not believe that it is possible to have
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> encourage people to perform sections.  

Why?  I would assume just the opposite.  The advantage of SECTIONS
(these days) is that it is an implicit PERFORM THRU.  When we don't need
to GO TO EXIT-PARAGRAPH, then the last argument in favor of drop through
paragraphs is gone.  (Don't tell me anybody uses sections for memory
swapping these days).

Thinking.  Do you believe that we will have people coding like this?
MY-SECTION SECTION.
MY-PARAGRAPH.
    READ MY-FILE  AT END EXIT SECTION.  (I forget the syntax)
    IF DEBIT-RECORD EXIT PARAGRAPH.
    ADD MY-AMOUNT TO TOTAL.
NEXT-PARAGRAPH.
    ...


I certainly hope not.   In my shop, we have sections in copy libraries. 
This means that the first line in my procedure is a dummy section name:
GET-RID-OF-SYNTAX-ERROR SECTION.

Our copy libraries also require PERFORM THRU to use them, often in the
somewhat unobvious format of PERFORM SECTION-NAME THRU PARAGRAPH-EXIT. 
(these were supplied by well known vendors).  So I will have to live
with this, but I will never like it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```
    I do not know what the standard says, but an old dos version
(3.1) of MF cobol
did not allow EXIT PERFORM except in a local perform, ie one
without a
paragraph name.

IE:
        PERFORM 10 TIMES
           ADD 1 TO XX
           IF XX > 5 EXIT PERFORM
           ADD 1 TO YY
        END-PERFORM.

    the following is not allowed

    PERFORM XYZ.
        BLAH BLAH BLAH


XYZ.
    display "hello".
    EXIT PERFORM.
    DISPLAY "SAILOR".

        This example would work with exit paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hm71i$u07$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8hksa6$nqa$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <393E4B8F.D5B2DF30@cusys.edu> <8hm4l4$i89$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Yes, EXIT PERFORM is for exiting from within an inline PERFORM.  EXIT
PARAGRAPH/SECTION is for exiting from a PARAGRAPH/SECTION.  (according to the
Standard)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Std; Exit Paragraph and Perform (was: Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hm1qf$22jf$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8hksa6$nqa$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <393E4B8F.D5B2DF30@cusys.edu> <8hm4l4$i89$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <8hm71i$u07$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <393ea296.233306563@207.126.101.100>`

```
[posted and mailed]

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <393ea296.233306563@207.126.101.100>...
>On Wed, 7 Jun 2000 14:15:38 -0500, "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
>>Yes, EXIT PERFORM is for exiting from within an inline PERFORM.  EXIT
>>PARAGRAPH/SECTION is for exiting from a PARAGRAPH/SECTION.  (according to
the
>>Standard)
>>
>
>And if you "Exit Paragraph" within an inline perform?

CD 1.8, page 447, 14.10.13.3, GR 10, control passes "to an
implicit CONTINUE statement immediately following the last
statement in the current paragraph." In other words, you EXIT
the PARAGRAPH.

What happens after that depends upon how control passed to
the paragraph. If control passed to the paragraph as the result
of "PERFORM paragraph-name", then it depends upon how
the implementor interpreted the following:

CD 1.8, page 487, 14.10.27.3, GR 3a, "... return is after the
last statement [of the paragraph]."

Since the rules for both EXIT and PERFORM refer to the same
point, "the last statement in/of the paragraph", it is ambiguous
whether control should return prior to the implicit CONTINUE
statement or after the implicit CONTINUE statement. If it is
interpeted as "prior to" then Exit Paragraph means fall-thru to
the next paragraph. If it is interpreted as "after" then Exit
Paragraph means return control to the end of the PERFORM
statement.

The question now is, does J4 think it ambiguous?
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Std; Exit Paragraph and Perform (was: Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hmj04$dtu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8hksa6$nqa$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <393E4B8F.D5B2DF30@cusys.edu> <8hm4l4$i89$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <8hm71i$u07$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <393ea296.233306563@207.126.101.100> <8hm1qf$22jf$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
I don't think that J4 would view this as "ambiguous" because they would
consider the "implicit CONTINUE" (that you go to via the EXIT
PARAGRAPH/SECTION) to *now* be the last statement in the paragraph, so
control would return to the PERFORM statement.

HOWEVER, if you really think those two rules are ambiguous, I suggest that
you send a note to the J4 chair asking that it be considered at the next J4
meeting (or as soon as possible).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hmcee$pho$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8hksa6$nqa$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <393E4B8F.D5B2DF30@cusys.edu> <8hm4l4$i89$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <8hm71i$u07$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <393ea296.233306563@207.126.101.100>`

```
Each type of EXIT statement does EXACTLY what it says.  If you do an EXIT
PARAGRAPH from within an inline perform, then it does exit that paragraph.
Similarly, if you do an EXIT SECTION from within a performed paragraph, then
it exits the SECTION.
```

```
PARAGRAPH will NOT get you out of a perform thru.




Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
> news:393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu...
> >
> > If good structured design can be done without using exits,
and
> > occasionally exits will cause problems (with cut and paste
coding), then
> > the only argument is whether it is easier to maintain code
with exits.
> > This argument will be solved once and for all once exit
paragraph
> > commands are available.
>
> I got ten bucks sez when EXIT PARAGRAPH becomes widely
available, we are
> gonna see a bleepload of paragraphs with MULTIPLE 'EXIT
PARAGRAPH' in them,
> in which case they may as well have been GO TO EXIT
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hm16h$mhs$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8hkuhs$7o2$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
*unfortunately* There is no rule that says you may not include an EXIT
PARAGRAPH and an EXIT SECTION within the same paragraph (assuming that
paragraph is within a section).

There have been a "number" of discussions at J4 about things that could be
done BETTER with the new EXIT features, but (for various reasons) none of
them have (will) be done.  The classic example of the POTENTIAL problem with
the new structures is:

Mainline Section.
  001-A.
     Perform 002-b
     GoBack
          .
Other-Sect Section.
   002-b.
       Exit Section.
 Other-2 Section.
    003-C.
        Display "unwanted fall-thru will occur".
```

```
Another reason to get rid of SECTIONS when we no longer have the excuse

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

    EXIT PARAGRAPH.
    DISPLAY 'HELLO WORLD'.
000-FINALE.
    DISPLAY 'GOOD-BYE WORLD'.
    GOBACK.
```

```
new constructs CAN be used in totally "ugly" ways.  (of course, so can many
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Multiple EXIT statements was Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39412694.AC7B14EC@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D214E.15BB77DF@worldnet.att.net> <393D2341.619A6177@cusys.edu> <qec%4.1607$ee7.118147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Multiple EXIT statements should pose no problem.  The use of any
construct should reflect the nature of the problem to be solved.  Any
construct can lead to confusion.  Frankly, I wanted these constructs 15
years ago and they are long overdue.  You will have to be careful when
you make the following change:

Before code --

    PERFORM UNTIL CONDITION-MET
       procedural code set 1
    END-PERFORM 

After code

    PERFORM PARAGRAPH-1 UNTIL CONDITION-MET
      .
      .
    .
PARAGRAPH-1.
    procedural code set 1
    .

I would assume that EXIT PERFORM CYCLE in the "before" code would have
the same effect as EXIT PARAGRAPH in the "after" code.  An EXIT PERFORM
in the "before" code would have no logical equivalent construct in the
"after" code.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca  
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
>  -- snip --
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PERFORM
```

- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <KLS_4.3626$HD6.79171@iad-read.news.verio.net> <HQS_4.3629$HD6.79035@iad-read.news.verio.net> <393D30EB.8BC@Azonic.co.nz> <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In article <7j6%4.3759$HD6.85131@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
  docdwarf@clark.net (NA) wrote:

>
> Eh?  PERFORM THRU and GO TO -EXIT (the latter I did not use, notice!)

If one does not use GO TO xxx-EXIT then why use PERFORM ... THRU
xxx-EXIT ?  If there are several paragraphs in the scope of the PERFORM
then the most obvious reason is that each may be PERFORMed separately
in which case performing them together with a THRU may be hazardous -
what if an additional paragraph is innocently inserted in this range ?

If would be better to issue several separate PERFORMs rather than a
collective in the cases where the paragraphs are able to be performed
sepately - less traps for the next programmer.

If there are several paragraph labels in the scope of the PERFORM and
these are entirely unreferenced by GO TO or by other PERFORMs then they
should be entirely removed or commented.

If there is only the paragraph and an EXIT then the EXIT paragraph is
entirely superfluous in the absense of a GO TO.

The _ONLY_ point of using THRU or of PERFORMing SECTIONs is to allow
the use of GO TO.  If there is no GO TO then there is no point to using
THRU or SECTIONs.

> are
> best not used because someone might choose a paragraph-name badly?
>

No, not bacause the name is bad, but because (as you had done) the
wrong name may be used.  If, for example, you had copied some code
(reused) and changed the PERFORM paragraph but not the name of the exit
referenced by the THRU (as may have been the case with your error) then
this can form a bug that the compiler _will_not_ detect (as you had
asserted) and the program may even run, some additional code may be
undetected or innocuous - most of the time.

I do actually think that B1934- is a very poor choice of name, but that
is another argument.  Support for such names seems to be based on how
easy it is to find in the listing (I may have agreed when I used
listings and card boxes 3 decades ago), or in the flow-chart (another
archaic mechanism).  With 'modern' tools (such as have been available
for the last couple of decades) the idea of having to 'order' code in
some way so it can be 'found' is rather wasteful.

"If you don't know what you are doing, do it neatly".



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-13adc7e31d"></a>
## Thread: Inserting into a table — 11 msgs

### Inserting into a table

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu>`

```
I have a sorted table and want to insert a field into it.  There are
several ways to do this, including using reference modification of a
redefines to bump the table up.

But this, while being efficient, is maintenance unfriendly.  What method
do you guys recommend?
```

#### ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NrV_4.1255$Hs5.167625@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu>`

```
Instead of reference modifying, why not use an extra index...

01  FILLER
   05 EXTRA-INDEX     USAGE INDEX

01 THE-TABLE OCCURS whatever INDEXED BY MAIN-INDEX

....

        SET MAIN-INDEX TO END-OF-TABLE-INDEX VALUE
        SET EXTRA INDEX TO MAIN-INDEX
         SET EXTRA-INDEX DOWN BY +1       << look backwards one
        PERFORM
          IF TARGET-VALUE < TABLE-VALUE (MAIN-INDEX) AND  TARGET -VALUE >
TARGET-VALUE (EXTRA-INDEX)
              (shuffle/insert  code, using SET to adjust both indices)
           ELSE
               SET MAIN-INDEX DOWN BY +1
               SET EXTRA-INDEX DOWN BY +1
           END-IF
        END-PERFORM

A bit more source code, but I'd find it more
"maintainance-programmer-friendly"...

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hhg6s0231u@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu> <NrV_4.1255$Hs5.167625@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I like this code, looks good.

I would probably just use some extra REGION and use a temp table or a 'work'
table.  The data is already sorted right?  It's no use "re-sorting it".
Clear the temp table and load it, matching keys to the 'insert' record.
When the keys pass or match, load the insert record to the temp table and
keep incrementing and writing entries from the first table.  Continue till
out of entries.  Duplicates optional.

Jeff

----------

<michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:


> Instead of reference modifying, why not use an extra index...
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qlY_4.778$xc6.72233@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu>`

```
Hi Howard, you are really sharpening your skills!

My suggestion (in PSUEDO-CODE)

01  item-to-insert

01  OLD-TABLE
    05  owhatever-1
    05  owhatever-2
    ...

01  NEW-TABLE
    05  nwhatever-1
    05 n whatever-2
    ...

move 1 to I,J
Perform varying I from 1 by 1 until > old-table-limit
    if item-to-insert < owhatever-1 (I)
        move item-to-insert to nwhatever-1 (J)
        add 1 to J
        move owhatever-1 (I) to nwhatever (J )
...
...
end-Perform

OR, another way -
SELECT SORT-TABLE assign to ?
..
SD SORT-TABLE

SORT SORT-TABLE ON ASCENDING KEY
    SD-WHATEVER-1
INPUT PROCEDURE GET-RECS
OUTPUT PROCEDURE RELOAD-TABLE.

GET-RECS SECTION.
MOVE item-to-insert to SORT-REC
RELEASE SORT-REC
PERFORM VARYINGN I FROM 1 B Y 1
    UNTIL I > old-tablesize
    MOVE owhatever-1 (I) to SORT-REC
   RELEASE SORT-REC
END-PERFORM

RELOAD-TABLE SECTION.
    MOVE  1 TO I
    PERFORM  UNTIL NO-MORE-RETURNS
        RETURN SORT-REC INTO owhatever-1 (I)
          AT END
                SET NO-MORE-RETURNS TO TRUE
        NOT AT END
                ADD 1 TO I
        END-RETURN
    END-PERFORM

news:393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "brucepbarrett" <brucepbarrett@email.msn.com>

```
If  your compiler supports table sort insert at end of table and resort the
table.  I have used the same method that you describe.  In some cases I have
written the table to a sequential file along with  the new entry, sorted and
rebuilt the table.  In cases where the table data is in an indexed file I
have added the entry and then rebuilt the file.  Guess it depends on the
situation taking into consideration effeciency and simplicity.
```

#### ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00

```
Howard,
  As I recall, you are on an IBM mainframe, so the following will NOT work -
but in the next Standard it will (and on some implementations it already
does).

I would add how ever many "new" records that I have at the bottom of the
table and use the SORT verb to sort the table.  (Probably not the most
efficient but certainly "self-documenting)
```

#### ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393C89A3.CB1F4CD6@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard,

The question is: Why do you need to keep the table sorted? Is it to be able
to look it up using SEARCH ALL? If this is the case, the entire approach is
wrong. SEARCH ALL (i.e. binary search) searches in O(log(N)) time, and
inserting an entry while keeping the table sorted obviously requires O(N)
time. This is both inefficient and complicated. The most natural thing to do
is use an algorithm that both  searches and inserts in O(1), i.e. constant
time, and the only such algorithm is hashing. If your table is not too
large, you can allocate a table several times the number of perspective
entries thus keeping it very sparse and even the simplest hashing scheme -
linear probing - performing times faster than the binary search, to say
nothing of the insertion. Since you undoubtedly know how to code hashing
with linear probing (or double hashing, for that matter, if you are
concerned about memory usage), I am leaving it out. Just wanted to remind
you of this possibility.

Kind regards,
====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
====================

> I have a sorted table and want to insert a field into it.  There are
> several ways to do this, including using reference modification of a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Inserting into a table

- **From:** "Rob Kemmer" <rob@tobin-kemmer.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hpb23$5pj$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<393BB313.77353D33@cusys.edu>`

```
Assuming a table of size "table-size" and that the table is not already full
try:

perform varying sub from table-size  by -1 until table-value (sub) <
new-value
        move table-value (sub -1) to table-value (sub)
end-perform
move new-value to table-value (sub + 1)

(I think!)

Rob Kemmer
```

```
I haven't heard anything from Merant yet, but from all these messages, it
appears that there will be some new runtine fees.  Does anyone have the
specifics or should I go to Merant?

For example, at one client, the data files are on a server and they have 3-5
people with the application on their PCs.  They would have to buy 3 to 5
runtime licenses?  At what cost?

For years I used the basic MicroFocus compiler to take care of old clients.
I created new applications in VB.  Then for use with Windows 95/98 and 32bit
ODBC drivers, I finally took the plunge and spent the almost $4000 on
NetExpress last summer.  Now, just 1 year later the rules have changed?
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Runtime Threads?

- **From:** "Timothy O. Nicholson" <kf4rtx@nospam.nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<ybU_4.12651$Gh.167123@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`


## Thread: Combining data vs String — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Combining data vs String

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000605160551.29446A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
Here is another idea....	

try using 	

"INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE(VARIABLE-1) 
TALLYING COUNTER FOR LEADING SPACES.
MOVE VARIABLE-1 (1:(VAR-LENGTH - CTR)) TO NEW-VARIABLE."	

this is more difficult but it ignores inbedded spaces using a second
counter can keep keep track of your current location.  Face it most data
does have spaces in it eventually. Especially names and locations.

suggest you look at the right justify section of the FAQ.	  

Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program
```

#### ↳ Re: Combining data vs String

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <taxberjef@naspam.naxs.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13a794742f7501c49896a2@news.naxs.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000605160551.29446A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
Charles Hammond wrote:
I am sure this is not what most folks have in mind but.. shit it is just about 
midnight and I thought what the hell!!:-)
> Here is another idea....	
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> MOVE VARIABLE-1 (1:(VAR-LENGTH - CTR)) TO NEW-VARIABLE."	

Interesting...

> 
> this is more difficult but it ignores inbedded spaces using a second
> counter can keep keep track of your current location.  Face it most data
> does have spaces in it eventually. Especially names and locations.

Okay... lets look at an example..

pic x(20) value "first  last".

So, I have several emended spaces in between first and last. If I just start my 
counter at 20 and keep reducing it by one until I locate a non space item(IE "t") I 
stop counting down. In my example I would move data from position 1 thru 11 to any 
field. 

All I would use is a little loop and determine the end position of the string and I 
can move it to anywhere I like. I know it is not very slick but it does work. And I 
guess that is what matters? HUH???:-)

I know that my logic is a bit flawed and the way I present is is not as good as most 
but guess what?? The code I would write works very well!!:-)

> suggest you look at the right justify section of the FAQ.	  
Let's not and just say we did. Okay????
> 
> Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program
> 
> 
```

```
David.
```
