```
    Check the LOCK MODE line in the select statement.

    Statements below refer to record sequential, relative, and
indexed files.
Line sequential files are different.  Files opened output are
different.

    Do not use lock mode is manual or automatic unless
you must.  If you leave the LOCK MODE out completely, you will
get the deny write mode when open input.

    If the runtime thinks that someone else might write to the
file,
performance pluments.  Always make work and tempory files
exclusive.

    Note when testing that some operating systems will take note
of  a files actual status.  Ie, have another user open the file
input
when testing.



<SNIP>
```

#### ↳ Re: Joining the group

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hm0oo$s44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
comp.lang.cobol is a USENET newsgroup - it is not a list-server.  Therefore,
there is no way to "subscribe" to it, you just need to view it via your
newsgroup reader.  If you do not have (easy) access to newsgroups - but do
have access to the web, then you may want to try www.deja.com - or any
similar newsgroup access web site.
```

## Thread: Recommendations for COBOL compilers for OS/2 & CICS — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Recommendations for COBOL compilers for OS/2 & CICS

- **From:** info-central@mindspring.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hp7l5$ndt$2@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hello out there...

I am looking for some recommendations on compilers
people have had good experience with for recompiling
16-bit Cobol programs into a 32-bit OS/2 - Warp4
compatible environment to run in CICS for OS/2 version
3.0 (or IBM Transaction Server version 4).

I am aware of IBM's VisualAge for COBOL and
MicroFocus's Mainframe Express.

Are there any others out there that do the job well?

Thanks for a posted or personal reply.

John Gaskill
gm@info-central-usa.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Recommendations for COBOL compilers for OS/2 & CICS

- **From:** Jason Edmeades <edmeades@uk.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3940E812.D1A01B9E@uk.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<8hp7l5$ndt$2@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The only ones we provide translators for are IBM's VA COBOL and
MicroFocus. I have never heard of any customer running another 32 bit
COBOL. 

Regards,
Jason
[CICS for OS/2 Change Team]

>

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> gm@info-central-usa.com
```

```
There are many other 32 bit COBOL compilers, but the only ones that
support OS/2 are VA and MF.  (Unless AcuCorp has an OS/2 runtime).

If you want 32 bit Windows, you can add Realia, Fujitsu, Liant,
ICOBOL, MBP etc etc to the mix.
```

<a id="t-d31b685b3f"></a>
## Thread: Need to Create a Catalogued Version of the Sorted File — 6 msgs

### Need to Create a Catalogued Version of the Sorted File

- **From:** "Mike Allen" <allenmk@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%6805.171$075.64374@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Hi all,

I have a problem for which I have been unable to find the solution.  I
have a program which takes in three files.  Each file has a separate
format.  An internal sort reformats them in the input procedure into
the sort record format.  In the output procedure, a called subroutine
is getting a S0C7 in an external data field being used for control
purposes.  I need to find a way to get a copy of the interim sort file
so that I can look at the data in the records.

I don't want to change the program to WRITE and READ the records from
the sort as the program has been in production for a long time and
would take to long to convert.

Mike
```

#### ↳ Re: Need to Create a Catalogued Version of the Sorted File

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000609173059.22842.00000577@nso-mf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<%6805.171$075.64374@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <%6805.171$075.64374@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>, "Mike Allen"
<allenmk@gte.net> writes:

>I have a problem for which I have been unable to find the solution.  I
>have a program which takes in three files.  Each file has a separate
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What environment?  I assume from the S0C7 that it might be 
IBM Mainframe.   Have you tried compiling the program with
TEST(NONE,SYM)?  This way you can get a CEEDUMP showing 
the content of all fields/files.  Based on the information in the dump
you should be able to determine the record/field  content.

This is also assuming that you are using an LE level compiler.
COBOL for MVS/OS390.....
```

```
DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Need to Create a Catalogued Version of the Sorted File

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hsj6i0mar@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<%6805.171$075.64374@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
   OPEN OUTPUT OUTPUT-FILE.

*     OUTPUT PROCEDURE F000-WRITE-OUTPUT   THRU F000-EXIT.
      OUTPUT PROCEDURE X000-MY-TEST        THRU X000-EXIT.

   CLOSE OUTPUT-FILE.
   GOBACK.

X000-MY-TEST.
    RETURN SORTFILE AT END GO TO X000-EXIT.
    MOVE SORTREC TO OUTPUT-REC
    WRITE OUTPUT-REC.
    GO TO X000-MY-TEST.

X000-EXIT.  EXIT.

----------
In article <%6805.171$075.64374@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>, "Mike Allen"
<allenmk@gte.net> wrote:


> Hi all,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Need to Create a Catalogued Version of the Sorted File

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394BCF6A.8E99B274@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<%6805.171$075.64374@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Assuming that you are getting the problem on an MVS platform
(S390/OS390), and you are a current COBOL, the CEEDUMP should have the
record in question as well as all of the pertinent working storage.  The
file areas and record related to the SD are available with the CEEDUMP
as of OS390, release 2.5 and may be available via PTF on even earlier
releases of OS390.  If worst comes to worst, the simplest way to get the
file is to create a test version of the program and add a WRITE
statement for the sorted record to a special file after every RETURN
statement in the program.  You don't want the intermediate sort work
file and depending on the amount of virtual storage available, it may
not even exist (or if it does exist it may not contain any data).  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca 

Mike Allen wrote:
> 
> Hi all,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mike
```

<a id="t-528aa704eb"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

- **From:** "Ron Langer" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vNc05.1093$907.26820@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

At Fujitsu Software, we are extremely committed to the COBOL market and
always try to put the needs of COBOL programmers first. We believe that
it's the key to earning your respect and building a long-term mutually
beneficial business partnership. We believe that COBOL programmers deserve
a reasonably priced COBOL compiler with high performance, unmatched
reliability, superior scalability, and royalty free COBOL run-times.

We want to make sure that the COBOL development community understands that
all existing Fujitsu COBOL run-time features that have been free in the
past ARE STILL FREE! In Version 5, we have created a new, optional
multi-threaded run-time system targeted for enterprise server-based
applications. We have tried to keep the prices low and to make the
licensing simple. There will be a one-time charge for this OPTIONAL
run-time system. COBOL programmers will continue to have access to the
royalty-free Fujitsu run-time system and may develop and deploy COBOL
applications with no license fees required (i.e. no strings attached�).
These applications may include Internet (web) based components that are
single threaded and may run on servers with no license fee required.

Fujitsu Software�s business model regarding English-language COBOL products
(sold outside of Japan, Korea and China) is to provide a robust, high
performance, single-threaded COBOL development environment with no required
run-time licensing fees. This allows developers to create and deploy COBOL
applications without paying additional run-time fees or deal with tracking
run-time licenses. Since we coexist with other established development
products such as Visual Basic�, C++, and Java, we are trying to keep the
price of our COBOL compiler and associated development tools competitive.

We believe that COBOL has a bright future. Our intention is to continue to
invest in creating quality tools and COBOL compiler technology. We plan to
keep our prices consistent with this optimistic outlook and help keep COBOL
at the forefront of business programming in the 21st century.

Sincerely,

Ron Langer
Director, COBOL Sales and Marketing
Fujitsu Software
Web: www.adtools.com
Email: cobol@adtools.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hrubq$o80$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<vNc05.1093$907.26820@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Ron Langer wrote in message ...

<and Donald snipped it>

Thanks for the clarification, Ron.  As a long time user of Cobol, I would
like to comment on Fujitsu's practice of making old versions available for
download.

I think it vital, if Cobol is to maintain a position of prominence in the
software game, that a good basic Cobol be available for no charge.  It
serves two purposes.  Firstly, it makes the language available for students
and experimenters, and secondly, it encourages a body of freeware/shareware
... *written* in Cobol, which is the best advertising that a computer
language can get.

I do not know if Fujitsu plans on continuing this practice, or if it was a
one time marketing method.  However, I would like to thank Fujitsu for doing
it, as a long time user and proponent of Cobol. I hope that you will forward
this on to your own superiors, and continue with your existing policy.
Those actions have been of benefit to the entire Cobol community.

Donald Tees
Willmack Automated Systems Inc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hs55d$3e70$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<vNc05.1093$907.26820@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8hrubq$o80$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I second Donald's comments.  Even though we license the Enterprise Version
5, we presently have two students using the free version that comes with
Thane's book "Learning Cobol in 24 Hours", and I feel that it is extremely
important that this practice continues.  Almost all of our mainframe
software is still written in Cobol and having the ability to develop PC
based applications to interface with the mainframe software, without
excessive run-time fees is a huge plus.  Keep up the great work!

Denny Brouse
FirstMerit Bank N.A. of Ohio
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

- **From:** "Ron Langer" <rjl@fs.fujitsu.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sNA25.3127$ds.53545@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vNc05.1093$907.26820@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8hrubq$o80$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,

We heartily agree with you that offering a free compiler encourages
students to program in COBOL. That's why we'll continue to offer free,
albeit older, versions of our Fujitsu COBOL compiler to students and other
COBOL beginners. Fujitsu Software is firmly committed to the academic
community and we want to do our part in helping train new COBOL
programmers.

And to demonstrate that this wasn't a one-time only marketing gimmick, we
are getting ready to debut our new Student Center on our web site (look for
it next month at http://www.adtools.com/student/index.htm). We will be
attempting to build a reference list of colleges and universities and how
they are using COBOL to build business-oriented and web-based applications.

I'm glad you feel our efforts have been beneficial to the entire COBOL
community. We really do believe that COBOL has a bright future and we will
continue to do what we can to ensure the long-term success of the world's
most popular programming language.

Ron Langer
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

- **From:** "Ron Langer" <rjl@fs.fujitsu.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<COA25.3129$ds.83836@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<vNc05.1093$907.26820@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <_Ih05.17638$c5.29408@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Bob,

Just as we always try to put the needs of COBOL programmers first, we at
Fujitsu Software also firmly believe in supporting third-party COBOL tool
vendors. We feel this support is shown in a number of ways.

First of all, we offer three pricing packages (Standard, Professional, and
Enterprise) so that COBOL customers can buy just a compiler if they so
choose and then add additional tools as required, either ours or those
offered by companies such as Flexus. Our goal is to help customers end up
with the best suite of tools for their particular development requirements.

Additionally, we prominently feature third-party COBOL tool vendors on our
web site - the entire Partner Center is dedicated to our Strategic Business
Partners, Technology Partners, and Complimentary Technology Providers
(http://www.adtools.com/partner/index.htm)

And finally, we are interested in taking these relationships one step
further by implementing some fresh ideas. One idea is to enrich the Partner
Center by inviting third-party vendors to display their company logos on
our web site along with a brief description of the products and/or services
offered. We feel this would provide more meaningful direction to our
customers who are searching for best-of-breed COBOL development tools.
Another idea is to investigate co-marketing opportunities so that we can
again present our customers with the best options in state-of-the-art COBOL
development. If you are interested in either opportunity, please contact us
at cobol@adtools.com.

We are committed to building a long-term mutually beneficial business
partnership with both COBOL developers and third-party COBOL tool vendors.
We are always open to new ideas and welcome your input at any time.

Sincerely,
Ron Langer

news:_Ih05.17638$c5.29408@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
> Thank you for your response. Your company's commitment statement will
enable
> our company (and I am sure many others) to proceed with long term
business
> plans based on COBOL.
>
> Regards, Robert Kovacic.
```

## Thread: Skills advancing for legacy programmers — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Skills advancing for legacy programmers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.rexx,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39415FF4.E51B3F94@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<393D73E6.4EB75618@cusys.edu> <393d95d1.85938442@news.transport.com> <393E62A4.F147D35B@cusys.edu> <393e9306.62235820@news.transport.com>`

```

I just got back my "Practical Usage of TSO REXX", and will study it as I
get time.  I tried to get my work to buy it and keep it in their
library.  The librarian asked the top techies if it was useful and they
nixed it.  She is always going to our programmer's meetings telling
about new JAVA books, but the "leading edge" programmers who advise her
are doing new technology - those who do old technology such as IDMS and
COBOL don't count.  There is no need for us to improve our skills - if
we're willing to work COBOL, obviously we're not interested in skills
anyway.  Except when we go to a new job with the promises of learning
new technology and when they need us too badly to keep their promises,
we go on to new jobs with the same promises.  So I buy an $81 book for
my own use and get knocked down come evaluation time for not being
offered new learning opportunities at work.
```

#### ↳ Re: Skills advancing for legacy programmers

- **From:** Ian <glenspamirspam@spamerolsspam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.rexx,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3941A42B.62239820@spamerolsspam.com>`
- **References:** `<393D73E6.4EB75618@cusys.edu> <393d95d1.85938442@news.transport.com> <393E62A4.F147D35B@cusys.edu> <393e9306.62235820@news.transport.com> <39415FF4.E51B3F94@cusys.edu>`

```
Your 'leading edge techies', as, it appears, most programmers, know almost
nothing about Rexx, even though it is right under their noses. Anything that
makes your work easier or faster, or extends your skilld, is worth getting.
If your present environment doesn't support you, and can't be persuaded to
do so, its time to start figuring out your exit strategy.

Rexx will always be a great tool to have in your toolbox, but you might
consider leveraging this knowledge to try NetRexx, as an easy entree into
Java. Many rexx once-offs will translate easily, so your main start-up
challenge will be setting up the java jdk environment (your systems people
might even be willing to set up a java environment on your mainframe). Your
'leading edge' people might look again at your skills when you can show
rapid generation of java classes that work! (And you can see the java code
generated by the NetRexx compiler, so if you keep the programs simple, the
java should make sense too.)

my 2 cents...

Ian

Howard Brazee wrote:

> I just got back my "Practical Usage of TSO REXX", and will study it as I
> get time.  I tried to get my work to buy it and keep it in their
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> offered new learning opportunities at work.
```

```
I love Rexx. Many of my favourite utilities are written in Rexx. I even paid
for a developer's copy of IBM Object Rexx. Heck, the macro language for my
text editor is a flavour of Rexx. However, I believe that Perl will
ultimately be more popular for scripting given the volume of free stuff
available in the that language, and the ease with which modules can be
retrieved from CPAN, a public repository of neat stuff, and incorporated
into an application.

- Karl
```

```
--begin quoted text:

So I buy an $81 book for
my own use and get knocked down come evaluation time for not being
```

```
"Michael W. Cocke" wrote:
> 
> I may be missing something, but it seems as if you're saying that you
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> sentence clear and coherent  8-)>  )

Sort of.  But it is as though my employers wanted me skilled in putting
nails into boards.  I suggest that I learn to use a nail gun, so they
check with the techies to find out what the current skills are and they
find out that the "in" skills are burning cd-roms and multi-media.  So I
can continue to use a hammer, or spend my own money to learn to use a
nail gun.   After I learn to use the nail gun on my own, I get good
grades for putting in lots of nails, but I get marked down for being too
busy getting their job done to attend their CD-ROM burning class.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skills advancing for legacy programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael W. Cocke <cocke@catherders.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.rexx,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<255905596098599527072558@cocke>`
- **References:** `<393D73E6.4EB75618@cusys.edu> <393E62A4.F147D35B@cusys.edu> <393e9306.62235820@news.transport.com> <39415FF4.E51B3F94@cusys.edu> <PLq05.4806$HD6.120592@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3944E9A2.D7E70214@cusys.edu> <165053996089134513417966@mike.catherders.com> <394632AB.80C2073B@cusys.edu>`

```
On Tue, 13 Jun 2000 07:10:03 -0600, Howard Brazee wrote:

>"Michael W. Cocke" wrote:
>> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>busy getting their job done to attend their CD-ROM burning class.

It's been a few years since I had to undergo the review process, but 
this sounds like something that used to happen to me on a regular basis.
Part of the problem is that the reviewer doesn't know what it is that 
you do, how you do it, or what's involved in the process. Another part 
of the problem has been mentioned in another message in this thread- 
they have to find something to ding you on in order to justify a smaller
raise.

My solution (not optimal for quality of life, but great for job 
security) is to maintain/enhance my qualifications independently of work
(IE night classes and seminars), and bring a copy of your current resume
to your evaluation.  Explain that you're using all of your work time for
production and doing education on your own time.  Show the reviewer your
resume (which should also include your continuing education info).

This serves a number or purposes:  First, the fact that you have 
continued your education is displayed, as is your motivation in doing it
on your own time.  The subtle thing this shows is that you have an 
up-to-date resume...  With the job market as tight as it is for IT 
people, that will make most executives pause for thought.

Over the course of a few years I went from second shift mainframe 
operator to D.I.T. using this approach.  It IS a pain, make no mistake, 
but it depends on your priorities.  It also helps if you're an insomniac
with no life.  After you're a few rungs further up the ladder you can 
see about developing a life.

Another alternative is to look around for another job with a smaller 
company.  Yes, I can hear the arguments to that statement already, but 
hear me out.  A smaller company will have a smaller budget, but you'll 
be getting a larger part of it, and with fewer middle managers, you'll 
be in a better position for raises and promotion opportunities.  My last
review was conducted by the owner of the company and ended in my being 
made D.I.T.  Another advantage of a smaller company is that you'll wind 
up getting to do all sorts of interesting projects that will, in turn, 
enhance the resume...  When I started here, I was (mostly) qualified as 
a programmer, PC hardware tech, and network designer / installer / 
administrator. Now I can also design / build / program automated process
control systems. Now, here's the interesting thing from the long-term 
point of view - my only formal training was in programming.  The other 
stuff came from occasional night classes and informal 'opportunities' 
that came up during one thing or another.  Remember, a lateral career 
move is one way of avoiding the 'legacy' label.


Mike-
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skills advancing for legacy programmers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Darrin Cardani <dcardani@buena.spamfree.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.rexx,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<130620000914252980%dcardani@buena.spamfree.com>`
- **References:** `<393D73E6.4EB75618@cusys.edu> <393E62A4.F147D35B@cusys.edu> <393e9306.62235820@news.transport.com> <39415FF4.E51B3F94@cusys.edu> <PLq05.4806$HD6.120592@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3944E9A2.D7E70214@cusys.edu> <165053996089134513417966@mike.catherders.com>`

```
In article <165053996089134513417966@mike.catherders.com>, Michael W.
Cocke <cocke@catherders.com> wrote:

> I may be missing something, but it seems as if you're saying that you 
> want them to evaluate you based on skills that you have, but those 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> sentence clear and coherent  8-)>  )

There's another aspect to this that you're not considering. The
employer probably isn't qualified to decide what tools the programmer
should be learning. I know that was the case at my last job. I knew far
better which things I should be learning in order to get my job done
than my employer. Yet, come review time, I got the same flack that
Howard did. Namely, "You haven't learned anything new this year, so
your raise is going to be lower." Of course, I had learned an enormous
amount, but it was all "under the hood" stuff that wouldn't be obvious
to someone who doesn't code.

Darrin
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skills advancing for legacy programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ian <glenspamirspam@spamerolsspam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.rexx,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394701BE.80B42511@spamerolsspam.com>`
- **References:** `<393D73E6.4EB75618@cusys.edu> <393E62A4.F147D35B@cusys.edu> <393e9306.62235820@news.transport.com> <39415FF4.E51B3F94@cusys.edu> <PLq05.4806$HD6.120592@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3944E9A2.D7E70214@cusys.edu> <165053996089134513417966@mike.catherders.com> <130620000914252980%dcardani@buena.spamfree.com>`

```
Its all in the advertising - unless you market your achievements to the ones who
make the evaluations, you might as well be spending your time surfing the web. It
shouldn't be that way, but it is. The other thing is that bosses have to make the
total money work out right, so even if you did a perfect job last year, they'd
still have to cook up some nonsense reason to short you on your increase!

Ian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Skills advancing for legacy programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robin Thomas <rob.thomas@sabre.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.rexx,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3947F7EB.2016CA5A@sabre.com>`
- **References:** `<393D73E6.4EB75618@cusys.edu> <393E62A4.F147D35B@cusys.edu> <393e9306.62235820@news.transport.com> <39415FF4.E51B3F94@cusys.edu> <PLq05.4806$HD6.120592@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3944E9A2.D7E70214@cusys.edu> <165053996089134513417966@mike.catherders.com> <130620000914252980%dcardani@buena.spamfree.com>`

```
Not to answer just to Darrin...

I had the exact opposite experience yet the results were the same.
During my Employee Development Assesment, I informed my manager that
I was interested in learning JAVA because it was so obvious that the world was
going this way.
She shrugged and said "I don't need this in my department !"

(Who is "emp devel" for ??? her or me ??)

I called the person in charge of Intranet Development (here at Sabre)
and inquired about her openings she had on Monster !!

Came the reply: "I'm sorry...We need experienced ! (click)"

I am soooo glad of the opportunities afforded to me as an employee !

The moral of the story...
When a company lies to you, it's not called lying...HR calls it "marketing"
Herein after I will seriously "market" myself.
Loyalty...hah....they will use you up and lay you off when they do not
need you any more.

This very thing happend to me at Nortel in Richardson, TX.
I informed my mgr that I knew that I was doing dated technology by
supporting VM and the Xedit and (in-house) email tools.  She promised
me that she "would take care of me."  When time came, she abandoned me by
moving me to another manager that did not need to honor previous promisses.
Eventually they jerked me around (trying to get me to quit) and eventually
laid me off.

(Sequel to this story is that I got them back at Y2K time.  They really paid
to get me over to Raleigh !)

Tomorrow, I have an interview out of town with a company that does
OS/390 to internet connectivity...right up my alley...They have what I want and
I have what they want.  Fair trade, nothing more, nothing less.

Loyalty will be based upon how my mgr and the company treats me.
Subject to change without notice, on both sides.
```

<a id="t-9d221066d5"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Runtime Fees - The Final Answer — 5 msgs

### NetExpress Runtime Fees - The Final Answer

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000616200016.12030.00002104@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I believe the following is the final response regarding runtime fees.  The
runtime fees will be waived if the following conditions are met:
 
     1.  Have five or less Net Express development licences.
     2.  Sell an application that costs less than $1000 per authorized user.
     3.  Have total company revenues of less than $1,000,000 for their prior
          fiscal year.
     4.  Have the principal of their company apply to MERANT on company
          notepaper confirming that they meet all three of the above conditions
     5.  Have a current maintenance contract for all Net Express development
          licences.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Runtime Fees - The Final Answer

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iefv5$sf2$1@news.chatlink.com>`
- **References:** `<20000616200016.12030.00002104@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
So in other words, Merant is still dictating what we can and cannot do
with OUR products that WE develop!

I think I will stay with release 3.0 thank you very much, but kudos to
Merant for finally giving up an answer - and it only took how long?

TL
```

```
Their solution seems pretty generous and more than fair when compared to
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Runtime Fees - The Final Answer

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394B6438.9FA3BF39@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20000616200016.12030.00002104@ng-fs1.aol.com> <8iefv5$sf2$1@news.chatlink.com> <8ierbq$fib$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Perhaps the good answer on run-time fees is to ship the run-time as a
separate chargable item so that the fee is charged once per site no
matter how many Merant based applications are shipped to the customer or
how many vendors the customer purchases Merant based applications from. 
If I'm buying many COBOL applications, all I need is one copy of the
run-time per system that is running one or more of the applications.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca
```

<a id="t-0bde2c4e46"></a>
## Thread: Runtime fees for software developers? — 25 msgs

### Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net>`

```


John Grznar wrote:
> 
>   I was just wondering, do other industries have the equivalent of run-time
> fees?

Yes.  The music industry does this.  The movie industry does this.  I
guess it has to do with how the product gets used.

If you write a play, someone can buy one copy and perform it a million
times.  Or zero times.  Should your royalty be the same in either case?
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i2si0$a1t$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net> <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu>...
>
>
>John Grznar wrote:
>>
>>   I was just wondering, do other industries have the equivalent of
run-time
>> fees?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>times.  Or zero times.  Should your royalty be the same in either case?

No, they do not.  The author/publisher of the music gets a per time royalty,
but the inventor of the language does not.  Nor do any of the musicians that
invented the scale, developed harmony, wrote the word processor that was
used for the script, or manufactured the paper that it is written on.

A compiler is specified as being a language processor.  If someone chooses
to implement that language by means of a called library rather than a
complete translation to binary, that is simple technique.

If I write a book, then I am entitled to royalties on it.  If someone
translates that book into another language, then I can negotiate any kind of
deal I want to with the translator. When, after I finish the book, someone
claims to be the "owner" of the language, and demands royalties thirty times
the price of the book, I get a tad pissed off.  Particularly when I spent a
good deal of my time as a youth contributing to that language.  Are MF
paying royalties to Warren?  Or Don?  Or Bill?

If MF feel they have a moral right to royalties on my Cobol code, then they
should feel an obligation to pay royalties to every programmer that ever sat
on an ANSII committee or submitted a bug fix.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3945553C.7D0BDFD9@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net> <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu> <8i2si0$a1t$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:

>

<snip>

> If MF feel they have a moral right to royalties on my Cobol code, then they
> should feel an obligation to pay royalties to every programmer that ever sat
> on an ANSII committee or submitted a bug fix.

   It is not a question of morality.  It is a question of paying a fair price.
It makes no difference whether you pay the cost for a compiler all at once up
front; finance it through a bank, a credit card company, or your vendor thereby
paying a fixed stream of payments; or via run-time fees resulting in a variable
stream of payments.  Anyway you do it, it still adds up to the cost you are
paying for the compiler.
   A thousand run-time fees of $87 each adds up to $87,000.  If a vendor list
the price of their compiler at $87,000 would you buy it?  Even if you could
afford to do so, would you buy it?
   I eat about one candy bar a month.  I could easily afford to pay $100 for a
candy bar.  But, I'll damned if I will.

John Grznar
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394556DF.6381D303@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net> <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu> <8i2si0$a1t$1@news.igs.net> <3945553C.7D0BDFD9@earthlink.net>`

```
John Grznar wrote:
> 
> > If MF feel they have a moral right to royalties on my Cobol code, then they
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> John Grznar

It is all fair - as long as you have a choice.  If you don't like the
pricing on that candy bar, don't buy the candy bar.  That's fair.  If
you like the pricing, it must be fair to you.   If they don't like the
offer you are willing to pay, they don't have to sell to you.  That's
also fair.

If a competitor makes more money by selling more candy bars at a lower
price - that's fair too.

The point is NOBODY is forcing you to accept any price.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i3pcc$ogo$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net> <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu> <8i2si0$a1t$1@news.igs.net> <3945553C.7D0BDFD9@earthlink.net> <394556DF.6381D303@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <394556DF.6381D303@cusys.edu>...
>John Grznar wrote:
>>
>> > If MF feel they have a moral right to royalties on my Cobol code, then
they
>> > should feel an obligation to pay royalties to every programmer that
ever sat
>> > on an ANSII committee or submitted a bug fix.
>>
>>    It is not a question of morality.  It is a question of paying a fair
price.
>> It makes no difference whether you pay the cost for a compiler all at
once up
>> front; finance it through a bank, a credit card company, or your vendor
thereby
>> paying a fixed stream of payments; or via run-time fees resulting in a
variable
>> stream of payments.  Anyway you do it, it still adds up to the cost you
are
>> paying for the compiler.
>>    A thousand run-time fees of $87 each adds up to $87,000.  If a vendor
list
>> the price of their compiler at $87,000 would you buy it?  Even if you
could
>> afford to do so, would you buy it?
>>    I eat about one candy bar a month.  I could easily afford to pay $100
for a
>> candy bar.  But, I'll damned if I will.
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>The point is NOBODY is forcing you to accept any price.

What if I bought their compiler for a reasonable price, then after I
invested $400,000 in Cobol code (the only reason that I bought it in the
first place), they tell me that I cannot use the code I wrote or recoup my
investment unless I pay them another $87,000?

Let's say that you, as an employee, work for five years on the basis of
getting paid $400,000 at the end.  The day you finish, and put your hand out
to collect your cheque, they tell you that you owe them another twenty years
of work? If they tell you that you can take or leave it, does that make it
better?

Keep in mind, Howard, that the fees are being imposed *after* a purchaser
has bought the compiler, and tied themselves into it. If they only imposed
the fee on new users, it would be quite a different thing.  That is not the
case.

It takes about three years to get a software product to the point that it
makes money.  If all the financial rules change every two years, then the
software business becomes a monopoly, and the only people allowed to write
software are the ones that work for the compiler writers.  That is precisely
what MS tried to do, by different methods, and precisely why they will be
bankrupt in three to four years.  I said that a year ago in this echo, and
was poo pooed.  Now, it is starting to become more obvious.

Get enough people pissed off, and the biggest company in the world can go
downhill like a toboggan.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i46io$32q$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net> <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu> <8i2si0$a1t$1@news.igs.net> <3945553C.7D0BDFD9@earthlink.net> <394556DF.6381D303@cusys.edu> <8i3pcc$ogo$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,  Correct me if I am mistaken, but MERANT is *not* requiring you to
upgrade your compiler (or run-time) to the version that has run-time fees.
Therefore, you can (may) still use the same one that you bought - under the
same constraints (or lack thereof).  What you will be MISSING is "upgraded"
functionality, bug-fixes, etc - and as far as "bug fixes goes" - I think that
MERANT may even continue to provide those for NE 3.0 - but am not positive of
that.

Therefore, you are truly STILL getting everything that you paid for - just
not what you were expecting.
```

```
should I do? Should I continue to work with a defective product

Yesterday, while working with NE 3.0, I received an error, the
software terminated and I lost the efforts I had been working on
for an hour.

Regards,
Rich
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Runtime fees for software developers?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3944FB19.25D78359@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<1xU_4.1175$Hs5.150773@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3942D3D8.7E9DC2A1@earthlink.net> <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu> <8i2si0$a1t$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3944E7CE.DF7D371F@cusys.edu>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> used for the script, or manufactured the paper that it is written on.

The tool which the players use is their script.  The tools which the
programmers use is their compiler.  These are comparable.
 
> A compiler is specified as being a language processor.  If someone chooses
> to implement that language by means of a called library rather than a
> complete translation to binary, that is simple technique.

If someone chooses to do this, then he can avoid royalties given to
someone who is selling the use of a different tool.   In the case of a
play, the players can extemporize instead of following the script.  Your
choice.
 
> If I write a book, then I am entitled to royalties on it.  If someone
> translates that book into another language, then I can negotiate any kind of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> paying royalties to Warren?  Or Don?  Or Bill?

MF is paying them what they required as a condition of their work.  They
chose to work without demanding royalties just as you and I have.
 
> If MF feel they have a moral right to royalties on my Cobol code, then they
> should feel an obligation to pay royalties to every programmer that ever sat
> on an ANSII committee or submitted a bug fix.

They only have a right if you agree to their terms and use their
product.  If you choose to use a competitor's product, they don't get
any money.  If you choose to write your own tool, they don't get any
money.  Their moral right only occurs if you agree to their terms.

IT IS YOUR CHOICE!!!!
```

## Thread: Netexpress 3.0 and WWW — 5 msgs

### Netexpress 3.0 and WWW

- **From:** "Carlos Ruivo Ferreira" <carlos@mb2s.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i3kk3$g24$1@srvlis16.teleweb.pt>`

```
I have a simple program just to validate a number and a password. As I'am
not very good in english, this is the message from my ISP (i'am in Portugal
and my site is in USA) surprised ? you must see the prices for 100mb of web
space in my country...,
Thanks in advance for your time and  if you have any anwser for that problem
pls let me know
CArlos Ferreira
carlos@mb2s.com

Message from ISP:
Hello,
Your .exe script (cgi-bin/pcontrol_server.exe) is causing our perl.exe and
some other server components to crash. Please check out what is going on, if
this happened again I will need to suspend your CGI access.
Thank you for your cooperation.
-Uri Foox
CFM Web Hosting http://www.cfmwebhost.com

>REsponse
I don't understand what can be happening. Our .exe is a big one because it
has all dll�s and run-time stuff linked to it. The only thing that the
program does is to accept three fields from an input form (number, number
and password). Then it reads a sequential file to validate the information
and if everything is OK the file is closed and nothing more happens. If the
information is not correct, the program displays an output form with an
error message. Nothing more simple then this.
We use Merant Netexpress 3.0 and we have some complex applications running
in NT servers (in the web) and as far as I know nobody as ever complains. I
can upload the source program in our site if you want (I must advise you
that Netexpress is Cobol.and I don't know how old are you :-)
PS. we are not accessing our site since 16:30 (Portugal) 11:30 (New York)
For now we are only testing and nobody knows that mb2s.com exists (I think)
```

```
Carlos Ruivo Ferreira wrote:
> 
> My web site as it's own cgi-bin folder with the right permissions. The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for your time and concern

1.  Run yourself from the command line.  Does it work?

2.  Wrap another CGI around it, and just put a text header on top of
what you are sending, run that and try and understand the results?

3.  Ask you ISP what debugging options they have on their web server. 
There is often an option that will send messages back.  What about the
log files, what do they contain?

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<3948145C.CF39582F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <8fq8fo0hla@enews4.newsguy.com> <8gvfe3$kdo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39456862.8EDDCBCC@home.com> <8i3utn$r6i$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> ;<)

Actually it's worse than you indicate. As soon as somebody tells them
it's officially summer they switch all the bloody heating off - ignoring
the fact that this 'sceptred realm' is an island surrounded by and
drenched in water !. 

Some quick reflections after a 25 year absence - bearing in mind I was
only in SW and NW. Motorway system superb for getting from A to B and
drivers, (including many, many trucks(lorries) with Continental plates),
extremely courteous - that excludes area immediately around Heathrow,
and of course London.

Went to Bentalls department store in Kingston-Upon-Thames, next to
Hampton Court Palace. The external facade had been retained, the
building gutted and they had a magnificent shopping mall as good and
swish as anything I've personally seen in N. America.

Food has vastly improved; you can now get a sandwich which actually has
a filling. Coffee is EXTREMELY strong, compared to Canadian standards
and would make our American cousins gag. Customer service and menus show
a large American influence :-

1) Roast Beef & Yorkshire Pud 2) Meat Lasagne, 3) Veggie Lasagne 4)
throw in something East Indian, 5) Beef Burger, 6) Veggie Burger and #
7) Jacket(Baked) potatoes with a choice of about 6 different fillings.

And of the course the ubiquitous KFCs and burger joints. Motorway
pulloffs are excellent and kept clean. Average meal price around 5-6
pounds. (I didn't translate anything to Canadian dollars - I didn't want
a heart attack !). 

I had a real job just getting a Ploughman's Lunch (Chunk of crusty
bread, Cheddar and pickled onions) in a pub. They threw in additionally
Branston pickle and a side salad turning it into a meal rather than
snack. The fancier restaurants have gone artsy-fartsy with 'haute
cuisine' - a minuscule pork chop centred in a plate with a 14 inch
diameter, garnished with a strawberry top left and a mint leaf bottom
right - it looks so pretty - so does the goddam price !

Southern Britain seems to be going to 'Estuary English' (that's London
to you mate !), nasal tone, drop t's at end of words and monotone sound,
a bit like the young royals, Edward and Di. The so-called BBC accent,
not the snotty one, but 'average-English', seems to be almost gone. I
didn't once hear my favourite Somerset dialect, "Tell oI where urs at
and oI'll cum where urs to". Nearest I got to a southern dialect was
from a Shropshire man who was living in Langholm (pronounced LANGHAM -
can't figure that one), just across the Scottish border from Carlisle -
close to Lockerbie. 

N.American TV is bad. Theirs is just plain BLOODY AWFUL, although I did
catch the last instalment of a documentary on Elizabeth I and a
reminisce of my very, very favourite comedy show, "Dad's Army" about the
Home Guard during WW2. 

Computer wise saw the Denenhams building, (department store group) in
Taunton, Somerset, on a Saturday so could only speak to a security guard
- still same operation, accounting and EDP. Saw Bellefield House in
Trowbridge, Wiltshire, where I first got involved in computing - now
called St. Ivel Software. Met a tanned young smart-ass outside who was
most uninformative. (Turns out they have a lot of Moroccans in
Trowbridge). The Unigate (milk and dairy products) that I knew has
dramatically changed. They sold off Cow & Gate baby foods to a Dutch
company long ago. On the return air trip I saw in the Telegraph that
they have now finally sold off the last of their milk interests to Dairy
Crest - they are into milk no more, not in any fashion.

The scenery - magnificent - so it should be with all that rain; just as
'tho Michael M. had been sent there with his paintbrush and only given a
choice of using different hues of green. Up in the Lake District and
Scotland the green was interspersed with sheep, dotted across the
landscape like white flowers. I saw all three of the houses I had owned
and the first, the people we sold to in '65 still living there, had been
through Calgary - may visit us next time through Canada.

Returning to Calgary by air, by comparison our countryside was a
yellowish green. So much for returning to your 'roots'. I enjoyed the
trip but I'm glad to be back home.

Jimmy
```

```
Please Help!

I am a new programmer so please bear with me.

I have a program that I need to update but I am stumped on one little
part.  We are going to the more updated compiler and I was givin the
task of making this happen.  Anyway, I can't get any overlays to
print.  Here is what I have so far.

OLD CODE:

FD  PRINTER
01  PRINT-REC.
    05  C-C                             PIC X.
    05  PRINT-REC-DATA                  PIC X(132).

01  OVERLAY-REC.
    05  VALUE-5A            PIC X, VALUE '!'.
    05  VALUE-00            PIC X, VALUE LOW-VALUE.
    05  VALUE-18            PIC X.
    05  VALUE-D3            PIC X, VALUE 'L'.
    05  VALUE-AF            PIC X.
    05  VALUE-D8            PIC X, VALUE 'Q'.
    05  VALUE-00            PIC X, VALUE LOW-VALUE.
    05  VALUE-00            PIC X, VALUE LOW-VALUE.
    05  VALUE-00            PIC X, VALUE LOW-VALUE.
    05  OVERLAY-NAME        PIC X(8), VALUE 'O1T     '.
    05  FILLER REDEFINES OVERLAY-NAME.
        10  OVERLAY-O1T     PIC X(3).
        10  OVERLAY-PHASE   PIC X(5).
    05  OVERLAY-X-COORD     PIC XXX.
    05  OVERLAY-Y-COORD     PIC XXX.
    05  VALUE-00            PIC X, VALUE LOW-VALUE.
    05  VALUE-00            PIC X, VALUE LOW-VALUE.

    *FILL IN REMAINDER OF OVERLAY RECORD
    MOVE OVERLAY-REC        TO PRINT-REC
    WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER POSITIONING C-C.

NEW CODE (SO FAR):

CONFIGURATION SECTION.
SPECIAL-NAMES.
    C01 IS CHAN1
    C02 IS CHAN2
    C03 IS CHAN3
    C04 IS CHAN4
    C05 IS CHAN5.

 SAME FD
 SAME OVERLAY-REC

    *FILL IN REMAINDER OF OVERLAY RECORD
    MOVE OVERLAY-REC        TO PRINT-REC
    WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER ?????

I have standard data that is not an overlay and it seems to be working
(using the SPECIAL-NAMES Paragraph), but the overlay is not.  I also
tried writing the overlay without any AFTER clause and I just get a
single line of garbage (my overlay-rec) printing on the paper.  Is it
something in the JCL?  What does my write line need to be?  How do I
correct this?  Any help would be great.  Thanks

Matt
```

```
Try this:  MOVE OVERLAY-REC TO PRINT-REC-DATA instead of PRINT-REC.  You
are overlaying your print control character so you don't get line
spacing.

Joe
```

- **From:** "Solar" <solar@axs2k.net>

```
I think if you move '0' to C-C, you will get a blank line between print
lines (a double space); if you move '-' to C-C, you will get two blank lines
(a triple space); if you move a blank to C-C, you get the default
single-spacing; if you move a '+' to C-C, you get no spacing, you print on
top of the previous line.  Anything else, from '1' to 'C' corresponds to the
channel code.  Normally '1' is top of page, 'C' is bottom of page, and
everything in between is evenly spaced in between ( a channel '6' should be
about halfway down the page).  But of course, this is a shop standard that
could  vary from installation to installation, depending on how they built
the print forms control buffers..  or carriage control tapes if they have
old 1403 printers....

NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:Y_g15.5163$HD6.138596@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <8i39un$fpr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Matt  <mattf73@my-deja.com>
wrote:

…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
I think that the values mentioned in this reply are the "ANSI carriage
control values"  (as in RECFM=FBA).  I believe that the values in the
original post were the "M" values (as in RECFM=FBM).  I do NOT think these
can be "mixed" in the same output file.  The previous suggestion (from
another writer) about checking the ADV compiler option (and the other one
about NOT over-writing the record layout with the control characters) are the
best bets.
```

- **References:** `<8i39un$fpr$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Y_g15.5163$HD6.138596@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3946a92e.0@news.axs2k.net> <8i6m26$gs0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8i8bi7$33h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 Jun 2000 16:23:59 GMT, Matt <mattf73@my-deja.com> wrote:

>In article <8i6m26$gs0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
>  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>has more suggestions... I will try anything.  Thanks again.

What was the question again?  You're trying to write after advancing
to a particular channel?  And I think you mentioned something about
the APF 5A records?

SPECIAL-NAMES.
    CHAN-1 IS C01
    CHAN-2 IS C02
    AFP-5A IS 5A.

WRITE OUTPUT-REC FROM LINE-1 AFTER ADVANCING CHAN-1
WRITE OUTPUT-REC FROM LINE-2 AFTER ADVANCING CHAN-2
WRITE OUTPUT-REC FROM AFP-LINE AFTER ADVANCING AFP-5A

I'm not sure if that AFP-5A thing is exactly correct, but I'd guess
it's in the AFP manual somewhere.

I don't think that ADV/NOADV have any affect on being allowed to use
the first position as the control character.  The only difference is
that using NOADV allows you to explicitly define the byte that will
hold the control character, whereas ADV has the compiler do it for
you.  What I mean by this is just because you use NOADV doesn't mean
that if you define LINE-1 as

01  LINE-1.
      05  L1-CC          PIC X VALUE '1'
      05  L1-DATA   PIC X(132) VALUE 'THIS IS A TEST'.

and then say "WRITE OUTPUT-REC FROM LINE-1 AFTER ADVANCING 2" you
won't actually advance to channel 1.  All that will happen is the
control character to advance 2 lines will overwrite the '1' in the
first position (well, I think it would actually do that in the FD
buffer area (OUTPUT-REC), not in the working-storage area).

Keep in mind I'm not at work right now, so I don't have access to
programs and manuals to verify all this!  Also keep in mind that this
all pertains to VSE.  If MVS or OS/390 is different, well, I don't
know.

If I have screwed something up I'm sure someone will let me know.

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Channel Problems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394A54DF.8CF80CB5@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8i39un$fpr$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Y_g15.5163$HD6.138596@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3946a92e.0@news.axs2k.net> <8i6m26$gs0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8i8bi7$33h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The ADV/NOADV option determines how an output print line is treated.  If
the ADV option is specified, the compiler prefixes every record with 1
byte into which the control character generated by the WRITE record-name
AFTER ADVANCING ... is stored.  Thus a 132 byte record in the program
source becomes a 133 byte record when written to disk or spool.  If the
NOADV option is specified, the compiler will place the generated control
character into the first byte of the area described by the record
description.  This gets to be more confusing if you inadvertently mix
WRITE record-name with WRITE record-name AFTER ADVANCING ...  (you
definitely do not want to mix WRITE record-name BEFORE ADVANCING ...
with WRITE record-name AFTER ADVANCING ...).  

In a historical digression, natively IBM impact printers were designed
as WRITE record-name BEFORE ADVANCING devices (i.e. the native machine
control codes were designed to print and then advance either 0 - 3 lines
or to a line determined by a punch in a carriage control tape).  The
COBOL compilers prior to COBOL 74 also had an extension WRITE
record-name AFTER POSITIONING control-character where the legal values
were space - advance 1 line, zero - advance 2 lines, hyphen - advance 3
lines, plus sign - advance 3 lines, 1 through 9 - advance to carriage
control tape positions 1 through 9, A through C - advance to carriage
control tape positions 10 through 12.  These values were labeled
American Standards Association values (now American National Standards
Institute).  By convention "1" was top of form and "12" was bottom of
page.     

It gets to be very interesting if you have packages from multiple
vendors and they differ on this option.    

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

Matt wrote:
> 
> In article <8i6m26$gs0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Channel Problems

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39463894.E7CFAD32@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8i39un$fpr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Matt wrote:
> 
..snipped some AFP stuff...

Matt,

Compiler lesson number 1.  New compilers come with different options.  

Does you file have a definition of FBA?  If it does then you have
fallen foul of the ADV clause (from memory)  There is a switch that
says you put in the first byte of a FBA dataset or the compiler does. 
The moment that you use advancing guess what you gain an extra byte.

In you sysout display go left or use display cc' (don't quote me here)
to show the first character, if it before the '!' then you have found
your problem.  The exclamation mark must be column 1.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

```
I have felt the same way about MERANT's interest in the "small" developer. I
talked to the NetExpress Product Manager and he gave me the impression that
they understood the concerns of the "small" developer.

But, the result of MERANT's review of the runtime fees will tell the "small"
developer where they stand!!

Norm Juers
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL for Linux

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<642A954DD517D411B20C00508BCF23B00127A31C@mail.sauder.com>`
- **References:** `<39421668$0$11564@news.compuserve.de> <3942E38B.82E0F131@zip.com.au> <39451C80.1EF7AEA3@att.net>`

```
The TinyCOBOL project is coming along quite nicely.

Tim Joslings COBOL compiler (GNU compliant) is DIFFERENT from
TinyCOBOL and is coming along.  

Tiny seems to be playing loose with the standards in an effort to get
something working quickly and they had a head start with source
provided for a DOS based compiler.

Tim's efforts are from scratch and are standards compliant and GNU
compliant.  When Tim gets his subset working that will allow expansion
of the compiler using this COBOL subset, I will try to get involved
and do some work on the project.

wrote:
```

### Re: string concatination ? - CORRECTION -

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uD415.34$wX2.19019@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <8hu9d6$9mt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8i04cn$jor$1@news.igs.net> <LhW05.2845$fS6.474361@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tXX05.4985$HD6.131238@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:tXX05.4985$HD6.131238@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <LhW05.2845$fS6.474361@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
> Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> >donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
> >news:8i04cn$jor$1@news.igs.net...
> >> I think you have missed the point.  A contractor is often hired and
paid to
> >> do Incredibly Stupid Things (IST, as opposed to IT).  When hired to do
IST,
> >> then one says "this is IST, not IT", and if the answer is "that is what
you
> >> were hired to do", then one does it.
> >>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> doing?

When asked correctly?

The interview for a consulting assignment should be a two-way interview;
that is, the prospective consultant should be interviewing the prospective
client as well as vice-versa. If the prospective consultant can't ask the
right questions, he/she deserves to get stuck doing ISTs. And if the
prospective client turns out to have misstated the terms of the engagement,
I can't see a moral barrier to giving a suitable notice and abandoning the
assignment.

This may seem a rather severe attitude about accepting/remaining on an
engagement, but it seems fair to me as long as once the consultant says,
"yes" and the work requested by the client is within the range of what was
discussed in advance, then the consultant does the work - no matter how
crummy or ill-advised - with the same amount of professional effort and
dedication as he/she puts into the 'cool' or 'fun' stuff.

My perspective is somewhat jaundiced by the fact that I rarely accept
engagements which call for "time" to do "whatever" - I look for "projects"
where there is a definable completion point. Those who accept "time"
engagements must accept the "whatever". Kinda like eating your vegatables in
exchange for having dessert, but that's life.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)

```
In article <uD415.34$wX2.19019@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
>news:tXX05.4985$HD6.131238@iad-read.news.verio.net...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>When asked correctly?

Answering a question with a question is no answer at all, Mr Mattias.

>
>The interview for a consulting assignment should be a two-way interview;
>that is, the prospective consultant should be interviewing the prospective
>client as well as vice-versa. If the prospective consultant can't ask the
>right questions, he/she deserves to get stuck doing ISTs.

*Really*, Mr Mattias... not only does a consultant need to 'ask the right
questions', s/he needs to be willing to call the interviewer a liar, too!

'What kind of debugging tools are installed here?'

'We have ABEND-Aid and InterTest.'

'Of *course* you do... now, can I see a SYSOUT and a CICS invocation which
support your assertion?'

(note - I have worked on sites and had the following interchange:

'Why don't you use InterTest?  Everything is out there that you need!'

'Oh... ol' Charlie was responsible for that and he up and got a better
job... and nobody's been able to figure it out since then.  Use EDF like
everyone else')

... and how about ...

'What kind of lattitude is a coder given in designing a solution?'

'We believe in fully empowering our workers... except if you work for
Louise, she doesn't like SEARCH ALL... and Sam won't allow any external
SORTs... and make sure you catc the QA crew when they're sober!'

>And if the
>prospective client turns out to have misstated the terms of the engagement,
>I can't see a moral barrier to giving a suitable notice and abandoning the
>assignment.

... and spending another (n) weeks lining up another.  Yes, The Highway is
always an alternative... but the goal is to spend more time on the job
than on the beach.
 
>
>This may seem a rather severe attitude about accepting/remaining on an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>dedication as he/she puts into the 'cool' or 'fun' stuff.

That was never argued, Mr Mattias; the question at hand was, as my
admittedly porous memory has it, 'Why should a consultant code a solution
in a horrid manner?', the answer being 'Because the one who signs the
timesheet wants it done that way.'

DD
```

- **References:** `<8hgqos$21k3$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <8hu9d6$9mt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8i04cn$jor$1@news.igs.net> <LhW05.2845$fS6.474361@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <tXX05.4985$HD6.131238@iad-read.news.verio.net> <uD415.34$wX2.19019@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> When asked correctly?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I went to an interview asked 20 prepared questions of three players, 
I got good answers off one lot, bad off another, and fantastic off the
third.  The money was right so I moved.  Guess what they were
bullshitting me as much as I did to get my first programming job!

The interview is a two way snow job.  You present a sales image, they
present a sales image.  The hard bit is to cut through the gloss and
find out what reality is about.  The best of us get it wrong
unfortunately.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

<a id="t-095e4b963f"></a>
## Thread: Constants — 6 msgs

### Constants

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3944DF61.DFE73346@cusys.edu>`

```
I was reading in the latest http://CobolReport.com


>> One advantage of constant directives over literals is that they make constants truly
>> constant across different applications.  That is, CURR-APR is always 9.125 and not 
>> 9.125 in one program and 9.13 in another. 

and 

>> You can turn the CONSTANT directive off so that the symbolic name is no longer  >> defined with the following: 

>>                       >>CONSTANT symbolic-name OFF.

These statements imply that constants are defined across an enterprise,
as opposed to within a program.  Is this a correct inference?
```

#### ↳ Re: Constants

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i3fqe$98g$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3944DF61.DFE73346@cusys.edu>`

```
1) The >>CONSTANT directive - has been changed to a >>DEFINE directive

2) It has gone back and forth, but I *believe* the current draft allows a
>>DEFINE to apply to the entire compilation group (this includes both nested
programs and a sequence of programs)

3) Some compilers can (and do) "optimize" for identified "constants" as
opposed to literals

4) Constants can be included in copy books (if appropriate)

5) The draft now distinguishes between "compilation variables" and
"constants" - HOWEVER, there is a method of getting constants from
compilation variables.  Compilation variables are used for conditional
compilation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Constants

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i2rc5$9ej$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3944DF61.DFE73346@cusys.edu>`

```
I think it depends on how you use them.  If you put them in a copybook they
can be(I've been playing with that).


Howard Brazee wrote in message <3944DF61.DFE73346@cusys.edu>...
>I was reading in the latest http://CobolReport.com
>
>
>>> One advantage of constant directives over literals is that they make
constants truly
>>> constant across different applications.  That is, CURR-APR is always
9.125 and not
>>> 9.125 in one program and 9.13 in another.
>
>and
>
>>> You can turn the CONSTANT directive off so that the symbolic name is no
longer  >> defined with the following:
>
>>>                       >>CONSTANT symbolic-name OFF.
>
>These statements imply that constants are defined across an enterprise,
>as opposed to within a program.  Is this a correct inference?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Constants

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3944F39E.E9A1A5BA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<3944DF61.DFE73346@cusys.edu> <8i2rc5$9ej$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> I think it depends on how you use them.  If you put them in a copybook they
> can be(I've been playing with that).

Then the article is misleading.  That isn't an advantage of constant
directives over literals.
 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3944DF61.DFE73346@cusys.edu>...

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
The major advantage that I see is the ability to consolidate an item that
might change into one place.  The occurs clause, in particular, though it
seems they could be used as an alternative to the copy replacing method.
Now that memory is not a problem, I have been coding a lot more error
checking of source code changes into the code using constants and "length
of" constructs.
```

```
On Sun, 11 Jun 2000 14:33:36 -0500, "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:


A few of my random comments:


>
>SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's stretching things a bit.  There are still drivers that need to
be written for each device you use on the PC.  Mostly these are
written by the company that supplies the hardware.  True Windows
allows you to just "PRINT" and retrieves the correct driver, but
Apple's OS does the same thing.  So it really has nothing to do with
more is better.

>>
>> For a long time Netscape held the market share in browsers.  I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

When I first got on the internet, I used Netscape for which I paid
nothing.  This was in 1993 and Netscape was given to me by the ISP I
signed up with.  Since then, I have voluntarily purchased the Netscape
suite (Communicator, Navigator etc.) one time for which I paid 74.98
(1998).  But since that time, I've been able to keep it current for
nothing.  Yes, IE was given away because that was the only way (at the
time) that MS could cut into Netscape's 85% share with an inferior
product.  Personally, I'd rather pay for a product that works and
doesn't have security holes than to get something for free that
doesn't.

>>
>> In terms of OS cost, what you describe will happen only if MS is
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That may be true, but you might also remember (or maybe you don't) at
one time threre were 3 other companies producing a DOS operating
system.  I remember buying PC-DOS (by IBM?) way back when and I'm sure
I didn't pay anywhere near $100 for it.  Now, let's face it, DOS is
darn near dead and outside of the fledgling Linux, there is no other
Intel x86 based OS available except Windows.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Doesn't 'expecting the unexpected" make the unexpected expected?




Boycott Mitsubishi Industries

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Microsoft (OT)

- **From:** Patrick Riley <p_riley@pipeline.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qdfaks08l25a0bqkqr5onp715qhvfidtna@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<393ED035.78397140@worldnet.att.net> <wfq05.359$Oq2.118732@nnrp1.sbc.net> <8htivr$5pv$1@news.igs.net> <AA1D7E30217A402F.CDCF8528CF3434AA.56277D017EF5C644@lp.airnews.net> <50h5ksk4stvmkub3jrh45ea45ej7c9si4j@4ax.com> <566DF13F92F4BCD4.5EAFBD6CF3126E46.CE53F60A371B04AE@lp.airnews.net> <9QR05.8141$613.376814@nnrp3.sbc.net> <86E33B2194E4F903.32C6A8C916DD5EB4.EF8F45E8B6A8CD78@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:

>That may be true, but you might also remember (or maybe you don't) at
>one time threre were 3 other companies producing a DOS operating
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Intel x86 based OS available except Windows.

PC-DOS was the same as MS-DOS with a different version # (one lower or
higher, I forget which). M'Soft was employed by IBM to write an
operating system for the PC but IBM, giving away the crown jewels,
allowed M'Soft to market the same thing and call it MS-DOS.

There was also DR-DOS, really Digital Research DOS but called Doctor
DOS by some. It had certain inconsistencies (or maybe bugs) with
MS-DOS which meant that some supposedly-compatible software would not
run under it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microsoft (OT)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3945E439.1FE@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<393ED035.78397140@worldnet.att.net> <wfq05.359$Oq2.118732@nnrp1.sbc.net> <8htivr$5pv$1@news.igs.net> <AA1D7E30217A402F.CDCF8528CF3434AA.56277D017EF5C644@lp.airnews.net> <50h5ksk4stvmkub3jrh45ea45ej7c9si4j@4ax.com> <566DF13F92F4BCD4.5EAFBD6CF3126E46.CE53F60A371B04AE@lp.airnews.net> <9QR05.8141$613.376814@nnrp3.sbc.net> <86E33B2194E4F903.32C6A8C916DD5EB4.EF8F45E8B6A8CD78@lp.airnews.net> <qdfaks08l25a0bqkqr5onp715qhvfidtna@4ax.com>`

```
> PC-DOS was the same as MS-DOS with a different version # (one lower or
> higher, I forget which). M'Soft was employed by IBM to write an
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> run under it.

Actually it was the other way round.  DR-DOS was a better DOS than
MS-DOS in a large number of ways but 'strangely' Windows started
'complaining' about 'incompatabilities'.  These were in fact completely
spurious and were just anothe MS ploy to eliminate competition (as
reported in, say, Dr Dobbs).

There were in fact many operating systems for the PC other than MS-DOS
(and PC-DOS), most superior in many ways, but they were driven out of
the market mainly by MS's marketing practices such as 'per box pricing'
whereby MS charged a fee for _every_ machine made by a maker regardless
of which OS shipped.  This put a price barrier on getting a different OS
- the customer had to pay for DOS/Windows _plus_ another OS.  

OS/2, Oasys/TheOS, QNX, Various Unix/Venix/Xenix/Zeus, Concurrent-DOS,
PC-MOS, BOS, UCSD, ....
```

```
dark public place, etc.
```

## Thread: Announce: Tiny COBOL release (0.1.0.1) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### Announce: Tiny COBOL release (0.1.0.1)

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39455F8D.7A55C746@arvotek.net>`

```
A new version of the Tiny COBOL compiler is available at sourceforge (1)
and metalab (2). This is a release (0.1.0.1) contains some bug fixes. 


Project Description:

language:       COBOL (COmmon Business Oriented Language)
package:        Tiny COBOL
version:        0.1.0.1 (pre alpha)
license:	GPL, LGPL (runtime)
parts:          Compiler (produces GNU assembler code for the 
                Linux i386 platform), grammar (yacc,lex), 
                documentation, examples, test suite, run-time 
                library, format conversion utility, source 
                code included.
author:         Rildo Pragana <rpragana@acm.org>
location:       http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/ (1)
		http://metalab.unc.edu/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/ (2)
description:    COBOL compiler for the i386 Linux platform.
conformance:    Partial (as close as possible) ANS COBOL 85. X/Open
                free format.
features:       Linux/UNIX extensions (command line, environmental 
                variables, standard input/output/error).
requires:       GNU assembler and linker to produce an executable
		binary.
ports:          Linux on i386 platform.
status:         Development status is active.
discussion:     tiny-cobol-users <tiny-cobol-users@sourceforge.net>.
updated:        2000/06/10



David
```

---

<a id="t-47ee851555"></a>
## Thread: Compiler listings — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler listings

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394508E4.3F416375@cusys.edu>`

```
Some people talk about compiler listings as being irrelevant.  I like to
use them for a few features, even if I don't print them out:

1.  I want to get a list of all fields from within copy members.   Often
I will print these out.
2.  I want to see actual field sizes and displacements (would prefer
position).  I pick an option which will put these on the same line as
the field.  With my IBM compiler, this also adds a bunch of junk on the
bottom, which wastes paper when I occasionally print it out.
3.  I like options where I can find unused or unchanged fields.  This
can be useful when you cut and paste from program to program - find out
why that field is unused.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler listings

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<642A954DD517D411B20C00508BCF23B00127A2FB@mail.sauder.com>`
- **References:** `<394508E4.3F416375@cusys.edu>`

```
On Mon, 12 Jun 2000 09:59:32 -0600, Howard Brazee wrote:

>Some people talk about compiler listings as being irrelevant.  I like to
>use them for a few features, even if I don't print them out:
[snip]

Listings (printed or otherwise) are also good for investigating a
large, unfamiliar, spaghetti-code program prior to making changes.

Another use is for correlating data and/or code addresses to memory
dump information in analyzing failed programs.

Printed listings are particularly suited to marking using pens, paper
clips, yellow sticky notes, and the like.  Using these on the monitor
screen gets a little messy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler listings

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39458d2b.15009268@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<394508E4.3F416375@cusys.edu>`

```

>Some people talk about compiler listings as being irrelevant.  I like to
>use them for a few features, even if I don't print them out:
```

<a id="t-604c132f22"></a>
## Thread: Help for vutil of AcuCobol — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help for vutil of AcuCobol

- **From:** genesispt@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i3061$8qf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello,
We are a Portuguese software company and we need help regarding the
vutil utility.
One of our clients has an IBM AIX Version 3 risc system/6000 where they
have some AcuCobol files (Vision version 3). We need to import the
contents of these files to another software that we are creating. For
this purpose we tried to use vutil, but the version that they have is
2.1.1 and it seems that this version doesn't have the "unload" option
with "-t" that outputs the files to a simple text file.
Can anybody help us find a new version of vutil ?
Thanks for any help.

Antonio Alberto Silva


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

```
For those same people who think plain math is BS. What are you comparing

compare with NY (or, God forbid, Boston) rates for a studio without
AC?

not the cost of living. It is the possibility that Hitlary may win the
election, in which case the peacable Tampa airport (BTW, voted to be the
best in the nation) will be flooded with Tampa-hating people escaping from
totalitarianism.
```
