When I decided to move from PA to FL for good, I simply corresponded the
costs of living in both (I had lived in both) and figured out that even
taking a 15% cut, I would have more money left in my pocket in FL than in
PA, and negotiated accordingly. The truth is, FLA _is_ less expensive to
live, and other factors like the absence of winter and benefits associated
with it, have their weight, too. As far as the wages go, yes, in Tampa they
are lower than say in Jacksonville or Miami, but in all years there, I did
not meet a self-respecting professional making wages on par with a
mechanic. The only exceptions were the H1-B guys, but we know what their
circumstances are.

> No, I'm not from Philly, but I was raised in the NE. I did live in

could not agree more. It is inland, and weather-wise, it is unbearable. The
areas where FLA provides quality living are coastal ones, and I generally
prefer Gulf to Atlantic.

> In closing, if you want to get paid for what you're worth, then demand
> it. Don't fall victim to these pimps who have nothing but THEIR best
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> check.

I despise pimps, and it has been a long time since I slaved for one. Been
consulting from my own self for years. Of course pimps would tell you
anything to get their cut as fat as possible, and would lie to you about
that cut with a straight face. Been there, seen that. In the case of FLA,
they at least tell one grain of truth - it is cheaper to live than up
North, but of course not as much cheaper as they want to lead you to
believe.

> BTW, I worked my way through school as a mechanic, so I would never
> revert back to getting paid like one.

Fair enough. I share you feelings on this one, even though my situation has
its own layer of complexity. When I started my way here in US, I worked in
a garage as a mechanic, too, not to mention pizza delivery. Either one paid
infinitely more than my job as a Senior Researcher, Ph.D. in Physical
Elecronics in the place whence I had come :-).

Kind regards,
================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl

On Sat, 17 Jun 2000 04:56:57 GMT, "Paul M. Dorfman"
<sashole@mediaone.net> wrote:


>It would disturb me, too. But a _professional_ would not do such a thing.
>When I decided to move from PA to FL for good, I simply corresponded the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>with it, have their weight, too.

Yes, maybe les expensive than PA, but do a Cost of Living comparison
betwen FL and where I live, Bryan Texas.  Still cheaper?  Not a
chance.  When I last moved it was from TN to TX, and the Cost of
Living between the two was about equal.  I also interviewed in IL.
They would not offer enough $$ to let me break even.   Things I
considered were:

State income tax
State sales tax.  Is the sales tax on food as well?
Cost of Utilities
How much does it cost to register a new car?  An older car?
Housing costs for an approx 2000 sq foot 3 br, 2ba home.  Property
tax?
Crime rate.

The only real hit coming to Texas was the property tax.  In TN I was
paying $482 on a house, and here it's about $1600.  Everything else
was pretty close.  

So when someone posts a job in FL and says "consider the cheaper cost
of living" they should understand that they may not be the cheapest,
and who knows someone might want MORE money to move there because they
are in a cheaper area.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

## Thread: JCL Call Statement for IBM OS/390 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL Call Statement for IBM OS/390

- **From:** Techno Bass <photonet01@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i68h8$icj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi!
In my COBOL program  I would like to call another program which would
perform a small operation -- instead of having the main program do all
the calculations. I would like to know
the JCL CALL statement for IBM OS/390. I would appreciate any info.

I think the statement looks something like -- CALL 'CALCWAGES' USING
.....

Here CALCWAGES is another program call within the main program.
I'm not sure of the exact syntax.  If you need more info please email
me.

Thanks.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Call Statement for IBM OS/390

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <Kerry.Ellis@msdw.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3946AE4E.C708B22C@msdw.com>`
- **References:** `<8i68h8$icj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
See:

 6.2.4 "COBOL Language Reference" [CALL Statement]


Techno Bass wrote:

> Hi!
> In my COBOL program  I would like to call another program which would
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Call Statement for IBM OS/390

- **From:** "Solar" <solar@axs2k.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3947fae5.0@news.axs2k.net>`
- **References:** `<8i68h8$icj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,

There is no "JCL" call statement, but you can call a subroutine either
statically or dynamically:

CALL 'CALCWAGES' using RATE, HOURS.

will call the subroutine named CALCWAGES which must be link-edited into your
load module.  It's easier if you

01  SUB-NAME  PIC X(8)  VALUE 'CALCWAGES'.
CALL SUB-NAME using RATE,HOURS.

That way, the subroutine is loaded dynamically (not linked with your main
program), and can be modified without having to recompile/link the main
program.  There are no JCL requirements; the subroutine is found in your
normal JOBLIB/STEPLIB or LNKLST library.


Techno Bass <photonet01@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:8i68h8$icj$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi!
> In my COBOL program  I would like to call another program which would
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Call Statement for IBM OS/390

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3948D529.37C7E923@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8i68h8$icj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3947fae5.0@news.axs2k.net>`

```
Solar wrote:
> 
> That way, the subroutine is loaded dynamically (not linked with your main
> program), and can be modified without having to recompile/link the main
> program.  There are no JCL requirements; the subroutine is found in your
> normal JOBLIB/STEPLIB or LNKLST library.

I wasn't figuring out what he was talking about with "JCL call", so I
passed.  But occasionally you will need to modify JCL when you add a
call.  Sometimes the called program references a file or database which
the calling program doesn't reference.  The JCL has to be adequate for
both the calling and the called program.   And in test environments, it
is possible to have your called program and your called programs in
different STEPLIBs which need to be concatenated correctly.
```

## Thread: Decimal to Comma Conversion? — 12 msgs

### Decimal to Comma Conversion?

- **From:** jonsio@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Does anybody have an idea on the best way to reformat decimal to commas
for European currency applications?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal to Comma Conversion?

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394648CE.A0E67E51@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Well, for string fields you could try 
INSPECT field-name REPLACING ALL '.' BY ','.

For PICTURE clauses use DECIMAL-POINT IS COMMA in your special-names
paragraph.  This exchanges the functions of the period and the comma in
picture strings and numeric literals.

jonsio@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Does anybody have an idea on the best way to reformat decimal to commas
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal to Comma Conversion?

- **From:** "brucepbarrett" <brucepbarrett@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uuNbOZU1$GA.283@cpmsnbbsa09>`
- **References:** `<8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
INSPECT xxx
                 REPLACING ALL "." BY ",".  Where xxx is the name of the
data element to change.

<jonsio@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Does anybody have an idea on the best way to reformat decimal to commas
> for European currency applications?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Decimal to Comma Conversion?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i5ljk$qt3$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The DECIMAL POINT IS COMMA statement should work for all your output.  If
you are using the "." in actual code (in string and unstring, for example),
and are converting the system to run either/or, then I'd suggest that you do
it with the new CONSTANT function, or put the character into a parameter in
your set-up file. Doing it with the CONSTANT method would require you
compile for a European version, while the other way would make it a setup
parameter.  Avoid hard-coding it like a plaque.  It will come back to haunt
you.

jonsio@my-deja.com wrote in message <8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Does anybody have an idea on the best way to reformat decimal to commas
>for European currency applications?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Decimal to Comma Conversion?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KXVGORZM=4MUkgOT1jXRUJVhnyrJ@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8i5ljk$qt3$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Although the "Decimal point is comma" works fine, it "can not"
normally be use in a program that has "input".
The reason for this is that up to a short time ago, most terminals
of Unix and others would not allow the "decimal" point on the numeric
keypad to be changed. (someone from the US forgot this "small" detail)

This poses a BIG problem because if developers use the "Decimal point"
ad standard, although this will save some programming time, it will
force the users to use both the normal keyboard (for the "comma") and
the numeric keypad for the numbers.

If one does not use that instruction, all input can be made on the
numeric keypad, and the programmer is responsible for replacing
the variables on output( either display or printer).

As for replacing the variables, I always find the using
inspect variable converting ",." to ".," easy enough to use.

as oposed to use three inspect replacing as someone mentioned before.


FF



On Tue, 13 Jun 2000 11:50:29 -0400, "donald tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>The DECIMAL POINT IS COMMA statement should work for all your output.  If
>you are using the "." in actual code (in string and unstring, for example),
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Decimal to Comma Conversion?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i69q1$ene$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It's amazing how we ever got our data displayed correct isn't it??

Looking through all the suggestions I'm confused, and I bet you are too.

This is how we do it far away from the Bermuda Triangle.

Put a DECIMAL POINT IS COMMA in Special-Names.

Move your currency fields to one of folowing display-fields

01 Display-Options.
     05 Less-than-1000    pic zz9,99-
     05 More-than-1000    pic zzz.zzz.zzz.zz9,99-

We fancy the sign after the amount. We fancy one digit in front of the
decimal-point (e.g. 0,37- )

Remember, that some countries in Europe must specify national currency and
EURO for a period of time. You need 6 decimals in calculations to get
satisfactory precission.

Hans Christian Andersen came from Denmark (Europe). Don't listen to other
fairy tales.

Good luck
Ib


jonsio@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen <8i5eog$v3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Does anybody have an idea on the best way to reformat decimal to commas
>for European currency applications?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

have had to make multi-lingual reports, e.g one in US-English
and one in French, etc, all from within the SAME program.
There you can clearly not use the special-names method.

Leif

This might work...(pardon the light-heartedness)


  01  AMERICAN-REAL-MONEY    PIC 9(8)V99 COMP-3.
  01  EURO-SILLY-PLAY-MONEY  PIC 99,999,999.99.

  MOVE AMERICAN-REAL-MONEY TO
       EURO-SILLY-PLAY-MONEY.
  INSPECT EURO-SILLY-PLAY-MONEY REPLACING ALL ',' BY LOW-VALUES.
  INSPECT EURO-SILLY-PLAY-MONEY REPLACING ALL '.' BY ','.
  INSPECT EURO-SILLY-PLAY-MONEY REPLACING ALL LOW-VALUES BY '.'.

Tim Coffey

(if you do not know how to use "inspect ... converting"
or the "inspect ... converting ",." ".,".

And I have to say that from the hundreds of customers
that I have worked with, 90% or more used(1) one or the other
to convert from ",." to ".$" which is the portuguese
monetary separator. like 100$50 which translate as
one hundred ESCUDOS and fifty cents.

FF


(1)- Not anymore because of the EURO.
 "Big time (bucks) converting programs..."

FYI,  the draft of the next Standard "easily" handles multiple methods of
displaying both numeric and monetary amounts in the same program - using
"LOCALE" logic (as defined in POSIX).

Check with your "vendor of choice" on what their plans are for implementing
this particular feature (before any Standard gets approved).  Some (e.g.
COBOL/400) have already done so.
```

## Thread: Has COBOL being unused? — 32 this month / 34 total

### Has COBOL being unused?

- **From:** "krayger" <krayger@teleline.es>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
Today's programmers does not use COBOL like their favourite language. COBOL
is an language for 60 and 70`s programmers or pupils who (in my case) are
commited to study it.
In my country, COBOL is being unused because it causes more problems than
beneficts to the companies that uses it. It is true that COBOL is a very
strong language which is very difficult to break down, but it is a language
that was designed and wrote in remote years ago and it is not very
flexible...

I am a Management Systems Technical and my opinion is not the one. In my
department all personal think like me.
```

Sounds like your opinion is valid for your company.  But without any
facts to support it, I am concerned that this may simply be a case of
self-fulfilling prophecy.  (You believe in something so strongly that
your personal actions make it be true.)

It has been my experience that every time I have met someone who
announced that a tool was no good, they were later exposed as being
ignorant of under what conditions/platforms the tool was supposed to
be used.

Pete

#### ↳ Re: Has COBOL being unused?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39478511.21DE62AA@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
What language(s) are being used in your country for business
applications?  Do they have fixed point decimal data types as opposed to
binary fixed or floating point?  What are the rules for accuracy in
calculating currency amounts and interest calculations?  In specific, do
these rules require rounding to a given number of decimal places or do
they require the calculation to be accurate to a given number of
significant digits?

These questions may lead you to a review of the choice of language for
at least some applications.  PL/1 and some proprietary languages also
have fixed point decimal data types so the change from whatever is being
used to a language that better handles fixed point decimal may not be to
COBOL.

In addition I am curious as what flexibility is needed that COBOL
doesn't provide?  I know some of the things I want in COBOL but this may
not be what your organization needs.  Also is the fact that COBOL is
strongly English Language based (very peculiar English or American but
...) seen as a major drawback in your country?   
Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

krayger wrote:
> 
> Today's programmers does not use COBOL like their favourite language. COBOL
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> department all personal think like me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Has COBOL being unused?

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3947892c.4236891@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
"krayger" <krayger@teleline.es> wrote:

>Today's programmers does not use COBOL like their favourite language. COBOL
>is an language for 60 and 70`s programmers or pupils who (in my case) are
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>flexible...

Krayger:

You say that COBOL causes more problems than benefits to the companies
who use it.  It is *easy* to make a claim like that, but you have not
provided us with a listing of problems.  If you would like the people
in the COBOL newsgroup to take you seriously, you must provide
*evidence*.  I could say that the moon is made from green cheese, but
unless I provide evidence, my claim cannot be proven.  Please provide
facts.....NOT just your opinion.

You say it is not very flexible.....please provide details.  Why is it
not very flexible?  When making a claim such as this, you should be
prepared to back it up with facts.

>I am a Management Systems Technical and my opinion is not the one. In my
>department all personal think like me.

You used the word, *opinion* but you have not presented us with facts.

At the risk of starting a pointless flame war, I encourage you to back
up your opinion with facts.  We are all waiting breathlessly.

By the way....just out of curiosity...are you a newsgroup troll?



Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

up.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Has COBOL being unused?

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3948d374.1774695@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es> <3947892c.4236891@news.epix.net> <8i8pnb$kjl$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
Hello Mr. Holy Kipper!

You'll be happy to know that I am not a fisherman.  It is also an
honor to be able to converse with such an esteemed fish as yourself!
I applaud your curiosity as well as your eagerness to learn and
encourage you to continue with your "schooling!"  (sorry....bad
joke...couldn't resist!)

;-)

Ahh!  The elusive "newsgroup troll" question!  Mr. Kipper, a newsgroup
troll is one who intentionally posts messages to usenet newsgroups
which are designed to upset and enrage the "regulars" of the
newsgroup.  This generally results in a "war of words" between the
regulars and the alleged troll.  This is also referred to as a "flame
war."

Our friend from Spain who posted his opinion of COBOL, while certainly
entitled to his opinion, may have been attempting to start such a war.
But without absolute proof, I can only assume that he/she is either an
unfortunate and ill-informed soul, too lazy to perform proper research
or perhaps simply lacking in mental acuity.

I hope this was helpful and wish you the cleanest of waters in which
to swim and may all nets you encounter contain very large holes!


"The Holy Kipper" <theholykipper@madasafish.com> wrote:

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Has COBOL being unused?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39491EB1.FEF016AF@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es> <A7749D0152325B14.900856A3865AB915.8A1B4CE9E182C404@lp.airnews.net>`

```

>In my country, COBOL is being unused because it causes more problems than
>beneficts to the companies that uses it. It is true that COBOL is a very
>strong language which is very difficult to break down, but it is a language
>that was designed and wrote in remote years ago and it is not very
>flexible...

I have been trying to figure out what "a strong language which is very
difficult to break down" means.  I can infer from the paragraph above
that strength has nothing to do with flexibility.

Does this mean that programs written in COBOL won't spontaneously fall
apart?  This is true of any language which is not making real-time calls
or invokes.  But Cobol can make real-time calls or invokes.

By what criteria would we rank languages by strength?  Being able to do
a lot of things is obviously flexibility, so by the standards of this
exercise, is not a valid criterion.
```

#### ↳ Re: Has COBOL being unused?

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gg6qks4edr2uinfrt8r1h3c3i7gnehshln@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
"krayger" <krayger@teleline.es> wrote:

>Today's programmers does not use COBOL like their favourite language. COBOL
>is an language for 60 and 70`s programmers or pupils who (in my case) are
>commited to study it.

ho hum. well, as soon as you aren't a pupil, or actually have actually
ported from cobol to language of the month, get back to why it isn't
in use. the *truth* is, the best language (whatever that term means)
may not be the language in which the original system was written. one
of the few things i have learned from economics (which i got a c in)
is that the market is based on demand and supply. so if you change to
a different language, as the number of programmers move out (retire or
die) the price of the conversion goes up to continue.

let me put it another way. it costs more to port a language than it
does to maintain it in absolute terms. is it cheaper to hire a
programmer who knows 2 languages or 1? obviously 1. and if the market
dries up, the labor price increases. other than company-hopping
management types who rely on buzzwords, somewhere does have to
maintain the older machines if they expect to get any benefit of
having a computer period.

>In my country, COBOL is being unused because it causes more problems than
>beneficts to the companies that uses it. It is true that COBOL is a very
>strong language which is very difficult to break down, but it is a language
>that was designed and wrote in remote years ago and it is not very
>flexible...

i don't know what flexibility means. if you mean the tools and source
code are expensive, that's a given. but you have to accept that on
other languages also.

>I am a Management Systems Technical and my opinion is not the one. In my
>department all personal think like me.

does your department write the software or someone else? if the answer
is the latter, your opinion counts for jack shit.


reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Has COBOL being unused?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395AC962.E45C8004@perfectcircle.com>`
- **References:** `<4jK15.31115$Vt5.172677@telenews.teleline.es> <gg6qks4edr2uinfrt8r1h3c3i7gnehshln@4ax.com> <39575449.36DE1ED8@cusys.edu> <39594fb5.18675073@news1.frb.gov>`

```
Hello All:
    This has been an interesting thread to read.  Many familiar names of
people I haven't seen for some time.  Some good laughs, even the fish
stories.

    We old time Cobolers can learn something from this young man.  He is
victim of and educational system that fails to teach computer system
fundamentals, data management, and business system design.  Probably, his
school projects did not require error handling, indexed data base access,
complex screens, or reasonable reports.  This young man has probably never
heard the term "Program Life Cycle", or maintained a program he wrote last
year, yet alone 20 years ago.  By any of our standards, this person is not a
"Professional Developer".

    But ... This young man probably knows more about computers than his
boss, and therefor is the person directing his company's IT directions.  He
is inundated with ads and articles regarding C/C++, Java, and the other
"hot" languages.  What does he see from the Cobol community to change his
perception of Cobol?  The Java community's PR machine says "java is the
language of the future".  He needs to hear from the Cobol community.  He
needs to hear "clear" is better than "clever" (thank you Jerry Garfunkle),
especially when developing business applications.

    There are hundreds of young, misguided, data processing "professionals?"
working in our industry.  They need to be educated as to why Cobol is such a
strong business application development platform.  How can we pick up the
torch and educate them?

    Fred Young, Perfect Circle Solutions




WDS wrote:

> On Mon, 26 Jun 2000 07:02:01 -0600, Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> they are "specialists" in the one language.
```

small portion of the big picture, it's the picture currently in focus.

We can hel educate by sending COBOL "to the web" success stories to
Ken Foskey.  A group of programmers I know is trying to get these
stores reported on in the general press.  Doing so lifts visibility
and acceptence.  See Ken's post in this group -- and send your
stories!

### OT: IBM specific question

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com>`

```
Does anyone know of a method/utility/product that can scan a loadlib
and report on the content of the PDS?  This utility also needs to be able
to locate and report the compile date of each member.

Other items that might be useful information would be the internal 'Program
Name'
and identify the primary compile language (PL/1, COBOL, C, ASM, .....)

I have already collected a sample from this newsgroup that is able to 
get the member names of a PDS.   The next piece that I am looking for in
particular is for the ability to change the DSN name associated to a DDname
so that the program can open and read each of the members to get the 
internal details for reporting.  

Any help/pointers to OS\390 manuals would be greatly appreciated.
I would need the Manual Titles in order to find the same documents 
on my set of reference CDs.   

Thanks in advance for all your help.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM specific question

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39497b83.86586695@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com> <8i9mcn$795c$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I use this IBM utility for reporting the Link-edit date/time of
specific loadlib members.  I have also written REXX to run this in
foreground and display the results online.


//STEP1    EXEC PGM=AMBLIST                  
//SYSLIB   DD DISP=SHR,DSN=<loadlibname>
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                       
//SYSIN    DD *                              
 LISTIDR MEMBER=<programname>

Pete

<terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:

…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: IBM specific question

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kKe25.5447$td1.2257470@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com>`

```
I just looked in the OS-390 / LE Migration guid :  NO SUCH TOOLS mentioned.
They tell you to gather info on the programs such as 'Compiler options'
Calls to Db2/ IMS/ other Languages, etc.


Go to ISPF 3.4 and browse the PDS:
You will see the AMOD / RMODE

You will find the rest very difficult as the Object module PDS members are
Variable Length Spanned Blocked Records.
The PDS record size is 0, blocksize exceeds one track.

There IS an IBM utility that unblocks object modules into  80 byte
records just for transmission to other systems where it can be
reblocked into load modules.  HOWEVER,  most likely that
 utility is NOT installed on your system AND OR access is very restricted.



Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com...
> Does anyone know of a method/utility/product that can scan a loadlib

'Program
> Name'

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

DDname

#### ↳ Re: OT: IBM specific question

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i9v56$97r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com>,
  sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:
> Does anyone know of a method/utility/product that can scan a loadlib
> and report on the content of the PDS?  This utility also needs to be
able
> to locate and report the compile date of each member.
>
> Other items that might be useful information would be the internal
'Program
> Name'
> and identify the primary compile language (PL/1, COBOL, C, ASM, .....)
>
> I have already collected a sample from this newsgroup that is able to
> get the member names of a PDS.   The next piece that I am looking for
in
> particular is for the ability to change the DSN name associated to a
DDname
> so that the program can open and read each of the members to get the
> internal details for reporting.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My shop uses PDSMAN, a collection of utilities from LEGENT, sold now by
CA. Very pretty tool, but not free.

In your case, you could use AMBLIST to analize the loadmodules. You can
call AMBLIST from your ASSEMBLER, COBOL or PL/I program and interpret
the output. SYSIN and SYSPRINT are '&&files'. Pseudocode:

read first membername of library
while not last membername of library:
  open SYSIN
  write commands to SYSIN
  close SYSIN
  call AMBLIST
  open SYSPRINT
  read SYSPRINT until EOF
  close SYSPRINT
  read next membername of library

AMBLIST is an IBM utility, you find the description in:
OS/390 V2R8.0 MVS Diagnosis: Tools and Service Aids, Document Number
SY28-1085-06

O yes, I forgot: always call utilities with a parameter, even if you
don't need a parameter. It has to be char varying, in COBOL declare it
like:
01 parm-to-utiltiy.
   05 ll pic x(4) binary value zero.
   05 parm-content pic x(100) value spaces.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: IBM specific question

- **From:** armin.horst@gmx.de (Armin Horst)
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39486f44.2862796@news.pop-stuttgart.de>`
- **References:** `<20000614224704.10594.00001922@nso-cd.aol.com>`

```
On 15 Jun 2000 02:47:04 GMT, sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:

>Does anyone know of a method/utility/product that can scan a loadlib
>and report on the content of the PDS?  This utility also needs to be able
>to locate and report the compile date of each member.
try http://members.aol.com/freepds (free)

or Startool from
Serena(http://www.serena.com/html/startool_datasheet.htm), commercial

but a "compile date" for a load module is not available, only a lked
date. you can report on compile date and translator of every CSECT
contained in a load module. 

documentation in IBM Manuals(if you want to write your own tool, and
IMHO, if you want to realize all your requirements, you have to do
this):

DFSMS/MVS V1R4 Program Management(or newer)
  Using the Binder Application Programming Interface
  "Interpreting Binder Output" or "Interpreting Linkage Editor Output"


OS/390 V2R4.0 MVS Diagnosis: Tools and Service Aids(or newer)
 Chapter 15.  AMBLIST

Armin

<a id="t-bf29aa2a71"></a>
## Thread: OT: IBM specific question — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: IBM specific question

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<642A954DD517D411B20C00508BCF23B00127A3AC@mail.sauder.com>`

```
Does anyone know of a method/utility/product that can scan a loadlib
and report on the content of the PDS?  This utility also needs to be able
to locate and report the compile date of each member.

Other items that might be useful information would be the internal 'Program
Name'
and identify the primary compile language (PL/1, COBOL, C, ASM, .....)

I have already collected a sample from this newsgroup that is able to 
get the member names of a PDS.   The next piece that I am looking for in
particular is for the ability to change the DSN name associated to a DDname
so that the program can open and read each of the members to get the 
internal details for reporting.  

Any help/pointers to OS\390 manuals would be greatly appreciated.
I would need the Manual Titles in order to find the same documents 
on my set of reference CDs.   

Thanks in advance for all your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: IBM specific question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394E1C95.29A80844@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<642A954DD517D411B20C00508BCF23B00127A3AC@mail.sauder.com>`

```


Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know of a method/utility/product that can scan a loadlib
> and report on the content of the PDS?  This utility also needs to be able
> to locate and report the compile date of each member.
 
This might be overkill:
//ZHBAMBST   JOB (24981,'NON-ENDEVOR DC COBOL/MVS'),
//             'U56,H BRAZEE',
//             MSGCLASS=W,CLASS=N,    WHITE PAPER
//             NOTIFY=D44201
/*ROUTE PRINT R0010
//******** LOOK AT LOAD MODULE ***************
//STEP1    EXEC PGM=AMBLIST
//SYSLIB   DD DISP=SHR,DSN=UMS.TEST.LOAD
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSIN    DD *
 LISTIDR
//
 LISTIDR MEMBER=SIPR702
```

<a id="t-fd4588f75b"></a>
## Thread: IPP (Internet Printing Protocol) — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### IPP (Internet Printing Protocol)

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10515b4a.f97f0c01@usw-ex0102-084.remarq.com>`

```
This relates to an e-mail thread posted earlier, but with a
different angle. Problem:

Client wants WEB form results to print on office printer.

Environment: Client has web site located at ISP on Windows NT
machine. Client has Windows98 PC in office with a dial-up web
connection.

Has anyone used IPP to print WEB form results using internet
printing. This may be right up FLEXUS' alley, I will leave that
for Bob to respond. I know HP, IBM, Lexmark and Xerox support
IPP, but what is involved (that is, does the client need to have
T1, DSL or HDSL, etc.)?

If someone has written software for this, do you have an example
of your COBOL cgi? I would think it would be as simple as
opening a printer for output, writing to it and closing.

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: IPP (Internet Printing Protocol)

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3947fd8f.268537059@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<10515b4a.f97f0c01@usw-ex0102-084.remarq.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 Jun 2000 14:19:55 -0700, Rich Rohde
<richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid> wrote:


>If someone has written software for this, do you have an example
>of your COBOL cgi? I would think it would be as simple as
>opening a printer for output, writing to it and closing.
>

I'm not Bob, but I know that COBOLForm Print Thin Client supports this
sort of printing -- and the mainline application need not be an Sp2
application.

#### ↳ Re: IPP (Internet Printing Protocol)

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3948da83.3582732@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<10515b4a.f97f0c01@usw-ex0102-084.remarq.com>`

```
Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid> wrote:

>This relates to an e-mail thread posted earlier, but with a
>different angle. Problem:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>T1, DSL or HDSL, etc.)?

Rich:

We don't support IPP directly, but because you currently use
FormPrint, you could use our Thin Client product to print directly
from the COBOL program across the Internet in one of two ways:

1.  As a stand-alone thin client/server program (an Internet program
which does NOT use a Web Browser)....or

2.  With the Web Browser Plug-In, you can print FormPrint forms
generated by the COBOL program on the server to the client machine (on
any installed printing device on the client machine).  The printing
request is made directly from the web page using an <embed> HTML tag.

Give me a call or send private e-mail and I'll provide more details so
as not to clog the newsgroup with my shameless commercial messages!

;-)

>If someone has written software for this, do you have an example
>of your COBOL cgi? I would think it would be as simple as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-6615ba80d8"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RE: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i8ngu$dig$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My email and the reply related to this earlier thread:

"Bill,

Thanks for your thoughts on clarifying Fujitsu's position on run-time
licenses. Yes, Fujitsu does charge for traditional run-time licenses in
Japan. But we have distinctly chosen NOT to implement a similar model in the
U.S. We stand behind our belief that COBOL programmers deserve a reasonably
priced COBOL compiler with royalty free run-times.

At the same time, we have implemented some valuable technology in Version 5
and feel it is appropriate to charge a small fee for these new innovations.
Users may OPTIONALLY purchase the multi-threaded run-time license for
enterprise applications that require higher performance. The Fujitsu COBOL
development system will ship with both the single (FREE) and multi-threaded
run-time systems to allow users to develop and test under either run-time
with no additional run-time requirements. Developers can easily invoke the
multi-threaded run-time system for development and testing with a simple
run-time switch - no additional configuration is required to switch between
the single threaded and multi-threaded run-time systems. If warranted, users
can then decide if they want to purchase the multi-threaded run-time.

Hope this helps!

Ron Langer
P.S. You can post this answer to COMP.LANG.COBOL
-----Original Message-----
From: William M. Klein [mailto:wmklein@ix.netcom.com]
Sent: Friday, June 09, 2000 4:08 PM
To: cobol@adtools.com; Ron Langer
Subject: Re: Fujitsu COBOL - A Restatement of Our Long-term Commitment to
COBOL

Ron,
  The one thing that this note does not address is the statement (earlier)
in
the thread that Fujitsu *does* charge for its (traditional) run-time in
Japan.  This was mentioned in relationship to "if they do it somewhere now,
they may do it in the US later".
  "
```

```


WOB Consulting wrote:
> 
> In article <39492C6F.A677B796@home.com>,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think they'd blow a fuse if I asked. As you'll see to reply to Thane I
was hoping maybe I could hijack some Java routines. I don't suppose it
would be that difficult to convert, once I understood Java syntax and
data types - some is easy, (I'm cheating from a COBOL book) :-

package java4cobol;
	public class ErrorMsg {
		public string msgtTxt;
		public int    msgSize
			....
		// some logic
			...
		//This is a comment line
	 }

Becomes, sort of :-

	*>----------------------------------------------

	Class-id.	java4cobol inherits from Base.	

	Class-control.	java4cobol	is class "java4cobol"
	
	*>--------
	FACTORY.
	*>-------
	END FACTORY.			
	*>--------
	OBJECT.
	*>---------------
	Method-id. "ErrorMsg".
	*>---------------
	Local-storage section.
	01 msgTxt	pic x(  ).
	01 msgSize	pic 9(  ).
	Procedure DIvision.
			...
	*> some logic
			...
	*> This is a comment line
	Exit Method
	End Method "ErrorMsg".
	*>---------------------
	END OBJECT
	END CLASS java4cobol.
 	*>---------------------------

I don't think the conversion would be particularly complex, just knowing
Java/C-type rules and data type formats, and many of my COBOL generated
lines being hard-coded literlas.

Jimmy
```

Jimmy,

Net Express 3.1 has the ability to make COBOL classes accessible
from Java and vice versa.  It also automatically converts data types
between the two languages.

This facility removes the need to convert your COBOL in to Java.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International

Paddy Coleman wrote:
> 
> Jimmy,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Paddy,

Many thanks for reply. Whoaaa ! I aint convertin' nuthin' from COBOL to
Java - I just commented that there were such routines <G>

I was ONLY interested in if there were 'converts from Java to COBOL' on
the basis that there might be the odd Java library routine that I could
code in ENGLISH ( i.e. COBOL ) and create my own OO COBOL class - would
then negate the need to call Java libraries. As yet my application is
for a desktop and doesn't use the Net - hence no need for a link to Java
or anything else at the current time.

As an example - Currently data is in (a) Imperial - input, storage,
calculating and printing but system has option for (b) Metric - but
still storing data in Imperial. OK, so at the current time it's no big
deal, I have methods which will convert :-

       Method-id. "imp-to-met". ) These account for over 90% of
       Method-id. "met-to-imp". ) any converts 
       Method-id. "c-to-f".
       Method-id. "f-to-c".
       Method-id. "kpa-to-psi".
       Method-id. "psi-to-kpa".

Following on that theme, as we, (the COBOL community), as yet don't have
much in the way of general business libraries for COBOL (either
structured or OO), I thought there might just be the odd Java library of
interest, where I could 'hijack' the code and include it in COBOL -
rather than making calls to Java libraries.

Here's an unsolicited plug. My application uses SOLELY NetExpress COBOL
:-

- Dialog Editor, OO & OO GUIs
- Flat files
- PC_PRINT

I'm delighted with N/E, (Caveat, not that bloody end-user fee <G>).
And there isn't a line of code that goes outside of N/E COBOL.

Ahhhh ! After the accolade, there's always a catch, and Steve Biggs will
grin when he reads this - yet another plug - give me examples on your
site for OO/GUIs - specifically the old ADMOUSE.CBL and some of the neat
stuff Petzold does in his Windows book - i.e., examples creating dynamic
dialogs, which you guys have done over and over again to create the N/E
IDE.

Again, many thanks

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java to COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jg3tr$gq6$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<39492C6F.A677B796@home.com> <hg7qkscc5biad7vploabhsdr3kqk1cjgbt@4ax.com> <3954FEC7.373D5AED@home.com> <3959F559.9D850312@netscape.net> <395a2941.226387605@207.126.101.100> <395B0B8D.49AB2686@merant.com> <395b74e7.311068068@207.126.101.100>`

```
thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>Sorry I was unclear.  I wanted to knw when an COBOL ap was wrapped in
>an Enterprise Java Bean (Man, I feel like I'm describing a burrito!)
>where the COBOL runtime is?  Suppose the server is an IBM mainframe.
>How does the Merant COBOL runtime work when the COBOL application is
>wrapped in a EJB?

The bean makes use of MERANT's mfcobol.runtime (Java) class, which loads 
the COBOL run-time when it is required. When the bean is called, it makes 
calls to the COBOL application via Java native methods, implemented as 
part of the COBOL run-time; the run-time does the parameter translation, 
such as Unicode to pic x, to/from the COBOL application.

The COBOL application can also make calls back, either via JNI or via the 
OO Java domain (which does all of the parameter translation again, and 
means that it looks like a "normal" OO COBOL program) on Java objects, 
classes or other EJBs (if it wishes).

To the Java programmer, everything still looks like Java; to the COBOL 
programmer, everything still looks like COBOL.

Net Express doesn't run on a mainframe, but you can obviously call the 
mainframe from your PC COBOL. At some point we hope to be releasing Unix 
versions of the same support (using the same mfcobol jar) for Server 
Express.

Hope that's clear!

Steve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hex calculator

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Jynx <X@Y.Z.net> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> God, I just hate it when someone is whimsical at my expense.

X'A' squared in hexadecimal is X'64' and I did it in my head.  

But it's a trick question.  X'A' is decimal 10, and 10 * 10 = 100. 
Everybody knows decimal 100 is hex 64, right?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hex calculator

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SOL45.6439$uf2.155094@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<39498133$0$49234$392904a7@news.execpc.com> <slrn8kkksc.vn8.X@animas.frontier.net> <gOC25.353$S53.79945@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
"Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> An IBM SE once told me that. I said: "OK, smartass, what's A times A
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> God, I just hate it when someone is whimsical at my expense.

Back when I was young (and still had a memory), one evening I was
browsing through the CRC Handbook mathematics section, and casually
examined a few of the common constants there.  The next day at lunch,
one of my friends, prompted by a contest in a programmer's magazine,
asked "Do you know the value of 100! ?"  I said "Sure, 9.33262 times
10 to the 157th power."  My friend was totally stunned for about 30
seconds.  By sheer cooincidence, the value had stuck in my memory
from the previous evening.  Did wonders for my reputation. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hex calculator

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** glass2@glass2.lexington.ibm.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j06rj$ops$1@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<39498133$0$49234$392904a7@news.execpc.com> <slrn8kkksc.vn8.X@animas.frontier.net> <gOC25.353$S53.79945@nnrp1.sbc.net> <SOL45.6439$uf2.155094@news4.mia>`

```
In <SOL45.6439$uf2.155094@news4.mia>, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> writes:
>"Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

Of course, 9.33262E157 is just an approximation.  The correct answer is:

93326215443944152681699238856266700490715968264381621
46859296389521759999322991560894146397615651828625369
7920827223758251185210916864000000000000000000000000

Or, to calculate it yourself, use Rexx:

/* Rexx */
Numeric digits 1000;
Factorial=1;
Do I=100 to 1 by -1;
Factorial=Factorial*I;
End;
Say Factorial;
Exit;

Dave

P.S. Standard Disclaimer: I work for them, but I don't speak for
them.
```

This sounds very much like the two train travelers riding through the
Colorado foothills when they pass a huge feedlot filled with beef cattle.
Just about all you could see for miles was cattle.  One spoke up and said,
"I wonder how many cows are out there."  The other spoke up, "That's easy.
There are 172,847 all total."  "That's amazing." the first fellow said, "How
do you know that?"  The second fellow responded, "I simply counted their
legs and divided by 4."

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hex calculator

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394E1B09.68795BF9@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<39498133$0$49234$392904a7@news.execpc.com> <iPC25.354$S53.80032@nnrp1.sbc.net> <8ieupp$4fq4$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <8iftqd$a4f$1@news.igs.net> <92513E752B6EF5F1.90D10B1B24AC2C0F.4005077FB4CD98C8@lp.airnews.net>`

```
When the calculators I liked seemed to stop being available I found a
Casio fx-410 with a bunch of extra buttons in the fold over cover.  I am
very careful not to lose this as it is very useful.

I can't figure out why good calculators are so hard to find.  Maybe the
money's in the palm tops.  Maybe they figure we don't need to calculate
when we're not connected to a computer.

Actually, I have liked it when I have worked with desktop printing
calculators.  If I have to add up a bunch of numbers, I usually do it by
hand instead of using my calculator, as I am more accurate in adding
than I am in entering the numbers.  With a printed copy, I can double
check my data entry.  I can set up my windows calculator to tape mode as
well.

I have also cut and pasted numbers from listings into my programmer's
editor and used a feature it has to allow me to do arithmetic with
selected data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hex calculator

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<coM45.6484$uf2.156125@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<39498133$0$49234$392904a7@news.execpc.com> <8iftqd$a4f$1@news.igs.net> <92513E752B6EF5F1.90D10B1B24AC2C0F.4005077FB4CD98C8@lp.airnews.net> <394E1B09.68795BF9@cusys.edu> <Yop35.6110$HD6.188027@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>Actually, I have liked it when I have worked with desktop printing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>well.

I use my HP48GX for that.  Though it is a handheld (not quite 'pocket',
maybe coat pocket) calculator, it has an infrared printer.  I enter all
the numbers in the 'stack', then print them on the printer.  I double
check the printout, correcting any errors.  When they are correct, I
simply all them all.  Very efficient and accurate, little wasted effort.
A few years ago my taxes for three years were audited.  They found not
a single arithmetic error.  I love RPN. :-)
```

I've got a Casio fx-991n that I've used since forever and still use.  I was
just curious as to whether something wasn't available for those times when
my hand-held wasn't around.  I appreciate all the responses, even those that
insinuated I and my co-workers were something akin to jerks for not knowing
about the scientific view of the windows accessory calculator.  My only
other comment is:  Boy!  It sure doesn't take much to get us old-timers to
wax nostalgic!
```

Has anyone noticed how hard hex calculators are to find lately?  They used
to be very prevalent, but when I went shopping for one last year, I could
only find two that had DEC, HEX, OCT, and BIN capabilities.  I got the
TI-36X Solar.

Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:iPC25.354$S53.80032@nnrp1.sbc.net...
> For less than $20 you can buy a Texas Instruments scientific pocket
> calculator. Then you can use it even if you're NOT connected to the
> mainframe.
```

```
I have been using a Radio Shack EC-4075 "Programmer's hex & time" calculator
for well over ten years.  No problem with it and it came in handy many times
for estimating when long print jobs would finish.

Terry Heinze wrote:

> Has anyone noticed how hard hex calculators are to find lately?  They used
> to be very prevalent, but when I went shopping for one last year, I could
> only find two that had DEC, HEX, OCT, and BIN capabilities.  I got the
> TI-36X Solar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hex calculator

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UhM45.6473$uf2.155973@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<39498133$0$49234$392904a7@news.execpc.com> <iPC25.354$S53.80032@nnrp1.sbc.net> <642A954DD517D411B20C00508BCF23B00127A4F0@mail.sauder.com>`

```
"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:
>
> Has anyone noticed how hard hex calculators are to find lately?  They used
> to be very prevalent, but when I went shopping for one last year, I could
> only find two that had DEC, HEX, OCT, and BIN capabilities.  I got the
> TI-36X Solar.

Has anyone noticed how difficult it is getting to find RPN calculators
anymore?  It is quite easy to demonstrate that RPN calculators are more
efficient and flexible than algebraic calculators.  A person using an
RPN calculator can always equal or beat an equally skilled person using
an algebraic calculator.  RPN never takes more keystrokes than algrbraic,
and often takes fewer keystrokes, to do the same operation.  Programming
using algebraic logic is extremely clumsy, compared to programming using
RPN logic.  And you never have a 'pending' operation.  Unfortunately,
most people today are too lazy to expend the little extra effort to master
RPN.  Hewlett-Packard used to make a whole series of RPN calculators, but
now they have dwindled to a only two or three.  My favorite is the 48GX,
which can do algebraic or RPN (and a zillion other things).  Fortunately,
I have a good supply of them, and they last a long time.  My HP15C
Scientific and HP16C Programmer's calculators still look and work like
new, even after 18 years.  Their sibling, the HP12C Financial calculator,
is so good that HP still sells them, even though HP has several very
powerful newer models.

Anyone who is unfamiliar with RPN and wants to play with it, can download
BIGCALC.ZIP from my website below.  BIGCALC is an RPN scientific calculator
with up to 1075 digit precision, written for fun.
```

Dave

Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com> wrote in message
news:39498133$0$49234$392904a7@news.execpc.com...
> One of the accessories with windows is the little pop-up calculator.
> Does any know of, or where I can get, a pop-up hex calculator?  I
> think it would come in handy while I'm logged on to the mainframe.
> Thanks.

The NT 4.0 system I'm using has a calculator under Start/Programs/Accessories/.
If you switch to SCINTIFIC mode you will be able to choose Hex, Octal, or even
Binary rather than the Decimal default.
```

View/Scientific.

In article <394ad1f8$0$94745$726baab@news.execpc.com>,
Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com> wrote:
>After receiving all the responses, I did feel rather foolish -- until I

So one might feel less foolish if one is surrounded by fools... hmmmm...
reminds me of something I posted to Mr Hamasaki's Mainframe Jobs
Newsletter:

--begin quoted text:
'We couldn't find these folks... and we were offering US$n/year!'

'US$n/year is a miserable pay-rate for the skills you want.'

'What are you talking about... they would've been the highest-paid folks
in our shop!'

... leading to a conclusion of 'Well, everyone in your shop seems to be
satisfied with less than a miserable pay-rate.'

(This is similar to a consultant/pimp exchange of:

'US$n/hr?  *Nobody* on this site gets that much!'

'Nobody on this site has the skills you want me to use... might we here
posit a cause-and-effect relationship?')

--end quoted text

DD

```
On Thu, 15 Jun 2000 20:21:17 -0500, "Dave Polzin" <dlpolzin@coredcs.com> wrote:

>One of the accessories with windows is the little pop-up calculator.  Does
>any know of, or where I can get, a pop-up hex calculator?  I think it would
>come in handy while I'm logged on to the mainframe.  Thanks.
>

I actually have a little hex calculator written in REXX that runs
from ISPF, if you're interested. It does addition, subtraction,
multiplication, division, AND, OR, and XOR on the two numbers
which you enter.

You can run it completely from the command line, in which case the
answer shows up in the ISPF short message line, or you can work
within a little pop up ISPF panel that is provided.

It's a simple little gadget, but I find it useful.

email me directly if you're interested.
```

<a id="t-f4a7ae6776"></a>
## Thread: Microsoft (OT) — 6 msgs

### Re: Microsoft (OT)

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ic0jf01d2b@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<393ED035.78397140@worldnet.att.net> <wfq05.359$Oq2.118732@nnrp1.sbc.net> <8htivr$5pv$1@news.igs.net> <AA1D7E30217A402F.CDCF8528CF3434AA.56277D017EF5C644@lp.airnews.net> <50h5ksk4stvmkub3jrh45ea45ej7c9si4j@4ax.com> <566DF13F92F4BCD4.5EAFBD6CF3126E46.CE53F60A371B04AE@lp.airnews.net> <9QR05.8141$613.376814@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
I have used Netscape since v1.2 and have never paid for it.

The only reason Microsoft is even still around is the fact that they got
into the BUSINESS world early.  While everyone else concentrated on home use
and education.  If, for example, Apple Computer had concentrated more on
business than on education and home use, then I'm sure we'd all be using
Macs right now.  Instead, poorly-managed Apple innovated while others waited
and imitated.  The mouse, the cd-rom drive, the desktop, etc.  Now Steve
Jobs is back as (still) interim CEO and Apple continues to innovate but at a
much smaller market.  Meanwhile Billy is rich.

Businesses have demanded exponentially more and more stuff, compared to
other environments.  Schools can get any kind of computer they want (it's
the government's money).  Home users have a choice too (when one is
'obselete', most people just trash it and buy a new one), although they
usually end up buying the same kind.

It's not easy for a business to throw away millions of dollars of equipment,
software, and training to go to another platform.  Therefore, businesses,
over time, have essentially gotten stuck using Windows.  Simply because it
was there first, not because it's better.

Windows is about the worst operating system ever made.  Period.  It is less
intuitive in every way then even DOS.  When I saw Windows 3.1 the first
time, I nearly pissed myself laughing.  If our mainframe was 100 times more
reliable then our best Windows applications, our business would crumble is
less than a day.

After using many other operating systems over the years, I cannot understand
why people continue to think Windows is the best.  Almost every feature of
the Windows operating system (except maybe the Start menu) is a direct
rip-off of some predecessor, albeit executed at about 10% of the
effectiveness.

Ever wondered why those whiz-bang MCSE people make so much money.  Because
the software is that bad, and that hard to fix.  Supply is lower than
demand.  Stop kidding yourself.

I know very little about Linux, but I think this is the future.  It is
immature but with the help of many programmers could become a robust ticket
for the future.

I am using Outlook Express right now, and use Internet Explorer, by choice,
also.  Only because they are better products.  I am not an idiot; I know and
use a better package when I see it.

It is unfortunate that the computer industry is so stagnant and uninspiring
these days....

Jeff
```

Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> After using many other operating systems over the years, I cannot understand
> why people continue to think Windows is the best.

They think it is the best for their needs/wants because they believe
other operating systems don't run the combination of programs which they
require/want.  

And if an OS won't run the program you want, it is not the best OS for
that program.

Also, if an OS is good enough - and new employees have already been
trained to use it, that OS may be the best OS for your job.

We have some computers we use to run Edify (voice response).  Edify has
stopped supporting that version which was designed to run on OS/2.  So
we will be downgrading the OS and upgrading the programs in the next few
months.
```

<a id="t-e8a16964f1"></a>
## Thread: *COMMENTS in COBOL — 12 this month / 16 total

### *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
I am wrapping up the development and testing of a new batch job I just 
wrote.  About 15,000 lines of code.  After finishing the job, I realized
that most of the code I left uncommented.

Now, I usually write a moderate amount of comments in my programs, but I
simply don't have time to go back and spend time writing comments and doing
library migrations.  I feel, IMHO, that my time is better spent doing
additional testing.

Here is basically what I did:
1) All programs have at least 2 or 3 sentences in the beginning of the
program describing overall functionality.

2) I spent enough time coming up with good paragraph and variable naming.

3) Tricky sections, like when I merge 2 two dimensional tables, I write
inline comments in the code, like this:

***--->>> INIT TABLE.
    XHGUIGHREIGUERHGURIG.
    GJIOGRJGOIREGJEROGPJGE.

***--->>> READ EACH RECORD IN THE CURRENT BLOCK.
    IOJGOIERGJROGREJGOGOPE
    JIOGJOIGJEROIG ( GJOIGRJEGOIRJG  )
        GJERIOGREJGOEIGJEROIGREJGOG TO JIGJREIGJRGERIG.

***--->>> AND CHECK TO SEE IF IT IS ELIGIBLE.
        IF GREGJREOIGG = GREJGIOREGJROIGRJO
            JGIROEJGOEIRGJOGJ
         ELSE
             OIGRJGOIRJGOIERG
        END-IF

***--->>> IF IT WAS ELIGIBLE, BUILD THE KEY ON THE NEW TABLE.
        JOIRJGORGJOGERJO
            JIOGJROGIREJGOEIRJ
            JOIJOIJOGIREJOIGERJG

***--->>> AND ROLL THE DATA OVER FROM THE CORRESPONDING
***--->>> WORK TABLE.
            GRGRGGREKGPOGK
            GREGKERPOGREKGPO TO GRGRGREGER (GRGG , GRERGR )
        END-IF

***--->>> KEEP GOING UNTIL OUT OF RECORDS IN BLOCK.
    END-OIPOGRKGJPOGKER.


My point is, that for the most part, I kept comments to a minimum, only to
describe tough sections.  I think there are two main problems with comments:

1) They become out of date.
2) They become inaccurate.

I would like to know what your opinions on this are.
How many comments do you normally write?  What are your feelings?  Remember,
in the business world, time is money, and I don't feel like spending more
than my usual 9-10 hours at work every day.

Should I go back and comment the code?  Isn't testing the consumer's product
a better idea?

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wCx25.3631$NB4.319883@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Why don't you test your documentation and comments?  Think of  a
modification the user is not too unlikely to ask for, give the code and all
other user and technical documentation you have produced to another
programmer, and ask if the new programmer could make the modification.

If he can, the documentation and comments are adequate. If he can't they
ain't.
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SKC25.351$S53.79299@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com
>
> Here is basically what I did:
> 1) All programs have at least 2 or 3 sentences in the beginning of
the
> program describing overall functionality.

Why? There are those who would say comments are unnecessary to a
sufficiently astute programmer.

>
> 2) I spent enough time coming up with good paragraph and variable
naming.

Again, if the program is to be reviewed by a sufficiently expert
programmer, just use sequential numbers - or random numbers - for both
paragraph and variable names.

<snip>

>
> My point is, that for the most part, I kept comments to a minimum,
only to
> describe tough sections.  I think there are two main problems with
comments:
>
> 1) They become out of date.
> 2) They become inaccurate.

I don't understand. Do comments become dated or inaccurate due to
'bit-rot,' or because a maintenance programmer was not sufficiently
diligent?

>
> I would like to know what your opinions on this are.
> How many comments do you normally write?  What are your feelings?
Remember,
> in the business world, time is money, and I don't feel like spending
more
> than my usual 9-10 hours at work every day.

If a programming project is defined as being complete only when the
code is sufficiently documented to facilitate ease of maintenance,
then the programming is not finished until the documentation and
commenting is complete. If the project's completion is defined as
delivery of a working product, then don't comment.

>
> Should I go back and comment the code?  Isn't testing the consumer's
product
> a better idea?

Sigh. A devil's choice. Remember the maxim: "We never have time to do
it right, but we always make time to do it over."
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iccrl$cuek$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```

I also agree.  Well chosen field names and paragraph/section names
eliminate, for the most part, the need for comments.  I do make exceptions,
like you, for code that is a bit tricky when first looking at it.  Comments
are nice initially, but I have rarely seen programmers update comments to
agree with the coding changes they've made, and misleading comments are
worse than no comments at all, in my opinion.

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3949B8E8.9F39DF72@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I am wrapping up the development and testing of a new batch job I just
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

Jeff,

We are going to go around in circles on this one because it has been
beaten to death before. From what you have written, from my perspective
it is more than adequate - in fact I wouldn't even bother with your
comments like :-

> ***--->>> AND CHECK TO SEE IF IT IS ELIGIBLE.

Given that you've used meaningful names and with a combination of the
clarity from the COBOL language, it just isn't necessary.

I keep my comments to an absolute minimum, sometimes with an opening
paragraph (essay style not program paragraph) explaining what this
program is doing - so that when I come back to it, my memory is
refreshed.

Now I know I'm writing SOLELY for myself, but I believe, given that
opening paragraph, (when considered necessary), and the other things you
have factored in, that should also be adequate for a medium to large
sized shop.

Just having returned from the last two days of J4 meetings in Newbury, I
found their stuff wallowing in detail and literally you are drowning in
a sea of words. Same goes with programming - overload the thing with too
much comment detail, and your objective, clarity, will be lost.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000616081618.02791.00001927@nso-da.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
In article <8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>, "Jeff Baynard"
<union27@macconnect.com> writes:

>
>Here is basically what I did:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It would seem that with meaningful paragraph and variable names,
the short comments should be all that is needed to get the general
idea of things.

You can never spend too much time in testing.   There is always going
to be that odd-ball set of circumstances that will find a chink in the armor
of your program.
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394A2A7E.EE0C6D39@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Why "go back" to make these comments?  At this level, it would be useful
to develop the habit of putting them in as you code.  You can even skip
code with such a comment and get back to it once you are finished with
the routine which clarifies how you want to make that test.

If the documentation is at a simple level like this, it will be useful
for the original coder's thought processes as well as for maintenance
programmers.


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> ***--->>> READ EACH RECORD IN THE CURRENT BLOCK.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>     END-OIPOGRKGJPOGKER.

...

> Should I go back and comment the code?  Isn't testing the consumer's product
> a better idea?
> 
> Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** rob.morsley@virgin.nospam.net
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394bec4e.16001453@news.virgin.net>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
As an infrequent poster here (mainly lurking!) I hate to risk the
upsetting anyone, but I do (frequently) have these discussions with my
colleagues at work. 

True, too many comments can be unwieldy, but too few few can be a real
ball-ache, especially at 3am. Sometimes looking at a bit of code I
just want to cry out to the programmer 'WHY - What is this bit REALLY
here for'. 

It is, of course, poetic justice if it's your own code you looking at,
but usually it's someone who left the company 2 years ago.... 

Maybe for the original design it's not SO important (I don't really
mean that, can't you tell?), but once there's changes to done it does
become useful (IMHO).

As far as testing is concerned, of course it's important. Crucial
even. You did properly estimate the job, didn't you, so you had
sufficient time to code+comment, and to test, and lets not forget
document as well! I find that the people who complain most about
realistic estimates are those who also complain the most if a bug
('mistake' to use the proper term) slips through because you've been
pushed to compromise on time!

<Rant Off> Thankfully I don't get called too much now, it's mainly the
younger folk who do it - OUCH, I think I just hurt myself!

##### ↳ ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000617193353.28970.00003602@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<394bec4e.16001453@news.virgin.net>`

```
Comments are falling out of use because few people use them intelligently.

In COBOL, comments should NEVER tell you WHAT the code is doing, it should tell
you WHY the code is doing it.

Here is an example of a BAD Cobol comment:

* Reject all type "Q" transactions and set
* message flag "F" to true.

Here is an example of a GOOD comment:

* A type "Q" item is now blocked
* because that service is no longer sold
* to PDQ customers.  We continue with 
* the transaction to process item types "P"
* and "D"; 
* the "F" flag gets set to trigger a
* "Sorry, Service Q NLA" message on the
* customer reply./NAM 06/17/00

Ideally, I like to paste in the Requirements/Func Specs and then code between
them.
Of course, often the Specs aren't logical enough to do that!

In Assembler or TAL or "C" I also like to see comments that say things like:

! I'm doing a bit shift here instead of 
! division

Because there one operation can be used for different purposes. 


>As an infrequent poster here (mainly lurking!) I hate to risk the
>upsetting anyone, but I do (frequently) have these discussions with my
…[91 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Jeff

Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

#### ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ic96m$3fj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
I agree with you, and seldom comment code unless it is very tricky. The
choosing of data names and paragraph names is vital to readability, and if
done well, should do the trick.  I also think that the methodology is
important ... for example, I seldom do inline performs.  The addition of the
paragraph name *is* documentation, and with modern compilers, make almost no
difference to runtimes.

…[48 more quoted lines elided]…

comments:

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>How many comments do you normally write?  What are your feelings?
Remember,
>in the business world, time is money, and I don't feel like spending more
>than my usual 9-10 hours at work every day.

product
>a better idea?

##### ↳ ↳ Re: *COMMENTS in COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394A2911.B79CB888@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8ic2uj0q4k@enews4.newsguy.com> <8ic96m$3fj$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> I agree with you, and seldom comment code unless it is very tricky. The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> difference to runtimes.

I don't comment WHAT I am doing - I comment WHY I am doing it.  Good
coding practices should make the WHAT obvious.  But someone debugging in
the middle of the night will often see a way to make the program run
without really understanding the business needs.  We don't want the
program to just run, we want it to produce the correct results.

The most dangerous bugs are those which we don't know about.
```

## Thread: Sending data across network — 22 msgs

### Sending data across network

- **From:** "Servizio Tecnico" <software@gescart.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ia2sp$p06$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
Hi,
anybody know the API to send and receive a string from PC to another across
the network.

For example:

my PC is 120.120.120.1 and another PC is 198.222.234.4. I must send the
string "Hello" from my PC to second and wait the acknowledgement.


Have you a simple source?
Thanks in advance.
Roberto
```

```

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Roberto

Look up sockets.   Thane has some cobol examples on his site from
memory.

One PC accepts the socket (server) and the other creates the link
using sockets (client).

Thanks

```
Yes is true for the rs232 communication but this PLC is particular. The PLC
is connected with a converter RS232-TCP/IP because the installation is done
over 15 metres (limit of RS232) from the PC.
The converter is like a client with IP address (xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx) in front of
PC and a NullModem in front of PLC.

With the PING command I see the converter but I don't know the API to
communicate.
Roberto.

the group can give you the correct API call.  I would be tempted, if the
original code worked (sounds like a conversion) to get two of them, and
stick one on the back of the PC as well.  Dirty, but would get existing code
working in a day.

supplier of than code may provide a subroutine library that would be easier

be faced with protocol matching.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sending data across network

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<642A954DD517D411B20C00508BCF23B00127A3F4@mail.sauder.com>`
- **References:** `<8ia2sp$p06$1@news.mclink.it> <8iafsp$qok$1@news.igs.net> <8iak5p$1sh$1@news.mclink.it> <8iaspb$6t0$1@news.igs.net> <8ib04h$6kr$1@news.mclink.it> <Fw7IrM.2xC@news.boeing.com> <8ib7d8$4mm$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A PLC is a programmable logic controller.  It is used to replace relay
driven logic at the plant level.  The normal design for industrial equipment
(like a large asphalt plant for example), is PC to PLC, then the PLC is
interfaced to the actual push buttons, motors, etc.

A PLC normally has I/O that conforms to standard wiring, so you can just
hook in a 660 volt motor starter, for example.  The same thing is true for
various industrial meters and flow control valves, etc.

Programming one is done by ladder diagrams, almost like programming the old
patch boards for unit record equipment, but done on a screen.  The program
is then flash blasted into ROM of some sort.  Communication to a computer is
normally via a serial port, often the same one that you connect a PC to for
programming.

The effect of the entire PLC is to provide manual backup for the system, as
well as a complete industrial interface subsystem. Years ago, when I first
started in this industry, those functions were provided for by huge banks of
industrial relays. The PLC gives you a programmable firmware equivalent.

To run the entire thing by computer, you then ask the PLC to "turn on" a
motor.  The PLC internal programming functions as the equivalent of a
dedicated channel controller, and ensures that you cannot override safety
requirements, for example. (a nice feature, BTW. Before the damned things
were invented, I once managed to get into an infinite loop that *filled* a
truck with the entire contents of a 250,000 ton silo of stone.  We had to
dig it out with a front end loader.  Even though I did ask the driver to get
out before the test, I was razzed about that for years)

Ken Mullins <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> > wrote in
message <8ib7d8$4mm$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>...
>What is a PLC?
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Nope, Not a chance.  Although a died-in-the-wool stoner, I cannot program
stoned. It affects short term memory too much, so I never even try to work
stoned, though I could as I am my own boss.

I have found, that I often get valuable insights when stoned, and often will
light a doobie in the evening to consider a problem, and often come up with
new algorithms then.  However, I always wait until the next day to try to
code.
