- **References:** `<8ia2sp$p06$1@news.mclink.it> <8iafsp$qok$1@news.igs.net> <8iak5p$1sh$1@news.mclink.it> <8iaspb$6t0$1@news.igs.net> <8ib04h$6kr$1@news.mclink.it> <Fw7IrM.2xC@news.boeing.com> <8ib7d8$4mm$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8ib8l5$det$1@news.igs.net> <39496F4A.270FD148@zip.com.au>`

```
You should have seen me trying to stop the thing.  I had designed the system
with a big panic switch, which, when hit, was supposed to cut power to the
interface. The pneumatic controls would then automatically shut the gate,
and stop material movement.  I hit the panic button, and some sonofabitch
had disconnected it.  So I raced to the fuse box.  The fuse room was locked.
The pile was getting higher.  I cut power to the computer (I was debugging
PROMS).  Nothing, the relay was latched on. I was running up and down
stairs, trying to find a key when the last rock emptied out of the silo.
Panic city.  You think a runaway printout is a problem ...


Ken Foskey wrote in message <39496F4A.270FD148@zip.com.au>...
>> To run the entire thing by computer, you then ask the PLC to "turn on" a
>> motor.  The PLC internal programming functions as the equivalent of a
>> dedicated channel controller, and ensures that you cannot override safety
>> requirements, for example. (a nice feature, BTW. Before the damned things
>> were invented, I once managed to get into an infinite loop that *filled*
a
>> truck with the entire contents of a 250,000 ton silo of stone.  We had to
>> dig it out with a front end loader.  Even though I did ask the driver to
get
>> out before the test, I was razzed about that for years)
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

Ralph Wade Phillips wrote in message ...
>Howdy!
>
>Servizio Tecnico wrote in message <8ib04h$6kr$1@news.mclink.it>...
>>Yes is true for the rs232 communication but this PLC is particular. The
PLC
>>is connected with a converter RS232-TCP/IP because the installation is
done
>>over 15 metres (limit of RS232) from the PC.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>        RwP

The line length limits on RS-232-C are also baud rate dependent, as the
primary problem is line capacitance. The 15 meter limit is at 19,200 baud,
lines well shielded.  At 1200 baud, I have driven RS-232   800 metres with
no problem.
```

Socket communication is simple with COBOL.

IBM provides easy examples for the mainframe (EZASOCKET).  Fujitsu has
Socket communications BUILT IN.
For NetExpress and other Windows based COBOL one need only make a few
simple API calls, and examples are available here:

http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/download.htm

Since this IS a COBOL news group and there are no other groups in the
post, I assume the poster desires a COBOL based solution.

Clayton Brendish wrote:
> 
> thank you
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Clayton, post the source - then we will have a clearer idea. First what
you've show above is suspicious :-

	'Open i-o recordname'

You open a filename NOT a record :-

FD CustomerNamesFile.
01 CustomerRecord.
   05 CustomerKey	pic x(07).
   05 CustomerName	pic x(30).
   05 Customer ...something else....
   05 etc.....

	Open i-o CustomerNamesFile

	*> if ws-filestatus <> "00" .... file error routine 
	*> End-if

	move "JOE" to CustomerKey
	read CustomerNamesFile key is CustomerKey
	  invalid key ........ do not found routine
	  not invalid key .... do something with the record
	End-read

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed file

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ic6e5$t2h$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8ibd4a$29ob$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <0d1f72ac.42b3025e@usw-ex0102-084.remarq.com> <8ibme7$2shv$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
If another program has the file locked, then you cannot.  For two programs
to access a file in I/O mode simultaneously, *both* programs have to open in
I/O mode, and the select clause has to specify a lock methodology. That is
regardless of language.

If the other process locks the file, then the file is locked, period.

If your company has converted from MVS to OS/390 and is going or has gone to LE (Language Environment) then IBM probably has supplied them with various tools to look thru the load libraries.  

Off the top of my head I don't remember what they called them but they gave you date compiled, res type (res nores) amode, rmode etc etc.

You may want to check with your sysprogs.

Eileen


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

>If your company has converted from MVS to OS/390 and is going or has gone to
>LE (Language Environment) then IBM probably has supplied them with various

We are on OS\390 and have had LE up and running for the past year.
The compiler and LE options only recently went thru a performance

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: IBM specific question

- **From:** joarmc@SWBELL.NET (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8kivft.ant.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<s948cc09.051@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <20000615193143.15983.00003532@nso-fk.aol.com>`

```
On 15 Jun 2000 23:31:43 GMT, Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote:
	[snip]
>
>We do not have a sysprog.  The contractor that was here did not mention 
>anything about such a utility.   Could you provide a little more info on what 
>I might be looking for under LE?

If you have FileAid from CompuWare, the ISPF portion can tell you probably
all you want to know. It will tell you the link-edit date and the compile
date of all the included programs. It will also tell you the compiler
used (assembler, PL/1, COBOL).

Also, I've heard that Serena has an even better product. 

If you are sysprog-less, you may not know of a really great web site for
downloading a LOT of MVS type programs. It is at http://www.cbttape.org .
Also, if you know mind a fairly active list, you might want to subscribe
to the IBM-MAIN list. This is sort-of like a newsgroup, except that you
get each article emailed to you, or you can get a daily digest. Or you
can just look at the archives at http://bama.ua.edu/archives/ibm-main.html
to see if you like it. If you do not subscribe (it's free!), then you can
read the archives, but you cannot ask questions. The archives are created
monthly. What I'd suggest that you do is go to 

http://bama.ua.edu/archives/ibm-main.html

Now click on "Join or leave the list". From there you can join the list and
set your options (such as digest mode to only get 1 email per day). When
you do join, an email will be sent to you and you must reply to it in order
to be subscribed. I don't know if you really want to join this list. It does
generate a lot of traffic. And sometime we "old-times" go off on weird 
tangents <grin>. But you get people from all over. There are people from
the various vendors, including IBM. Australians, Brits, French, Germans,
Israeli, South Americans (Brazil mainly as I recall), and others.

Just a thought. Hope it is of some use to you. Oh, this really is for
sysprog type questions. There are very few application type questions. 

John
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: IBM specific question

Jeff

<a id="t-2c82612dea"></a>
## Thread: Microsoft (OT) — 1 msgs

### Re: Microsoft (OT)

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ic0ki11d2b@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<393ED035.78397140@worldnet.att.net> <wfq05.359$Oq2.118732@nnrp1.sbc.net> <8htivr$5pv$1@news.igs.net> <AA1D7E30217A402F.CDCF8528CF3434AA.56277D017EF5C644@lp.airnews.net> <50h5ksk4stvmkub3jrh45ea45ej7c9si4j@4ax.com> <566DF13F92F4BCD4.5EAFBD6CF3126E46.CE53F60A371B04AE@lp.airnews.net> <9QR05.8141$613.376814@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
I have used Netscape since v1.2 and have never paid for it.

The only reason Microsoft is even still around is the fact that they got
into the BUSINESS world early.  While everyone else concentrated on home use
and education.  If, for example, Apple Computer had concentrated more on
business than on education and home use, then I'm sure we'd all be using
Macs right now.  Instead, poorly-managed Apple innovated while others waited
and imitated.  The mouse, the cd-rom drive, the desktop, etc.  Now Steve
Jobs is back as (still) interim CEO and Apple continues to innovate but at a
much smaller market.  Meanwhile Billy is rich.

Businesses have demanded exponentially more and more stuff, compared to
other environments.  Schools can get any kind of computer they want (it's
the government's money).  Home users have a choice too (when one is
'obselete', most people just trash it and buy a new one), although they
usually end up buying the same kind.

It's not easy for a business to throw away millions of dollars of equipment,
software, and training to go to another platform.  Therefore, businesses,
over time, have essentially gotten stuck using Windows.  Simply because it
was there first, not because it's better.

Windows is about the worst operating system ever made.  Period.  It is less
intuitive in every way then even DOS.  When I saw Windows 3.1 the first
time, I nearly pissed myself laughing.  If our mainframe was 100 times more
reliable then our best Windows applications, our business would crumble is
less than a day.

After using many other operating systems over the years, I cannot understand
why people continue to think Windows is the best.  Almost every feature of
the Windows operating system (except maybe the Start menu) is a direct
rip-off of some predecessor, albeit executed at about 10% of the
effectiveness.

Ever wondered why those whiz-bang MCSE people make so much money.  Because
the software is that bad, and that hard to fix.  Supply is lower than
demand.  Stop kidding yourself.

I know very little about Linux, but I think this is the future.  It is
immature but with the help of many programmers could become a robust ticket
for the future.

I am using Outlook Express right now, and use Internet Explorer, by choice,
also.  Only because they are better products.  I am not an idiot; I know and
use a better package when I see it.

It is unfortunate that the computer industry is so stagnant and uninspiring
these days....

Jeff


----------

<bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:

> SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-162b13e875"></a>
## Thread: Question about number format — 6 msgs

### Question about number format

- **From:** "Jamie Nordmeyer" <jamie.nordmeyer@mcgnw.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xHs25.851$wh2.41050@sea-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Hi.  I was wondering if one of you might be able to help me with something.
I'm not a COBOL programmer, so I don't know its number formats.  I have a
number 00009843H, and its format is S9(7)V99.  Could somebody please tell me
how to interpret the number and the format?  You can email me at
jamie.nordmeyer@mcgnw.com.  Thanks in advance.

Jamie Nordmeyer
MCG Northwest, LLC.
Systems Department
```

#### ↳ Re: Question about number format

- **From:** PManzari <pmanzariNOpmSPAM@bankofny.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<06284168.474c71b0@usw-ex0109-068.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<xHs25.851$wh2.41050@sea-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Welcome to the world of EBCDIC notation.

In a nutshell, what you have here is a +0000984.38

The "H" that you see translates to "C8" in hexidecimal
notation, the C being the sign of the numeric field.  (C is
+, D is -).  The other numbers that you can clearly see
translate to Fx, where x is either 9 or 8 or 4, etc. in
hexidecimal notation.

S9(7)V99 means that you have 7 bytes to the left of the
decimal point and 2 bytes to the right.  The V is an
implied decimal point and the S indicates that the number
is signed (hence the C8).

Peter Manzari




* Sent from AltaVista http://www.altavista.com Where you can also find related Web Pages, Images, Audios, Videos, News, and Shopping.  Smart is Beautiful
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question about number format

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tFA25.617$tw3.7148@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<xHs25.851$wh2.41050@sea-read.news.verio.net> <06284168.474c71b0@usw-ex0109-068.remarq.com>`

```
Sorry Peter, that is only partly correct and doesn't explain the HOW.

The Picture is PIC S9(7)V99  WITHOUT SIGN IS  TRAILING SEPARATE.

It is called 'OVERPUNCH'.  Whereby the + or - is shown in the same position
as
the Number.   Also, this has NOTHING to do with EBCDIC.

This is accomplished by changing the last digit so:

0 = {
+1 = A
+2 = B
..
+8 = H
+9 = I
-1 = J
-2 = K
..
-9 = R

PManzari <pmanzariNOpmSPAM@bankofny.com.invalid> wrote in message
news:06284168.474c71b0@usw-ex0109-068.remarq.com...
> Welcome to the world of EBCDIC notation.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> * Sent from AltaVista http://www.altavista.com Where you can also find
related Web Pages, Images, Audios, Videos, News, and Shopping.  Smart is
Beautiful
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question about number format

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394CF3AC.403@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<xHs25.851$wh2.41050@sea-read.news.verio.net> <06284168.474c71b0@usw-ex0109-068.remarq.com>`

```
PManzari wrote:
> 
> Welcome to the world of EBCDIC notation.

Not necessarily.  This could be either EBCDIC or ASCII - there is no indication of which 
in this example. It is "zoned decimal" where the last character is a letter to indicate 
the sign.  It started in the days of punch cards, when there was an extra hole punched 
in the last digit position in one of the top two rows of the card to indicate sign, in 
addition to the digit itself being punched in one of the lower ten rows. Since with 
punch cards, two holes also indicated a letter this shows up as a letter in the last 
line.

Although the letter was based on EBCDIC interpretations, the same letters have 
conventionally been used in ASCII representations.

 
> In a nutshell, what you have here is a +0000984.38

Correct.

> S9(7)V99 means that you have 7 bytes to the left of the
> decimal point and 2 bytes to the right.  The V is an
> implied decimal point and the S indicates that the number
> is signed (hence the C8).

Correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question about number format

- **From:** "brucepbarrett" <brucepbarrett@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#jz7B9W2$GA.291@cpmsnbbsa09>`
- **References:** `<xHs25.851$wh2.41050@sea-read.news.verio.net> <06284168.474c71b0@usw-ex0109-068.remarq.com> <394CF3AC.403@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
I think it started with tabulating equipment i.e. tabulating cards where the
low order position LOP (right most) of a field contained the sign (11
Punch).

http://www.cs.uiowa.edu/~jones/cards/codes.html

Incidentally Binary Coded Decimal (BCD) preceded EBCDIC and was used on the
IBM305 RAMAC on which the US Air Force retail supply system ran on until the
later 1960s.  My first computer experience.
http://www.net.org/html/history/detail/1956-ramac.html

I would like to find a copy of the "Code Card" if anyone happens to know of
one.

Hi,

You can edit this field by doing following:

                              Pic Z(9).99 This will not give you the 
signed field. This picture clause will only zero suppress the numberic 
field.

Pic Z(9).99 CR.   This will give you the credit sign for the negative 
values. 

Pic  ---,---,---.99. This will suppress the leading zeros with floating 
minus sign for the negative values.

## Thread: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language] — 20 msgs

### [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au>`

```

I just pulled this off another group.  This is contentious:  The code
is the design.   I very much like the thoughts contained and would
like you opinions.

Please note that the word C++ is defined as 'Common Business Oriented
Language' also know as Cobol :-}

Thanks
Ken Foskey

-------- Original Message --------
From: "Marco Papacchini" <papacchini.marco@tin.it>
Subject: Re: Code as design language
Newsgroups: comp.object

Thanks everybody for your reply.

Following the suggestion of Robert Martin, I read the article
written by Jack Reeves on the subject.
It is one of the most lucid and enrapturing writing about
software development I've ever read.

I post the URL for everyone interested:
http://www.bleading-edge.com/Publications/C++Journal/Cpjour2.htm

Regards,
Marco Papacchini.
```

#### ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au>`

```
I looked at the date.  Not April 1st.   No smilies.  This can't be serious.

A design is the relationship between a requirements/specifications and code.
It should explain why the code exists, in terms of how each fragment of code
supports some part of the specification.

The author of this piece never seems to define a design.  So I suppose he
can declare victory by using undefined terms.   But that's not an reasoned
argument.
Lucid seems like the wrong term.

Example:   (paraphrased from the article)  "if you view code as design,
then software is nearly free because the compilers do all the work".
If you buy that argument, you should be happy to code in assembler,
too, as they "do all the work".

I stopped reading after this point.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ilg6u$lqr$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au> <sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I quite liked the article, and tend to agree with the author very strongly.
Your note regarding assembler misses the point.  The article does not claim
that the tool is irrelevant, but only that the compiler has to be looked at
as a design tool rather than a production tool. The production tool is the
OS.

Ira D. Baxter wrote in message ...
>I looked at the date.  Not April 1st.   No smilies.  This can't be serious.
>
>A design is the relationship between a requirements/specifications and
code.
>It should explain why the code exists, in terms of how each fragment of
code
>supports some part of the specification.
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394E8E2D.A796ACD8@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au> <sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com> <8ilg6u$lqr$1@news.igs.net>`

```
A lot of these ideas are from extreme programming (without any
attribution to Kent Beck that I noticed). 

You can read about XP at xxx.xprogramming.com. Beware - it may
make you think! Kent Beck also has a good book out about XP.

IMO this does capture a fundamental fallacy of current thinking -
the idea that programming (coding) is like manufacturing. Because
the 'designers' cannot think of everything, coders must
(re-)design. So the usual problems occur - the 'design' bears
little relationship to the code and it goes downhill fast.
Another effect is that programmers get treated like process
workers.

Tim Josling

> >
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Marco Papacchini" <papacchini.marco@tin.it>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8io21a$e25$1@nslave1.tin.it>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au> <sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com> <8ilg6u$lqr$1@news.igs.net> <394E8E2D.A796ACD8@melbpc.org.au> <8im5ek$20f5$1@news.hitter.net>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
8im5ek$20f5$1@news.hitter.net...
> The article in question was copyrighted in 1992. It is possible,
> perhaps likely, that the article preceeded or paralleled Beck's
> work. In that case, attribution to Beck is temporally inconsistent!
> ------------------
> Rick Smith

I think so too.
If someone is interested in XP, there are useful informations
at:
http://c2.com/cgi/wiki?ExtremeProgrammingRoadmap
http://www.extremeprogramming.org
http://www.xprogramming.com

Actually it is a very debated subject.
Many well known authors love it, others express
their criticism.
In these days in Italy there is an international workshop
(see http://numa.sern.enel.ucalgary.ca/extreme), with
experts like Kent Beck, Rober C. Martin, Martin Fowler,
Ron Jeffries.

In "comp.object" there is an hot debate on the subject.
In particular (and this was the sense for the link to
Reeves' article) in connection with a particular aspect
of XP: code as design language.
Software development is a very iterative process, in which
you produce a lot of design documents (UML diagrams --
I'm speaking about OO development).
You have to do a lot of rework on such diagrams in order
to synchronize them with code.
So, XP experts say it is more efficient to have a language
and code structuring techniques, that let you express
your design decisions directly in code.

They simply have the need of expressing in code
what they express in UML diagrams by means of
rectangles and other graphic marks, saving in this way
a great amount of time.

As I told before, many agree with XP, many don't.
I'm not an XP expert, and so these are the only
informations I can give you for the moment.
I'm studying the subject.

Regards,
Marco Papacchini.
```

would execute.  After all, a programming specification is only an incomplete
program.  It is one of the reasons that my business cards have, as my title,
"programmer", in spite of the fact that I own the firm.

strongly.

at

the

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394F84F0.2DFCB918@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au> <sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com> <8ilg6u$lqr$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> I quite liked the article, and tend to agree with the author very strongly.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> OS.

IMHO: Jack argues that the construction phase IS the compiler. 
Everything up to and including the source code is the design.  Since I
can compile three months design and coding work in just over one
minute then I can consider the process free.

He definitely does not state that the code is the ONLY design.  It is
just one important facet of the design phase.  The design process can
be augmented by other methods such as waterfall (YUCK) or rapid cyclic
development as per extreme programming.

The nature of the language has great bearing on the choice.  If we can
do direct design to source to compile without a programmer then this
is the optimal solution.   I think that this is where tools such as
Rational Rose come in 'when' Rose supports OO cobol the overhead of
generating the additional code is neutralized leaving the
implementation of the real solution to the programmer.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8io3jp$7ae$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au> <sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com> <8ilg6u$lqr$1@news.igs.net> <394F84F0.2DFCB918@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message

>IMHO: Jack argues that the construction phase IS the compiler.
>Everything up to and including the source code is the design.  Since I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>just one important facet of the design phase.  The design process

<snip>

Surely.  However, as soon as you say the code is part of the design, or
better yet, that the design must be specified in such a way that code is
included, then I think you are getting closer to the mark.

There is probably not a single person that has been programming for any
length of time that has not received a specification containing "stuff" that
is not clearly enough specified to be coded. To my mind, that is an
incomplete specification.

However, as soon as you say that enough information must be specified that
the code can be written, then the next question is "Why not write a program
to do it?".  After all, if the specification is "Complete", then the process
could be automated.

It boils down to language.  The natural language usually used to write a
"specification" is simply not precise enough to completely specify
everything.

A similar situation exists with traditional engineering, and is overcome by
the use of blueprints.  A scale drawing of the end result, complete with
measurements and enough detail to actually build the unit.

The only way that the same level of detail can be given for a software
specification is by the use of a specialized language.  We have known that
for years.  Actual computer languages are designed to do just that.  They
describe a proccess in sufficient detail that a computer can write a program
to do it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Code as design language]

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394FF5EB.93A8D58C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<394C37A9.2E2C0198@zip.com.au> <sks9jq9oe7f15@corp.supernews.com> <8ilg6u$lqr$1@news.igs.net> <394F84F0.2DFCB918@zip.com.au> <8io3jp$7ae$1@news.igs.net>`

```

I'd love to get into this one but it requires too much detail, so I'll
confine myself elsewhere as to 'What is OO Design ?'. But as regards
this particular thread :-

donald tees wrote:
> 
> It boils down to language.  The natural language usually used to write a
> "specification" is simply not precise enough to completely specify
> everything.

That was most certainly true when I started and you were still a young
student with Grace <G>. However, we've come a long way since then, and
the systems analysts (something you have admitted you would dread
being), and programmers, have picked up a lot of skills and tools since
those days.

Refrain on an old tune - Decision Tables if used properly, present you
with a finite set of rules. Design wise (from analyst's perspective),
you still have to be a thinker, artist, psychologist, politician ..... 
etc. to put the overall design together.

As opposed to the old style of 'Here's a program spec of what I want the
machine to do...It sort of covers everything...', the analyst now has
the tool (Decision Tables) to cover every "Yes" and "No" to turn the
whole thing into a decent design for the programmer to code. (As Richard
Plinston said elsewhere, take the business analyst's tables and code
from them ).

Based on some changes my end-user gave me to-day, I'm taking his
thoughts and will turn them into Decision Tables. Then I will e-mail
these to him - and I wont start a line of code until he confirms "YES".

Jimmy
```

Industry statistics that have been proven many times is that only 30%

the code work for the specification and therefore upping the code
rate  (XP).  Others, using Z like the british govt get less than 10%
coding time.

I had the opportunity to take a course on "Function Point Analysis" and even
though I didn't see the final results of the implementation and use I
thought that the concept was sound.  The concept is to assign values to
files/records and number of data elements along with some 14 envionment and
operations elements for each program.  Once base lined new/modification time
to history is captured and delivery points calculated using the function
points and time.  As experience is gained it appears that a more accurate
estimated can be attained vs LOC.

Home page of the user group.  http://www.ifpug.org/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAC Address

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8it9se$vq7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<sCf35.15176$FC6.295445@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <8ilrmv$ust$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <sl3fi3oie7f45@corp.supernews.com>`

```
If you have a copy of the '85 Standard, you could "copy" (photocopy) the
"Composite Language Skeleton" - which would give you the full "Standard"
syntax.  UNFORTUNATELY, this will be dropped in the next Standard.  Also,
assuming an IBM user, the diagrams there use the "old"
curli-bracket/parentheses notation and not IBM "railroad tracks".

Micro Focus always used to have a "summary"  booklet - but I don't know if
you can purchase it alone.  (They are also going more and more to online
information.)
```

Boycott Mitsubishi Industries

#### ↳ Re: MAC Address

- **From:** John Chafin <jhchafin@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jitp0$ov1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<sCf35.15176$FC6.295445@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <sCf35.15176$FC6.295445@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>,
  "Bill Wood" <beavis27@nospambigfoot.com> wrote:
> Does anyone have code in COBOL to get the MAC Address for a machine?
I have
> sample C code, but I'd rather not have to translate it.  Any help
would be
> appreciated.
>
>
Bill,
I finally got around to writing the COBOL code to get the mac address.
I used CA-Realia COBOL Workbench 3.0. The code is in the Realia cbl
format which drops columns 1-6. You will probably have to insert 6
bytes at the beginning of each line of code unless your compiler
supports this format. Obviously, if you use another compiler,  you'll
have to translate the data defs and api call syntax. I included code in
the sample program code to get the computer and user names as well. If
you or anybody else out there wants the code, email me and I'll send
it to you.

John Chafin


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-cbb5d9b80d"></a>
## Thread: how to convert packed COBOL data? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### how to convert packed COBOL data?

- **From:** "Heini" <no.spam@online.de>
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8il589$9q0$1@news.online.de>`

```
I'm receiving packed COBOL data - integers (P), single precisions or
currencies (P), dates (D) - in a file and must read out these values using
Visual Basic.
Checking the file under DOS I found they contain ascii chars < 20H. Where
can I
found some information to convert these byte fields into text fields?

Heini
```

#### ↳ Re: how to convert packed COBOL data?

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394e6ba0.11284135@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<8il589$9q0$1@news.online.de>`

```
I think there are tool kits for this, and several people can probably
provide you with home-grown solutions.

However, in my opinion the BEST solution is to modify the program that
builds this data for you to re-format it into non-packed.   The reason
I say this is because if the data is going through an EBCDIC to ASCII
conversion, it is possible for valid EBCDIC packed numbers to cause
things to go bonkers during the conversion to ASCII.

As a rule, we NEVER process packed data in ASCII here....

Pete


On Mon, 19 Jun 2000 14:53:21 +0200, "Heini" <no.spam@online.de> wrote:

>I'm receiving packed COBOL data - integers (P), single precisions or
>currencies (P), dates (D) - in a file and must read out these values using
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to convert packed COBOL data?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394ED928.3A5F@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<8il589$9q0$1@news.online.de> <394e6ba0.11284135@news.transport.com>`

```
Parser/Converters like ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm) can convert 
files like that.  They can also make allowances for files which started off as EBCDIC 
and got garbled by the default character conversions to ASCII when moving from the 
mainframe to the PC.


Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> I think there are tool kits for this, and several people can probably
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

#### ↳ Re: how to convert packed COBOL data?

- **From:** Alexander Meins <alex@nikocity.de>
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394E6D20.22CDB01D@nikocity.de>`
- **References:** `<8il589$9q0$1@news.online.de>`

```
A usual format for numeric values in COBOL is PACKED.
The number +123456 would be Hex '0123456F'. Where F is
the sign (positive). C is also positive, D is negative.

Alex

Heini wrote:
> 
> I'm receiving packed COBOL data - integers (P), single precisions or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Heini
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to convert packed COBOL data?

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394eb188.29180479@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<8il589$9q0$1@news.online.de> <394E6D20.22CDB01D@nikocity.de>`

```
But it does *NOT* look like '0123456F' if it has been converted to
ASCII.  (And Visual Basic is usually running with ASCII data.)

Pete


On Mon, 19 Jun 2000 20:57:36 +0200, Alexander Meins <alex@nikocity.de>
wrote:

>A usual format for numeric values in COBOL is PACKED.
>The number +123456 would be Hex '0123456F'. Where F is
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.gohip.com/free_video/
```

How do you convert a EBCDI code of X'0123456F' to ASCII then??

A.

> 
> But it does *NOT* look like '0123456F' if it has been converted to

#### ↳ Re: how to convert packed COBOL data?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Uo445.117$az.54490@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8il589$9q0$1@news.online.de>`

```
1. Read and/or download:
http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html

This explains the difference between IEEE and COBOL datatypes, and also
deals with EBCDIC/ASCII conversions: A brilliant treatise by a talented
programmer and writer. (Me). (It's not visually too appealing, but I'm not
an artist type at all).

2. Conversion, huh? It just so happens that I am looking for a VB-programmer
type to use my FREE (*) 32-bit DLL which converts COBOL to IEEE data, as
well as doing EBCDIC to ASCII conversions.

I have a complete kit with PowerBASIC souce code, test files, executables,
etc which you can use to convert COBOL data to somehting VB can read. Write
directly to me with a reply e-mail-address.

(*) Free - almost: I INSIST that you "pay" for your use of this product by
doing me the courtesy of letting me know how it worked out, what was
unclear/wrong/missing from the documentation, etc. So far for the
twenty-seven kits I have sent I have received only, "well, I haven't gotten
around to it yet..." and often that's been because I sent a followup.
(Except for one guy, who did comment as requested).
```

Hi,

a Motif solution, or as the best alternative, a Thin Client GUI with the

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM/COBOL for Linux and MOUSE

- **From:** gnrfan@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8imh2p$cdj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<griqksstdd7jaoolfirlffr2nbhjpltmpm@4ax.com> <394DA296.5A3181D8@aol.com>`

```
Hello everyone.

My boss has a program written in COBOL that initially was running
on an SCO unix box using the RUNCOBOL command. Now we need to run
the same cobol program on a Linux box. Our distro is Conectiva Linux.
Our kernel has the iBCS module that afaik lets me run SCO apps under
Linux. We are using the RM/COBOL SCO program as interpreter. We have
the sources and some binaries and perhaps we can compile it in the
linux box. The problem we have is with CONSOLE CHARACTERS they
are all screwed up and changed. The program worked on another linux
installation. I picked the terminal type running on that working setup
with INFOCMP > linux.ti and then I took that file to my machine and
compiled it with TIC to obtain the terminal file. Previous to that
change there was almost no output in the terminal of type "linux".
Now with this "new" terminal (TYPE=linscobol) most thing work ok,
the problem is some characters are displayed wrong. This pointed
me to try with several fonts to match the proper encoding. I am
guessing the correct encoding using SETFONT with all the .psf console
fonts under Linux but havent get a nice match yet. The working linux
installation in a linux box used cp850-8x14 as font. Using SETFONT
cp850-8x14 or CONSOLECHARS -f cp850-8x14 changes the font but I cant
still get the correct mapping match. This cp850 encoding seems to be
"IBM Latin-1" but using Latin-1 fonts havent fixed the problem too
much. So after reading the whole case WHAT IS WRONG? WHAT AM I MISSING?
Got an idea? I am in kind of desperate need for a fix of this problem
so your help is extremely valuable.


Thanks a lot.

Antonio


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: CreateProcess Win32API Part 2 — 2 msgs

### CreateProcess Win32API Part 2

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000619110601.18141.00003609@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

By using the the CreateProcess or CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT I am able to run programs
like NOTEPAD and WORDPAD.  I have also been able to execute other third party
software.  But, the software that I am trying to execute seems to hang until I
exit the COBOL program.  I am trying to execute a program called "QUICKCAM.EXE"
from LogiTech.  This program provides an interface to a USB internet video
camera.  I execute the program with the CreateProcess API and receive back a
return code that indicates that the QUICKCAM.EXE has been started.  I get an
hour glass mouse pointer that last about one minute.  The system then stops at
the WaitforSingleObject API waiting for the termination of the "QUICKCAM"
program.  If I then stop animating my COBOL program the QUICKCAM software will
startup and appear on my screen.  I have tried using the application name
instead of the command line in the API but the results are the same.  Has
anyone had this type of problem before with this API ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>

- **References:** `<20000619110601.18141.00003609@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
This sounds exactly like a problem I am having, though I start the
Workbench v4.x Animator ANIM2WG.EXE.  The splash screen for the animator
will hang for @30 seconds, then the animator will start.  This only
happens when I use the "WaitForSingleObject" API call to wait for
execution to finish.  If I just use the "CreateProcess" call by itself,
the splash screen displays for about 3 seconds and the animator starts
immediately.

I think it is an issue with animator...

TL

<a id="t-c8cfaf8339"></a>
## Thread: CreateProcess WIN32API Part 3 — 3 msgs

### CreateProcess WIN32API Part 3

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000619163905.11814.00002283@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I am using the CreateProcess API to run a MS-DOS batch (BAT) file.  The system
executes the batch file fine, but does not shut down the window that gets
created to run the batch file.  Once I shut the window down processing
continues as normal.  Does anyone know of a way to tell the CreateProcess API
to close a MS-DOS window when the command have been completed ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: CreateProcess WIN32API Part 3

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8injc2$n8m$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000619163905.11814.00002283@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Hi Bob,

By default Win9x will leave the console on the screen to see any message.
If you want to make sure it closes then use some code along the lines of:-

           move z"command.com /C batchfile.bat" to executable
      * We create the process
           call WINAPI CreateProcess using
               by value 0 size 4,
               by reference executable,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value CREATE-DEFAULT-ERROR-MODE,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by value 0 size 4,
               by reference StartupInfo,
               by reference ProcessINFO
               returning ws-Okay
           end-call

The /C switch makes sure that the window is closed.

Regards
David.

Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000619163905.11814.00002283@ng-fs1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
>
> I am using the CreateProcess API to run a MS-DOS batch (BAT) file.  The
system
> executes the batch file fine, but does not shut down the window that gets
> created to run the batch file.  Once I shut the window down processing
> continues as normal.  Does anyone know of a way to tell the CreateProcess
API
> to close a MS-DOS window when the command have been completed ?
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CreateProcess WIN32API Part 3

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000620082309.00143.00003089@ng-co1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8injc2$n8m$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Hi David,

Thanks for the help I will give that a try.

Bob Hennessey

the Win 2000 (Professional) and Fujitsu COBOL.

for the next year.  I have ordered (with a special thanks to Roger
Johnson) a single copy of Professional Version 5.0 for my own use THIS

Merant's Net Express (University Edition).

worked without problem on Win 2000.  I think I remember some comments
about version 4 having conflicts in this environment.

Floyd Johnson

## Thread: DG AOS/VS II Cobol Rev 4.3 Problem — 14 msgs

### DG AOS/VS II Cobol Rev 4.3 Problem

- **From:** "John A. Simpson" <jasimp@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net>`

```
One of my clients recently decided to upgrade their Cobol compiler from
ver 1.3 (not a typo) to 4.3. I've done the necessary source code syntax
changes and compiled and linked some modules to do some testing. Lo and
behold, all of a sudden, using the new version, I can't open any
existing ISAM or sequential file, nor can I even create one without
getting a status 91 error. These programs have been working properly for
20+ years.

The supplier is no help so far. Has anyone seen this problem, or does
anyone have any ideas? I've been programming in Cobol for over 20 years,
but I don't have a clue what might be wrong here. I know it's not the
code. Help, I'm desperate!

Thanks in advance.

John
```

- **References:** `<394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net>`

```

It cant be Microfocus here...
DG AOS/VS II  is Data General's Operating system which runs on their
MV computer series. This was a popular machine in the beginning of 80's.
The cobol compiler is DG's own version and the db system is called INFOS II.

#### ↳ Re: DG AOS/VS II Cobol Rev 4.3 Problem

- **From:** "pwmeister" <pwm@nomail.se>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ipr6d$9vo$1@news1.enator.se>`
- **References:** `<394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net>`

```
Hi

I've done some Coboling in the early-mid 80's on the DG MV/series.
A couple of things that I can think of:

- Are you using the right compile/link macro?
- Is your searchlist properly set to point to the right macro?
- You might want to check in the parameters for COBOL and LINK
  execute lines. With a new release, you get a standardized macro for
  compiling which might overwrite your earlier compile/link macro.
- There might be a version conflict with the new compiler. A release notice
  should be located in :UTIL:COBOL (:UTIL:COBOL.4.3).
  Check if compatible with AOS/VS,  INFOS_II, SWAT and/or SORT/MERGE.

- I cant remember exactly what stat=91 was. But if it is file structure
error
  (as suggested earlier in this thread) you must use IRECOVER on ISAM/DBAM
  files. I have some old manuals at home and I can check it for you if you
like.

regards
PMeister


John A. Simpson wrote in message <394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net>...
>One of my clients recently decided to upgrade their Cobol compiler from
>ver 1.3 (not a typo) to 4.3. I've done the necessary source code syntax
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>John
```

#### ↳ Re: DG AOS/VS II Cobol Rev 4.3 Problem

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ioma4$hkn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net>`

```
The Isam internals have obviously been changed sometime between then and
now.  A 91 error is a malformed file.

You are going to have to dump the Isam files to sequential, and reload them
under the new compiler.  Possibly, you can do that with a pair of utilities
... one from the old version and one from the new.  If not, a relatively (or
isamilly, if you prefer<G>) trivial program under the old compiler will dump
them, and a similar one under the new will reload them.

John A. Simpson wrote in message <394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net>...
>One of my clients recently decided to upgrade their Cobol compiler from
>ver 1.3 (not a typo) to 4.3. I've done the necessary source code syntax
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DG AOS/VS II Cobol Rev 4.3 Problem

- **From:** "pwmeister" <pwm@nomail.se>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<haj55.6816$rH5.16753@nntpserver.swip.net>`
- **References:** `<394FCEB9.E7CF8DC5@earthlink.net> <8ioma4$hkn$1@news.igs.net>`

```

donald tees skrev i meddelandet <8ioma4$hkn$1@news.igs.net>...
>The Isam internals have obviously been changed sometime between then and
>now.  A 91 error is a malformed file.

Wrong. With the release of DG AOS/VS COBOL 3.40, file status 91 meant:
  "(1)File does not exist on OPEN INPUT, OPEN EXTEND or OPEN I/O.
   (2) File already exists on OPEN OUTPUT, (3) file access denied
   (4) a DELETE of a file has failed because the files name condition
contains
   an illegal character. (DG manual 093-000289 rev 3.40)" .

pwmeister
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<394FE9BE.5FD4B4EB@home.com>`

MASSIMO wrote:
> 
> I would like have some information about Cobol Object Oriented.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank YOU !!

For a quick list of products go to the COBOL FAQ -  

	URL: http://www.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

As an intro to OO COBOL on PCs check both Fujitsu and Merant (Micro
Focus) sites. Fujitsu will shortly release a student version and Merant
has the University edition of NetExpress; each of course has a 'full
blown' version - at a price !

The biggest obstacle you will find is Windows GUIs unless you are
already familiar with these. Look to Flexus for their SP2 product which
can be used with any of the COBOL compilers, serving as the link between
COBOL and Windows.

In addition there are Liant (RM/COBOL) and Merant (Micro Focus) versions
for Linux.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Object Oriented ??

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>

```
Massimo,

The MERANT Net Express 3.1 product contains full support for
Object COBOL.  There are base, GUI, OLE classes and so on.
Net Express run of Microsoft Windows 32 bit platforms.

Our UNIX products (of which there is on for LINUX) also support
Object COBOL.

More details can be found at the MERANT web site:

http://www.merant.com

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sending data across network (Acknowledgments)

- **From:** "Servizio Tecnico" <software@gescart.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iqce3$4b2$1@news.mclink.it>`
- **References:** `<8inagl$qec$1@news.mclink.it> <8inmok$gv$1@news.igs.net>`

```

"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> ha scritto nel messaggio
news:8inmok$gv$1@news.igs.net...
> Well done.  I have been meaning to ask you who makes that
Ethernet/RS-232-C
> interface you are using?  It sounds like a good way to map a large number
of
> serial ports onto a PC.
>

Normally we use our software and hardware (badge reader see www.leggo.it)
with RS232 and RS485 but in some firm there are many problem to connect with
RSxxx and we must connect with LAN.

We connect the badge reader with ABLELINK from Oltremare (www.oltremare.net)
with RS232 and the converter with LAN.

Today we can connect the PLC in many manners like this example:

1) Badge Reader -> RS232 -> PC

2) Badge Reader(1) -> Badge Reader (2) -> up to Badge Reader(32) -> RS485->
Converter 485/232 -> PC

3) Badge Reader -> Converter Ethernet -> LAN -> PC
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sending data across network (Acknowledgments)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iqh3p$pa9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8inagl$qec$1@news.mclink.it> <8inmok$gv$1@news.igs.net> <8iqce3$4b2$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
We do a very similar thing with barcode data and scale data. Wiring is
always a problem, as a lot of our stuff goes outside, and a lot is spread
out over a few hundred acres of plant.  I rather like the idea of using the
Ethernet cable as a data collection device(your number three).  It
simplifies wiring a whole lot.

<a id="t-be703f6e7b"></a>
## Thread: IBM DFSORT vs SyncSort — 4 msgs

### IBM DFSORT vs SyncSort

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ip36j02ekm@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Our shop implemented DFSORT, taking out trusty SyncSort on Sunday evening.
By Monday morning, I was having problems with some test jobs.  Apparently
DFSORT doesn't allow concatenated datasets for sort space (SORTWK).  My boss
was prepared to stop the schedule when I told him, but it pressed on (poor
judgement).  As soon as our heavy batch window hit at 6:00PM, several jobs
abended.  SyncSort was rolled back in and the mainframe IPLed (30 minutes
lost in production LPAR batch window).

They haven't put DFSORT back in yet; waiting for some answers from IBM.

Sad part is, the only reason we switched was to 'standardize' with our
larger, mother company.  I suppose it will pay off in the long run.

Anyone else seen these abnormalities?

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM DFSORT vs SyncSort

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<L_V35.3063$uf2.4594@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<8ip36j02ekm@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
IBM's response will be something like
    " read the manual.  The examples for SORTWKdd, do not mention
concatenation
of work data sets.  Since with BLOCKSET you can have 255 SORTWKdd, and w/o
up to 32, there should be no need to use this technique."

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8ip36j02ekm@enews4.newsguy.com...
> Our shop implemented DFSORT, taking out trusty SyncSort on Sunday evening.
> By Monday morning, I was having problems with some test jobs.  Apparently
> DFSORT doesn't allow concatenated datasets for sort space (SORTWK).  My
boss
> was prepared to stop the schedule when I told him, but it pressed on (poor
> judgement).  As soon as our heavy batch window hit at 6:00PM, several jobs
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lbd1ls8ck9dq8d79l0j1spgo2372blu0gv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8ip36j02ekm@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
On Tue, 20 Jun 2000 20:45:04 -0400 "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
wrote:

:>Our shop implemented DFSORT, taking out trusty SyncSort on Sunday evening.
:>By Monday morning, I was having problems with some test jobs.  Apparently
:>DFSORT doesn't allow concatenated datasets for sort space (SORTWK).  My boss
:>was prepared to stop the schedule when I told him, but it pressed on (poor
:>judgement).  As soon as our heavy batch window hit at 6:00PM, several jobs
:>abended.  SyncSort was rolled back in and the mainframe IPLed (30 minutes
:>lost in production LPAR batch window).

:>They haven't put DFSORT back in yet; waiting for some answers from IBM.

:>Sad part is, the only reason we switched was to 'standardize' with our
:>larger, mother company.  I suppose it will pay off in the long run.

:>Anyone else seen these abnormalities?

Question out of curiosity:

Why do you concatenate SORTWK rather than specifying additional SORTWK
ddnames?
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM DFSORT vs SyncSort

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3950DFAE.7907D736@vnet.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<8ip36j02ekm@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:

> ... Apparently
> DFSORT doesn't allow concatenated datasets for sort space (SORTWK).

Yes, that's true.  You should have received the following message:

ICE148A 0 SORTWKdd CONCATENATION NOT ALLOWED

I'm sorry you ran into this problem with your migration, but the two products,
though similar, are NOT identical.  The DFSORT hotline (sort@vnet.ibm.com) can
help you with differences you find.

In this case, you need to change your concatenated SORTWKs to separate DD
statements.  Alternatively, you can use dynamic allocation of work space instead
of JCL SORTWKs.  One way to do that without changing your JCL is to use ICEMAC
option DYNAUTO=IGNWKDD.  This tells DFSORT to deallocate your JCL SORTWKs and
use dynamic allocation instead.  For some more information on dynamic
allocation, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmadyn.htm

For general information on DFSORT, see the DFSORT home page at:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

You can access all of the DFSORT publications online from:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmpub.htm

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
```

#### ↳ Re: query reports from VSAM files

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<394fb274.62531435@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000620124627.23795A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
Have not seen it.

We use EasytrievePlus.
EasytrievePlus can do everything that a batch-cobol program can, but
with 1/10 the lines of source code.  And it has a killer automated
report formatter.  I still choose to write in COBOL for the most
complicated application work though.  The high-level logic that
EasytrievePlus is coded in some times gets clunky when doing difficult
routines.

Pete

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

I'm a fan of EASYTRIEVE also.  Even for a very simple report, I'd prefer to
create an extract file using COBOL and send the output into an EASYTRIEVE
program.  If the report ever needs modifying, it's probably 10 times quicker
to modify the EZT pgm than the COBOL one.

<a id="t-9f928ee219"></a>
## Thread: Program Logic?? — 41 this month / 43 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Program Logic??

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
I wonder if anyone could help with some logic?

Records are stored line sequetially in a file. One of the fields ( a single
numeric item ) is a product type code in the range 1 to 6 inclusive. Many
records can have the same type code and the file is stored in ascending type
code.

My program needs to read a record and do some simple calculations and print
the results. If a change of type code is detected the program prints out a
total of all the calculations for that type code ( like sub-totals ).

My logic is as follows-
*startup
        open files
        read a record
        store type code

*main loop until end of file flag set
        if type code just loaded different from stored type code then print
sub-totals and return to here
        do calculation
        print individual details of record
        read next record
        end of file? - set flag
*loop back

As the logic goes; when the end of the file is reached - drop out of the
loop. Problem is that  I still haven`t printed out the last of the sub
totals.
I use a control paragraph - ie ;

perform startup
perform main loop until end of file
perform shutdown
stop run

and I can easily add another jump ( after the `perform main loop......` ) to
print the sub totals, but the code looks messy and not very ( dare I say )
modular.
Is there a neater solution out there somewhere that someone would like to
share ;-)  ( and of course it would add to my `not so great` experience )

Thanks in advance

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8irrtl$ke4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```

as the first line of the shutdown routine?

>

I like to program code that ( I believe ) if it looks neat and smooth
running, then it will run neat and smooth. I thought there might be a way of
actually keeping all the work of printing the individual details and the
printing of the sub-totals within the main-loop, rather than it having to
drop out of the loop only to do something that is actually happening within
the loop. ( I think you`ll understand what I mean ).

Thanks anyway

Colonel

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer (was Program Logic??)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8itsr0$bc7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8irrtl$ke4$1@news.igs.net> <iAe45.203$9W1.14929@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8is4s9$m8u$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <39525A17.B89E3F2F@earthlink.net>`

```
Yes, many shops use Report Writer (as a "shop standard").  Yes, there are
many shops that don't use it at all.  It is being enhanced in the next COBOL
Standard (and will be required for all conforming implementations - while the
current version is optional).

Report Writer along with internal SORTs (using input/output procedures) can
*easily* handle many "one-time" report requests.  For developers who don't
need any "hard-copy output" this is (obviously) unimportant.  However, for
many shops this is a major ongoing "development" task.
```

I think I'm a bit confused (normal state of affairs for me - just ask hubby). 
You want a 'modular' program but don't want to code a module to do sub totals? 
Personally I think Donald's sugestion the way to go.  I have one paragraph
(module if you will) that does the start up, one that controls the main
processing, one that does details, one that does sub totals, one that does the
shut down, and perform them as necessary where necessary. Paragraph name should
be self documenting so it doesn't matter where they are altough it is 'pretty'
to have them together based on function.  Having more than one function in a
paragraph can be a maintenance nightmare.

Eileen

choose, not the algorithm that the code needs to express.  In the type of
situation that you name, having a final total in the ending routine is so
common that most people would look for it there.  The close out routine

ubiquitous that I cannot see why you would code it any other way.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
Colonel wrote in message ...
>OK OK, I`ve been rumbled. It is homework, and I have solved the problem
set.
>I am just certain that my logic could be improved ( maybe with a few
>clues ). I`d like to do the actual code myself so that way someone else is
>not telling me answers, but merely guiding me.

I read your original message again, and would like to make a few comments.
These are personal, and I expect you will get a lot of other advice both pro
and con on what I say, so keep that in mind.

First of all, I think it  a bad idea to try to put that logic into the main
loop.  It is not "wrong", but to my mind, poor style.

Style is something that every programmer should work on constantly.  There
is going to always be a conflict between the personal style that you evolve,
and the style that your employer likes, but style is still crucially
important. The reason for that is habit.  As a professional programmer, you
are going to write millions of lines of code in your life.  If you adopt a
good personal style, it will tend to be good code.  If you whack it out,
then try to clean it up to "hand in" after the fact, most of it will be
crap.

Cobol, as a language, was developed specifically to allow a programmer to
develop a self documenting style.  The vast bulk of business code is
written, then changed over and over again to reflect changes in the real
world. The crucial thing to understanding Cobol is that it was developed so
that easily READ code could be written.  Note that.  Not so that code would
be easy to write, but so that code would be easy to READ.  The Cobol style
that you evolve should reflect that.

IMO, the most important tool that you have for readable code is the
paragraph name.  Each paragraph should not only be reasonably short, but
reflect a specific task, and the name of that paragraph should tell you
exactly what to expect that code to do.  Each paragraph should fit on a
screen, ideally, so that you can scan it easily.

Cobol has the ability to write straight line code, like any other language.
You can nest performs and try to document them with indentation, C-style, to
your hearts content.  However, doing so adds almost nothing to efficiency,
and defeats the purpose of the language to a very large extent.  The
overhead of a perform is negligible in 99.9% of all cases.  The only time it
will make much difference is in that rare (for business code)  case of a
highly computationally bound program doing weird mathematical computations
millions of times ... things like matrix multiplication.

Having said all that, I would write the code you want something along the
following lines.  Note that the code is simple and easy to follow.

01 END-FLAG                 PICTURE X VALUE "N".
       88 END-OF-FILE       VALUE "Y".
***
MAINLINE.
       PERFORM START-UP.
       PERFORM MAIN-LOOP UNTIL END-OF-FILE.
       PERFORM CLOSE-OUT.
       STOP RUN.

START-UP.
       OPEN INPUT FILENAME.
       READ FILENAME AT END
           PERFORM EMPTY-FILE-ERROR-MESSAGE
           STOP RUN.
       MOVE RECORD-CODE TO CURRENT-RECORD-CODE.

MAIN-LOOP.
       IF RECORD-CODE IS NOT EQUAL TO CURRENT-RECORD-CODE
           PERFORM SUBTOTAL-ROUTINE.
       PERFORM PRINT-THE-RECORD-ROUTINE.
       READ FILENAME AT END
           MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG.
******* MANY PEOPLE WOULD USE "SET" HERE

CLOSE-OUT.
       PERFORM SUBTOTAL-ROUTINE.
       CLOSE FILENAME.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Here is the correct style, lean and mean with no redundant, confusing
code. End-Verbs not added due to laziness.

MAINLINE.
	PERFORM START-UP.
	PERFORM MAIN-LOOP UNTIL END-OF-FILE.
	PERFORM CLOSE-OUT.
	STOP RUN.

START-UP.
	OPEN INPUT FILENAME.

MAIN-LOOP.
	READ FILENAME AT END
		MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO RECORD-CODE
		MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG.
	IF END-FLAG = 'N'
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-COUNTER.

	IF RECORD-COUNTER = 1
		IF END-FLAG = 'Y'
			PERFORM EMPTY-FILE-ERROR-MESSAGE
		ELSE
			MOVE RECORD-CODE TO CURRENT-RECORD-CODE
	ELSE
		IF RECORD-CODE IS NOT EQUAL TO CURRENT-RECORD-CODE
			PERFORM SUBTOTAL-ROUTINE
			MOVE RECORD-CODE TO CURRENT-RECORD-CODE.

	IF END-FLAG = 'N'
		PERFORM PRINT-THE-RECORD-ROUTINE.

CLOSE-OUT.
	CLOSE FILENAME.
	GOBACK.




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There is a trade off here.  I prefer the original code where you do a
priming read, then don't need a switch telling me that I am at the end
of my file.  You prefer having only one read, then bypassing code with a
switch.

I could live with either as a shop standard though.  If that's what
people expect, your style is not much more confusing than mine.  In a
shop without such standards, I will will side strongly with the
simplification of putting the read at the end of the MAIN-LOOP
paragraph.

shine98@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Here is the correct style, lean and mean with no redundant, confusing
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu>`

```
I must admit I like the idea of only having all of the PRINT-SUBROUTINE
jumps only within the mainloop. After all it is what I wanted. I have been
taught to code as my original code was, I found  it is easier to read
 after ripping out the unnecessary comments!! ). I have been taught to check
in the style of `file-at-end = yes`, so it didn`t even enter my head to
check for `file-at-end = no`.

Learn new ideas everyday.

Thanks everyone.

Colonel

news:39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu...
> There is a trade off here.  I prefer the original code where you do a
> priming read, then don't need a switch telling me that I am at the end
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```

Colonel wrote in message ...
>I must admit I like the idea of only having all of the PRINT-SUBROUTINE
>jumps only within the mainloop. After all it is what I wanted. I have been
>taught to code as my original code was, I found  it is easier to read
> after ripping out the unnecessary comments!! ). I have been taught to
check
>in the style of `file-at-end = yes`, so it didn`t even enter my head to
>check for `file-at-end = no`.

Well, that is what I meant when I said it was all personal<G>.  I am very
use to a priming read, and do not find the read at the end of the main loop
confusing at all.  One of the things I like about a priming read is that
there is usually a different error routine for an empty file.  When
processing data for updates, for example, the priming read can create a new
file if it does not exist.  In real code, you would check the file status
for a '35" on the open, and create the empty file.  If you simply want the
sub-total PERFORM in the main loop, it would be a simple matter placing it
in the AT END paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net>`

```
A common standard is to have a single location for any I/O.  This is one
reason some people don't like the priming read.  I find that the
advantages of a priming read are quite significant myself.

Another result of this policy is the one-statement print or call
paragraph which is performed from all over the place.  To me, this is
accomplishing nothing.  So what if the actual WRITE is only coded once,
if the PERFORM 9999-WRITE-ROUTINE is repeated all over the place. 
Nothing got simplified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fjJ55.128$dN1.50893@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu...
> Another result of this policy is the one-statement print or call
> paragraph which is performed from all over the place.  To me, this is
> accomplishing nothing.  So what if the actual WRITE is only coded once,
> if the PERFORM 9999-WRITE-ROUTINE is repeated all over the place.
> Nothing got simplified.

What gets simplified with this method is error-handling code. It only needs
to be included in the 9999-WRITE-ROUTINE.

This assumes a write error can be treated as 'fatal' to the program -
usually the case. But even if it isn't, you can deal with that in your write
as well, For instance, dealing with an indexed file where you want to add if
not present, update if found...


9999-WRITE ROUTINE.

   WRITE  The-record
   EVALUATE TRUE
      WHEN File-OK           << file status conditions
        continue
      When dup-key
        perform rewrite-routine
      when other
        report file status and abort
   END-EVALUATE.


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu>`

```
One reason for single location I-O is that many times there is not only
the write but common related code such as status code checking or in the
case of printing, line counting.  I am in the habit of using the 1
instance of a given I-O contruct per file or record for that reason.  It
also makes replacement or modification of I-O to that file (replacing it
with a data base call for example) easier.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> A common standard is to have a single location for any I/O.  This is one
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program Logic??

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39577A55.89246385@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu> <395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca>`

```
Correct, but I have often seen it with paragraphs containing a single
statement.  Sometimes even the performing paragraph looks at the error
code (especially for first time logic).  Where it works well is when a
print paragraph checks line count and performs the heading routine, etc.

Which is another place where I often like a primer.  This is slightly
less intuitive than a read primer, but it is common enough to not throw
COBOL programmers, but I like to start off with my line count set to
888.  When I perform the print routine, it sees that the page is full
and prints new titles.  I do not set my line count to 999 as I have seen
people use pic X(03), and increment the line count by one, getting 000. 
It is easier to get into a habit of avoiding extremes.

case of input files for instance.  I don't mind 2 or maybe 3 PERFORMs of an
I-O for a file, but there are lots of reasons (maintainability) for having
that I-O appearing in only 1 paragraph/section.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3959F9FA.3003815@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu> <395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca> <39587B6A.500D7EDE@zip.com.au> <3958DE4C.1DA5DE36@dced.state.ak.us>`

```


Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> Personally, I use a MAX-PAGE-LINES variable. I don't feel that hard-coding
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken Foskey wrote:

I do the same thing with all of my tables, of which a printed page is a
variation.

The following is part of an example of a table which gets sorted:
01  WRK-MAX                     PIC 9(5)     VALUE ZERO.
01  WRK-IDX                     PIC 9(5).
01  WRK-OCCURS                  PIC 9(5)     VALUE 3000.
01  WRK-EXTRA                   PIC 9(5)     VALUE 3001.
01  WRK-TABLE.
    02 WRK-RECORD               OCCURS 3001 TIMES.

When the table is built, WRK-MAX is incremented and compared to
WRK-OCCURS.  When it is accessed, WRK-IDX is used (UNTIL WRK-IDX >
WRK-MAX).  The WRK-EXTRA is used for the comb-sort to hold an extra copy
of the record.  It would be nice to define WRK-OCCURS and WRK-EXTRA as
constants, but I don't want to have 3000 & 3001 hard coded into the
PROCEDURE DIVISION.

For efficiency, the numbers can be packed and even signed (although
signing them makes more work in displaying).
```

- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com> <3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu> <395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca> <39587B6A.500D7EDE@zip.com.au> <3958DE4C.1DA5DE36@dced.state.ak.us> <3959F9FA.3003815@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote >
> The WRK-EXTRA is used for the comb-sort to hold an extra copy
> of the record.

Howard,
It is good to see that you use comb-sort. I had occasion to meet
with Richard Box (co-inventor of comb-sort) a little while ago.
He tells me that it has been impossible to publish comb-sort
in any of the 'academic' journals. It is not academic enough
and is usually confused with Shell-sort. Shell-sort has an
N**1.3 dependence on the number (N) of entries, while
comb-sort has a true N*logN dependence, just like
quick-sort.
```

may be the fastest.
