###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is COMB SORT ?

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395C1C3E.96E0AE01@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu> <395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca> <39587B6A.500D7EDE@zip.com.au> <3958DE4C.1DA5DE36@dced.state.ak.us> <3959F9FA.3003815@cusys.edu> <1ho65.68923$cH1.592344@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <395A843F.4CB0A0FC@cusys.edu> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000629081457.6496B-100000@eclipse>`

```
Jonathan Nash wrote:

> Is COMB SORT like MERGE SORT ?

It is not like a merge sort - the underlying algorithm is entirely
different.

> Is there a web page describing COMB SORT ?

Yes,  http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm , but the code posted
there, especially the monstrosity they have the audacity of calling
quicksort is a rather poor parody on programming.

> |Comb sort is so easy and simple, virtually as simple as a bubble sort.
> |Even with bad files it is pretty fast, and with mostly sorted files it
> |may be the fastest.

There is no such thing as a universal 'bad' file to sort, as for different
sorting methods, 'bad' files have different properties. That is, for a
'classic' quicksort (i.e. for which no measures have been taken to
pseudo-randomize the choice of a pivot), a perfectly sorted file is the
worst case scenario. For insertion, Shell, bubble, Comb, and some others
that would be a reversed-sorted file.

> |> It is good to see that you use comb-sort. I had occasion to meet
> |> with Richard Box (co-inventor of comb-sort) a little while ago.
> |> He tells me that it has been impossible to publish comb-sort
> |> in any of the 'academic' journals.

The inventors of the technique should have communicated the method directly
to Donald Knuth. The absence of a thorough theoretical explanation of the
way Comb sort works should not be a hurdle, for the same is true of Shell
sort.

> It is not academic enough
> |> and is usually confused with Shell-sort.

There is a good reason for the confusion. Comb sort is based on improving
bubble sort in exactly the same way Shell sort improves insertion sort. So,
the idea was not new. Moreover, one reading about Batcher's parallel sort
in Knuth, v.3, and Comb sort in 'Byte' would have a hard time getting rid
of the impression that there are a lot of similarities.

> Shell-sort has an
> |> N**1.3 dependence on the number (N) of entries, while
> |> comb-sort has a true N*logN dependence,

Right. But the O(*) dependence does not tell the whole story. If it takes a
zillion hours to sort 1000 entries, and the sorting time grows as
O(N*logN), nobody needs this kind of sort. That is not to say that Comb
sort exhibits such a tendency; it does not. However, in the real world of
practical coding, it does not outperform Shell by any considerable margin
for those values of N where using of either makes sense.

> just like
> |> quick-sort.

Again, that quicksort has the same big-O dependency, is not the whole
story. I have coded and benchmarked almost all sorting algorithms in a
number of languages (including COBOL, PL/I, Fortran, C, SAS, etc.), and
Comb sort stands the comparison with quicksort only where N does not exceed
several thousand keys. Above that, and certainly at upwards of 100,000
records, quicksort rapidly runs away. However, quicksort is not a panacea,
either. As a matter of fact, the oldest sorting method used in the 1923 IBM
sorting machine  - LSD radix sort - is the fastest on modern computers
where memory references and computations can be highly overlapped. Besides,
it sorts in linear time, that is, O(N). I have compared it with quicksort
using IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1, SAS 6.09e and SAS 8.0,  all run
under OS/390 on IBM 9672-R56. It takes LSD radix sort about 3 CPU seconds
to order a table with 1,000,000 8-digit keys. Quicksort takes about 2-3
times as long. Interestingly, the results for either SAS version (using
double float for all internal numeric operations) and COBOL (all COMPs and
TRUNC(OPT)) were practically the same.

Kind regards,
====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
====================
```

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
> 
> > |Comb sort is so easy and simple, virtually as simple as a bubble sort.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> that would be a reversed-sorted file.

Of course. I was referring to comb sort bad files.
 
...
 
> Again, that quicksort has the same big-O dependency, is not the whole
> story. I have coded and benchmarked almost all sorting algorithms in a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> TRUNC(OPT)) were practically the same.

I would be interested in learning about this LSD radix sort.  But I have
to say that the number # criterion for coding a sort in a COBOL program
is that it be easier (to code and maintain) a hard coded sort than to
break up the program and use a utility sort.  The only times I have hard
coded sort code was to sort an internally generated and internally used
table.  When we have a command to sort an internal table, I won't use it
at all.  Certainly complex sorting mechanisms have no place being coded
in a COBOL program.
```

```
Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> Personally, I use a MAX-PAGE-LINES variable. I don't feel that hard-coding
> values that might change enhances maintainability. If you do hard-code them
> however, then I agree with Ken, it should be in only 1 place.

You are improving the situation but if you change the fundamental way
lines are counted how many places do you need to modify the code?

I would probably not make an issue about this in real life, I am
making this point here to so that we understand the difference and
trade off in our code.

Centralize the business logic of printing a report, holding lines
together, etc. into a single routine.  This takes a mind set shift
from generating a report inside the calculation bit.  Separate your
presentation from your calculation.  Separate your screen from your
business logic.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

#### ↳ Re: Program Logic??

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tox45.1245$ei2.169381@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<B8e45.71$9W1.6951@news-west.usenetserver.com>`

```
Ah, trying to master the control break, huh?

You could always use (pseudo-code)..

PERFORM UNTIL The-file-is-at-end
   READ The-file
     AT END
        PERFORM SUBTOTALS
     NOT AT END
     IF New-category
        PERFORM SUBTOTALS
     ELSE
        update current subtotals
     END-IF
   END-READ
END-PERFORM
```

Can anybody suggest any sites or books that deal authoritatively and
ACCURATELY with the History of Computing. No, not Charles Babbidge,
Hollerith etc.., but 1930 onwards.

Reason for request - the latest historical travesty from Holywood -
U571. (Seems the film producers forgot it was the RN who got their hands
on ENIGMA, not USN, and the existence of a certain gentleman at
Bletchley Park Signals Unit, called Alan Turing). Oh boy ! Go take a
look at SocialHistory.WWII newsgroup.

It would be nice to know who really did what in the early days of
computing.

Jimmy

I'm not specifically interested in the ENIGMA saga, although it's

remember title, catch it if you see a reference to it - there was an

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: History of Computing

- **From:** B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39528154.8BD467B@fridaycs.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<395164B7.982944CE@home.com> <3951A318.7B369D6@worldnet.att.net> <39523387.497306D2@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> William Lynch wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

I think that you will find that Alan Turing Way is in Manchester, not
Birmingham - he was working at Manchester University and committed
suicide in Manchester in 1954.

As far as I am aware, the first stored program computer (ie. the
forebear of todays computers) was built at Manchester University in 1948
to test the William's Storage Tube and was nick-named 'Baby'. This was
followed by a larger machine known as the Mark I. I believe that the
concepts of paging/virtual memory were developed here as well.

A replica machine was built for the 50th aniversery and is on display at
the Manchester Museum of Science.

Shortly afterwards, EDSAC was built at Cambridge (England) University
using mercury delay line storage (core for the mark 2) - this is the
line from which the LEO machines were derived.

We are in the 50th aniversery year of the Pilot ACE (built for the
National Physics Laboratory - Teddington), again Alan Turing was very
involved with the design. A meeting was held at the Science Museum,
London, sponsored by the British Computer Society - Computer Consevation
Group a couple of weeks ago, with speakers who were involved with the
project and worked with Turing. A full scale ACE was later built and ran
for many years. Again, I think that a replica is being built.

Donald Davis CBE FRS the pioneer of packet switching (without which the
internet wouldn't function) died on May 28th. He worked with the ACE
group at the NPL with Alan Turing. His design was used by the NPL
Network and Arpanet.

Simulators of (some of) these early machines can be found on the British
Computer Society - Computer Conservation Group Web Site
http://www.cs.man.ac.uk/CCS/

The British certainly come up with the concepts and ideas even if we
can't seem to exploit them commercially, as can the Americans (and it
seems most other countries).

Brian
```

Jim, my favorite book on the subject is "The Computer from Pascal to
von Newmann", Herman H. Goldstein, Princeton University Press, C 1972.
Don't know if it's still in print, but if you want to know Who, When
and How, Goldstein was intimately involved.
```

- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<395164B7.982944CE@home.com> <00N45.6547$uf2.156624@news4.mia>`

```
An earlier book (again, probably out of print but might be in libraries) is "Automatic 
Digital Calculators", by Andrew and Kathleen Booth (then of the University of 
Sakatchewan).  First published in 1953 by Butterworths - several later updates. My 1965 
edition deals with Fortran, but no reference to COBOL.  The latest picture in it is an 
IBM 1311 disk drive!

With VS COBOL II R4, IBM used to have a 'Application Programming
Reference Summary', it was Document Number SX26-3721-05. Does anybody
know whether this manual is still available?
I looked at:
http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/coblib.htm
but there is no more 'old' documentation and with the 'new' compilers,
there is no more Reference Summary.

Does anybody know of a (printable) source of a Reference Summary either
from IBM or another party?

#### ↳ Re: Language Reference Sunnary?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ircj4$cjp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8iqoca$b9o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Daniel,
  It was several products ago that IBM "dropped" that document.  (My memory
is that the original AD/Cycle SAA COBOL/370 had one - but none since then
did.)  When this manual was "first dropped,"  I (semi-officially) complained
to the IBM COBOL DOX people - but was basically told that the thing had
gotten "too big" and that with people using online documentation (and help)
it was no longer (thought to be) needed.

I still disagree with the decision - and suggest that you contact:

 A) IBM COBOL DOX people (via the address in any manual)
 B) You IBM "sales" channel

requesting that this manual be "resurrected".

P.S.  The other manual that I REALLY miss is the "cross-manual index" that
was available in very early releases of VS COBOL II - but not since.
```

Maybe you can write a program to print out the alphabet using the ordinal
value of the alphabet.  I am thinking there is some sort of chatacter
conversion file being accessed like pc communications uses.  Try a simple
program in C or Pascal on the system without being in COBOL and maybe one
inside of the COBOL Compiler environment.  Maybe there is a setting for how
the compiler or the Linux environment chooses the file structure to store
the file in.  If you cant write that in COBOL try it in pascal.  I know you
can assign a customized sorting sequence why not on your editor!

Good Luck.

<a id="t-da075f90a7"></a>
## Thread: wrong characters on COBOL in Linux — 1 msgs

### wrong characters on COBOL in Linux

- **From:** gnrfan@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.sco,comp.unix.linux,alt.unix.wizards
- **Message-ID:** `<8iqo20$b4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello everyone.

My boss has a program written in COBOL that initially was running on an
SCO unix box using the RUNCOBOL command. Now we need to run the same
cobol program on a Linux box. Our distro is Conectiva Linux. Our kernel
has the iBCS module that afaik lets me run SCO apps under Linux. We are
using the RM/COBOL SCO program as interpreter. We have the sources and
some binaries and perhaps we can compile it in the linux box. The
problem we have is with CONSOLE CHARACTERS they
are all screwed up and changed. The program worked on another linux
installation. I picked the terminal type running on that working setup
with INFOCMP > linux.ti and then I took that file to my machine and
compiled it with TIC to obtain the terminal file. Previous to that
change there was almost no output in the terminal of type "linux". Now
with this "new" terminal (TYPE=linscobol) most thing work ok, the
problem is some characters are displayed wrong. This pointed me to try
with several fonts to match the proper encoding. I am
guessing the correct encoding using SETFONT with all the .psf console
fonts under Linux but havent get a nice match yet. The working linux
installation in a linux box used cp850-8x14 as font. Using SETFONT
cp850-8x14 or CONSOLECHARS -f cp850-8x14 changes the font but I cant
still get the correct mapping match. This cp850 encoding seems to
be "IBM Latin-1" but using Latin-1 fonts havent fixed the problem too
much. So after reading the whole case WHAT IS WRONG? WHAT AM I MISSING?
Got an idea? I am in kind of desperate need for a fix of this problem
so your help is extremely valuable.


Thanks a lot.

Antonio




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

Jim, I get all your posts as private mail.  Then, ten minutes after I
respond, I find them in here and am not sure if I should respond again or
not.

 I'll cut and paste from my "sent message".

These are simple(but nested) source code copies ... if it will compile there
is no problem.
    IE:
program one has in it:
    COPY "ABC.CBL".
ABC.CBL has in it:
    COPY "DEF.CBL".
DEF.CBL has in it:
    COPY "XYZ.CBL".



James J. Gavan wrote in message <3951057A.36F3872B@home.com>...
>

```

#### ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tm845.773$0M2.1047@client>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net...
> I am doing some design, and want to use nested copies.  They do work on
> Fujitsu, but I have never used them before.  Is this something that is
going
> to jump up and bite me?  IE: is this common now, or is it a Fujitsu
"extra"?

Donald,

I cannot imagine an implementation that does not allow for nested COPY
statements, though the 1985 standard is silent about this.  I don't recall
whether the Cobol Compiler Validation Suite attempts nested COPY statements.

RM/COBOL allows for nesting to 5 levels, with a relaxation to 9 levels if
one uses a "tail COPY" technique wherein the last statement of a COPY file
is a COPY statement (the limit of 9 is imposed by the listing format that
has one print position for COPY nesting level).

These limits have been in place since RM was the OEM compiler for many
midrange computers.  I am unaware of anyone ever constrained by these
limits.

So, follow your nesting instinct.  :)

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Nested copies *are* prohibited in the '85 Standard.  (Send me private email
if you need/want an explanation of this.)  HOWEVER, they are a very common
extension in many implementations today.

They will be EXPLICITLY allowed (as a substantive change) in the next
Standard.

There is a "place" where you *may* run into problems - and that is if you use
REPLACING.  I believe that the draft Standard explicitly disallows any
REPLACING phrase in nested COPIES.  Some existing extensions allow it in the
"bottom" level of nested copies.   If you have a compiler that allows it at
other than the bottom level, I think you will find NON-portable results.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395140E4.D1CBB01B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Nested copies *are* prohibited in the '85 Standard.  (Send me private email
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don,

I checked the NetExpress on-line for specific comments that Tom (Liant)
made - could see no limit on number of copies. However Bill's point
above, N/E does specifically exclude REPLACING in nested copies.

Jimmy
```

I know that the current implementation of REPLACING is terrible.  (Most
people want to call up a current copy module, replacing the prefix with
another prefix), but what is the reason for this proscription?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8it3p7$c83$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39520E9D.87F43702@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <39520E9D.87F43702@cusys.edu>...
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
>>
>> There is a "place" where you *may* run into problems - and that is if you
use
>> REPLACING.  I believe that the draft Standard explicitly disallows any
>> REPLACING phrase in nested COPIES.  Some existing extensions allow it in
the
>> "bottom" level of nested copies.   If you have a compiler that allows it
at
>> other than the bottom level, I think you will find NON-portable results.
>
>I know that the current implementation of REPLACING is terrible.  (Most
>people want to call up a current copy module, replacing the prefix with
>another prefix), but what is the reason for this proscription?

How about a level one copy that copies in a list of second level copies,
replacing the extensions for different compilers depending on a switch?   It
would be neat to be able to do ...
(not that I have any intention of even trying it)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8itacj$nda$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39520E9D.87F43702@cusys.edu>`

```
The problem with REPLACING is when it gets turned on and off - and what it
impacts.  For example:

Copy level1  replacing ==level2== by ==levela==.

where level1 has

    Copy level2.

Does this or does this not change the name of what is copied? (the rules of
when replacing takes place would USUALLY say no).

Also, how about

Copy level1 replacing ==text1== by ==text2==.

Level1 has

copy Level2 replacing ==text1== by ==text3==.

Does the original REPLACING change the replacing in the 2nd COPY or not?
(arguments pro and con both exist).

finally, consider

Copy level1 replacing ==text1== by ==text2==.

Level1 has

 Copy level2.

and Level2 has

   05  Text1  Pic X.

Is the original "replacing" still in effect or not?  Probably it would be -
but the "owner" of Level1 that THINKS they are doing a COPY without replacing
would be surprised by this.

Generally, rules COULD be written for all of these, but they would be more
complex than most people would want and the committees didn't think the
benefits would outweigh the problems.

FYI,
  You *may* use the (newly enhanced) REPLACE statement in these situations to
do what you (probably) want - so the functionality is there, just not the
specific syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested copies

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395244D8.75BD3812@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39520E9D.87F43702@cusys.edu> <8itacj$nda$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> FYI,
>   You *may* use the (newly enhanced) REPLACE statement in these situations to
> do what you (probably) want - so the functionality is there, just not the
> specific syntax.

I suspect because the current REPLACE clause was so poorly thought out
that it isn't used very much - this won't be a problem.

But I would like to have nested copies with my program's prefix for
every name.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested copies

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8itsic$tv7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39520E9D.87F43702@cusys.edu> <8itacj$nda$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <395244D8.75BD3812@cusys.edu>`

```
The draft standard includes "leading" and "trailing" options for *BOTH*
COPY/REPLACING and REPLACE statement  - to allow for prefix changes (without
using the "(TAG)" type circumvention).
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lwc45.4074$uf2.4691@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net>`

```
It is generally supported with the caveat that nested 'copy' stmts can't use
"REPLACE".

news:8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net...

going

### cics error aica

- **From:** john_lev@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iu2up$qfl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
how to process aica error in cics to continue endless loop ?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: cics error aica

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iucfb$18i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8iu2up$qfl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8iu2up$qfl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  john_lev@my-deja.com wrote:
> how to process aica error in cics to continue endless loop ?
>
> Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
> Before you buy.
>

Abend AICA is a CICS Runaway (Looping) Task. The timeout-interval for
runaway tasks is defined to the SIT (System Initialization Table). A
common value is 5000ms/10000ms (milliseconds).

As far as how to prevent AICA's ? You will have to debug the program
using a 3rd party debugger (or, if you're handy with CEDF, that could
work also). You'll then have to locate the loop and correct the problem.

Making the determination as to what the problem could be must be
performed by the programmer. There are no hard and fast reasons. Just
grunt time to figure it out.

AICA's are VERY common in CICS Applications, so don't despair. you'll
find it.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: How to compile mixed lang. for animator — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to compile mixed lang. for animator

- **From:** majorchaos <markNOmaSPAM@markcoulter.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f248716.196d43a5@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com>`

```
Hi,

We have been trying to get our programs to work with animator,
with no success.

We are running on SunOS 5.5.1 (unix).  Using Object Cobol 4.1
for Unix.

We have several c functions that we call from our cobol
programs.  For setup and testing animator, I wrote 3 very small
programs.  Two cobol and one c.

Compile command:
cob -axv mytest.cob mytest2.cob hello.c

They compiled ok.  Mytest executes as it should.  However, there
are no hello.int or hello.gnt files.  So when you anim mytest,
when the cobol program performs the call c-func it returns an
error 173.  I have tried several thing to get this to work.  We
don't have any dll's, that I know of.

Thanks

Mark Coulter

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

#### ↳ Re: How to compile mixed lang. for animator

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39528103.873C911A@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<1f248716.196d43a5@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com>`

```
This probably isn't what you are looking for, but I would use a system
call to the O/S.

01  UNIX-Command-Do-Hello.
     02                          PIC X(5)  VALUE  "hello".
     02                          PIC X      VALUE  LOW-VALUES.

Procedure division.
       Call "SYSTEM" Using UNIX-Command-Do-Hello

Animator, of course, is not going to step through your C program, and
you will need to compile your C program separately, but this will get
the job done.

Now as for passing data between the programs without writing to a temp
file, if you find out, let me know, will ya?

John Grznar

majorchaos wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.keen.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to compile mixed lang. for animator

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uMv45.43093$7o1.1157685@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<1f248716.196d43a5@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com> <39528103.873C911A@earthlink.net>`

```


"John Grznar" <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:39528103.873C911A@earthlink.net...
> This probably isn't what you are looking for, but I would use a system
> call to the O/S.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> file, if you find out, let me know, will ya?

You might want to look into the interprocess communication mechanisms
supported by your OS. Use man or info to find out whether pipes, messages,
semaphores and shared memory are supported. Most of them are part of the
System V standard.

- Karl



>
> John Grznar
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How to compile mixed lang. for animator

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3953269A.952FB6D0@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<1f248716.196d43a5@usw-ex0105-037.remarq.com>`

```
If you create a new "runtime" with your 'C' modules in, then you will
be able  animate the COBOL and call the 'C' functions.

For example:

cc -c hello.c
ar crv libhello.a hello.o
cob -vxo myrts -L. -lhello
cob -via *.cob
COBSW=+A ./myrts mytest
```

## Thread: DG AOS/VS II Cobol Ver 4.3 Problem --- Update — 1 msgs

### DG AOS/VS II Cobol Ver 4.3 Problem --- Update

- **From:** "John A. Simpson" <jasimp@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395270EA.75EB6B55@earthlink.net>`

```
First, thanks to all of you for responding both to the group and via
email.
It was greatly appreciated.

I was finally able to get hold of a Data General tech rep, even though
they no longer support the product. I even got help for no $$. Anyway,
it turns out that this Cobol ($350.00) does not support indexed files.
Can you believe that??? Truly the evil twin. The solution is to buy the
Data General INFOS package that does the ISAM file handling for
$5600.00. I can't wait to see the look on my client's face when he hears
that!

OK, off to the web to find an alternative solution.

Thanks again Guys and Gals.

John
```

form:

Manfred

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

#### ↳ Re: MF-Cobol function length ()

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j5826$vtg$1@news.chatlink.com>`
- **References:** `<3953F957.FCFACA0E@excite.com>`

```
I do alot of VB to Micro Focus Cobol mixed-language programming, and
these are the methods I use.  Not saying that they are the best, but
they have worked for me for the past 5 years with no coding changes.  I
develop on a Windows NT/9.x platform, as well as (used to) an OS/2
platform.

* Define a signed Long Integer (4 bytes):
01  PICX-LENGTH  PIC S9(9) COMP-5.

* Move the length to the long integer:
MOVE LENGTH OF PIC-X-VARIABLE TO PICX-LENGTH

* Call the C program:
CALL "c_program to call" USING
                    PIC-X-VARIABLE
                    PICX-LENGTH

Also, I have always had better luck passing all integer and long numeric
data before string data.  Hope it helps ...

Todd Lucas
E-Mail:  Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net
Web:   http://www.c-cubed.net


Manfred Friedrich <mfriedrich@excite.com> wrote in message
news:3953F957.FCFACA0E@excite.com...
> Hello,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Manfred
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF-Cobol function length ()

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j5q7c$9jq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3953F957.FCFACA0E@excite.com> <8j5826$vtg$1@news.chatlink.com> <39564797.A7F27E6F@home.com>`

```
Jimmy - well "yes and no",

It is true that the "LENGTH" special register is a Micro Focus (well
originally IBM) extension - which will *not* be part of the next COBOL
Standard.  *HOWEVER*, the next Standard *will* allow the omission of the
"noise" word FUNCTION before any of the intrinsic function names - IF AND
ONLY IF - you declare in your (new) repository paragraph that you haven't
used them as "user-defined words".

FYI,
  You may also specify certain functions that are used this way - while
others still require the word "function".  You may also "declare"
user-defined functions to be used this way as well.  The word "function"
remains required as the default - but not in all case.

FYI2,
  Of course the use of COMP-5 already takes the program out of being
Standard-conforming in this - or the next Standard.  To  make this "standard
conforming" today, you would need to use "FUNCTION LENGTH" *and* USAGE COMP
with the COMP-5 (as I recall) directive to turn COMP into COMP-5.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF-Cobol function length ()

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j6jcs$eep$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3953F957.FCFACA0E@excite.com> <8j5826$vtg$1@news.chatlink.com> <39564797.A7F27E6F@home.com> <8j5q7c$9jq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3956939A.85FB5153@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
 <snip>
>
> Thanks for the clarifications Bill - but I think the solutions you offer
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you want to use "ANSI/ISO conforming binary fields" that are in intel
"little-endian" format, then define them with COMP (not COMP-5) as the
usage - and use the COMP-5 (I think) Micro Focus directive.  The only problem
that you would have is if you ALSO are getting some fields in "big-endian"
format (say from an IBM mainframe) into the same application at the same
time.  There is a "solution" to even that problem, but it is a "little
obscure" and I won't go into it, unless someone really cares.

My guess is that if you compile your existing COBOL code with the FLAGSTD
directive you would be AMAZED at how much "non-standard" code you have.  This
is true for NON-OO code, much less OO code - and true for any implementor who
is FIPS compiliant (and includes a "FIPS flagging" mechanism).
```

#### ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j27r3$mvm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8j101u$muv$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <8j101u$muv$1@news.igs.net>,
  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
> Does anybody have any words of wisdom regarding telephone numbers?  I
am
> going to be revising the telephone numbers in a set of master records
to
> handle fax service over the next few months.  Currently, the system
is only
> being used in North America, but I would like to be able to handle
Europe as
> well, as I have several potential orders over there.

I did research some time ago because I had heard that the North
American System was running out of numbers.  I found a report on plans
for an expanded telephone numbering system for North America at
ftp://ftp.atis.org/pub/clc/inc/99121025.doc (WARNING this is a Word
document).  It appears that there is no agreement on the format. Some
of the proposals are 4+3+4, 4+4+4 and 1+3+3+4.  However, there seems to
be general agreement that it will contain no more than 12 digits.

For a global system, you would have to allow space for punctuation
(since it varies by country), so a single 14 character field should be
adequate.  This would handle any of the proposed new standards.  It
should also be adequate for other countries - at least until China or
India start to approach North American levels of phone use.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3954ACEA.1E6BF34A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8j101u$muv$1@news.igs.net> <8j27r3$mvm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne wrote:

> For a global system, you would have to allow space for punctuation
> (since it varies by country), so a single 14 character field should be
> adequate.  This would handle any of the proposed new standards.  It
> should also be adequate for other countries - at least until China or
> India start to approach North American levels of phone use.

It has been a while but the siemens switch uses 16 digit numbers
internally.  My memory might be bad though.

I think that the format of the numbers is more important.  If you want
to store just the digits then you should store the international
number so my country code is '61' my region is '2'.  I would allow
entry on the screen as 02 .... (the zero is dropped internationally)
and reformat on output.  This allows the numbers to cross country
boundaries with ease and make the format routine a little easier.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

digit country code.

IIRC, there are some countries that use multiple formats.  Knowing the
country code is not enough to format the number correctly.  There are
also many different formats used throughout the world.  If you want an
accurate international system, its easier to let the users set the
formatting.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39552817.F74D8999@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8j101u$muv$1@news.igs.net> <8j27r3$mvm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
14 digits is enough? Australia from America
"+61-3-9557-6157x4321" (my old phone number) = 20 characters. 

How many m^dBillions of dollars have been wasted because someone
tried to optimize field size (eg Y2k)? How big is the database?
How much would the extra few bytes actually cost vs the cost of
fixing it later?

Given the cost of disk space vs the cost of changing all the
programs and databases later it is sensible to err on the side of
larger fields. IMHO.

Certainly one thing to avoid is any assumption that the phone
number is seven digits. Numerous US web sites reject my phone
number for this reason.

Tim Josling

Ed Milne wrote:
> ... For a global system, you would have to allow space for punctuation
> (since it varies by country), so a single 14 character field should be
> adequate.  This would handle any of the proposed new standards.  It
> should also be adequate for other countries - at least until China or
> India start to approach North American levels of phone use...
```

Hi Volker,


It is Report Program Generator. Originally developed to ease development 
on IBM S370 and IBM S360 mainframes. These mainframes use to work with the 
card readers and programers on the deck of punched cards. The purpose was 
to have less punched cards with less coding for printing programs. RPG 
than transferred to S3X, where it was used extensively, with latter 
developments of RPGII and RPG/400 and now RPG/IV or RPG/ILE it is not much 
more a structed language.

admin@business-generator.com

Hi,

                     TV Brand                      19 inch colour

                       Sony                     599             999

Junwen

#### ↳ Re: Table Descriptions

- **From:** "V���ronique BILLARD" <veronique.billard@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jgani$qkv$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<3954b12d$0$16996$7f31c96c@news01.syd.optusnet.com.au>`

```
Hello from the most beautiful country in the world !
I have stop cobol three years ago (for Visual FoxPro). So my explanations
can be little wrong... but Its something like...
01   my-TV-table.
02        some-libs.
    10    a-lib01    filler pic X(20) value "Sony".
    10    a-lib02    pic 9(3) value 599.
    10    a-lib03    pic 9(3) value 999.
*
    10    b-lib01   pic x(20) value "Sharp".
    10    b-lib02 pic 9(3) value279.
and so on...
*
02  some-cell redefines some-libs.
    10    cells occurs 5.
        20    tv-brand pic X(20).
        20    manual pic 9(3).
        20    remote pic 9(3).
You may have problems with levels... but can adjust them
Bon courage from France
Eric
```

#### ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com>`

```
The main words of wisdom is to code for easy maintenance as things will
change.

An example is the recent US phone policy of having two area codes
sharing the same geographic location, both as local calls requiring us
to enter area codes without a 1 prefix.  That broke dialing programs and
PBXs.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3957C747.34E29F79@larich.com>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com> <395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> An example is the recent US phone policy of having two area codes
> sharing the same geographic location, both as local calls requiring us
> to enter area codes without a 1 prefix. 

Really?  Here in Cleveland they've recently split us into 3 area codes
and you have to dial the 1 prefix for any number outside the area code
you're in.  The phone company figures out if the call is really 'long
distance' or should be considered a local call.  Our dialing programs
were not affected since they already knew the local area code and could
simply add the 1 prefix when needed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3958A561.2FC384A4@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com> <395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu> <3957C747.34E29F79@larich.com>`

```


"David W. Furin" wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply add the 1 prefix when needed.

Really.  I moved from one Denver suburb to another changing area codes
as I described above.  I have family members around the country, and
there are no consistent rules.  Sometimes they get new zip codes when
they split a region.  Sometimes the phone companies work it as you
described above.  I have also seen payment plans where it is a local
call between, say East St. Louis and St. Louis, but out of state calls
from suburbs of each city to the other.  Business software and PBXs want
to know if they are extra charge calls.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39590669.3051679@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com> <395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu> <3957C747.34E29F79@larich.com> <3958A561.2FC384A4@cusys.edu> <3958FC69.BAF25313@larich.com>`

```
I foresee the time when we do not call phone numbers.  We won't even
call phones, we will call people or their electronic agents.

But your calling software has to be smart enough to know who you are
wanting to call, and certainly your receiving software (agent) has to be
very flexible.

The agent will look at features of the caller and call, check to see
what you're doing, and then decide whether to buzz, ring, leave voice
mail, leave e-mail, ask the caller to phone back later, report the
caller to the authorities, or tell the caller to bug off.

When you get back to your computer, you browse through messages and
listen to the voice mail you desire, or read your agent's description. 
Getting phone calls will be better.

Sending calls?  I suppose this might be harder to set up.  Once you
establish a nick name, you would use it, but you might have to say "call
my wife as soon as both she and I are back at our desks with our
computers on and my boss isn't in my cubicle with me".

So what will we save in our business databases?  We don't want to be
tracked as spammers in a customer's database.  Our database will
probably use an agent to talk to the customer's agent periodically to
check on data currency.  Our customer will have privacy rules which have
been customized for different callers, but he might be willing to let us
query on how much soap his rest rooms have.

Don't you hate it when a salesman for the newspaper you already
subscribe phones you to offer you a great deal (which isn't offered to
existing customers)?  Why don't they KNOW you are a customer?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

```
Worse, why don't they offer the same deals to their *loyal* customers.
Insurance companies (at least mine) know how to do it. Your discount
increases based on the number of years your've been with the company.
('Course they generally raise their rates more than the discount increases,
but at least they raise their rates for everyone, not just for their loyal
customers.)

As for dialing by name, I've had that on my phone for over 5 years now.
(Voice-activated would be nice however.) It's totally ruined my ability to
remember my daughter's phone number. Thank God my mother hasn't moved or
changed her phone number since I left home.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3959FB84.EFA62E4F@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com> <395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu> <3957C747.34E29F79@larich.com> <3958A561.2FC384A4@cusys.edu> <3958FC69.BAF25313@larich.com> <39590669.3051679@cusys.edu> <39593D22.F028DB6C@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> As for dialing by name, I've had that on my phone for over 5 years now.
> (Voice-activated would be nice however.) It's totally ruined my ability to
> remember my daughter's phone number. Thank God my mother hasn't moved or
> changed her phone number since I left home.

I've never been good at remembering names or numbers - I can remember
weird trivia which has meaning, but labels have no meaning.  I can
remember the phone number I had in grade school without thinking
(Clifford-4 3079), but have to stop and think to remember my current
phone number.  I don't know any other phone numbers at all (fortunately,
my wife is usually near by when I want to call my mother, daughter, son,
or sister who live locally).

When I was a bachelor, I wanted to learn my phone number, but why?  If I
called it, either there was nobody home, or the line was busy!!  8^)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3957D827.CE28D90B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com> <395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> The main words of wisdom is to code for easy maintenance as things will
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> PBXs.

However you implement it, make sure this is a variable (easily changed,
too). I'll bet the Cleveland arrangement (no "1" prefix) will change
when the demand for new numbers gets to a certain level. IIRC, we had
the same setup locally (NYC) when TPC first broke NYC into multiple area
codes, 212 & 718 the first time around. Originally NYC was all 212, then
Brooklyn, Queens & Staten Island were split off into 718, then The Bronx
was split off into 718, too, leaving just Manhattan using 212. Next TPC
added another area code, which is pagers & cell phones (my cell phone &
my son's cell phone (he lives in Brooklyn) are both 917), my best
friends' cell phone (who also lives in Brooklyn) is 646). NYC is an
extreme situation because of the explosive demand for pagers, cell
phones, fax machines, etc. for business use; but it's the direction
other areas are headed, IMHO. On the bright side, the calls amongst
these area codes are still local calls. 

Bill Lynch

(TPC = The Phone Co.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3958f5d7.23231125@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <39543362.663F62AE@home.com> <395759F8.6C5D8848@cusys.edu> <3957D827.CE28D90B@worldnet.att.net>`

```
William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>these area codes are still local calls. 

Bill:

Here in Eastern Pennsyltucky, the phone company implemented "overlaid"
area codes a couple years ago for the same reason.  Now you have to
dial the area code for ALL calls whether they are local or long
distance, outside or within the same area code.

It's possible that the guy next door has a different area code than
any of his surrounding neighbors, but calling him is still a local
call.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Graphical User Interface — 6 msgs

### Graphical User Interface

- **From:** "Steven Helsen" <s.helsen@planetinternet.be>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j15e1$mjf$1@news.planetinternet.be>`

```
Hi,

I'm planning on making a program (a quiz) in Cobol.
To make it more attractive, I would like to make use of a GUI (I'm kinda fed
up with typing 100s of lines in the screen section just to obtain some lousy
dos-screebs). I also want to work with *.gif or *.jpg pictures (i.e.
displaying them on the screen during the program). I'm using CA-Realia 3.0
on a Win-platform.
My questions:
* How can I get a more attractive "lay-out" for a Cobol-program?
* Is it possible to work with pictures in Cobol and, if yes, how to do so?
Thanks in advance,
Steven
s.helsen@planetinternet.be

PS: sorry for accidently sending this message twice
```

```

> * Is it possible to work with pictures in Cobol and, if yes, how to
do so?

Hi:

Check out SP2 from Flexus www.flexus.com - you can do graphics (BMP)
sounds, etc.

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sort of off-topic

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j43uc0jss@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<mta55.35905$9W1.886983@news-west.usenetserver.com> <39554F9D.B2144C7F@worldnet.att.net>`

```
If you are currently working, see if you're employer has positions for 
junior programmers.  Many companies try to hire "from within" when they can
and may have oppurtunities for people with little or no experience.

This is how I started in the "real world", about 2 years ago.  I had worked
for my employer (a large grocery store chain) for about 4 years when they
sent out a bulletin expressing interest in hiring from within about 10 new
programmers.

My company employs 27,000 people and it only makes sense that out of that
many people, there is bound to be 10 people who can program.  Companies
sometimes benefit from doing this kind of thing because they hire people who
have a better feel for how the company does business.

Getting a programming job from "off the street" with nothing on your resume
will be difficult.  You may have to start off somewhat lower on the
corporate rung.  Maybe a 'help desk' (or as we call it, the call
re-direction center) associate or a customer service rep or something like
that.  If the employer is worth anything, they will see your talents and put
you somewhere that you can do more good.

Good luck,
Jeff
(OS/390, COBOLII, IBM 4680/4690 O/S Point Of Sale system and SAV2
Supermarket Application)

----------
In article <39554F9D.B2144C7F@worldnet.att.net>, William Lynch
<wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote:


> Colonel wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch
```

Colonel wrote:

It depends upon the company.  There are some cheap companies which are
always looking for any programmers at all because they don't match
prevailing wages.  Governments are often in this boat as well.

## Thread: Sorting table.... — 8 msgs

### Sorting table....

- **From:** mburdette@infoave.net
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39550dbc.4741118@nntp.infoave.net>`

```
HI

I have populated a table with dates and I want to read the table in
ASCENDING order based on the day of the month in the table element. 

I have put 

    KEY IS WT-DATE ASCENDING

in the table declaration in WORKING STORAGE but am not reading it in
that order when i go to write out my data. I am stepping thru the
table by incrementing the INDEX, which I know is probably my problem;
any thoughts on how to solve this?

If you can, please email any responses to mburdette@infoave.net

Thanks in advance!
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting table....

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j373d$m29$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39550dbc.4741118@nntp.infoave.net>`

```
The "ascending key" describes how the data is STORED - not how it is
accessed.  Therefore, if your table isn't populated with the data in
ascending order, using that phrase will not "force" it into that order.

I don't know which compiler you are using, but some COBOL products have an
extension to the current Standard (which will be part of the next Standard)
to allow you to SORT a table - using the SORT verb.

As indicated in another note, your problem is (most likely) a "homework
assignment" - in which case, I suggest you look at the technique that your
class is expected to use, however, if you are using a COBOL compiler that
supports the "sort aTable" feature, you might find this a useful thing to
learn about.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting table....

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TV955.383$bJ5.145521@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<39550dbc.4741118@nntp.infoave.net>`

```
<mburdette@infoave.net> wrote in message
news:39550dbc.4741118@nntp.infoave.net...
> HI
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> that order when i go to write out my data.


Well, since it confused me lo those many years ago....

When you use the KEY IS xxx ASCENDING (or DESCENDING) in a table definition,
it does NOT "sort it for you."

The KEY IS xxx ASCENDING phrase means *you* will provide the table in sorted
order, which is what I suspect the point of this homework problem is.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting table....

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395647F9.85ABB78A@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<39550dbc.4741118@nntp.infoave.net>`

```
Assuming that the dates you are talking about are contained in a file,
you have, essentially, three alternatives:

1) Use the sort utility provided with your OS to sort the file by the
field representing the dates, and then, in your COBOL program, populate
the table from that file.
2) Sort the file via USING-GIVING in COBOL (INPUT/ OUTPUT procedures
appear to be an overkill in this case), and then populate the table.
3) First, populate the table from the unsorted file and code a few extra
lines of COBOL to sort the table in place.

From the computer standpoint, the variant 3 appears to be most sensible.
Sorting algorithms work fastest when an entire file is in memory - and
this is exactly what a table is. If your table has a couple of dozen
occurrences, any algorithm, even as poor as bubble or insertion sort,
will do the job rapidly enough. With about 10,000 entries, a more
efficient scheme, like Shell sort or so-called CombSort (that improves
bubble sort much in the same way Shell improves insertion sort) should be
used. With over 100,000+ keys, you cannot go very much wrong using
QuickSort, which is the best comparison-based all-round sorting method.
All the algorithms above are amply described in the literature. With the
exception of maybe QuickSort, they are no-brainers to code. Besides,
coding things like these is an excellent programming logic exercise.

Lastly, you can take advantage of the fact that your keys are dates, and
therefore they can be very easily mapped in one-to-one manner to a set of
natural numbers ranging no wider than 0 to 36500 for an entire century.
It means that, after transforming the keys to such integers via a simple
rapid computation, the table can be sorted in several lines of code using
key-indexed (distribution counting) sort - the fastest existing sorting
method. Distribution counting can also be found in any book on
algorithms, for instance, D.Knuth, R.Sedgewick, or
T.Standish.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
==================

mburdette@infoave.net wrote:

> HI
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance!
```

- **References:** `<39550dbc.4741118@nntp.infoave.net> <395647F9.85ABB78A@mediaone.net>`

```

<a id="t-3e793914da"></a>
## Thread: A question of style — 7 msgs

### A question of style

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j1dbp0jv5@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
A newbie, although seemingly competent programmer showed me some code that
he didn't like.  He commented about it having long paragraphs (they were
about 1 page long).

I bit my tongue and didn't want to say anything.  But I felt like saying
something like "Well, as long as the code is readable"  or  "I don't know
what it's doing, so I can't comment."  Instead, I shrugged not knowing what
to say.

I like to write nice little easy to read programs.  But sometimes, you get a
real humdinger, that is all over the place, and hard to understand or read.
I have coded one and one/half page paragraphs, sometimes 2 pages long.  I
could care less.

To me, sometimes, a nice, sequential listing of instructions is very clear.
GO TO's or not.  If I have a paragraph which represents a logical unit then
I don't really care how long it is.  As long as it's flow is clear and
correct.

Well, anyways, I had a little fun tonight.  I met up with a friend at the
Baltimore Inner Harbor (just beautiful on a nice night, Doc you've been
there I'm sure).  It was a guy friend ( I'm not gay, trust me on that ) but
we were just chillin, ya know?  I felt like I was in Mexico or something.
Everyone was partying and drinking and just having a great time.  Old,
young, colored, white, tall, short, pretty, ugly, all types of people.  It's
nice to see black and white people operating in perfect harmony, dancing and
just being happy.  What a better world this would be if people just got
along, and also if they didn't tailgate you while driving about 5 feet away
at 60 or 70 miles per hour.

It was the Operation Sail event where huge, almost unbelievable sailboats
sail into the harbor and stay a week or so.  Next week, all the boats go to
NYC for Independence Day.  Very cool.  Businesses were raking in the dough.

Anyways,  I guess the moral of the story is, don't say anything if you can't
say something nice.  And that is what I learned today.

Stoned,
Jeff
```

> GO TO's or not.  If I have a paragraph which represents a logical unit then
> I don't really care how long it is.  As long as it's flow is clear and

I'm with you on this, Jeff. I've always believed the "paragraph should
fit on a page" was arbitrary (capricious, too <g>) in the extreme. FWIW,
if it's straight line code, it's straight line code and I don't care if
it runs 4-5 pages (print images). Many years ago (1984) I wrote a
program to print vouchers for a commodities trading firm (precious
metals) and IIRC once I'd drilled down to cranking out print lines it
ran several pages (seemed like a lot, then).

Bill Lynch (who doesn't use GOTO's)

Solutions product) which is a COBOL 4GL.  Does anyone know of a book or
tutorial on Xgen?  It would be very helpful in learning Xgen if I had a book
or other literature besides the Manual.  I've done searches on the Internet
from several different engines as well as searching the Modis Solutions and
Unisys websites.  I think I saw something sometime ago about a book on Xgen,
but can't seem to find it again.

contact me at:  turleypd@gatecity.com

P. Turley

## Thread: Telephone numbers — 7 msgs

### Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@nospambigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nUr55.40763$FC6.938892@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET>`

```
While we are at it, does anyone know if the US postal service is going to
expand zip codes past zip+4?  I'm in the process of upgrading some field
lengths and I thought if anyone knew of any proposals for increasing it now
would be the time to fix the fields.  TIA.


donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET...
> Does anybody have any words of wisdom regarding telephone numbers?  I am
> going to be revising the telephone numbers in a set of master records to
> handle fax service over the next few months.  Currently, the system is
only
> being used in North America, but I would like to be able to handle Europe
as
> well, as I have several potential orders over there.
>
>
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Telephone numbers

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8J101U$MUV$1@NEWS.IGS.NET> <nUr55.40763$FC6.938892@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
If you are using the code in Canada, you only need six, but you should do an
uppercase conversion on them as the Canadian equivalent of a postal code is
alphanumeric.

>While we are at it, does anyone know if the US postal service is going to

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-96a5a3cd70"></a>
## Thread: Success stories for Cobol — 2 this month / 77 total

### Success stories for Cobol

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3957598C.D68D6138@zip.com.au>`

```

Following on from an email from Pete Dashwood.  Do we have a
collection of real success stories of migrating the current system to
a web enabled application using Cobol and tools.

I am sure we can get one from Vendors but this is really about the
guys at the coal face having success so that it is not seen as a
marketing blurb (well it is but it is for the programmers).

quote from Pete>>>>>>>>>>>>>>

What we need are some Application successes so that when the spotty
nerd
comes in and says: "we are going to replace COBOL with python", the
Senior
Exec can say: "Interesting you mention COBOL. I know nothing about it
of
course, but the TIMES this morning said that our competitors are using
it
with great success. Apparently they saved a fortune revamping their
Life
and Pensions Group and are poised to grab a market slice, after we
thought
they were no longer a threat. I'll be interested to see the backing
for
your recommendations...and the costs..."

Nerd goes away and grabs the TIMES. Now he consults with his mentor
back at
AA. They agree it would be wise to propose something that is likely to
be
readily accepted, so they propose a review of all existing COBOL
applications with a view to funding enhancement and new development
within
the same paradigm. They still get their team in and everyone is happy.

If you think the above scenario is far-fetched, I can assure you it is
(very loosely) based on actual events which I have personally been
privy
to.

Remember also that newspapers prefer items which go "against the
flow".

<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

I personally think this is a great idea.  We really need to advertise
the power of cobol.  I would go further than this.  

<Anology follows>

There was a major change in thinking for the apple industry here in
Australia, they used to advertise that apple A was better than apple
B.  They changed their tune, they realized their competition was
chocolate bars and now they are going from strength to strength.

What is the point of this ditty,  well Cobol is the apples.  We have
vendors thinking they are competing for the same market share, in
reality they are shrinking the market share by missing the major
point,  portability.

</anology>

We desperately need the vendors to closely work together to come up
with a better minimum standard including some of the basic tools that
programmers can easily get elsewhere.  This set needs to work on the
basic four Web, IBM, Unix and PC.

Cobol vendors are not competing for Cobol market share they are
competing with Delphi, Visual Basic (yuk), Small talk, etc.  When
cobol was king it had the VSAM file access built into the language
when other languages were just thinking about it,  this is what set it
aside from the other languages.  To be blunt what does it offer me
now.  Don't give me readable code, give me less code to write so I am
more productive.

We need the vendors and the standards committee to pull their fingers
out and really work on a minimum standard operational set for today's
computers including screen handling,  tools such as collections,
persistent objects, etc.  If the vendors fail to do this Cobol will
frankly loose my programming vote.

Now should I tell you what I really feel....

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

#### ↳ Re: Success stories for Cobol

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8j9q5f$q71$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3957598C.D68D6138@zip.com.au>`

```

MERANT has a number of high profile successes in this area.  For

http://www.merant.com/customer_stories/

All the best.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International

<a id="t-0bcdc08c58"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Question (and news on Support Net Renewal) — 4 msgs

### NetExpress Question (and news on Support Net Renewal)

- **From:** "Dave Begley" <dbegley@houston.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ncN55.65853$cH1.555258@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
(Sorry if this appears twice - I didn't see the message after my first try
at posting this).

Short Story:
How do I get a "Removal Key" so I can take NetExpress off one computer and
put it on another?

Long Story:
I am an independent developer and I use NetExpress for a couple clients
(they started out on MicroFocus COBOL years ago and I have been supporting
them).

I have used a notebook computer as my main system for a couple of years but
I now have a new desktop system that I would like to use for development.  I
tried for a couple months to get a second key for my desktop but I have been
unable to get Merant to see things my way.  I would like to develop on the
new system, but keep NetExpress on the notebook for when I visit clients, so
I can make last minute changes if needed.  All other software vendors have
given me the second key, but Merant won't do it.

Now no one answers me at all.  I think I have been 'black-balled' for
complaining for 2 months.  I have tried to find out how much a second
license would cost, but I haven't been able to get that answer.

Then I decided to remove NetExpress off my notebook, but I don't know how to
get a 'removal key' (whatever that is).  (I decided I could just dial in to
my main computer when I was on the road to do any last minute changes).  No
phone or email message has been returned.  So I am stuck.  I have a great
new machine and the 30 day use of NetExpress ended almost 2 months ago.

WHY IN THE WORLD WON'T MERANT LET ME RUN THEIR SOFTWARE ON TWO COMPUTERS?
WHY WON'T THEY TELL ME HOW MUCH A SECOND LICENSE COSTS?  WHY WON'T THEY GIVE
ME THE SUPPORT TO MOVE THE SOFTWARE TO A DIFFERENT
COMPUTER??????????????????????????????

Annual Renewal Information:
I received my renewal for NetExpress.  It is $650 and there is no mention of
any runtime fees.  I don't know if that means there are no fees, or if they
will surprise me with the additional charges after I pay the $650.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Question (and news on Support Net Renewal)

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000626171328.04827.00000971@ng-cp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ncN55.65853$cH1.555258@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Good Afternoon Dave,

Check the bottom of the letter they sent you for the new NetExpress Version 3.1
release:

"Please refer to your license agreement or contact your sales representative
for details on deployment charges with NetExpress 3.1".

Thanks,
Bob
```

I

been

so

to

to

<a id="t-cfa1547197"></a>
## Thread: Legacy systems in cybertime — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Legacy systems in cybertime

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3957D4E0.EDFE4D01@cusys.edu>`

```
The June 19th Computerworld had an article by Ed Yourdon, in which he
discussed the problems of legacy systems (nobody's around who programmed
them when they need maintenance) - and applied this to e-time.  He
suggested the same types of solutions we have been working at in our
older legacy systems to web processing.  Things such as extreme
programming, dual programming, knowledge transfer, etc.

It is interesting to think of systems which become legacy within 6
months - written in languages which are not designed to make maintenance
easy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Legacy systems in cybertime

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395812C5.B5865FC8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3957D4E0.EDFE4D01@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> The June 19th Computerworld had an article by Ed Yourdon, in which he
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> easy.

Especially from Yourdon, who was as wrong as a person could be about the
Y2K rollover (anyone remember that?). He bought into the & spouted "The
End of the World As We Know It" theory & moved to Santa Fe. Nice place
to live, but he moved for the wrong reason. Had a book out, also,
entitled something about "Timebomb".

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Legacy systems in cybertime

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3958A443.39442B5A@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<3957D4E0.EDFE4D01@cusys.edu> <395812C5.B5865FC8@worldnet.att.net>`

```


William Lynch wrote:
> 
> Especially from Yourdon, who was as wrong as a person could be about the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

He's been wrong more often than not.  But he HAS been influential,
although I doubt this article will influence many.  The reality behind
it might, but maybe cybertime is a throw away society.  Maybe people who
build quick applications actually WILL throw them away as scheduled,
replacing them with the next generation.  

Stuck in a mainframe Cobol world, I see companies keeping their tools
way past the original plans, but it is possible that the larger world is
different.
```

#### ↳ Re: Legacy systems in cybertime

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slhhk3odjev167@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3957D4E0.EDFE4D01@cusys.edu>`

```
"Legacy" systems exist because they service some crucial need.
They aren't going to go away, even if the original programmers do,
regardless if they are written in COBOL (the first legacy language :-)
or  C# (Microsoft's "new" language, about to be become a source of
legacy code).

The only hope for managing such legacy systems is
a) better design information capture
b) better tools for handling/inspecting/updating the design information
     and the the code.

-- IDB



Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:3957D4E0.EDFE4D01@cusys.edu...

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-af87eead2e"></a>
## Thread: [Fwd: STRING, UNstring & INSPECT are not recommended] — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### [Fwd: STRING, UNstring & INSPECT are not recommended]

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.cics-l
- **Message-ID:** `<39587336.17BE2312@istar.ca>`

```
I am forwarding this to comp.lang.cobol to verify my impression that VS
COBOL II, COBOL for MVS and VM and COBOL for MVS and VM have no
restrictions on the use of STRING, UNSTRING and INSPECT.  All of these
are 31 bit compilers.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

-------- Original Message --------
Subject: STRING, UNstring & INSPECT are not recommended
Date: Tue, 27 Jun 2000 09:14:00 GMT
From: Patrick Lee <patricklee@whbhk.com>
Organization: Deja.com - Before you buy.
Newsgroups: bit.listserv.cics-l

Dear all,
According to IBM APAR II06138, we should not use STRING, UNSTRING,
INSPECT because they will eat up our 24bit Getvis area. Any
recommendation of alternatives for these 3 words????

Thanks.

Patrick.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: STRING, UNstring & INSPECT are not recommended]

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.cics-l
- **Message-ID:** `<8jalpm$d5t$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39587336.17BE2312@istar.ca>`

```
I believe that you are correct.  I tried to find this documented in the COBOL
manuals, but couldn't (easily).  HOWEVER, I can find the list of what "is"
restricted under the current COBOL with CICS - and it doesn't include those
verbs.  Check out:


http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYPG204/4%2e10%2e1%2e6
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: STRING, UNstring & INSPECT are not recommended

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <Kerry.Ellis@msdw.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.cics-l
- **Message-ID:** `<3959284F.3C8D8027@msdw.com>`
- **References:** `<39587336.17BE2312@istar.ca> <8jalpm$d5t$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
OS/VS Cobol was oblivious to the CICS environment.  The Inspect,
String and Unstring statements invoked routines that issued OS (not
CICS) Getmains.  Because OS/VS Cobol programs had to be term-
inated with an EXEC CICS RETURN, the Cobol runtime routines
were never given a chance to clean up; eventually, the CICS region
would run out of what used to be called "OSCOR" and incur an OS
abend such as S80A.  Nowadays, with Cobol II and the "new" LE
conforming compilers, all run time storage acquisition requests are
handled by environment specific library routines, so Inspect, String
and Unstring are given CICS DSA storage that is not different than
any other piece of transaction storage.  In addition, such programs
can be ended with a STOP RUN or an EXEC CICS RETURN;
both will go through normal program/run unit cleanup; however,
any task duration storage that is missed will be freed by task term-
ination.  (BTW, the restrictions against String, Unstring and Inspect
were dropped IIRC with the arrival of the Cobol II compiler which
was æons ago.)

Kerry


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I believe that you are correct.  I tried to find this documented in the COBOL
> manuals, but couldn't (easily).  HOWEVER, I can find the list of what "is"
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu>`

```
Hi Madeline,

The Fujitsu thing is a COBOL development tool for PC's I think.

Personally, if you were developing cobol programs on PC's or on Unix, I
would recommend the MicroFocus products from Merant.

For more information go to www.merant.com/products/microfocus

Regards,
Paul


"cathy wang" <1PPW2264@mtsac.edu> wrote in message

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395B9934.DD29B954@netins.net>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
For FUJITSU check out "http:\\www.adtools.com". They have a free (though a
bit dated) version of COBOL for use in windows system.  A better source for
the compiler, because it includes some hand holding as well, is Thane
Hubbell's book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours".  You can also get a
slightly newer version of the compiler (though with fewer add-on's) in
"Mastering COBOL", a rather massive reference work.

Merant and Fujitsu seem to be the major source of COBOL compilers for the
MS Windows environment.  Both have educational programs that allow
discounts to students and educational institutions.

As to which is best, it would seem to be up to the individual instructor
and/or institution to make that decision.  I am still trying to decide to
what would be best for my students.  I am in the midst of evaluating these
two systems and hoping to make a decision sometime early next year.  I will
need to notify our ITS department sometime in early spring so they can
purchase and install the chosen product prior the start of the fall 2001
semester.

Hope that helps.


> "cathy wang" <1PPW2264@mtsac.edu> wrote in message
> news:395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395BAB7C.32E181@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395B9934.DD29B954@netins.net>`

```
Not having used it I don't know how Fujitsu compares to Merant, but I found
MicroFocus (Merant) COBOL Workbench (obsolete version now-they've changed the
name to Net Express, I think) to be both pricey ($2500/person IIRC) and
difficult to learn. The learning difficulty is the development environment BTW,
not the language. (Learned COBOL on the mainframe about 14 years ago, which
probably makes me a relative newcomer.)

Might have just been me, but we started moving from mainframe to Windows about
5 years ago. (Single-person project, me.) After 6 months with no progress (just
couldn't figure out how to set up an application in Workbench-version 3 at that
time) I finally got approval for an expensive ($4K IIRC) class from MicroFocus.
With that kickstart things have gone pretty smoothly since. The only other PC
languages I've used are Realia COBOL for DOS and, recently, VB. I found
Workbench's interface much more difficult to learn, but possibly the best I've
used once I learned it.

Floyd Johnson wrote:

> For FUJITSU check out "http:\\www.adtools.com". They have a free (though a
> bit dated) version of COBOL for use in windows system.  A better source for
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

```
I remember the Workbench you are talking about and I know what you mean.
However, you will find that NetExpress 3.1 is very far removed from that
older product. It's not a name change: it's a modern, very easy to use
graphical IDE similar to the one's available for C++, Java etc but for a
language that is actually maintainable, self documenting etc etc.

Calvin,

I had similar issues myself when I joined Micro Focus nearly eight years
ago.  Even then I was used to using Windows development tools and it
took me a long time to get my head around the Workbench IDE.

However, I would also agree with you that once learnt it is very good for
COBOL development.  The IDE in Net Express is different again and
conforms to the standard Windows interface and the learning curve is
greatly reduced.

All the best

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.
