###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net>`

```
Ah! you got me!!

Actually, I am not on commission. Working for Merant, you could say that I
was baised but let me just say this: one of the reasons I joined this
company just less than a year ago is that I was already a user of the
MicroFocus products of some years standing and had begun to use NetExpress,
and as such I beleive in COBOL's continued future and I beleive in the
current products and solutions. I would not have joined the company
otherwise.

I will not go into all the stuff about the benefits of using COBOL, that
stuff is already covered in other postings to this news group. What I will
mention is that Merant are in the driving seat as far as the development of
the cobol language is concerned and the vast majority of the additions to
COBOL in the next release of COBOL (due 2002/2003) are MicroFocus
innovations and our NetExpress (PC) and ServerExpress (Unix) products
already support them.

Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<395ccda3.53651730@207.126.101.100>`

```
... Posted and mailed ...

On Fri, 30 Jun 2000 08:51:06 +0100, "Paul Barnett"
<paul.barnett@merant.com> wrote:


>I will not go into all the stuff about the benefits of using COBOL, that
>stuff is already covered in other postings to this news group. What I will
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Very ambitious statements Paul.

Does NE support User Defined Functions?
Does NE support the Repository Statement?
Does NE support numeric items of 31 digits?
Does NE support "VALIDATE"?
Does NE support BASED STORAGE?
Does NE support Conditional Compilation?
Does NE support using Functions in expressions other than COMPUTE?
Does NE support PIC 1 Items?  b_OR, b_AND, b_XOR etc?
Does NE support PIC N Items?
Does NE support the new binary items (Float-Long, etc? -- I'm not
talking usage COMP-1 and COMP-2 Either)
Does NE support "Standard Arithmetic"?  This is a real sore spot with
me and my experience with MF products -- the Math results vary from
release to release and differ from INT to GNT and are often different
from other platforms?

I'm not saying ANY vendor does, but I just listed "A Majority" of the
changes in the new standard that I want BADLY to make use of.  I don't
*THINK* Merant supports ANY of the things I listed yet. (Please,
correct me if I am wrong!)  Want to make up some ground, and get on
teh leading edge in COBOL Compiler technology?  Support the above
ASAP!
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jir00$7hh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395ccda3.53651730@207.126.101.100>`

```
I could be mistaken, but I think there are a number (definitely NOT ALL) of
the items that you mention that ARE already supported by N/E - some with
different syntax than the draft - but that is true of everyone supporting
anything in the draft.  Included in what is supported (already) from your
list:

 Does NE support numeric items of 31 digits?
 Does NE support Conditional Compilation?
 Does NE support using Functions in expressions other than COMPUTE?
 b_OR, b_AND, b_XOR  (functionality - not syntax)
 Does NE support BASED STORAGE? (functionality - not syntax)

If you aren't certain how to use any of these in the current product, let me
(or your MERANT support channel) know - and I suspect that we can help you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jj941$mvt$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395ccda3.53651730@207.126.101.100> <8jir00$7hh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <395d1322.71445799@207.126.101.100>`

```
maybe not a function persay, but using protypes, you can create your own
verbs...

Merant cobol

       Program-ID. "cmEnableWindow" is external.
      ******************************************************************
      * Enable a window or dialog control...                           *
      * Input = hWindow                - handle of window to be enabled*
      * Output= N/A                                                    *
      * Cut and Paste the following sample implementation...           *
      ******************************************************************
      *    cmEnableWindow WindowHandle is ??????
      *                                OR                              *
      *    cmEnableWindow ??????
      ******************************************************************
       Special-Names.
       Call-Convention 74 is WinAPI.
       Linkage Section.
       copy "W003SD.CPY".
       01 hWindow                      handle.
       Procedure Division WinAPI using by value hWindow
                 invoked as == cmEnableWindow
                               [Windowhandle is] <hWindow> ==.
       End Program "cmEnableWindow".



      $Set case Defaultcalls"74"
       Program-id. "cmDisableWindow".
       Special-Names.
       Call-Convention 74 is WinAPI.
       Working-Storage Section.
       copy "W003SD.CPY".
       01 hWindow                      handle value 0.
       01 APIReturn                    bool value 0.
       Procedure Division WinAPI using by value hWindow.

           Call WinAPI 'IsWindowEnabled'
             using by value hWindow
             returning APIReturn.

           If APIReturn equal 0
             exit program
           end-if.

           Call WinAPI "EnableWindow"
             using by value hWindow
                   by value 0
             returning APIReturn.

           Exit program.




Now you can invoke

    cmEnableWindow Hwnd.

or

  cmEnableWindow WindowHandle is Hwnd.


kenmullins
```

- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395ccda3.53651730@207.126.101.100>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
  <snip>
> Does NE support "Standard Arithmetic"?  This is a real sore spot with
> me and my experience with MF products -- the Math results vary from
> release to release and differ from INT to GNT and are often different
> from other platforms?
>

Standard Arithmetic is an "interesting" issue.  IBM has publicly stated that
they doubt that ANY customer of theirs will ever use it.  (Their users have
EXPLICITLY stated that they want the SAME results - not more
accurate/correct/portable results.)

Furthermore, since the Standard defines intermediate results in terms of a
32-digit (precision) decimal floating-point item, it is quite likely that (at
least early implementations) will perform just slightly slower than counting
on your fingers <G>.

I have always supported this feature - and think that it should be useful,
but I did want to suggest that it is an area that may OR MAY NOT ever get
used in "production" code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cutting and Pasting my COBOL code

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00

- **References:** `<7_s65.11869$_b3.286417@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <KQz65.367$Tp.85920@nnrp3.sbc.net> <TrI65.15384$NP5.361728@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
AFAIK a COBOL source code file is simply a text file (usually with a COB or CBL
extension) and can be opened in any text editor. You would need a COBOL
compiler/editor to apply syntax coloring, find & insert copy files, etc. but the
basic source code should be readable.

Billy wrote:

…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cutting and Pasting my COBOL code

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FlT65.75$gB5.65962@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<7_s65.11869$_b3.286417@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <KQz65.367$Tp.85920@nnrp3.sbc.net> <TrI65.15384$NP5.361728@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```

Billy <mcduffie95@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:TrI65.15384$NP5.361728@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
> Jerry,
>
> I do know how to send an attachment in an E-mail without looking at
the HELP
> menu. Do you know how to cut and paste the COBOL code to a regular
document?

Yes, I do. I can think of probably half a dozen. Just for starters:

1. The source code is almost assuredly an ASCII text file (in your
words, "a regular document" it just has a funny extension). Use Word,
Notepad, Wordpad, etc., click Open, click on the file. You may have to
change the browse mask to "*.*"
2. Direct the compiler output listing to a file. That FOR SURE is an
ASCII text file.
3. In the unhappy event that the original is not a uniform text file
(has tabs or other strange characters), the editor has a provision -
almost always - to produce source that is straight ASCII (Realia
editor, for example).
4. Open the editor in a DOS window. Tap [Ctrl]-[PrtScreen]. That puts
the whole thing on the Windows clipboard. From there it may be pasted
virtually anywhere.
5. Write a COBOL program to convert the (assumed bizzare) CBL file to
ASCII text and use that.

I suspect you did not know you can move something from a DOS window
(not full screen) to the Windows clipboard. In which case, your
first-time status with COBOL was not the issue and your question would
be better directed to a Windows (or Unix, or whatever) newsgroup.
```

## Thread: COBOL Student: NEED HELP! — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### COBOL Student: NEED HELP!

- **From:** cathy wang <1PPW2264@mtsac.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395AC4C3.184D013B@mtsac.edu>`

```
Hello fellow debuggers,

Im a CIS Major whose having a hard time on ADV. COBOL...
Do you have some code tips to make my studentlife  more easier..

Thanks,
Madeline
kylrock107@philmusic.zzn.com

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
> Posting-Frequency: twice a month
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Student: NEED HELP!

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000629093439.26339A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`
- **References:** `<395AC4C3.184D013B@mtsac.edu>`

```
First of All I would suggest forming a study Group.  Good Ideas are best
shared.  Second go to class and take notes.  Third read the textbook!  4th
When coding anything for the first time dont try to write the program all
at once.  Just do the simplest most fundamental thing first, like opening
and closing the file.  Often it is the operation system specific tasks
like reading a file that is the hardest.  There are always things the
instructor leaves out like what type of file access needs to be used, or
if you have to use a copy statement, or they havent made the file yet!

Here is a tip for you when you describe the file use a file-status storage
item and check the file status when you open the file and if it does not
equal '00' display what it does say.  That way if you get a dump you can
read the variable with the file status.

Also use displays so you know if the file is open at the right point.  or
if you got to the end of the process.  If you think there is an endless
loop put a counter in the middle and tell it to stop when it gets to 50.
or when you get to page 50 of the printing.

COBOL is an easy language to learn.  All programming is done one step at a
time with loops and counters.

Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program
```

- **From:** "mike" <mikman@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jJ65.1833$ozS2.38142086@tomcat.sk.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<395ACA55.CD5BB2DA@mtsac.edu>`

```
It really doesn't have anything to do with your cobol
experience. To attach a cobol program or any other file to
an email you need to open your email program (i.e. netscape
or outlook, or other that you may use if yu are in a
different environment). type your message, then find the
attach option and search for your file then send the email.
thats it.

If you are working in a linux or unix environment from a
University that is text based, and no graphical user
interface it is still possible I'm sure but you would be
better off asking one of the network administrators how to
do it...

Hope this helps
Mike
```

```
First tip.  Post your message ONCE instead of 10 times.  We see it.

Next - what area are you having trouble in?  If it's just in general,
I not so humbly suggest that you pick up a copy of my book - Sams
Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.  It will give you the good grounding
and introduction you need.  It's chock full of code examples.

Also, if you post the compiler you are using for the course - brand
and version - we can be more specific in our assistance.
```

<a id="t-4979350ea8"></a>
## Thread: rebuild error — 2 msgs

### rebuild error

- **From:** "Christopher Vella" <chrisv@kemmunet.net.mt>
- **Date:** 2000-06-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jd6e81200i@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Hi,

I ran rebuild on my data files and got this message:

OORL01.dat***Error on input file - status 9-041 - records read =         0

Version:                              cobol v4.1.30-e (updated from
ftp.merant.com)
OS:                                    SCO Unix Openserver 5.05

Can anyone tell me what the error means and how to fix it

Thanks and regards,
Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: rebuild error

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jf2sb$dcu$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8jd6e81200i@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Chris,

A 9/41 error normally indicates that the index portion of the file
has become corrupt.  The normal way to resolve this is to delete
the index and to recreate it.  This is done by specifying the key
structure on the REBUILD command via the /K switch.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International

Christopher Vella <chrisv@kemmunet.net.mt> wrote in message
news:8jd6e81200i@enews3.newsguy.com...
> Hi,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL and MS Windows 2000 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL and MS Windows 2000

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395A6810.E69B96B8@netins.net>`

```
This is a follow-up to a conversation we had last spring about the issue
of compatibility of Fujitsu COBOL and MS Win 2000.

I will be teaching my annual section of "File Processing Using COBOL"
this coming Fall.  My plan is to continue Fujitsu COBOL (v. 3) in the
classroom.  We have enough copies of this product to allow its
installation in the classroom and students will receive personal copies
with their textbook (Thane's "TY COBOL in 24 Hours").  I have ordered
one copy of Fujitsu COBOL (v. 5) (thanks to Roger Johnson) for my own
use.

If my use of v. 5 goes well (I expect it will), we will either adopt
Fujitsu v. 5 (Standard or Professional) or NetExpress (University
Edition) for use in the Fall 2001 semester.  My experience with COBOL is
primary with the Fujitsu product, though Will Price did provide a
week-long introduction last summer to NetExpress' OO and web programming
features.  A final decision is still six months away.

As best I remember from our earlier conversation, both Fujitsu version 3
and version 5 of the compiler functioned satisfactorily in the WIN 2000
environment.  I think I remember some mention of there being
compatibility problems with Fujitsu version 4.

Given that we have now gone 4 to 5 months since that early advice, does
anyone have additional facts or anecdotes that would help with this
summer's upgrade?

Thank you for any feedback and advice.  Your help is always appreciated.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL and MS Windows 2000

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jf2m7$dcq$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<395A6810.E69B96B8@netins.net>`

```
Floyd,

I have taken the liberty of forwarding your note to our academic programme
manager and asked her to contact you.  I can tell you that Net Express 3.0 +
SP1 and Net Express 3.1 are fully compatible with Windows 2000.

Details of MERANT's academic programme can be found at:

http://www.merant.com/services/aprograms/

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman

MERANT International.
```

<a id="t-8ebbf61bec"></a>
## Thread: Sending Code in a Message — 1 msgs

### Sending Code in a Message

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000629094610.26339B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
It may be best to just cut and paste.  If you send a bunch of
stuff from something like a Fujitsu compiler or a MAC computer it can not
be read unless it is in text (ASCII) format.  Never had a problem with a
compiler.  I always did a copy inside the compiler and opened notepad and
pasted what I copied.  Most of the time the file extension is program.cbl
or something like that and that confuses notepad Or past the code right
into the message itself. 

Usually a dos compiler saves the source as text and then changes the file
extension so just try changing it to: program.txt(make a copy first).
Or try "copy a:\program.cbl a:\program.txt"
If you are using a specific compiler find out what it is and tell the
newsgroup.

Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program
```

<a id="t-6daac9c8c0"></a>
## Thread: Splitting output file — 10 this month / 27 total

### Splitting output file

- **From:** petert@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi all
I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that is over 8Gb
in size.
I want to split the output and produce say 4 files.  Can anyone think of
a way to write say 10000 records to one file then the next 10000 to the
second file and so on until finished?
Or should I sort the output in a second step to create the four files?
Any help would be appreciated
Thanks
Peter


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000630103332.01242.00000521@nso-cd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, petert@my-deja.com writes:

>I want to split the output and produce say 4 files.  Can anyone think of
>a way to write say 10000 records to one file then the next 10000 to the
>second file and so on until finished?
>Or should I sort the output in a second step to create the four files?
>

It all depends on the needs of the following processes.
What is going to use the output files from this program?
is there any requirement for order?
I would first consider keeping a bytes-written counter to force
splitting the files on a specific byte-count boundary, keeping the
original order of the file intact.  If the original order is inconsequential,
then I would pass the output thru a sort job that would SPLIT the file
into a predetermined set of files containing an equal number of records
regardless of overall file size.

There would seem to be a need for more details regarding the Environment
this is to operate in (I assume that the target is a PC [2G file size] and 
the origin is a Mainframe [8G file]).  The purpose/use of the resulting 
SPLIT output files.  ....
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395D14B6.29D7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
petert@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi all
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any help would be appreciated

If you do it in a second step, at least you'll know how much data you are dealing with 
because you can count it on the first step.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395D1593.E9803C68@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <395D14B6.29D7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```


Ed Guy wrote:
> 
> petert@my-deja.com wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> because you can count it on the first step.

So what do you do, write out a record with the record count and read it
in with a second cobol program?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395D5F75.1C91@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <395D14B6.29D7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <395D1593.E9803C68@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ed Guy wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> in with a second cobol program?

Actually, I used C++, but the principal is the same.  I wrote out every record to an 
intermediate file and counted them as I was doing it.  Then I closed the file, opened it 
and did reads of the intermediates and writes to a permanent file.  When you have 
written enough records for a reasonable distribution between files, you close the output 
file and start a new one with the next record from the intermediate file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wp975.2607$Tr1.173422@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Without using dynamic allocation (which I know can be done by I don;t know
how to do it), the only way I can think of is to use multiple SELECT..
ASSIGN statements in the program and a corresponding number of DD names in
JCL, and counting, as in..

SELECT FILE1 ASSIGN FILE1
SELECT FILE2 ASSIGN FILE2
..
SELECT FILEn ASSIGN FILEn

....

999-WRITE-OUTPUT.

  EVALUATE RECORDS-WRITTEN
   WHEN < 10000
     WRITE FILE1-RECORD
   WHEN <20000
     WRITE FILE2-RECORD
    ...
   WHEN <  (n* 10000)
     WRITE FILEn-RECORD


(Crude, but so is my brother-in law)

MCM

<petert@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi all
> I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that is over 8Gb
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or should I sort the output in a second step to create the four files?
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xc_65.10126$HD6.275274@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <petert@my-deja.com> wrote:
>Hi all
>I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that is over 8Gb
>in size.
>I want to split the output and produce say 4 files.

Many folks want to do a variety of things... might I ask what is the
reason which causes you to have this desire?  Years ago I wrote an I-O
module for a company too lazy to clean out its VSAM files and it would be
best, I'd say, to re-think your design in order to avoid such things.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395C9D91.915F10FB@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xc_65.10126$HD6.275274@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


NA wrote:
> 
> In article <8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <petert@my-deja.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> best, I'd say, to re-think your design in order to avoid such things.

I remember in olden times when we didn't have big DASD space.  One
technique to create large sorted files was to create a bunch of small
sorted files and then merge them.  The trick then was to find out how
many tape drives we would likely have available to figure out how many
generations of merging we needed to plan for.
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395C9CC8.6257F5DA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


petert@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi all
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> second file and so on until finished?

That's an odd question which looks as if you have never programmed
before.  You use a counter and an IF statement.

> Or should I sort the output in a second step to create the four files?
> Any help would be appreciated

Now this implies you have some familiarity with mainframe processes. 
I'm pretty sure a sort utility can do this and I would like to see some
replies to this part of your question.  (I am afraid people will assume
your question is homework and not get around to reading this part of
it).

One note is that neither of these methods will size the output files
depending on the total size.  You might consider opening all 4 files
simultaneously and then taking turns writing to each in turn.  That way
they will all be within a record of the same size when you're done.
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395CA207.885A9D12@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
petert@my-deja.com wrote:

> I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that is over 8Gb
> in size.
> I want to split the output and produce say 4 files.  Can anyone think of
> a way to write say 10000 records to one file then the next 10000 to the
> second file and so on until finished?

Perform a write paragraph to handle all output to your file(s).  On the
basis of a counter it would write to the proper file or close and reopen
with a different name as needed.
```

<a id="t-4dcf1ccc4e"></a>
## Thread: Interface directly with OCX from NetExpress — 3 msgs

### Interface directly with OCX from NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000630115708.01769.00000706@ng-cq1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I have an OCX that I would like to be able to communicate with.  I have used
the "Type Library Assistant" to create a copy file.  But I am confused how to
interface that to my COBOL program.  I do not use Object COBOL and would prefer
to stay with the plain old COBOL that I am used to.  Does anyone have samples
or advice on how to interface with OCX's ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Interface directly with OCX from NetExpress

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jiov2$qsf$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000630115708.01769.00000706@ng-cq1.aol.com>`

```
bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:
>Good Afternoon,
>
>I have an OCX that I would like to be able to communicate with.  I have used
>the "Type Library Assistant" to create a copy file.  But I am confused how to
>interface that to my COBOL program.  

The Type Library Assistant has two uses:
1) To generate details about OLE Automation interfaces found in a type 
library in COBOL format, such as enumerations, ids and so forth, including 
commented out method descriptions. These can then be used when talking to 
Automation servers using the Object COBOL OLE domain support - most 
documentation etc. gives these values and details in C or VB, so the 
Assistant provides a valuable COBOL utility for this.

2) To generate OLE class library-like skeleton classes which can be edited 
to provide access to "raw" (non-Automation) interfaces that the type 
library describes. See the OLE class library sources, shipped with Net 
Express, for examples of how "raw" COM interfaces can be used/called from 
COBOL.

>I do not use Object COBOL and would prefer
>to stay with the plain old COBOL that I am used to.  Does anyone have samples
>or advice on how to interface with OCX's ?

Using Object COBOL is much easier for any COM work, as COM maps much 
better to instances of interfaces than "normal" (non-OO) COBOL. 
If you don't want to use the Object COBOL support, you need a decent 
understanding of COM - such as "interfaces are pointers to pointers to a 
table of procedure pointers (functions)" - and using such pointers in 
COBOL.

There is an example of using OCXs (which is an ActiveX control - ie 
usually has a GUI interface) in
\netexpress\base\demo\oledemos\contain\ocx
and
\netexpress\dialogsystem\demo\ocxds

Most of the other demos in \oledemos just use OLE automation servers, such 
as Word or Excel. If what you want to use is just an automation server, 
these would be a better bet.

Steve.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Interface directly with OCX from NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000630133623.04085.00000840@ng-fe1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8jiov2$qsf$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Hi Steve,

Thanks I will give it a look.

Bob
```

## Thread: Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ? — 4 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ?

- **From:** "canfin-edp" <canfinhomes@vsnl.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jia4m$iab$1@news.vsnl.net.in>`

```
Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ?

Thank you

Ellen Mandhana
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ?

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395CC870.14A0A378@netins.net>`
- **References:** `<8jia4m$iab$1@news.vsnl.net.in>`

```
You are in luck.  Last week I was cleaning off my shelves and put two
books on MS COBOL out on our freebie shelf.  However, and here is the
good news,  students will not be here to claim them for another two
months.

The books are:

     "Microsoft COBOL" by Ken Seidel (Dilithium Press, 1983).
     "Microcomputing in COBOL:Structured Interactive Applications"
          by Roy Martin Richards (Science Research Associates, 1987)

Both books claim to use MS COBOL as a base, though they do not detail
which version they are describing.  I expect that they are pretty
generic,
though; I used them to teach myself Ryan-McFarland (?) COBOL on an old
TRS-80 Model-I.

You are welcome to them, if we can find a way to get them to you.  Let
me know if I can be of help.

Floyd Johnson

====================================================

canfin-edp wrote:

> Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ?
>
> Thank you
>
> Ellen Mandhana
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ?

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-06-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jho2j$nge$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8jia4m$iab$1@news.vsnl.net.in>`

```
Ellen,

Grief, you are going to have your work cut out on this one.  I believe
Microsoft
COBOL 2.1 was their own product i.e. it was not a rebadged version of
Micro Focus COBOL.

The last version of Microsoft COBOL was 5.0.  This was dropped by
Microsoft in 1995 and was based on Micro Focus COBOL 3.0 (16 bit).

Micro Focus did not receive a documentation from Microsoft when we took
over support of their existing Customers in 1995.  If no one on this group
has
a manual then you may want to consider upgrading to a later COBOL
product.

MERANT can supply both 16 and 32 bit COBOL development tools for
Windows, NT and UNIX.  There are accedemic programmes available as
well to help students wishing to learn COBOL.

For more details see the MERANT web site at:

http://www.merant.com/products/microfocus/

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

```
Getting ready for the "soon to be announced" move of the COBOL FAQ, I have
been going thru my old (and VERY OLD) list of "to do" items for updating the
FAQ.  I have updated most of the things that I think are fairly significant
or pressing (but do have LOTS of minor stuff still to do - whenever <G>).

I would appreciate anyone who thinks that they have sent me updates to verify
that the current version includes them (and includes them as you WANTED
them).

Having said that, let me warn you that part of the reason for the
soon-to-be-announced move is that I have been "close" to running out of
bandwidth at the end of the last few months.  THEREFORE, on June 30th, some
of you may get an "unable to access" message - if too many people look at it
today.  If this happens, it should be visible again tomorrow.

As always, the FAQ can (currently) be viewed at:

 http://home.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

Please feel free to email me (don't reply to this NG post) or post to the NG
with any comments or corrections that you have.
```

```
Subject: Programmers with COBOL and Insurance expertise wanted


Job Title: ..................... Programmer / Analyst
Term: .......................... Full Time
Location: ...................... Chicago, IL
Salary: ........................ Negotiable
Reply to: ...................... brisco@enteract.com

Responsibilities:

o Create COBOL systems to interface with client insurance administration
systems.
   This includes but is not limited to:
    o Writing the extract programs in COBOL.
    o Producing the documentation for the system.
    o Debugging and regression testing the system.
    o Installing the system at the client site.
    o Assisting the client with questions.


Requirements:

o College diploma or University degree in Computer Science or equivalent;
o Minimum of 2 years of COBOL programming experience;
o Fluent with a PC and the Windows NT Operating System;
o Working knowledge of Microsoft Access;
o Excellent communication and interpersonal skills for communication with
clients;
o Ability to work independently as well as with a team;


Nice to have but not required:
o Insurance / Actuarial experience in UL, Annuity, Traditional Life
Insurance
o MicroFocus NetExpress Experience
o Knowledge of and/or experience in UNIX, Visual Basic, MS Access, or FoxPro


Company:
o We are a consulting firm located in downtown Chicago close to the train
stations.
o We provide an excellent working environment.
o Most work is project orientated.
```
